pages.title: Opening
pages.text: # The ASEN 3501 Journey: From Science Fair to Test Engineer Welcome to ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods. This course is one of the places where engineering starts to feel real. Up to this point, a lot of your work has been about getting the right equation, the right answer, or the right plot. Those skills matter. But sooner than you think, someone is going to ask you a harder question: **Why should I trust your prediction?** That question is where this course lives. It is the difference between finishing a homework problem and making an engineering decision. When a team is deciding whether hardware is ready, whether a simulation is credible, or whether a design change actually helped, confidence has to be earned. You earn it by knowing what was predicted, what was measured, how uncertain each piece is, whether the difference matters, and what you would do next. ASEN 3501 is about building that kind of judgment. ## Where This Course Fits You are taking this course in your junior year for a reason. Up to this point, much of your engineering education has focused on developing the analytical tools needed to predict behavior: physics, math, dynamics, structures, fluids, thermodynamics, controls, and computation. Those tools are essential. But in practice, every prediction eventually has to face the physical world. ASEN 3501 is the physical modeling side of that bridge. It runs in parallel with computational methods because the two courses are connected. Computational methods help you generate predictions from models. Experimental methods teaches you how to test those predictions, quantify what you do and do not know, and decide whether the evidence is strong enough for the engineering decision in front of you. That bridge matters again in your senior year. In capstone, you will not simply be asked to build something that looks plausible. You will be asked to make choices, defend those choices, test a system, interpret evidence, and communicate whether the system performs as intended. The habits in this course are meant to make you better prepared for that work: defining the claim, choosing the measurement, understanding the uncertainty, comparing against a model, and explaining what the result actually supports. And after graduation, this is not a classroom exercise anymore. This is what engineers are paid to do. Professional engineers are often asked to answer questions where the stakes are real: Will this aircraft component survive the load case? Does this sensor measure the quantity we think it measures? Is this simulation credible enough to support a design decision? What evidence would change our mind? A test engineer is the person who can turn those questions into a defensible experiment. ## How The Course Works The course is flipped by design. That means you are expected to read the assigned material and watch the short primer recordings before class. We will use the beginning of class for clarification questions, then spend our time working through examples, comparisons, and lab-connected problems that help you make progress on the assignments. The lab activities are the center of the course. They are where the concepts become real. You can read about uncertainty, model comparison, sensor limits, calibration, and validation, but you do not really internalize those ideas until you have to use them on a physical system, make a choice, and defend the result. This course also uses open educational resources wherever possible to keep your out-of-pocket costs low. AI has also been used in the design and delivery of the course to improve feedback quality, reduce turnaround time, and create more personalized support. You will not be required to use AI, but you will be encouraged to develop fluency with it through curated resources and targeted examples, because evaluating AI-assisted technical work is becoming part of professional engineering judgment. ## The Sequence We begin with a deliberately simple lab: electrical power, voltage, current, and uncertainty. The setup is not simple because the course lacks ambition. It is simple because the first thing you need is the language of measurement. A number by itself is incomplete. If you report a measured value without uncertainty, method, units, and a defensible interpretation, you have not yet produced an engineering result. You have produced a reading. From there, we move into Tier 1. Tier 1 takes experiments that may feel closer to science fair in complexity and uses them to build real engineering habits. You will execute an experiment, compare measurement methods or sensors, propagate uncertainty, and connect data to a theoretical model. Then you will do something more important: you will make a prediction before seeing the data. That blind prediction step matters because it forces you to commit to your reasoning before the result can influence your story. In Tier 1, the main lesson is not that your prediction should be perfect. It will not be. The lesson is that discrepancy is information. When the model and measurement disagree, the right question is not "How do I explain this away?" The right question is "What does this difference reveal about the model, the measurement system, the assumptions, or the uncertainty?" That mindset is the beginning of test engineering. Tier 2 raises the level. Now you are working with a more complex aerospace experiment, and the prediction-before-measurement requirement becomes central. Before your group touches the equipment, you will develop a predictive model, identify the expected measurement behavior, define what agreement would mean, and think through what patterns would indicate a problem. This is not procedural gatekeeping. It is the point. A good prediction can reveal that your sensor is inappropriate, your expected signal is too small, your sampling strategy is flawed, or your acceptance criterion is meaningless before you spend time collecting data. After the Tier 2 experiment, your job is to compare prediction and outcome, diagnose the discrepancy, review calibration and measurement limits, and propose targeted improvements. Not generic improvements. Not "use better sensors" as a magic phrase. Targeted and justified improvements: what specifically limits the experiment, why that limitation matters, and what change would most improve the credibility or usefulness of the result. The course culminates in Tier 3. In Tier 3, you are not handed a fully specified experiment. You design one. The deliverable is a defensible experimental design and verification and validation plan for an aerospace-grade question. You will define the model claim, identify the variables and controls, select instrumentation and data acquisition choices, estimate expected uncertainty, define validation metrics and acceptance criteria, cite relevant standards and prior art, and defend the design orally to a technical audience. This final project is substantial because the professional version of this work is substantial. Before an engineering organization builds a test, someone has to decide whether the proposed experiment is worth running, what data it would produce, whether the instruments are appropriate, whether the uncertainty is small enough to answer the question, and whether the result would actually inform the model. By the end of ASEN 3501, you should be able to make that argument. ## The Mindset The deeper goal of this course is not to make you memorize a particular lab procedure. The goal is to change how you think when you see a claim about a physical system. You should start asking: What is the model? What does it predict? What evidence would support it? What uncertainty remains? What would make the prediction fail? What experiment would tell us something useful before we commit to a design decision? That mindset is what separates engineering from observation, trial-and-error, and technical performance without judgment. Engineers do not just produce artifacts. Engineers make defensible claims about systems. They know what evidence would support those claims, what evidence would weaken them, and how much confidence the decision deserves. That is the journey of this course: from simple measurements to uncertainty, from uncertainty to model comparison, from model comparison to prediction, from prediction to validation, and from validation to defensible experimental design. We start with something that looks almost like science fair. We end with the mindset of a test engineer.

pages.title: Syllabus
pages.text: # ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods ## Course Syllabus — Draft v1 ### [Semester TBD] | University of Colorado Boulder --- ## Contact Information **Instructor:** Bobby Hodgkinson **Email:** hodgkinr@colorado.edu **Office Location:** AERO 150D **Office Hours:** By request — use the scheduling link provided on Canvas. **Response time:** Emails will be answered within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. --- ## Instructor Bio Bobby Hodgkinson — Associate Teaching Professor, Smead Aerospace Engineering Sciences Hi everyone. I'm Bobby Hodgkinson, and I'll be your instructor for this course. My background is in vehicle controls, with experience working on aerial unmanned systems (UAS) and underwater vehicles, where I've tackled challenges in dynamic modeling, system stability, automation, sensors, and actuators. I'm passionate about applying dynamics and controls principles to real-world aerospace problems and helping students bridge the gap between theory and practice — which is exactly what this course is designed to do. Outside of engineering, I'm interested in the intersection of AI and education. I serve on the executive board of the Rocky Mountain AI Interest Group, a regional non-profit focused on exploring and promoting the ethical use of AI in our community. ASEN 3501 will engage with AI directly — not as a subject of study, but as a professional reality that shapes how I've designed the course, the assessments, and the expectations for your work. --- ## About the Course ### Course Description ASEN 3501 develops the skills required to design, execute, and critically evaluate aerospace experiments with engineering discipline. The overarching goal is to train test engineers — professionals who design tests, characterize systems, and validate models for engineering organizations. The course is organized around a single question that opens Week 1 and closes Week 15: *How do you know this prediction is right?* Students work through three progressively demanding segments — executing experiments they are given (E1: Weeks 1–5), predicting before measuring in complex aerospace systems (E2: Weeks 6–10), and designing their own experiments from scratch (E3: Weeks 11–15) — with the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework (AMVF) as the organizing structure throughout. By the end of the course, students are not just able to run an experiment and report a number; they can justify belief in a result, quantify what they don't know, and design experiments that support credible engineering decisions. This is the physical modeling branch of the curriculum — the connection between mathematical models and the physical world. ### Course Learning Outcomes By the end of this course, students will be able to: CLO 1. **Design and conduct** aerospace experiments by applying measurement fundamentals, instrumentation principles, and best practices in small team environments. CLO 2. **Apply** statistical methods and uncertainty analysis to quantify experimental error and establish confidence in results. CLO 3. **Integrate** theoretical models with experimental data to validate and evaluate aerospace system behavior. CLO 4. **Implement** signal conditioning, sampling, and data acquisition techniques to ensure accurate experimental measurements. CLO 5. **Communicate** experimental results effectively through technical reports and presentations, with clear description of methods, results, and limitations. CLO 6. **Identify, evaluate, and create** experimental methods or instrumentation beyond those provided by the instructor to foster independent professional growth. ### Prerequisites Completion of [ASEN XXXX / statics, dynamics, or introductory aerospace engineering — TBD by instructor] or instructor approval. Students are expected to have prior exposure to MATLAB and to the fundamental mathematics required to interpret physical models (calculus, basic linear algebra, introductory physics). No prior laboratory methods experience is required. ### Required Materials - **No required textbook.** There is no textbook for this course. Instead, open educational resources and other course materials will be linked as appropriate through Canvas and related course materials. - **Reference standards:** ASME PTC 19.1 and the GUM provide the course's measurement-uncertainty basis. ASME V&V 10-2006 (*Computational Solid Mechanics*) and ASME V&V 20 (*Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer*) are domain examples used when relevant. Students will explain why a selected standard applies rather than cite one mechanically. - **Supplementary reference:** Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*, 4th edition — available through CU libraries and on reserve. Specific chapters assigned as the course progresses. - **Course uncertainty convention:** The Week 2 one-page reference defines the required Coleman & Steele–aligned terminology. Every reported or plotted interval must identify its type, method, and coverage basis. - **MATLAB** is the primary required computing platform for all labs and assignments. Available at no cost through CU Boulder's license — installation instructions on Canvas. Students who prefer to use Python (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) or another platform may do so, but **the teaching team will prioritize assistance for MATLAB first and foremost**. Students using other platforms are responsible for translating any provided MATLAB code, functions, or toolbox references on their own. No special accommodation will be made for non-MATLAB workflows during lab sessions or office hours. - **GitHub account** (free) — required for course documentation and portfolio. A tutorial is provided in Week 1. Students are not expected to have prior GitHub experience. - **Project supplies:** Students may choose to purchase supplies for project activities (no required purchase). The approach is similar to first-year projects — if you want to run your chosen experiment with your own materials, that is encouraged but never required. Students are encouraged to **keep any components they acquire** during course activities — sensors, microcontrollers, breadboards, or other hardware — as these may be useful in future senior design projects. --- ## Communication Canvas is the primary channel for course-wide announcements. Enable your Canvas notifications so you do not miss time-sensitive information. Email is appropriate for individual coordination, emergencies, and course-related concerns that should not be discussed publicly. Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. Responses will be provided within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). If you have not received a response within 48 hours, a follow-up email is appropriate. In the event of a medical or family emergency, contact the Office of Student Support and Case Management at https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm. They will help coordinate across all of your courses and connect you with campus resources. --- ## Method of Instruction ASEN 3501 is an in-person laboratory course. Sessions include lectures, hands-on lab activities, in-class discussions, oral assessments, and peer review exercises. The course is organized into three segments of five weeks each: - **E1 (Weeks 1–5):** Phase I — Execution & Quantification - **E2 (Weeks 6–10):** Phase II — Predictive Modeling & Comparative Validation - **E3 (Weeks 11–15):** Phase III — Experimental Design & V&V Planning Some assigned content — readings, reference standards, AI literacy viewing, and tutorial materials — will be completed outside of class to maximize in-person time for labs, discussions, and oral assessments. Canvas is the platform for all materials, assignment submission, and course communication. Attendance is expected. In-person sessions are the primary learning environment. Oral assessments, comparative analysis exercises, and peer evaluations normally happen in class. If a makeup is needed, email the instructor to coordinate the time and place and include **five different available options during normal business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm)**. A makeup is not confirmed until the instructor replies with an approved option. --- ## Student Responsibilities and Expectations ### Credit Hour Expectations ASEN 3501 is a 3-credit course. Per CU Boulder's credit hour policy, 3 credits corresponds to approximately **9 hours of total student effort per week** — 3 hours in class plus 6 hours of out-of-class work (reading, lab preparation, assignment completion, and portfolio documentation). Each assignment and lab document will include an estimated time to complete to help you plan accordingly. Students who are consistently exceeding these estimates should contact the instructor. ### Preparation Review assigned readings, reference materials, and any pre-class viewings before each session. Lab documents will be posted in advance — you are expected to have read them before the lab begins. Arriving unprepared to a lab session reduces your own learning and slows your team. ### Asking for Technical Feedback At some point this semester you will ask a teammate, a TA, or the instructor to look at your work. How you do that matters. Engineers do not ask "is this right?" They say: *"I'm trying to do X. Here's my approach, and here's why I think it's correct. The parts I'm least confident in are Y and Z."* That framing is not a courtesy — it is a professional discipline. It tells the reviewer exactly what to look for, demonstrates that you have thought critically about your own work, and makes the feedback you receive far more useful. Coming to a review without that preparation wastes everyone's time, including yours. Practice this habit starting in Week 1. By the time you reach your E3 oral defense, it should be automatic. ### Engagement and Attendance Active participation is expected in every session. This is not a course where you can recover missed content by watching a recording — lab activities, oral assessments, and comparative analysis exercises are conducted in person. Attendance is not formally taken, but participation is monitored and is a graded component. To request a makeup for a missed in-person assessment, email the instructor and propose **five different options during normal business hours** for an equivalent time-and-place arrangement. Failure to complete either the scheduled assessment or the coordinated makeup may result in no credit. ### Timesheet Requirement Weekly timesheets are required throughout the semester. These are a brief record of how you spent your time on course activities each week. The purpose is professional habit formation — tracking your own effort is a skill that matters in every engineering job — not surveillance. Format and submission details will be provided on Canvas. *(Details TBD — see review table.)* ### GitHub Portfolio Students will use GitHub to document and publish work throughout the course. A lightweight tutorial is provided in Week 1. By the end of the course, you will have a documented portfolio of your experimental methods work — something you can share with future employers. This is a professional skill that has immediate practical value. --- ## Course and University Policies ### Generative AI (AI Usage Policy) This policy applies to ASEN 3501. It is not a department or university policy. **The short version:** AI usage is expected. The course is designed around that assumption. The assessments are designed to reveal real understanding regardless of how content was produced. **The full version:** We are at a point in engineering practice where AI tools can generate a lab report, a data analysis, a literature review, or a slide deck in a fraction of the time it would have taken a person. That is not a problem to resist — it is a fact to prepare for. The workload expectations in this course assume that you are using AI augmentation in your work. If you are not using it, you should be. Knowing how to work effectively with AI tools is part of what it means to be a well-prepared engineer right now, and that bar will only rise. Here is what follows from that premise: **Creation is cheap.** AI can produce a technically plausible lab report with appropriately structured sections, populated figures, and reasonable-sounding conclusions. That means the ability to *produce* a report is no longer an adequate demonstration of understanding. What AI cannot do — at least not without a human who deeply understands the domain — is tell you *what is right, what is wrong, what is missing, and how to make it better.* That critical evaluation skill is what this course is designed to develop. It is also what engineering employers are increasingly paying for. **The primary assessment mechanism in this course is critical evaluation of engineering content** — not the production of engineering content. You will frequently be given a report, a data analysis, or a presentation — produced by AI, by me, by a peer, or by a prior student — and your grade will depend on the quality of your technical critique. What is correct and well-supported? What is incorrect or unsupported? What is missing? What specific change would most improve it? Answering these questions well requires real understanding that cannot be faked. **Authentic assessments counterbalance AI use in three ways:** 1. **In-person oral assessments.** You will explain your work, your choices, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team — in person, without notes, without preparation time. You cannot delegate this to AI. Everyone who passes this course will have clearly articulated an understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. 2. **In-class comparative analysis exercises.** These are closed-book, in-person exercises where you evaluate provided content. You cannot use external tools. Your grade depends on the technical accuracy and depth of your critique. 3. **The E3 oral defense.** You will defend your own experimental design to a technical audience and respond to direct questions about every decision you made. **What this is not:** This is not a policy against AI, and it is not designed to catch or punish AI use. It is a policy that acknowledges the professional reality you are entering and prepares you for it. Using AI to help draft, analyze, refine, or audit your work is not just permitted — it is encouraged. The course even includes an early viewing assignment on AI literacy. The expectation is that you use these tools intelligently and that you take responsibility for everything you submit. **Student use of AI:** Students will not be required to use AI to complete this course. However, students are strongly encouraged to use AI tools where helpful and appropriate within the guidance of the course content, assignment instructions, and learning objectives. **Transparency:** If AI tools substantially contributed to a submission, note it briefly at the end. Describe what tool you used and what you used it for. This is not a disclosure requirement designed to penalize you — it is professional practice. **What is not acceptable:** - Submitting AI-generated content as your own without engagement or modification - Using AI to fabricate data, measurements, or results - Using any external tool during closed-book, in-class assessments Students with concerns about this approach are welcome to discuss them with the instructor. The goal is to prepare you for tomorrow, not to argue about whether tomorrow is coming. *The instructional team may use AI tools to aid in feedback generation and to gain insight into student progress. We will never use AI to produce numerical scores on summative assignments. AI-augmented feedback will always be reviewed and revised by a human before it reaches you.* ### Instructional Team Use of AI The instructional team uses AI tools in the design and delivery of this course. This section describes that use openly — the same transparency we ask of students applies to us. **Why we use it:** The goals of AI use on the instructional side mirror what we want you to develop on the student side. We use it to improve the overall quality of the educational product, to reduce the turnaround time between your submission and meaningful feedback, and to increase the level of personalization we can bring to this course. A course of this size, with oral assessments and written deliverables across fifteen weeks, generates more feedback load than a traditional lecture-only course. AI helps us keep feedback timely and substantive rather than generic and delayed. Generative AI platforms including OpenAI ChatGPT, Anthropic Claude, and Google Gemini were used to aid in the creation of this course content. Images used in the course were generated primarily with OpenAI ChatGPT. The combination of open educational resources and generative AI is intended to support a high-quality, relatively low-cost, and efficient learning experience for you. **Current and anticipated use cases include:** - **AI-augmented grading and feedback.** Rubric-based assessment of written deliverables may be supported by AI tools to generate a first-pass evaluation. That draft is always reviewed, revised, and approved by a human member of the instructional team before it reaches you. AI will never produce final numerical scores on summative assignments — a human makes that call every time. - **Content creation and course development.** Lecture materials, assignment documents, readings, and supplemental resources in this course were developed with AI assistance. The instructor has verified and revised all content before it was included in the course. If you find something that looks wrong, flag it — the editorial standard applies regardless of how content was produced. - **Personalized feedback and student-specific communication.** AI tools may be used to help the instructional team craft feedback that is more specific to your work and your progress in the course, rather than relying on generic comment banks. - **Assessment design and calibration.** AI may be used to generate sample responses at different quality levels for rubric calibration, or to produce the AI-generated content used in comparative analysis exercises. **What we will not do:** We will not use AI to replace human judgment on consequential decisions — final grades, academic integrity determinations, or pass/fail calls on oral assessments. We will not use AI in ways that compromise the privacy of your submitted work beyond what is already disclosed in CU Boulder's data policies. **Want to know more?** The instructor welcomes any and all discussion related to these topics as they relate both to this course and to the bigger picture. If you have questions about how AI is being used in any part of the course — grading, content generation, feedback, or course design — ask. There is no part of this that is meant to be opaque. --- ### Lab Environment Policy Safety is the first priority in every lab session. Anyone violating safe conduct may receive a zero for that lab and may lose access to lab facilities. Use of lab facilities is a privilege. Failure to wear required safety gear will result in a 10% grade penalty per infraction. Endangering yourself, others, or lab equipment is not acceptable under any circumstances. Professional behavior and respectful communication are expected at all times. Eating and drinking inside the laboratory is prohibited. ### Lecture Recording Notice Lectures may be recorded for educational purposes. Students who do not wish to appear in recordings must notify the instructor at least 72 hours in advance. --- ## Assignments and Grading ### Submission Policies **Platform:** Assignments must be submitted via Canvas unless otherwise stated. Submissions must include the names of all contributing members. **Individuals whose name does not appear on a group submission will not receive credit for the assignment. Groups are encouraged to leave off the names of individuals who did not contribute to the lab assignment.** **File format:** Follow the file format requirements specified in each assignment. Submissions that do not follow the required format may not be accepted. **Late work:** Late submissions will not be accepted without prior approval or a documented emergency. Extension requests must be submitted at least 24 hours before the due date. **Regrade policy:** If you would like to submit a regrade request for any assignment, you must submit the request via email to the instructor within **2 weeks** of the graded assignment return date. All regrade requests will be reviewed and approved by a course instructor — not teaching assistants, teaching fellows, or lab assistants. a. The regrade request must clearly state the reason you are requesting the regrade and what you believe the correct grade to be. Note that disagreement on the established rubric allocation of points is not a valid reason for a regrade and will not be considered. b. The regrade request must include, in a single combined PDF: an introductory statement addressing the above, a PDF copy of the original submission with portions highlighted that pertain to the regrade request, and any additional supporting information. c. **Points can be added OR removed** based on correctness. If a mistake was made in grading and too few points were awarded, the regrade request may increase the final score; however, if the instructor finds a mistake was made in grading and too many points were awarded, the regrade request may lower the final score. d. Regrades submitted in the **final month** of the course will only be entertained if the regrade alters an individual's final letter grade. We reserve the right to make changes to the course schedule based on emerging circumstances. Advance notice will be provided through Canvas announcements. The current syllabus and schedule will always be posted on Canvas. ### Assessment Philosophy This course assesses two distinct capabilities: the ability to *produce* experimental engineering work, and the ability to *critically evaluate* it. Both are graded. In some assessments, the critique grade is weighted equally to or more heavily than the production grade. This is intentional. As AI tools become more capable of producing technically plausible content, the engineering skill that retains and increases in value is the ability to evaluate whether that content is correct, complete, and fit for purpose. The comparative analysis exercises in this course — where you evaluate provided reports, analyses, and presentations on technical merit — are not supplementary activities. They are the primary assessment mechanism. Every student who passes ASEN 3501 will have: - Produced structured experimental work across all three course segments - Critically evaluated provided content (AI-generated, instructor-selected, or peer-produced) in multiple in-class exercises - Articulated their understanding of key fundamentals in in-person oral assessments — multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways - Defended an original experimental design in a formal oral presentation (E3) Formative assessments are frequent and low-stakes. The purpose of formative feedback is to show you where you are relative to expectations and to help you develop, not to produce a grade. Take them seriously — students who engage with formative feedback consistently outperform those who treat it as overhead. The final third of the course (E3, Weeks 11–15) is driven by you. You choose your experimental design direction. You select your instrumentation. You define the validation logic. The instructor's role shifts from source of knowledge to critical reviewer. --- ### Grading Structure > ⚠️ **DRAFT — GRADING SCHEME NOT FINALIZED** ⚠️ > > **The complete grade structure is TBD. No gate, point, tier, threshold, reattempt, or letter-grade rule below is approved for student use until the instructor publishes a final grading supplement.** > > The following grade tier structure is a working placeholder that reflects the instructor's grading philosophy and approach. It is modeled on the ASEN 1030 tiered grading system. The specific requirements, thresholds, and weights are subject to revision before the course launches. Do not treat these as final. > > **Gate-based passing structure (intended):** The final grading scheme will incorporate a gate-based structure where each major deliverable set must be passed before progressing. Students who fail to pass all gates by two weeks before the end of term will not be eligible to participate in the final A-level expo. The specific gate definitions, draft-feedback-revise cycle, and AI+peer feedback evaluation components will be specified in the individual assignment documents. See `grade_breakdown.md` for full concept description. > > **Assignment creation note:** All assignments should include: (a) a draft-feedback-revise cycle; (b) a peer feedback step with grading on feedback quality; (c) a comparison-to-AI-feedback step; (d) an expected time-to-complete estimate; (e) a pre-submission checklist including an AI audit step. This course uses a **tiered grading system** focused on demonstrated understanding, engagement, and professional discipline — not on accumulated points. The approach is designed to reflect real-world performance expectations: meeting the minimum standard keeps you in the game; effort beyond the minimum is recognized and rewarded. **Grading Philosophy:** A grade of 'A' represents superior/excellent performance. A grade of 'B' represents good/better-than-average performance. A grade of 'C' represents competent/average performance, which is consistent with CU Boulder grading policy. Intermediate grades (C+, B–, B+, A–) will be assigned based on partial completion of the next tier's requirements, at the instructor's discretion. Individual grades may be adjusted based on instructional team observations — exemplary performance may increase a grade; consistently poor engagement may decrease one. **Final Grade Tiers (Draft)** | Requirement | C | B | A | |-------------|---|---|---| | Pass all lab reports (all required sections, adequate technical execution) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Pass minimum oral assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | 75%+ on in-class comparative analysis exercises and formative assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete all weekly timesheets | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete all E1 and E2 written deliverables | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Strong comparative analysis performance (demonstrated critique quality above threshold) | | ✓ | ✓ | | Strong E3 design project (complete V&V plan, instrumentation justification, oral defense) | | ✓ | ✓ | | Exceptional oral assessment performance across the semester | | | ✓ | | Outstanding E3 project with genuine beyond-provided-hardware investigation (CLO 6) | | | ✓ | *Note: This grading structure is a draft placeholder. Final grading scheme to be determined by instructor before course launch.* **Points System (Draft — to be revised before course launch):** This course uses a points-based participation and performance system layered on top of the pass/fail gate structure above. Points are earned above the minimum threshold and determine final letter grade. The following describes the intended system — exact point values and letter grade thresholds will be finalized before the course starts. **Point-earning activities:** - **In-lecture participation:** Questions asked, answers given, and contributions to in-class exercises during lecture — awarded based on participation and correctness. - **Supplemental activity completions:** Reading optional materials, watching supplemental recordings, completing E0_supplemental exercises — awarded upon verified completion. - **AI comparative analysis work:** Completing AI-generated content comparisons, evaluating AI feedback quality in assignments — additional points above the standard assignment grade. - **MathWorks Academy completions:** Completing MathWorks training modules (MATLAB, AI, data analysis tracks) earns bonus points. - **Approved student proposals:** Students may propose a supplemental activity (find something interesting, connect it to the course, submit for approval) — if approved, points are awarded upon completion. - **Above-expectations performance:** The grading team may issue bonus points for assignment performance that goes meaningfully beyond expectations. **Scheduling priority:** Students with higher point totals receive priority access to sign-up slots for mid-tier E2 lab assignments and in-class lightning talk time slots. Sign-up windows open in tiers: top point holders first, then remaining students. **Grade thresholds (draft — to be finalized):** Passing all core assessments (gates) earns a C−. The number of points needed for B and A grades will be published before the semester begins. An A is achievable without AI tools but requires substantially higher activity volume (MathWorks Academy completions, multiple supplemental activities, consistent participation). An A with AI fluency requires demonstrably higher quality output in fewer total activities. **Note:** This system is a draft. Exact point values, grade thresholds, and proposal review process will be published in a course supplement before the first week of class. See `grade_breakdown.md` for the full concept description. --- ### Assessment Types **Lab Reports** Structured written deliverables produced after lab activities. Required sections are specified in each assignment and evolve in complexity across the three course segments — from the first structured lab report in E1 (Week 2) to the full V&V plan in E3 (Week 13). All lab reports require MATLAB figures with captions that explicitly state the analytical conclusion. MATLAB is the required analysis tool throughout. **Assignment creation note:** Each lab report should designate a portion of the grade to peer evaluation and a portion to AI-generated content comparative analysis — students will evaluate the quality of feedback from both a peer and from an AI tool, and will be graded on the quality of that evaluation. The rubric for each assignment should make these dimensions explicit. This applies across E1–E3 deliverables. **Oral Assessments** In-person, in-lab assessments in which you explain your work, your methods, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team. These assessments cannot be substituted or made up without a documented emergency — no exceptions without documented circumstances and prior coordination. Oral assessments are scheduled throughout the semester during lab sessions. They are not surprise evaluations — you will know when they are coming and what they will cover. The purpose is the same as the code interviews in ASEN 1030: to ensure that demonstrated understanding is real, not performed. Every student who passes this course will have articulated their understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. **In-Class Comparative Analysis Exercises** Closed-book, in-person assessments in which you evaluate a provided report, data analysis, or presentation. The content being evaluated may be AI-generated, instructor-produced, or drawn from prior course iterations or professional literature. Your grade depends on the technical quality of your critique: what is correct and well-supported, what is incorrect or unsupported, what is missing, and what specific improvement would most strengthen the work. These exercises occur at least once in each course segment (E1 Week 3, E2 Week 9, E3 Week 14). **Lightning Talks and Peer Presentations** Short in-class presentations (3–5 minutes) in which you present experimental results, predictions, or design decisions to the class. Graded on clarity, technical accuracy, and the quality of responses to peer questions. Students also evaluate each other's lightning talks using structured written forms. **Peer Evaluation** Structured written evaluation of peers' work — including design frames, presentations, and experimental reports. Graded on the quality of the critique: specificity, accuracy of technical assessment, tone, and actionability of improvement recommendations. A vague or uniformly positive peer evaluation will not receive full credit. The skill being graded is the same as in the comparative analysis exercises — the source of the work being evaluated (peer, AI, or professional) does not change the standard. **E3 Design Project** The capstone of the course, spanning Weeks 11–15. You design an original aerospace experiment from scratch: define a predictive model and hypothesis, select sensors and DAQ configuration, specify signal conditioning strategy, produce a MATLAB-based uncertainty analysis, write a full V&V plan, and defend your design in a formal oral presentation. The project is documented in your GitHub portfolio. **Optional:** You may record a brief video presentation of your experiment (private YouTube link — unlisted, accessible only to those with the URL). This is not a graded requirement — it is an opportunity to build a professional portfolio artifact that can be shared through the instructor's network if you choose. If recording presents a concern, notify the instructor and an alternative will be arranged. This is the E3 segment in its entirety — not a single assignment. --- ## Academic Integrity All students are subject to the CU Boulder Honor Code. Academic integrity requirements apply to all work submitted in this course, regardless of the tools used to produce it. AI usage is explicitly permitted and encouraged per the AI Usage Policy above. However, students are fully responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and completeness of everything they submit. Using AI to produce plausible content and submitting it without understanding or verification is a failure of academic integrity — not because of the AI, but because of the misrepresentation. Submitting AI-generated content without engagement, fabricating data or results, or failing to disclose substantial AI contributions where disclosure is required may result in academic penalties under the Honor Code. If you are unsure whether a specific use of AI is acceptable in a given context, ask the instructor before submitting. The standard is straightforward: would you be able to explain every claim, every number, and every decision in your submission if asked in person? --- ## Accommodations CU Boulder is committed to providing equitable access for students with disabilities. Students needing accommodations must provide their accommodation letter to the instructor as early in the semester as possible. As a departmental policy, students should expect to receive accommodations for timed assessments only if the instructor receives the accommodation letter at least **5 business days before the assessment**. Late accommodation requests cannot be guaranteed. Contact Disability Services at https://www.colorado.edu/disabilityservices for assistance. --- ## Campus Resources Your success matters beyond this course. If you are experiencing challenges — academic, personal, or otherwise — please use these resources: - **Student Support and Case Management:** https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm — for emergencies, complex personal situations, and coordination across multiple courses. - **Counseling and Psychiatric Services (CAPS):** https://www.colorado.edu/counseling — for mental health support. - **Academic Advising:** Contact your department advisor for academic planning questions. - **Writing Center:** https://www.colorado.edu/writingcenter — for support with technical writing. If you are struggling in this course and are not sure what to do, email me. That is what I am here for. --- ## University Policies A complete copy of CU Boulder's university policies — including the Honor Code, Title IX, and non-discrimination policies — is available at https://www.colorado.edu/policies and through the CU Resources page on Canvas. --- ## Agent Notes - **Instructor bio:** Adapted from the 1030 syllabus. The core paragraph is preserved almost verbatim. The AI/non-profit mention is retained per task instruction. The second paragraph is lightly updated to frame AI engagement in the experimental methods context (assessment design) rather than the 1030 framing (course content supplementation). - **AI policy section:** Written at length per task and instructor notes emphasis. Draws directly from notes.txt (3/20 entry) and instructor_clarification_notes.md Sections 1 and 6. The policy is substantially more forward-leaning than the 1030 version, which was more focused on disclosure/transparency. The 3501 version frames AI as an assumed tool with authentic assessments as the counterbalance. The "creation is cheap" framing is from notes.txt verbatim. - **Grading structure:** Modeled on the 1030 tiered system (C/B/A requirements table). MATLAB trainings replaced with 3501-appropriate requirements. Marked clearly as DRAFT throughout. Specific thresholds and weights not specified — flagged for instructor review. - **Oral assessments:** Named "Oral Assessments" rather than "Code Interviews" (the 1030 term). The 1030 rationale paragraph for code interviews is adapted and reframed for experimental methods context. - **Assessment types:** The 1030 quizzes/labs/MATLAB trainings structure is entirely replaced with 3501-appropriate assessment types from the week-by-week blueprints. - **Prerequisites:** Placeholders used — instructor must supply specific course numbers. - **Timesheet details:** Approach confirmed in instructor notes (notes.txt). Specific format and submission requirements TBD. - **YouTube portfolio:** The instructor notes mention YouTube videos (private links). Not included as a formal syllabus requirement because it is not confirmed as a graded element — flagged for review. --- ## ⚠️ Items Requiring Instructor Review Before Finalization | # | Item | Location in Syllabus | Notes | |---|------|---------------------|-------| | 1 | Prerequisite course numbers | Prerequisites section | Placeholder used — "ASEN XXXX / statics, dynamics, intro AE TBD" | | 2 | Semester/year | Header | "Semester TBD" used | | 3 | Office hours details / scheduling link | Contact Information | "By request / use scheduling link on Canvas" per 1030 pattern | | 4 | Grading structure — all specifics | Grading section | Full draft placeholder — thresholds, weights, and tier logic need finalization | | 5 | Timesheet policy details | Student Expectations | Approach confirmed in notes.txt; format and submission method TBD | | 6 | GitHub grading details | GitHub section | Mentioned as required but grading weight not specified | | 7 | Lab report rubrics | Assessment Types | Not included — separate document needed per CLO assessment mapping | | 8 | Oral assessment scheduling and format | Assessment Types | "Scheduled during lab sessions" used; specific timing TBD | | 9 | Canvas assumed as submission platform | Submission Policies | Assumed Canvas per 1030; confirm for 3501 | | 10 | YouTube portfolio requirement | Not in syllabus | Mentioned in instructor notes but not confirmed as graded — include or exclude? | | 11 | MATLAB version / installation guidance | Required Materials | "CU license" stated; specific installation instructions noted as Canvas resource | | 12 | Specific lab report required sections | Assessment Types | Referenced as "specified in each assignment" — requires lab-level documents to exist | | 13 | Oral assessment pass/fail threshold | Grading section | "Pass minimum oral assessments" in grade tier table — threshold not defined | | 14 | Comparative analysis exercise dates | Assessment Types | E1 W3, E2 W9, E3 W14 noted; confirm against final schedule | | 15 | E3 project — executed vs. design-only | E3 Design Project | Conceptual Arc notes some experiments may remain as designs; policy for 3501 not confirmed | --- *ASEN 3501 Syllabus — Draft v1* *Generated by Claude Sonnet 4.6 — agentic_course_creation/claude_V1 | 2026-04-09*

chunks.text: # ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods ## Course Syllabus — Draft v1 ### [Semester TBD] | University of Colorado Boulder --- ## Contact Information **Instructor:** Bobby Hodgkinson **Email:** hodgkinr@colorado.edu **Office Location:** AERO 150D **Office Hours:** By request — use the scheduling link provided on Canvas. **Response time:** Emails will be answered within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. --- ## Instructor Bio Bobby Hodgkinson — Associate Teaching Professor, Smead Aerospace Engineering Sciences Hi everyone. I'm Bobby Hodgkinson, and I'll be your instructor for this course. My background is in vehicle controls, with experience working on aerial unmanned systems (UAS) and underwater vehicles, where I've

und is in vehicle controls, with experience working on aerial unmanned systems (UAS) and underwater vehicles, where I've tackled challenges in dynamic modeling, system stability, automation, sensors, and actuators. I'm passionate about applying dynamics and controls principles to real-world aerospace problems and helping students bridge the gap between theory and practice — which is exactly what this course is designed to do. Outside of engineering, I'm interested in the intersection of AI and education. I serve on the executive board of the Rocky Mountain AI Interest Group, a regional non-profit focused on exploring and promoting the ethical use of AI in our community. ASEN 3501 will engage with AI directly — not as a subject of study, but as a professional reality that shapes how I've

. ASEN 3501 will engage with AI directly — not as a subject of study, but as a professional reality that shapes how I've designed the course, the assessments, and the expectations for your work. --- ## About the Course ### Course Description ASEN 3501 develops the skills required to design, execute, and critically evaluate aerospace experiments with engineering discipline. The overarching goal is to train test engineers — professionals who design tests, characterize systems, and validate models for engineering organizations. The course is organized around a single question that opens Week 1 and closes Week 15: *How do you know this prediction is right?* Students work through three progressively demanding segments — executing experiments they are given (E1: Weeks 1–5), predicting before

through three progressively demanding segments — executing experiments they are given (E1: Weeks 1–5), predicting before measuring in complex aerospace systems (E2: Weeks 6–10), and designing their own experiments from scratch (E3: Weeks 11–15) — with the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework (AMVF) as the organizing structure throughout. By the end of the course, students are not just able to run an experiment and report a number; they can justify belief in a result, quantify what they don't know, and design experiments that support credible engineering decisions. This is the physical modeling branch of the curriculum — the connection between mathematical models and the physical world. ### Course Learning Outcomes By the end of this course, students will be able to: CLO 1. **Design

nd the physical world. ### Course Learning Outcomes By the end of this course, students will be able to: CLO 1. **Design and conduct** aerospace experiments by applying measurement fundamentals, instrumentation principles, and best practices in small team environments. CLO 2. **Apply** statistical methods and uncertainty analysis to quantify experimental error and establish confidence in results. CLO 3. **Integrate** theoretical models with experimental data to validate and evaluate aerospace system behavior. CLO 4. **Implement** signal conditioning, sampling, and data acquisition techniques to ensure accurate experimental measurements. CLO 5. **Communicate** experimental results effectively through technical reports and presentations, with clear description of methods, results, and

imental results effectively through technical reports and presentations, with clear description of methods, results, and limitations. CLO 6. **Identify, evaluate, and create** experimental methods or instrumentation beyond those provided by the instructor to foster independent professional growth. ### Prerequisites Completion of [ASEN XXXX / statics, dynamics, or introductory aerospace engineering — TBD by instructor] or instructor approval. Students are expected to have prior exposure to MATLAB and to the fundamental mathematics required to interpret physical models (calculus, basic linear algebra, introductory physics). No prior laboratory methods experience is required. ### Required Materials - **No required textbook.** There is no textbook for this course. Instead, open educational

ired. ### Required Materials - **No required textbook.** There is no textbook for this course. Instead, open educational resources and other course materials will be linked as appropriate through Canvas and related course materials. - **Reference standards:** ASME PTC 19.1 and the GUM provide the course's measurement-uncertainty basis. ASME V&V 10-2006 (*Computational Solid Mechanics*) and ASME V&V 20 (*Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer*) are domain examples used when relevant. Students will explain why a selected standard applies rather than cite one mechanically. - **Supplementary reference:** Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*, 4th edition — available through CU libraries and on reserve. Specific chapters assigned as the

Analysis for Engineers*, 4th edition — available through CU libraries and on reserve. Specific chapters assigned as the course progresses. - **Course uncertainty convention:** The Week 2 one-page reference defines the required Coleman & Steele–aligned terminology. Every reported or plotted interval must identify its type, method, and coverage basis. - **MATLAB** is the primary required computing platform for all labs and assignments. Available at no cost through CU Boulder's license — installation instructions on Canvas. Students who prefer to use Python (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) or another platform may do so, but **the teaching team will prioritize assistance for MATLAB first and foremost**. Students using other platforms are responsible for translating any provided MATLAB code,

or MATLAB first and foremost**. Students using other platforms are responsible for translating any provided MATLAB code, functions, or toolbox references on their own. No special accommodation will be made for non-MATLAB workflows during lab sessions or office hours. - **GitHub account** (free) — required for course documentation and portfolio. A tutorial is provided in Week 1. Students are not expected to have prior GitHub experience. - **Project supplies:** Students may choose to purchase supplies for project activities (no required purchase). The approach is similar to first-year projects — if you want to run your chosen experiment with your own materials, that is encouraged but never required. Students are encouraged to **keep any components they acquire** during course activities —

ncouraged but never required. Students are encouraged to **keep any components they acquire** during course activities — sensors, microcontrollers, breadboards, or other hardware — as these may be useful in future senior design projects. --- ## Communication Canvas is the primary channel for course-wide announcements. Enable your Canvas notifications so you do not miss time-sensitive information. Email is appropriate for individual coordination, emergencies, and course-related concerns that should not be discussed publicly. Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. Responses will be provided within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). If you have not received a response within 48 hours, a follow-up email is appropriate. In the event of a medical or family

you have not received a response within 48 hours, a follow-up email is appropriate. In the event of a medical or family emergency, contact the Office of Student Support and Case Management at https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm. They will help coordinate across all of your courses and connect you with campus resources. --- ## Method of Instruction ASEN 3501 is an in-person laboratory course. Sessions include lectures, hands-on lab activities, in-class discussions, oral assessments, and peer review exercises. The course is organized into three segments of five weeks each: - **E1 (Weeks 1–5):** Phase I — Execution & Quantification - **E2 (Weeks 6–10):** Phase II — Predictive Modeling & Comparative Validation - **E3 (Weeks 11–15):** Phase III — Experimental Design & V&V Planning

II — Predictive Modeling & Comparative Validation - **E3 (Weeks 11–15):** Phase III — Experimental Design & V&V Planning Some assigned content — readings, reference standards, AI literacy viewing, and tutorial materials — will be completed outside of class to maximize in-person time for labs, discussions, and oral assessments. Canvas is the platform for all materials, assignment submission, and course communication. Attendance is expected. In-person sessions are the primary learning environment. Oral assessments, comparative analysis exercises, and peer evaluations normally happen in class. If a makeup is needed, email the instructor to coordinate the time and place and include **five different available options during normal business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm)**. A makeup is

include **five different available options during normal business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm)**. A makeup is not confirmed until the instructor replies with an approved option. --- ## Student Responsibilities and Expectations ### Credit Hour Expectations ASEN 3501 is a 3-credit course. Per CU Boulder's credit hour policy, 3 credits corresponds to approximately **9 hours of total student effort per week** — 3 hours in class plus 6 hours of out-of-class work (reading, lab preparation, assignment completion, and portfolio documentation). Each assignment and lab document will include an estimated time to complete to help you plan accordingly. Students who are consistently exceeding these estimates should contact the instructor. ### Preparation Review assigned readings, reference

onsistently exceeding these estimates should contact the instructor. ### Preparation Review assigned readings, reference materials, and any pre-class viewings before each session. Lab documents will be posted in advance — you are expected to have read them before the lab begins. Arriving unprepared to a lab session reduces your own learning and slows your team. ### Asking for Technical Feedback At some point this semester you will ask a teammate, a TA, or the instructor to look at your work. How you do that matters. Engineers do not ask "is this right?" They say: *"I'm trying to do X. Here's my approach, and here's why I think it's correct. The parts I'm least confident in are Y and Z."* That framing is not a courtesy — it is a professional discipline. It tells the reviewer exactly what

t in are Y and Z."* That framing is not a courtesy — it is a professional discipline. It tells the reviewer exactly what to look for, demonstrates that you have thought critically about your own work, and makes the feedback you receive far more useful. Coming to a review without that preparation wastes everyone's time, including yours. Practice this habit starting in Week 1. By the time you reach your E3 oral defense, it should be automatic. ### Engagement and Attendance Active participation is expected in every session. This is not a course where you can recover missed content by watching a recording — lab activities, oral assessments, and comparative analysis exercises are conducted in person. Attendance is not formally taken, but participation is monitored and is a graded component. To

are conducted in person. Attendance is not formally taken, but participation is monitored and is a graded component. To request a makeup for a missed in-person assessment, email the instructor and propose **five different options during normal business hours** for an equivalent time-and-place arrangement. Failure to complete either the scheduled assessment or the coordinated makeup may result in no credit. ### Timesheet Requirement Weekly timesheets are required throughout the semester. These are a brief record of how you spent your time on course activities each week. The purpose is professional habit formation — tracking your own effort is a skill that matters in every engineering job — not surveillance. Format and submission details will be provided on Canvas. *(Details TBD — see

every engineering job — not surveillance. Format and submission details will be provided on Canvas. *(Details TBD — see review table.)* ### GitHub Portfolio Students will use GitHub to document and publish work throughout the course. A lightweight tutorial is provided in Week 1. By the end of the course, you will have a documented portfolio of your experimental methods work — something you can share with future employers. This is a professional skill that has immediate practical value. --- ## Course and University Policies ### Generative AI (AI Usage Policy) This policy applies to ASEN 3501. It is not a department or university policy. **The short version:** AI usage is expected. The course is designed around that assumption. The assessments are designed to reveal real understanding

ge is expected. The course is designed around that assumption. The assessments are designed to reveal real understanding regardless of how content was produced. **The full version:** We are at a point in engineering practice where AI tools can generate a lab report, a data analysis, a literature review, or a slide deck in a fraction of the time it would have taken a person. That is not a problem to resist — it is a fact to prepare for. The workload expectations in this course assume that you are using AI augmentation in your work. If you are not using it, you should be. Knowing how to work effectively with AI tools is part of what it means to be a well-prepared engineer right now, and that bar will only rise. Here is what follows from that premise: **Creation is cheap.** AI can produce a

right now, and that bar will only rise. Here is what follows from that premise: **Creation is cheap.** AI can produce a technically plausible lab report with appropriately structured sections, populated figures, and reasonable-sounding conclusions. That means the ability to *produce* a report is no longer an adequate demonstration of understanding. What AI cannot do — at least not without a human who deeply understands the domain — is tell you *what is right, what is wrong, what is missing, and how to make it better.* That critical evaluation skill is what this course is designed to develop. It is also what engineering employers are increasingly paying for. **The primary assessment mechanism in this course is critical evaluation of engineering content** — not the production of

*The primary assessment mechanism in this course is critical evaluation of engineering content** — not the production of engineering content. You will frequently be given a report, a data analysis, or a presentation — produced by AI, by me, by a peer, or by a prior student — and your grade will depend on the quality of your technical critique. What is correct and well-supported? What is incorrect or unsupported? What is missing? What specific change would most improve it? Answering these questions well requires real understanding that cannot be faked. **Authentic assessments counterbalance AI use in three ways:** 1. **In-person oral assessments.** You will explain your work, your choices, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team — in person, without notes, without

in your work, your choices, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team — in person, without notes, without preparation time. You cannot delegate this to AI. Everyone who passes this course will have clearly articulated an understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. 2. **In-class comparative analysis exercises.** These are closed-book, in-person exercises where you evaluate provided content. You cannot use external tools. Your grade depends on the technical accuracy and depth of your critique. 3. **The E3 oral defense.** You will defend your own experimental design to a technical audience and respond to direct questions about every decision you made. **What this is not:** This is not a policy against AI, and it is not designed

t questions about every decision you made. **What this is not:** This is not a policy against AI, and it is not designed to catch or punish AI use. It is a policy that acknowledges the professional reality you are entering and prepares you for it. Using AI to help draft, analyze, refine, or audit your work is not just permitted — it is encouraged. The course even includes an early viewing assignment on AI literacy. The expectation is that you use these tools intelligently and that you take responsibility for everything you submit. **Student use of AI:** Students will not be required to use AI to complete this course. However, students are strongly encouraged to use AI tools where helpful and appropriate within the guidance of the course content, assignment instructions, and learning

AI tools where helpful and appropriate within the guidance of the course content, assignment instructions, and learning objectives. **Transparency:** If AI tools substantially contributed to a submission, note it briefly at the end. Describe what tool you used and what you used it for. This is not a disclosure requirement designed to penalize you — it is professional practice. **What is not acceptable:** - Submitting AI-generated content as your own without engagement or modification - Using AI to fabricate data, measurements, or results - Using any external tool during closed-book, in-class assessments Students with concerns about this approach are welcome to discuss them with the instructor. The goal is to prepare you for tomorrow, not to argue about whether tomorrow is coming. *The

s them with the instructor. The goal is to prepare you for tomorrow, not to argue about whether tomorrow is coming. *The instructional team may use AI tools to aid in feedback generation and to gain insight into student progress. We will never use AI to produce numerical scores on summative assignments. AI-augmented feedback will always be reviewed and revised by a human before it reaches you.* ### Instructional Team Use of AI The instructional team uses AI tools in the design and delivery of this course. This section describes that use openly — the same transparency we ask of students applies to us. **Why we use it:** The goals of AI use on the instructional side mirror what we want you to develop on the student side. We use it to improve the overall quality of the educational product,

or what we want you to develop on the student side. We use it to improve the overall quality of the educational product, to reduce the turnaround time between your submission and meaningful feedback, and to increase the level of personalization we can bring to this course. A course of this size, with oral assessments and written deliverables across fifteen weeks, generates more feedback load than a traditional lecture-only course. AI helps us keep feedback timely and substantive rather than generic and delayed. Generative AI platforms including OpenAI ChatGPT, Anthropic Claude, and Google Gemini were used to aid in the creation of this course content. Images used in the course were generated primarily with OpenAI ChatGPT. The combination of open educational resources and generative AI is

course were generated primarily with OpenAI ChatGPT. The combination of open educational resources and generative AI is intended to support a high-quality, relatively low-cost, and efficient learning experience for you. **Current and anticipated use cases include:** - **AI-augmented grading and feedback.** Rubric-based assessment of written deliverables may be supported by AI tools to generate a first-pass evaluation. That draft is always reviewed, revised, and approved by a human member of the instructional team before it reaches you. AI will never produce final numerical scores on summative assignments — a human makes that call every time. - **Content creation and course development.** Lecture materials, assignment documents, readings, and supplemental resources in this course were

course development.** Lecture materials, assignment documents, readings, and supplemental resources in this course were developed with AI assistance. The instructor has verified and revised all content before it was included in the course. If you find something that looks wrong, flag it — the editorial standard applies regardless of how content was produced. - **Personalized feedback and student-specific communication.** AI tools may be used to help the instructional team craft feedback that is more specific to your work and your progress in the course, rather than relying on generic comment banks. - **Assessment design and calibration.** AI may be used to generate sample responses at different quality levels for rubric calibration, or to produce the AI-generated content used in

rate sample responses at different quality levels for rubric calibration, or to produce the AI-generated content used in comparative analysis exercises. **What we will not do:** We will not use AI to replace human judgment on consequential decisions — final grades, academic integrity determinations, or pass/fail calls on oral assessments. We will not use AI in ways that compromise the privacy of your submitted work beyond what is already disclosed in CU Boulder's data policies. **Want to know more?** The instructor welcomes any and all discussion related to these topics as they relate both to this course and to the bigger picture. If you have questions about how AI is being used in any part of the course — grading, content generation, feedback, or course design — ask. There is no part of

eing used in any part of the course — grading, content generation, feedback, or course design — ask. There is no part of this that is meant to be opaque. --- ### Lab Environment Policy Safety is the first priority in every lab session. Anyone violating safe conduct may receive a zero for that lab and may lose access to lab facilities. Use of lab facilities is a privilege. Failure to wear required safety gear will result in a 10% grade penalty per infraction. Endangering yourself, others, or lab equipment is not acceptable under any circumstances. Professional behavior and respectful communication are expected at all times. Eating and drinking inside the laboratory is prohibited. ### Lecture Recording Notice Lectures may be recorded for educational purposes. Students who do not wish to

prohibited. ### Lecture Recording Notice Lectures may be recorded for educational purposes. Students who do not wish to appear in recordings must notify the instructor at least 72 hours in advance. --- ## Assignments and Grading ### Submission Policies **Platform:** Assignments must be submitted via Canvas unless otherwise stated. Submissions must include the names of all contributing members. **Individuals whose name does not appear on a group submission will not receive credit for the assignment. Groups are encouraged to leave off the names of individuals who did not contribute to the lab assignment.** **File format:** Follow the file format requirements specified in each assignment. Submissions that do not follow the required format may not be accepted. **Late work:** Late submissions

each assignment. Submissions that do not follow the required format may not be accepted. **Late work:** Late submissions will not be accepted without prior approval or a documented emergency. Extension requests must be submitted at least 24 hours before the due date. **Regrade policy:** If you would like to submit a regrade request for any assignment, you must submit the request via email to the instructor within **2 weeks** of the graded assignment return date. All regrade requests will be reviewed and approved by a course instructor — not teaching assistants, teaching fellows, or lab assistants. a. The regrade request must clearly state the reason you are requesting the regrade and what you believe the correct grade to be. Note that disagreement on the established rubric allocation of

the regrade and what you believe the correct grade to be. Note that disagreement on the established rubric allocation of points is not a valid reason for a regrade and will not be considered. b. The regrade request must include, in a single combined PDF: an introductory statement addressing the above, a PDF copy of the original submission with portions highlighted that pertain to the regrade request, and any additional supporting information. c. **Points can be added OR removed** based on correctness. If a mistake was made in grading and too few points were awarded, the regrade request may increase the final score; however, if the instructor finds a mistake was made in grading and too many points were awarded, the regrade request may lower the final score. d. Regrades submitted in the

in grading and too many points were awarded, the regrade request may lower the final score. d. Regrades submitted in the **final month** of the course will only be entertained if the regrade alters an individual's final letter grade. We reserve the right to make changes to the course schedule based on emerging circumstances. Advance notice will be provided through Canvas announcements. The current syllabus and schedule will always be posted on Canvas. ### Assessment Philosophy This course assesses two distinct capabilities: the ability to *produce* experimental engineering work, and the ability to *critically evaluate* it. Both are graded. In some assessments, the critique grade is weighted equally to or more heavily than the production grade. This is intentional. As AI tools become more

que grade is weighted equally to or more heavily than the production grade. This is intentional. As AI tools become more capable of producing technically plausible content, the engineering skill that retains and increases in value is the ability to evaluate whether that content is correct, complete, and fit for purpose. The comparative analysis exercises in this course — where you evaluate provided reports, analyses, and presentations on technical merit — are not supplementary activities. They are the primary assessment mechanism. Every student who passes ASEN 3501 will have: - Produced structured experimental work across all three course segments - Critically evaluated provided content (AI-generated, instructor-selected, or peer-produced) in multiple in-class exercises - Articulated

ated provided content (AI-generated, instructor-selected, or peer-produced) in multiple in-class exercises - Articulated their understanding of key fundamentals in in-person oral assessments — multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways - Defended an original experimental design in a formal oral presentation (E3) Formative assessments are frequent and low-stakes. The purpose of formative feedback is to show you where you are relative to expectations and to help you develop, not to produce a grade. Take them seriously — students who engage with formative feedback consistently outperform those who treat it as overhead. The final third of the course (E3, Weeks 11–15) is driven by you. You choose your experimental design direction. You select your instrumentation. You define the

11–15) is driven by you. You choose your experimental design direction. You select your instrumentation. You define the validation logic. The instructor's role shifts from source of knowledge to critical reviewer. --- ### Grading Structure > ⚠️ **DRAFT — GRADING SCHEME NOT FINALIZED** ⚠️ > > **The complete grade structure is TBD. No gate, point, tier, threshold, reattempt, or letter-grade rule below is approved for student use until the instructor publishes a final grading supplement.** > > The following grade tier structure is a working placeholder that reflects the instructor's grading philosophy and approach. It is modeled on the ASEN 1030 tiered grading system. The specific requirements, thresholds, and weights are subject to revision before the course launches. Do not treat these as

specific requirements, thresholds, and weights are subject to revision before the course launches. Do not treat these as final. > > **Gate-based passing structure (intended):** The final grading scheme will incorporate a gate-based structure where each major deliverable set must be passed before progressing. Students who fail to pass all gates by two weeks before the end of term will not be eligible to participate in the final A-level expo. The specific gate definitions, draft-feedback-revise cycle, and AI+peer feedback evaluation components will be specified in the individual assignment documents. See `grade_breakdown.md` for full concept description. > > **Assignment creation note:** All assignments should include: (a) a draft-feedback-revise cycle; (b) a peer feedback step with grading

reation note:** All assignments should include: (a) a draft-feedback-revise cycle; (b) a peer feedback step with grading on feedback quality; (c) a comparison-to-AI-feedback step; (d) an expected time-to-complete estimate; (e) a pre-submission checklist including an AI audit step. This course uses a **tiered grading system** focused on demonstrated understanding, engagement, and professional discipline — not on accumulated points. The approach is designed to reflect real-world performance expectations: meeting the minimum standard keeps you in the game; effort beyond the minimum is recognized and rewarded. **Grading Philosophy:** A grade of 'A' represents superior/excellent performance. A grade of 'B' represents good/better-than-average performance. A grade of 'C' represents

uperior/excellent performance. A grade of 'B' represents good/better-than-average performance. A grade of 'C' represents competent/average performance, which is consistent with CU Boulder grading policy. Intermediate grades (C+, B–, B+, A–) will be assigned based on partial completion of the next tier's requirements, at the instructor's discretion. Individual grades may be adjusted based on instructional team observations — exemplary performance may increase a grade; consistently poor engagement may decrease one. **Final Grade Tiers (Draft)** | Requirement | C | B | A | |-------------|---|---|---| | Pass all lab reports (all required sections, adequate technical execution) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Pass minimum oral assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | 75%+ on in-class comparative analysis exercises and
