ution) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Pass minimum oral assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | 75%+ on in-class comparative analysis exercises and formative assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete all weekly timesheets | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete all E1 and E2 written deliverables | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Strong comparative analysis performance (demonstrated critique quality above threshold) | | ✓ | ✓ | | Strong E3 design project (complete V&V plan, instrumentation justification, oral defense) | | ✓ | ✓ | | Exceptional oral assessment performance across the semester | | | ✓ | | Outstanding E3 project with genuine beyond-provided-hardware investigation (CLO 6) | | | ✓ | *Note: This grading structure is a draft placeholder. Final grading scheme to be determined by instructor before course launch.* **Points System (Draft — to be

t placeholder. Final grading scheme to be determined by instructor before course launch.* **Points System (Draft — to be revised before course launch):** This course uses a points-based participation and performance system layered on top of the pass/fail gate structure above. Points are earned above the minimum threshold and determine final letter grade. The following describes the intended system — exact point values and letter grade thresholds will be finalized before the course starts. **Point-earning activities:** - **In-lecture participation:** Questions asked, answers given, and contributions to in-class exercises during lecture — awarded based on participation and correctness. - **Supplemental activity completions:** Reading optional materials, watching supplemental recordings,

and correctness. - **Supplemental activity completions:** Reading optional materials, watching supplemental recordings, completing E0_supplemental exercises — awarded upon verified completion. - **AI comparative analysis work:** Completing AI-generated content comparisons, evaluating AI feedback quality in assignments — additional points above the standard assignment grade. - **MathWorks Academy completions:** Completing MathWorks training modules (MATLAB, AI, data analysis tracks) earns bonus points. - **Approved student proposals:** Students may propose a supplemental activity (find something interesting, connect it to the course, submit for approval) — if approved, points are awarded upon completion. - **Above-expectations performance:** The grading team may issue bonus points for

d, points are awarded upon completion. - **Above-expectations performance:** The grading team may issue bonus points for assignment performance that goes meaningfully beyond expectations. **Scheduling priority:** Students with higher point totals receive priority access to sign-up slots for mid-tier E2 lab assignments and in-class lightning talk time slots. Sign-up windows open in tiers: top point holders first, then remaining students. **Grade thresholds (draft — to be finalized):** Passing all core assessments (gates) earns a C−. The number of points needed for B and A grades will be published before the semester begins. An A is achievable without AI tools but requires substantially higher activity volume (MathWorks Academy completions, multiple supplemental activities, consistent

quires substantially higher activity volume (MathWorks Academy completions, multiple supplemental activities, consistent participation). An A with AI fluency requires demonstrably higher quality output in fewer total activities. **Note:** This system is a draft. Exact point values, grade thresholds, and proposal review process will be published in a course supplement before the first week of class. See `grade_breakdown.md` for the full concept description. --- ### Assessment Types **Lab Reports** Structured written deliverables produced after lab activities. Required sections are specified in each assignment and evolve in complexity across the three course segments — from the first structured lab report in E1 (Week 2) to the full V&V plan in E3 (Week 13). All lab reports require MATLAB

from the first structured lab report in E1 (Week 2) to the full V&V plan in E3 (Week 13). All lab reports require MATLAB figures with captions that explicitly state the analytical conclusion. MATLAB is the required analysis tool throughout. **Assignment creation note:** Each lab report should designate a portion of the grade to peer evaluation and a portion to AI-generated content comparative analysis — students will evaluate the quality of feedback from both a peer and from an AI tool, and will be graded on the quality of that evaluation. The rubric for each assignment should make these dimensions explicit. This applies across E1–E3 deliverables. **Oral Assessments** In-person, in-lab assessments in which you explain your work, your methods, and your reasoning to a member of the

ents** In-person, in-lab assessments in which you explain your work, your methods, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team. These assessments cannot be substituted or made up without a documented emergency — no exceptions without documented circumstances and prior coordination. Oral assessments are scheduled throughout the semester during lab sessions. They are not surprise evaluations — you will know when they are coming and what they will cover. The purpose is the same as the code interviews in ASEN 1030: to ensure that demonstrated understanding is real, not performed. Every student who passes this course will have articulated their understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. **In-Class Comparative Analysis Exercises**

f the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. **In-Class Comparative Analysis Exercises** Closed-book, in-person assessments in which you evaluate a provided report, data analysis, or presentation. The content being evaluated may be AI-generated, instructor-produced, or drawn from prior course iterations or professional literature. Your grade depends on the technical quality of your critique: what is correct and well-supported, what is incorrect or unsupported, what is missing, and what specific improvement would most strengthen the work. These exercises occur at least once in each course segment (E1 Week 3, E2 Week 9, E3 Week 14). **Lightning Talks and Peer Presentations** Short in-class presentations (3–5 minutes) in which you present experimental results,

ing Talks and Peer Presentations** Short in-class presentations (3–5 minutes) in which you present experimental results, predictions, or design decisions to the class. Graded on clarity, technical accuracy, and the quality of responses to peer questions. Students also evaluate each other's lightning talks using structured written forms. **Peer Evaluation** Structured written evaluation of peers' work — including design frames, presentations, and experimental reports. Graded on the quality of the critique: specificity, accuracy of technical assessment, tone, and actionability of improvement recommendations. A vague or uniformly positive peer evaluation will not receive full credit. The skill being graded is the same as in the comparative analysis exercises — the source of the work being

full credit. The skill being graded is the same as in the comparative analysis exercises — the source of the work being evaluated (peer, AI, or professional) does not change the standard. **E3 Design Project** The capstone of the course, spanning Weeks 11–15. You design an original aerospace experiment from scratch: define a predictive model and hypothesis, select sensors and DAQ configuration, specify signal conditioning strategy, produce a MATLAB-based uncertainty analysis, write a full V&V plan, and defend your design in a formal oral presentation. The project is documented in your GitHub portfolio. **Optional:** You may record a brief video presentation of your experiment (private YouTube link — unlisted, accessible only to those with the URL). This is not a graded requirement — it

periment (private YouTube link — unlisted, accessible only to those with the URL). This is not a graded requirement — it is an opportunity to build a professional portfolio artifact that can be shared through the instructor's network if you choose. If recording presents a concern, notify the instructor and an alternative will be arranged. This is the E3 segment in its entirety — not a single assignment. --- ## Academic Integrity All students are subject to the CU Boulder Honor Code. Academic integrity requirements apply to all work submitted in this course, regardless of the tools used to produce it. AI usage is explicitly permitted and encouraged per the AI Usage Policy above. However, students are fully responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and completeness of everything they submit.

above. However, students are fully responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and completeness of everything they submit. Using AI to produce plausible content and submitting it without understanding or verification is a failure of academic integrity — not because of the AI, but because of the misrepresentation. Submitting AI-generated content without engagement, fabricating data or results, or failing to disclose substantial AI contributions where disclosure is required may result in academic penalties under the Honor Code. If you are unsure whether a specific use of AI is acceptable in a given context, ask the instructor before submitting. The standard is straightforward: would you be able to explain every claim, every number, and every decision in your submission if asked in person? ---

d: would you be able to explain every claim, every number, and every decision in your submission if asked in person? --- ## Accommodations CU Boulder is committed to providing equitable access for students with disabilities. Students needing accommodations must provide their accommodation letter to the instructor as early in the semester as possible. As a departmental policy, students should expect to receive accommodations for timed assessments only if the instructor receives the accommodation letter at least **5 business days before the assessment**. Late accommodation requests cannot be guaranteed. Contact Disability Services at https://www.colorado.edu/disabilityservices for assistance. --- ## Campus Resources Your success matters beyond this course. If you are experiencing challenges

ices for assistance. --- ## Campus Resources Your success matters beyond this course. If you are experiencing challenges — academic, personal, or otherwise — please use these resources: - **Student Support and Case Management:** https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm — for emergencies, complex personal situations, and coordination across multiple courses. - **Counseling and Psychiatric Services (CAPS):** https://www.colorado.edu/counseling — for mental health support. - **Academic Advising:** Contact your department advisor for academic planning questions. - **Writing Center:** https://www.colorado.edu/writingcenter — for support with technical writing. If you are struggling in this course and are not sure what to do, email me. That is what I am here for. --- ## University Policies

struggling in this course and are not sure what to do, email me. That is what I am here for. --- ## University Policies A complete copy of CU Boulder's university policies — including the Honor Code, Title IX, and non-discrimination policies — is available at https://www.colorado.edu/policies and through the CU Resources page on Canvas. --- ## Agent Notes - **Instructor bio:** Adapted from the 1030 syllabus. The core paragraph is preserved almost verbatim. The AI/non-profit mention is retained per task instruction. The second paragraph is lightly updated to frame AI engagement in the experimental methods context (assessment design) rather than the 1030 framing (course content supplementation). - **AI policy section:** Written at length per task and instructor notes emphasis. Draws

ourse content supplementation). - **AI policy section:** Written at length per task and instructor notes emphasis. Draws directly from notes.txt (3/20 entry) and instructor_clarification_notes.md Sections 1 and 6. The policy is substantially more forward-leaning than the 1030 version, which was more focused on disclosure/transparency. The 3501 version frames AI as an assumed tool with authentic assessments as the counterbalance. The "creation is cheap" framing is from notes.txt verbatim. - **Grading structure:** Modeled on the 1030 tiered system (C/B/A requirements table). MATLAB trainings replaced with 3501-appropriate requirements. Marked clearly as DRAFT throughout. Specific thresholds and weights not specified — flagged for instructor review. - **Oral assessments:** Named "Oral

hout. Specific thresholds and weights not specified — flagged for instructor review. - **Oral assessments:** Named "Oral Assessments" rather than "Code Interviews" (the 1030 term). The 1030 rationale paragraph for code interviews is adapted and reframed for experimental methods context. - **Assessment types:** The 1030 quizzes/labs/MATLAB trainings structure is entirely replaced with 3501-appropriate assessment types from the week-by-week blueprints. - **Prerequisites:** Placeholders used — instructor must supply specific course numbers. - **Timesheet details:** Approach confirmed in instructor notes (notes.txt). Specific format and submission requirements TBD. - **YouTube portfolio:** The instructor notes mention YouTube videos (private links). Not included as a formal syllabus

- **YouTube portfolio:** The instructor notes mention YouTube videos (private links). Not included as a formal syllabus requirement because it is not confirmed as a graded element — flagged for review. --- ## ⚠️ Items Requiring Instructor Review Before Finalization | # | Item | Location in Syllabus | Notes | |---|------|---------------------|-------| | 1 | Prerequisite course numbers | Prerequisites section | Placeholder used — "ASEN XXXX / statics, dynamics, intro AE TBD" | | 2 | Semester/year | Header | "Semester TBD" used | | 3 | Office hours details / scheduling link | Contact Information | "By request / use scheduling link on Canvas" per 1030 pattern | | 4 | Grading structure — all specifics | Grading section | Full draft placeholder — thresholds, weights, and tier logic need

med Canvas per 1030; confirm for 3501 | | 10 | YouTube portfolio requirement | Not in syllabus | Mentioned in instructor notes but not confirmed as graded — include or exclude? | | 11 | MATLAB version / installation guidance | Required Materials | "CU license" stated; specific installation instructions noted as Canvas resource | | 12 | Specific lab report required sections | Assessment Types | Referenced as "specified in each assignment" — requires lab-level documents to exist | | 13 | Oral assessment pass/fail threshold | Grading section | "Pass minimum oral assessments" in grade tier table — threshold not defined | | 14 | Comparative analysis exercise dates | Assessment Types | E1 W3, E2 W9, E3 W14 noted; confirm against final schedule | | 15 | E3 project — executed vs. design-only | E3

pages.title: AI Fluency and Course Value
pages.text: # AI Fluency and Course Value This page is optional support for students who want more context on why the course treats AI the way it does and why verification-and-validation judgment remains central. ## AI Literacy vs. AI Fluency AI literacy is knowing what AI is and what it can do. AI fluency is knowing when, how, and why to use it, and when not to. This course expects fluency, not just literacy. Creation is now cheap. The durable skill is evaluation: deciding what is correct, what is weak, what is missing, and how to make it better. That is why ASEN 3501 keeps returning to critique, validation, and defended engineering judgment. ## Why This Course Uses Authentic Assessments As AI gets better, the assessments that still reveal real understanding are the ones AI cannot take for you: live explanation, in-class reasoning, oral review, and design defense. That is why the backbone of this course is not "produce more words." It is "defend what those words mean." ## The Employer Question One question is worth carrying through the entire semester: *How will this task make me a valuable asset to a future employer?* The point is not to avoid foundational work because AI can do parts of it. The point is to use the foundational work to become the engineer who can judge whether the AI-assisted result is any good. ## A Practical Way to Use AI in This Course - use AI to get unstuck on mechanics - use AI to surface alternative explanations or literature leads - use AI to critique a draft before submission - do not hand over final judgment on correctness, scope, or engineering adequacy If AI gives you something plausible, your job is still to ask: is it right, what evidence supports it, and what would make me distrust it?

pages.title: Basic Statistics for Experimental Data
pages.text: # Basic Statistics for Experimental Data This page is optional support for students who have not had much statistics yet. The goal is not to turn you into a statistician before the first lab. The goal is to give you enough language to describe repeated measurements without pretending that one number tells the whole story. Statistics is how engineers talk about variation. If you measure the same thing several times, you will rarely get exactly the same value each time. The differences may come from instrument noise, setup variation, changing environmental conditions, or real changes in the system. Statistics helps you describe that spread, compare it to your model, and make claims that are appropriately cautious. ## Center: Mean, Median, and Mode The **mean** is the arithmetic average. Add the values and divide by the number of values: `mean = (x1 + x2 + ... + xn) / n` The mean is useful when the data are roughly balanced around a central value. It can be misleading when one or two extreme values pull the average away from most of the data. The **median** is the middle value after the data are sorted. If there is an even number of values, it is the average of the two middle values. The median is useful when the data include outliers. The **mode** is the most common value. It is useful for repeated categories or rounded measurements, but many engineering datasets have no clear mode. A good lab report does not automatically use all three. It chooses the measure of center that matches the data and explains why. ## Spread: How Much Do Repeated Measurements Vary? Two datasets can have the same mean and very different reliability. That is why center is not enough. The **range** is the largest value minus the smallest value. It is easy to compute, but it depends strongly on the most extreme measurements. The **standard deviation** describes the typical spread of individual measurements around the mean. A small standard deviation means repeated measurements cluster tightly. A large standard deviation means repeated measurements are more spread out. In this course, spread matters because uncertainty is not decorative. If the uncertainty is larger than the effect you are trying to detect, the experiment may not be sensitive enough to support the claim. ## Probability: Not Certainty About One Trial Probability describes how likely an outcome is under a stated model or assumption. It is not a guarantee about what will happen on the next trial. If a fair coin has a 50% probability of landing heads, that does not mean every two flips produce exactly one head. It means that over many flips, the fraction of heads should approach 50%. Engineering measurements work the same way. A probability statement is always tied to assumptions about the measurement process, the model, and the data. ## Distributions: The Shape of Possible Values A **distribution** describes the values a measurement could take and how likely those values are. The **normal distribution** is the familiar bell-shaped distribution. It is useful when many small, independent sources of variation combine and values cluster around a mean. The **uniform distribution** is flat across a range. It is useful when all you know is that a value lies somewhere between a lower and upper bound. For example, if a datasheet gives only a tolerance limit and no probability shape, a uniform distribution may be a better starting assumption than inventing a bell curve. Distributions matter because uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo simulation both depend on what you assume the inputs can be. ## Fit Metrics and Significance: R² and p-Values **R²** describes how much of the variation in the observed data is explained by a fitted model. An R² value near 1 means the fitted curve accounts for most of the variation in that dataset. It does not prove the model is physically correct, and it does not prove the model will predict new data. A high R² can come from a useful model, a narrow test range, or a flexible curve that was tuned after seeing the data. A **p-value** belongs to a specific statistical test. It is the probability, assuming a stated null model and the test assumptions are true, of seeing a result at least as extreme as the one observed. It is not the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It is also not a measure of engineering importance. A result can be statistically detectable and still too small to matter physically, or physically important but hard to prove with limited data. ## Samples and Populations A **population** is the full set you would like to know about. A **sample** is the subset you actually measured. You usually do not get to measure the entire population. You measure a sample and use it to estimate something about the larger behavior. More measurements can improve the estimate of random variation, but they do not automatically remove bias. If the instrument is miscalibrated, taking more readings with the same instrument will repeat the same problem more precisely. That distinction appears throughout ASEN 3501: - repeated trials help characterize random error - calibration checks help identify systematic bias - residual plots help reveal structured patterns that a simple average can hide ## How This Connects to the Course When this course asks for an uncertainty interval, it is asking you to say more than "my answer is about 12." It is asking: - What value did you estimate? - How much variation or uncertainty surrounds that estimate? - What assumptions produced that uncertainty? - Is the uncertainty small enough to support the engineering decision? Monte Carlo simulation is just repeated sampling from assumed input distributions. Residual plots are just a way to see whether differences between model and measurement look random, biased, or structured. Confidence language is a way to avoid overstating what the data can prove. **The Takeaway:** Statistics is not a separate math topic sitting outside the lab. It is the language that lets you describe variation, compare evidence, and make engineering claims without pretending the measurement is more certain than it is.

pages.title: GitHub Basics for Course Documentation
pages.text: # GitHub Basics for Course Documentation ## Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It _This page is optional support. It is no longer part of the required E1 Week 1 reading sequence, but it preserves the full GitHub tutorial content for students who want that guidance while setting up their course documentation workflow._ An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened. In professional aerospace practice, an experiment that exists only in memory is worth nothing to the next engineer who needs to reproduce, extend, or audit your work. --- Engineering documentation has two jobs: it preserves what you did and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain meticulous records not because they enjoy paperwork, but because the value of a measurement result depends entirely on the ability of a third party to evaluate it independently. A voltage reading without context is noise. A voltage reading with a documented procedure, sensor specification, calibration record, and uncertainty estimate is evidence. **GitHub** is a platform for hosting and sharing documents using version control, a system that tracks every change you make to a file over time, along with who made the change and when. In software development, this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you'll use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio where each experiment you conduct has a documented home and a traceable history. ![A screenshot-style illustration of a GitHub repository page for a hypothetical student project called "ASEN3501-Portfolio." The repository shows a README.md file rendered at the top with a brief description: "ASEN 3501 experiment documentation — Weeks 1–15." Below the README, a file tree shows folders labeled "E1_week_1/", "E1_week_2/", and files including "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." The interface uses GitHub's actual visual style — white background, dark top navigation bar, file tree with icons — rendered as a clean flat illustration rather than a screenshot. A small commit message is visible below the file tree: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 experimental design and AMVF diagram.""](../../E1/week_1/images/E1_W1_R2_image1.png) The mechanics are straightforward. A **repository** (repo) is a folder with version history: a project folder that remembers every saved version. A **README.md** is a text file written in **Markdown**, a lightweight formatting language, that appears on the repository's front page and describes what the project is. You'll write your experiment documentation in Markdown: plain text with simple syntax for headers (`# Title`), bold (`**term**`), and lists (`- item`) that renders cleanly in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser. The workflow for adding files has three steps: *stage* (select which files to include in this snapshot), *commit* (save a labeled snapshot with a short note about what changed and why), and *push* (send that snapshot to GitHub's servers so it is accessible online). For this course you won't need the full depth of version control (branches, pull requests, merge conflicts), but understanding the commit as "a labeled save with a note about what changed" is the fundamental concept. Your commit messages are part of your documentation narrative; write them as though you're explaining your lab session to a colleague six months from now. ![A three-step horizontal workflow diagram. Step 1 is labeled "Stage" and shows a folder icon with two files highlighted — "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." Step 2 is labeled "Commit" with a text bubble showing the message: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 design: hypothesis, variables, AMVF diagram." Step 3 is labeled "Push" with an upward arrow pointing to a cloud icon labeled "GitHub repository." Arrows connect each step left to right. Flat-design icons, CU Boulder gold accent color on the step labels, black and white otherwise.](../../E1/week_1/images/E1_W1_R2_image2.png) Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new repository for ASEN 3501, and uploading your first file, which will be the pre-lab design work from this week's Tier 1 Phase 1 design session. That first file should include your experimental hypothesis, your identified variables, and a description of your annotated AMVF diagram. The repository structure you establish now will carry forward through all subsequent experiment deliverables. Use AI tools freely to help with syntax questions, Markdown formatting, or workflow steps. This is exactly the kind of mechanical task where AI augmentation is efficient and appropriate. One habit worth building from the start: GitHub is a record of your process, not a drop box for final products. A repository with one massive commit at the end of the semester tells no story. Frequent, small commits with clear messages create a record that shows how your thinking developed. That process record is a professional artifact. Engineers and employers who look at your GitHub portfolio are reading your technical judgment over time, not just the last thing you submitted. --- **The Takeaway:** The Tier 1 Phase 1 pre-lab work from this week's design session is your first portfolio entry. Commit it to your repository with a clear description, and begin the documentation habit that every subsequent lab deliverable will extend.

pages.title: Technical Communication Support
pages.text: # Technical Communication Support This page is optional support for students who want a stronger structure for reports, executive summaries, captions, and presentations. ## What a Strong Technical Document Does A useful engineering document does more than store results. It tells the reader what question was asked, how the answer was produced, how uncertain that answer is, and what decision the result supports. That is why this course keeps pushing on captions, graph framing, result statements, and plain-language explanations. A technically correct result still fails if the reader cannot tell what it means. ## Model Report Placeholder An annotated model brief lab report should eventually live in this supplemental collection. When added, it should show: - why each section exists - what a correct result statement looks like - how a caption moves from description to argument - what "meets the standard" looks like early in the course ## Executive Summary Structure When a full report is unnecessary, an executive summary is often the better engineering format. Use this structure: 1. What was the goal? 2. How was it done? 3. What was found? 4. What does it mean for the system or decision? The challenge is not making it shorter. The challenge is making it shorter without losing the argument. ## Presentation Advice Students often show a figure, a component, or a result without first locating it in the larger system. That creates the "disembodied part" problem. Before showing any figure or component, answer: 1. What system are we talking about? 2. Where does this thing fit in that system? 3. Why does it matter? ## Figure Checklist For any serious MATLAB figure or technical plot, ask: 1. Why does this figure exist? 2. What is on each axis, with units? 3. Where did the data come from? 4. What uncertainties are shown? 5. What trend is visible? 6. How does the trend compare to the model? 7. What could explain the discrepancy? 8. What does this imply about the system?

Use this structure: 1. What was the goal? 2. How was it done? 3. What was found? 4. What does it mean for the system or decision? The challenge is not making it shorter. The challenge is making it shorter without losing the argument. ## Presentation Advice Students often show a figure, a component, or a result without first locating it in the larger system. That creates the "disembodied part" problem.

e, a component, or a result without first locating it in the larger system. That creates the "disembodied part" problem. Before showing any figure or component, answer: 1. What system are we talking about? 2. Where does this thing fit in that system? 3. Why does it matter? ## Figure Checklist For any serious MATLAB figure or technical plot, ask: 1. Why does this figure exist? 2. What is on each axis, with units? 3. Where did the data come from? 4. What uncertainties are shown? 5. What trend is visible? 6. How does the trend compare to the model? 7. What could explain the discrepancy? 8. What does this imply about the system?

pages.title: Tools and Setup Support
pages.text: # Tools and Setup Support This page is optional support for students who need quick setup guidance for the tools that appear in the course. ## MATLAB Mobile and Vision Tools MATLAB Mobile gives students a fast path to phone-based sensor data collection. In Tier 1 work, it is a practical way to stream and log accelerometer or orientation data without dedicated hardware. Computer vision tools provide a second path: using video as a sensor. That matters in this course because one recurring design principle is measuring the same quantity with more than one sensing method. The important course idea is not "use the fanciest sensor." It is "choose a sensing approach you can justify." ## Overleaf and LaTeX Some course deliverables are submitted as LaTeX source plus a compiled PDF. For students who are new to LaTeX, the goal is not mastery of the whole ecosystem. The goal is a working command of sections, figures, equations, and clean technical formatting. The minimum set of tasks students usually need is: - create sections and subsections - insert and caption figures - write equations - fix basic syntax errors when the document does not compile ## Portfolio Mindset Optional portfolio-building artifacts can be useful when they genuinely show engineering reasoning, not just polished packaging. That means a repository history, a short presentation, or a clear technical summary can all be valuable if they show how the work was defended, revised, and communicated.

They exist to help students get unstuck, build better habits, and go deeper on the course practices that support the required work. Use this area when you need one of four things: - extra course framing about AI, credibility, and professional value - basic statistics support before working with uncertainty, distributions, and residuals - setup help for GitHub, MATLAB, or Overleaf - support for technical communication and presentations - optional portfolio-building guidance The cards on this page open standalone supplemental resources so students can go directly to the topic they need without digging through one long page. AI Fluency and Course Value This page is optional support for students who want more context on why the course treats AI the way it does and why verification-and-validation judgment remains central. Basic Statistics for Experimental Data This page is optional support for students who have not had much statistics yet. The goal is not to turn you into a statistician before the first lab. The goal is to give you enough language to describe repeated measurements without pretending that one number tells the whole story. It is no longer part of the required E1 Week 1 reading sequence, but it preserves the full GitHub tutorial content for students who want that guidance while setting up their course documentation workflow. Tools and Setup Support This page is optional support for students who need quick setup guidance for the tools that appear in the course.

pages.title: Lab 1: P=IV Power & Uncertainty
pages.text: # ASEN 3501 — Lab 1: Power, Voltage, and the Language of Uncertainty ## Student-Facing Assignment **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Week:** 2 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 5 **Assessment:** Brief lab report (support document) + 3-minute oral interview **Estimated time:** 3–5 hours total (in-lab activity: ~1 hour; report preparation: 2–4 hours) --- ## Why This Lab Exists Every engineering measurement you take comes with a number and an uncertainty. The number alone is incomplete. It tells you what the instrument displayed, not what the physical quantity actually is or how confident you should be in that display. This lab is your first chance to practice treating uncertainty as a required output, not an afterthought. The physical setup is deliberately simple: a resistor, a power supply, and a multimeter. The simplicity is intentional. There is nothing here to distract you from the core question: *given that your instruments have limits, what can you actually claim about the power dissipated in this circuit?* That question, and the rigorous way to answer it, is the foundation this course is built on. --- ## Learning Goals By the end of this lab and its associated activities, you should be able to: 1. Measure voltage and current using a digital multimeter, correctly read and record the result, and assign an appropriate uncertainty from the instrument datasheet. 2. Apply first-order uncertainty propagation (the general method using partial derivatives) to compute the uncertainty in a derived quantity, electrical power, from measurements of voltage and current. 3. Explain why different algebraic forms of the same physical law (P = IV, P = V²/R, P = I²R) produce different uncertainty estimates for the same quantity, and articulate which form is most sensitive to which measured input. 4. Run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to estimate uncertainty using the same manufacturer specifications, and compare the Monte Carlo result to the partial-derivative result. 5. State a result in the course reporting form: *estimate ± interval (units; interval type, method, and coverage basis)*. ### Course reporting convention Use `E1_W2_R3_reading.md` for every uncertainty statement. Convert all elemental inputs to standard uncertainties before propagation. Report Taylor-series results as combined standard uncertainty `u_c`, and, when requested, as expanded uncertainty `U = k u_c` with `k` stated. Report Monte Carlo percentile bounds as coverage intervals, not confidence intervals. Label every plotted interval with its basis. Type A and Type B describe **how an uncertainty is evaluated**, not whether an effect is random or systematic. A Type A evaluation uses data from repeated observations. A Type B evaluation uses other information, such as a datasheet, calibration certificate, manual, prior measurements, or engineering judgment. After converting each input to a standard- uncertainty basis, combine them as `u_c`. A stated coverage factor `k` is the multiplier used to form expanded uncertainty, `U = k u_c`; `k = 2` is not automatically a 95% frequentist confidence interval unless the coverage basis supports that interpretation. 6. Explain the core concepts of this lab clearly and concisely to someone who has not taken this course. --- ## Background: The Circuit You will build a simple series circuit: a DC power supply, a known resistor, and a multimeter. You will measure: - **V** — voltage across the resistor (voltmeter, in parallel) - **I** — current through the resistor (ammeter, in series) From these measurements you will compute electrical power three ways: | Equation | Measured inputs | What you derive | |---|---|---| | P = IV | I and V directly | Power from both measurements | | P = V²/R | V and R | Power using voltage and resistance | | P = I²R | I and R | Power using current and resistance | Each form uses a different set of measured inputs. These are different **measurement models**, even though the circuit law is algebraically equivalent under ideal conditions. The estimates can differ because V, I, and R are acquired differently, at different times, with shared inputs and different loading or thermal effects. Algebra alone does not create new information. ### Which resistance value to use: two valid cases For the two forms that use R (P = V²/R and P = I²R), there are two defensible ways to get a value for R and its uncertainty: - **Case 1: Nominal + manufacturer tolerance.** Use the resistor's labeled nominal value and the tolerance printed on it (e.g., 200 Ω ± 5%). Treat the printed tolerance as a symmetric half-width `a_R`; with a uniform Type B model, use `u_R = a_R/√3`. - **Case 2: DMM-measured + instrument accuracy.** Measure the resistor's resistance directly with the multimeter and use the DMM's resistance-measurement accuracy specification (from the datasheet) to calculate its symmetric half-width `a_R`, then convert it to `u_R = a_R/√3` under the same uniform Type B model. **You are required to compute both cases** for P = V²/R and P = I²R, not just pick one. This is intentional, and it's the real point of this part of the lab: Case 1 and Case 2 use different information about R and will generally produce different point estimates and different uncertainty bounds. Compare their point estimates and labeled intervals, but do not interpret overlap as locating the true value. These estimates are not independent: they share measurements and may share instrument effects. Agreement is an internal-consistency check; disagreement is a prompt to inspect assumptions, timing, loading, self-heating, and the uncertainty model. ### A note on system warm-up and drift The power supply and multimeter in this circuit are not perfectly static the moment you turn them on. It's common to observe the voltage reading drift gradually over the first several minutes of operation. For example, it might start a little high right after power-on and settle to a slightly lower, stable value after about five minutes. This is typically caused by the power supply warming up to its normal operating temperature, not by an error in your circuit or technique. If you power-cycle the supply (turn it off and back on) with the circuit still connected, the reading will usually jump back up and begin drifting again. That's a useful check if you want to confirm the supply, rather than the resistor or DMM, is the source of the drift. **Practical implication for data collection:** take your five repeated V and I readings close together in time (within a minute or two of each other), rather than spacing them out over the full lab session. This keeps your repeatability trials measuring genuine instrument/reading variation, rather than accidentally capturing warm-up drift and mistaking it for repeatability. If you are using **Case 2** (DMM-measured resistance), there is an additional interaction to be aware of: if you measure R with the DMM *before* the resistor is in the powered circuit, that measurement reflects the resistor at room temperature ("cold"). Once current flows through the resistor for an extended period, it will self-heat slightly, and its actual in-circuit ("hot") resistance may differ from your cold measurement. You don't need to correct for this quantitatively, but flag it as a limitation if your Case 1 and Case 2 results in Section 6 don't overlap as cleanly as you'd expect. --- ## The Instrument — Reading Your Multimeter ### What the display is telling you A digital multimeter display shows a numerical reading that is updated at a fixed rate. On most benchtop multimeters, the least significant digit, the rightmost digit, will fluctuate during a measurement. This isn't noise in the measurement. It's the instrument's resolution limit combined with real variation in the signal. **Recording rule:** Cover the fluctuating digit with your finger. Read and record the stable digits. Assign an uncertainty of ±½ of the last stable digit's place value. **Example:** Your voltmeter reads 4.93, 4.92, 4.94. The units digit in the tenths place is stable (4.9), but the hundredths digit fluctuates (2, 3, 4). You record **4.9 V** and assign an uncertainty of **±0.05 V** (half of one unit in the tenths place). This is a *conservative but not overcautious* recording strategy. You aren't ignoring variation. You're bounding it at the resolution of the instrument. The tradeoff matters: if you assign unnecessarily large uncertainties, the systems designed around your measurements will be over-engineered and expensive. If you assign uncertainties that are too small, those systems might fail. The correct answer is the smallest defensible bound. ### Two methods, two different jobs You now have two different ways to assign an uncertainty to a measurement: the fluctuating-digit procedure above, and the manufacturer datasheet specification below. They aren't competing methods where you pick your favorite. They answer different questions: - **The fluctuating-digit method is a field technique.** It's what you do at the bench, in real time, with no datasheet in hand, just by watching the display. It's a fast, physical sanity check on resolution, and it's what you record and describe in Section 2 of your report to demonstrate you can read an instrument correctly. - **The datasheet specification is evidence for a specification-based Type B evaluation.** It's what you use for every partial-derivative and Monte Carlo calculation in this lab. The reason it takes priority over the fluctuating digit is that a display can look perfectly stable and still be systematically wrong by more than the resolution suggests. The datasheet's accuracy spec captures that systematic error, which the fluctuating digit alone cannot. If you ever find that your fluctuating-digit uncertainty is *larger* than the datasheet spec, that's worth a sentence of discussion in your report. It usually means the signal itself is genuinely noisy (e.g., an unstable supply or a marginal connection), not that the instrument's formal accuracy is worse than advertised. ### Using the datasheet The instrument datasheet specifies accuracy in the form: ``` ± (% of reading + % of range) ``` For example: ± (0.5% of reading + 0.1% of range). This is a manufacturer limit, not automatically a standard uncertainty. Unless current calibration is verified, label this exercise **specification-based**. Treat the symmetric limit `±a` as a Type B input with a uniform model, so the standard uncertainty is `u=a/√3`, unless the datasheet states another coverage basis. State that assumption before propagation. **Careful:** these are percentages, not fractions. 0.5% means 0.005, not 0.5. A common mistake is to use 0.5 directly in the calculation, which inflates the uncertainty by a factor of 100. Double-check your decimal conversion before propagating. Use the bundled [Keysight 34460A/34461A/34465A/34470A datasheet](). Before you begin any measurements, locate the 34461A specification for: - DC voltage accuracy in the range you are using - DC current accuracy in the range you are using Write these down. They are your primary uncertainty inputs. Datasheets typically list several tolerance columns depending on how long it has been since the instrument's last calibration (e.g., 90-day, 1-year, 2-year). Use the **2-year column** unless your instructor tells you otherwise. The DMMs in this lab were purchased in **2019**, and their listed accuracy specification assumes the stated calibration conditions. If current calibration cannot be verified, the exercise remains a specification-based estimate rather than a calibration-supported result. Do not invent a drift correction; state this limitation in Section 6. The datasheet also lists an additional accuracy derating as a function of deviation from a reference room temperature. **For this lab, assume the lab room is at the datasheet's reference temperature and do not compute a temperature correction term.** This is an intentional scope decision, not an oversight: part of engineering judgment is recognizing which sources of uncertainty are negligible for a given situation and which are not. You are still expected to **state this assumption explicitly** in your report (Section 1 or 3) as your justification for excluding the temperature term. A documented assumption, even a simple one, is part of a complete uncertainty analysis. ### Measurement-system limitations to identify At a conceptual level, note four effects: voltmeter and ammeter **loading** can alter the circuit; using one DMM sequentially makes V, I, and R non-simultaneous while the supply and resistor drift; resistor **self-heating** changes the hot resistance from the cold ohmmeter measurement; and quantities derived from shared V, I, or R inputs are correlated. You do not need to estimate covariance numerically in Lab 1, but you must not call the three power estimates independent. --- ## In-Lab Procedure The procedure has three parts: building and measuring (steps 1–5), uncertainty analysis from the datasheet (steps 6–8), and Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB (steps 9–10). **Note:** a single multimeter can't measure voltage and current at the same time. It must be configured and connected differently for each. You'll measure V and I as two separate steps, not simultaneously. ### Lab configuration | Nominal resistance | Tolerance | Power rating | Supply target | Expected current | Expected power | Power-rating factor of safety | |---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:| | 200 Ω | ±5% | 0.25 W | 5.00 V | 25 mA | 0.125 W | 2× | Keep the power supply **off** while building or changing the circuit. Before applying power, verify the resistor rating, DMM lead jacks, measurement mode, and wiring. The expected values are pre-lab checks, not substitutes for your measured values. 1. Build the circuit: connect the resistor to the power supply so current can flow through it. Record the nominal resistor value and tolerance from its label. Also measure the resistor's resistance directly with the DMM (easiest done before wiring it into the powered circuit) and record this measured value. You'll need both the nominal and measured values for the resistance-uncertainty comparison described above. 2. With the circuit verified and the supply still off, set the power supply target to **5.00 V** as specified in the Lab configuration table, then apply power. 3. **Measure voltage first.** Configure the multimeter as a voltmeter and connect it in parallel across the resistor. Read and record V from the display using the fluctuating-digit procedure above. Take five repeated V readings, close together in time (see the warm-up note above), to check for repeatability. ![Digital multimeter configured as a voltmeter, connected in parallel across the resistor, displaying a voltage reading.](images/voltage1.png) 4. **Then measure current.** Reconfigure the multimeter as an ammeter and reconnect it in series in the loop (this requires briefly breaking the circuit to insert the meter in the current path; it can't stay connected the way it was for the voltage measurement). Read and record I from the display using the same fluctuating-digit procedure. Take five repeated I readings, close together in time. ![Digital multimeter configured as an ammeter, connected in series with the resistor, displaying a current reading.](images/current1.png) 5. Compute P three ways from your recorded V and I values. Note the differences. 6. Using the manufacturer specifications from the datasheet, calculate the symmetric limits `a_V` and `a_I`, then convert them to the Type B standard uncertainties `u_V = a_V/√3` and `u_I = a_I/√3` under the uniform model. 7. Apply first-order uncertainty propagation (partial derivatives) to compute the combined standard uncertainty `u_P` for each of the three forms. Show your work: write out the partial derivatives, substitute standard uncertainties, and state the result as P ± u_P. 8. Answer: which form of the power equation is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Where would you spend money on a better instrument if minimizing uncertainty in P was the goal? 9. Using the same manufacturer limits, run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB for each of the three power measurement models. For each manufacturer input, sample uniformly over its original bounds, such as `[V-a_V, V+a_V]` and `[I-a_I, I+a_I]`. Use the corresponding original tolerance bounds for each resistance case. Do not use `u_V`, `u_I`, or `u_R` as the uniform half-widths; those symbols are standard uncertainties after division by `√3`. Use at least 10,000 samples. Plot the resulting distribution of P for each model with appropriate labels and captions. If a manufacturer or calibration source explicitly supplies a Gaussian standard deviation or coverage basis, you may use that documented model instead and must cite it; do not invent a Gaussian `σ` from an unlabeled tolerance. 10. Compare like quantities across methods: compare the Monte Carlo output standard deviation with the Taylor-series combined standard uncertainty `u_P`, and report any Monte Carlo percentile bounds as clearly labeled coverage intervals. State whether the results are consistent and explain meaningful differences. --- ## Deliverable: Brief Lab Report You'll prepare a brief lab report to bring to your oral interview. This document is your support material for the interview. You may refer to it during the conversation, but the interview questions will probe your understanding, not your ability to read your own document. **The report isn't graded independently. It's evidence that you prepared.** ### Required sections **1. Circuit description and measurement record** One paragraph and a simple schematic or sketch. What did you build? What did you measure? Include your recorded V and I values with units. Include the datasheet specification you used. **2. Fluctuating digit — what you did and why** Describe the fluctuating digit procedure in your own words. What did the display show? What did you record? What uncertainty did you assign from the display, and why? **3. Uncertainty propagation — partial derivatives** Show the three partial derivative derivations for u_P under each power equation form. For the two forms that depend on R (P = V²/R and P = I²R), compute u_P **twice each**: once using Case 1 (nominal R and its tolerance) and once using Case 2 (DMM-measured R and its measurement uncertainty). Present the final result for each as P ± u_P with units. A table is the clearest way to present this (one row per form/case combination). **4. Sensitivity analysis** Which form is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Answer with numbers, not just words. Where would you invest in a better instrument, and why? **5. Monte Carlo simulation** Include your MATLAB figure(s) showing the distributions of P for each equation form. Each figure must have a title, labeled axes with units, a legend, and a caption that states the key takeaway in one sentence. Include the key MATLAB code (the simulation loop and distribution plot — not every setup line). **State the original manufacturer half-widths `a`, show the uniform bounds you sampled, and report the simulated output standard deviation and any selected percentile coverage interval.** **6. Comparison: partial derivatives vs. Monte Carlo** Are the results consistent? State the comparison numerically. Don't just say "yes they agree." What does the comparison tell you about when you might prefer one method over the other? Also briefly address: given that the DMMs were purchased in 2019, is it reasonable to trust the datasheet's 2-year-column specification at face value, or should you flag possible drift beyond spec as an additional (unquantified) source of uncertainty? Finally, compare your Case 1 and Case 2 results for P = V²/R and P = I²R: do the two P ± u_P ranges overlap? Treat overlap only as an internal-consistency clue under your stated assumptions; it does not locate the true value. State what you observed and identify shared inputs or effects that prevent the cases from being independent. **7. Result statement** State your best estimate of P in the correct engineering form: *P = [value] ± [uncertainty] [units] ([interval type; method; coverage basis])* **8. Plain-language explanation** In 3–5 sentences, explain what you did in this lab and what your result means to someone who has never taken an engineering course. Assume your audience is a curious high school student. This section should be in your introduction or conclusion, not appended as an afterthought. ### Format and submission Submit your report as a single `.zip` archive containing your LaTeX source (`.tex` file(s) and any figure files) and the compiled PDF, uploaded to Canvas before your scheduled interview slot. Bring the compiled PDF (printed or on a device) to your interview; the LaTeX source lets the AI-assisted grading pipeline check your work against the required sections consistently. See **E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX** if you haven't used LaTeX before. **Submission:** See Canvas for the due date. Power interviews are scheduled during lab on Monday, Sep 14 and Wednesday, Sep 16. --- ## Oral Interview The oral interview is a 3-minute, one-on-one conversation with a member of the teaching team. You may bring your lab report. No other materials are permitted. The interview is a pass/fail assessment. You must pass this interview to satisfy the Lab 1 requirement. Note that Learning Goal 6, explaining this lab's core concepts to someone without an engineering background, isn't just a report-writing exercise. It's one of the categories the interviewer may probe (see Category 5 in the student rubric), so it's worth practicing your plain-language explanation out loud, not just writing it in Section 8. **See the separate file `LAB1_PIV_rubric_student.md` for the question categories and what a passing response looks like.** Prepare by reviewing that document before your interview. ### Preparing with the course AI tutor The course AI tutor persona is described in the E0 supplemental materials. You can upload this assignment document and the student rubric to a Claude or ChatGPT session configured with the tutor persona, and practice your oral responses before the interview. Example prompts: - *"Ask me an oral interview question about why P = IV and P = V²/R give different uncertainty estimates."* - *"Pretend you are the instructor and ask me to explain the fluctuating digit procedure."* - *"Give me a harder version of a question about when Monte Carlo is preferable to the partial derivative method."* The AI tutor cannot predict the exact questions you will be asked, but it can give you targeted practice on the core concepts. Use it. --- ## Resources - E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo - E1 Week 2 Lecture: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty - Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*, 4th ed. — Chapter on uncertainty propagation (reference copy on course page) - [Keysight 34460A/34461A/34465A/34470A multimeter datasheet]() — use the 34461A accuracy tables and 2-year column - MATLAB starter script — available on Canvas (sets up the Monte Carlo loop structure; you complete the equation forms and plotting) - E0 Supplemental: Virtual Multimeter Simulator (optional bonus activity) - E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice - E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX - For multimeter technique when measuring voltage and current as separate steps in the same circuit: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y6xnLkiUMn8 --- *ASEN 3501 — Lab 1 Assignment | released E1 Week 2; interviews Course Week 5* *Evergreen — physics content; Update-friendly — multimeter datasheet specifications, MATLAB starter script, power supply target voltage*
