E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data. ================================================================================ slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same (Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.") Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester. ================================================================================ slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise (Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.") Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions. ================================================================================ slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random (Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.) Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected. ================================================================================ slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim (Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed arrow spanning ±0.4 N. To the right: "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (expanded uncertainty, k=2, Taylor-series method)." Below the bar, two evidence boxes feed into combined standard uncertainty: "Type A — data-driven: repeated observations" and "Type B — information-driven: datasheets, certificates, prior information, judgment." A final note reads: "Type A/Type B describe evaluation methods, not random/systematic effects. Always name the interval and its basis.") Talking Point: Uncertainty helps convert a raw measurement into an engineering claim, but the symbol after the plus-or-minus sign must be identified. Type A and Type B tell us how an uncertainty was evaluated. Type A is data-driven, using statistics from repeated observations. Type B is information-driven, using sources such as datasheets, calibration certificates, manuals, prior information, or engineering judgment. These labels do not mean random versus systematic; either evaluation method may be used to characterize different effects. Coleman and Steele define a standard uncertainty as an estimate of a standard deviation; standard uncertainties have no percentage confidence or coverage by themselves. We combine components on that common basis to obtain a combined standard uncertainty, u-c. If the result is then multiplied by a stated coverage factor, we report expanded uncertainty, U equals k times u-c. The coverage factor k is a multiplier, not a confidence level by itself, so k equals 2 does not automatically create a 95 percent frequentist confidence interval. A frequentist confidence interval and a Monte Carlo coverage interval are different constructions and must keep their own names. Our course convention is simple: state the estimate, units, interval type, method, and coverage basis every time. ================================================================================ slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions (Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.) Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size. ================================================================================ slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean (Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.") Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================ slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows (Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.) Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester. ================================================================================ slide 3: Variability —

Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. or comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of

average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected. ================================================================================ slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim (Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. mbined standard uncertainty: "Type A — data-driven: repeated observations" and "Type B — information-driven: datasheets, certificates, prior information, judgment." Talking Point: Uncertainty helps convert a raw measurement into an engineering claim, but the symbol after the plus-or-minus sign must be identified. Type A and Type B tell us how an uncertainty was evaluated. Type A is data-driven, using statistics from repeated observations. Type B is information-driven, using sources such as datasheets, calibration certificates, manuals, prior information, or engineering judgment. These labels do not mean random versus systematic; either evaluation method

prior information, or engineering judgment. These labels do not mean random versus systematic; either evaluation method may be used to characterize different effects. Coleman and Steele define a standard uncertainty as an estimate of a standard deviation; standard uncertainties have no percentage confidence or coverage by themselves. We combine components on that common basis to obtain a combined standard uncertainty, u-c. If the result is then multiplied by a stated coverage factor, we report expanded uncertainty, U equals k times u-c. The coverage factor k is a multiplier, not a confidence level by itself, so k equals 2 does not automatically create a 95 percent frequentist confidence interval. ent frequentist confidence interval. A frequentist confidence interval and a Monte Carlo coverage interval are different constructions and must keep their own names. t more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. ved quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size. ================================================================================ slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean (Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom,

Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.") Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on

idence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a

This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and

ity vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same

ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an

in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch

A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If

your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the

But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================ slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows (Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty,

defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards.

entist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators,

w 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer.

Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart.

================================= slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.")

± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end

away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================ slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows (Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two

ncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.) Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty —

t matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability,

sing only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — What Is Missing from This Conclusion? (Image: A bar graph showing three side-by-side bars on a white background. Blue bars labeled "Measured Power" at three voltage levels (3V, 6V, 9V) with values approximately 1.8 W, 7.1 W, 16.4 W. Orange bars labeled "Model Prediction (P = V²/R)" at the same voltage levels. The bars appear close in height. No error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction

error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is missing?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this figure and caption. [Pause — allow student responses.] The conclusion is not defensible. Without uncertainty bounds on the bars, you cannot determine whether the visual agreement is meaningful or coincidental. The gap between each blue and orange bar might exceed the measurement uncertainty — in which case you have a discrepancy requiring explanation. Or the gap might be negligible — in which case agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison

se agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty intervals on every bar, the conclusion has no engineering basis. A professional report that made this claim would be returned immediately for revision. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that

mental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Opening quote "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything" — Carl Richards —

(2026-05-14). - Opening quote "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything" — Carl Richards — incorporated in the title slide talking point per the W2 L1 blueprint. - Three-way distinction (variability / measurement error / uncertainty) is the organizing spine of the lecture, as specified in the blueprint Purpose statement. - Confidence interval interpretation (slide 7) uses the correct frequentist framing — students need this for the W3 comparison work. - Repeatability vs. reproducibility (slide 8) references ASME and ISO standards, consistent with the professional standards thread from Week 1. - Slide 10 explicitly connects this lecture forward to W3, W4, and W5 — AMVF continuity maintained without forcing it. - Monte Carlo content deferred to W2 L2 per

e forward to W3, W4, and W5 — AMVF continuity maintained without forcing it. - Monte Carlo content deferred to W2 L2 per blueprint structure. - Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — missing uncertainty in bar graph claim). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 9): Slide 5 talking point — added sentence attributing experimental uncertainty requirements to ASME PTC 19.1, distinguishing from ASME V&V 10-2006 which is the computational modeling standard. - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 6 talking point — added t-distribution note for N < 30 with MATLAB reference. Slide 7 talking point — added one sentence on Bayesian credible interval as an alternative framework, noting course uses frequentist
ing point — added one sentence on Bayesian credible interval as an alternative framework, noting course uses frequentist CI per ASME/ISO standards.

pages.title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Lecture Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Introduces first-order partial derivative error propagation using electrical power as the working example: P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R. The forms use different measured inputs for the same physical quantity, so the measurement models have different sensitivities and dependencies. Students see how justified input models produce different uncertainty estimates in the derived output. Monte Carlo simulation is introduced as a computational complement to the partial derivative method. Every result must carry an estimate plus a clearly labeled standard uncertainty, expanded uncertainty, confidence interval, prediction interval, or Monte Carlo coverage interval, as appropriate. **Graph presentation framework:** This lecture should include a dedicated segment on how to present a graph — a sequential checklist that students will apply to every MATLAB figure they produce in the course: (1) motivation — why does this graph exist, what question does it answer; (2) axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale; (3) data source — where did the data come from, how was it collected; (4) uncertainties — bounds shown and labeled with their basis and coverage; (5) trend — what pattern the data shows; (6) model comparison — how the trend compares to the predictive model; (7) discrepancy — what are possible causes for any gap between data and model; (8) system-level implication — what does this graph tell you about the overall system. This framework should also be provided as a required handout/reading in E1 W3, and students should be held to it in every subsequent deliverable. Distinguish variability, measurement error, and uncertainty, and explain why the distinction matters for engineering claims — CLO 2 Apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty in a derived quantity — CLO 2 Explain how different mathematical representations of the same physical quantity yield different sensitivities to measurement variation — CLO 2 Run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to model uncertainty in a derived quantity and interpret the resulting distribution — CLO 2 E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities (Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.") Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively. ================================================================================ slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method (Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.) Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point. ================================================================================ slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms (Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.") Talking Point: Power equals IV, V-squared over R, and I-squared R are algebraically equivalent for an ideal resistor, but the lab implements them as different measurement models. Each uses a different combination of measured V, I, and R values, and those measurements may be taken sequentially, share instrument effects, load the circuit, or reflect different resistor temperatures. The partial derivatives describe sensitivity within each model; algebra alone does not create independent evidence. In Lab 1, identify these limitations conceptually and do not treat the three estimates as independent. ================================================================================ slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest (Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.") Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break (Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.") Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding. ================================================================================ slide 7: Manufacturer Bounds and Monte Carlo Inputs (Image: A uniform distribution centered at measured value x with endpoints x−a and x+a. A bracket labels a as the manufacturer half-width used for Monte Carlo sampling. Below it, an arrow shows the conversion u_B = a/√3, labeled "standard uncertainty used in Taylor-series propagation." A side note reads: "Use a Gaussian only when a source supplies σ or a defensible coverage basis; do not invent σ from an unlabeled tolerance.") Talking Point: Keep the manufacturer limit and the standard uncertainty as different quantities. If a datasheet gives a symmetric tolerance plus or minus a, and that is all you know, represent the Type B input as uniform over the original bounds x minus a to x plus a. Those original bounds go into the Monte Carlo sampler. The standard uncertainty of that same uniform input is u-B equals a divided by square root of three, and that standard uncertainty goes into the Taylor-series propagation. Sampling only from x minus u-B to x plus u-B would make the simulated input too narrow. A Gaussian input is appropriate only when a manufacturer, calibration certificate, or explicit assumption supplies a defensible standard deviation or coverage basis. Do not invent sigma from an unlabeled tolerance. Compare the Monte Carlo output standard deviation with the Taylor combined standard uncertainty, and label percentile bounds as coverage intervals. ================================================================================ slide 8: Comparing the Three Measurement Models — A Diagnostic, Not a Truth Region (Image: Three output distributions labeled by the P measurement model. Shared-input arrows connect them. A note over the overlap reads "internal consistency clue" and a warning reads "overlap does not locate the true value." A checklist names loading, non-simultaneous readings, drift, self-heating, and shared-input covariance.) Talking Point: Compare the three output distributions as a diagnostic. Substantial agreement can support internal consistency under the stated assumptions, but overlap does not prove where the true value lies, and the estimates are not independent because they share measurements and instrument effects. If they diverge, inspect the measurement models: meter loading, sequential timing, supply drift, resistor self-heating, and the Type B input assumptions. Covariance is an awareness item here; you are not required to calculate it in this first exposure. ================================================================================ slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework (Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.") Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward. ================================================================================ slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example (Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.") Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a major new element in Iteration 5 not present in the prior version. Blueprint specifies this framework should be in W2 L2 and provided as a reading in W3. V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Slides 9-10 retained per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS; motivating transition sentence added to slide 9 talking point: "Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive." P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction. Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint. Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method, overlap region as key output — per blueprint. "Where would you spend your money/budget" framing embedded in slide 5 sensitivity matrix. Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — formula choice based on sensor uncertainties). V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 3 talking point — added worked partial derivative calculation step: ∂P/∂V = 2V/R for P = V²/R, evaluated at V = 12 V, R = 4.8 Ω, showing sensitivity coefficient of 5 W/V. E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty.
