Iteration 5 blueprint addition: "A brief segment should cover how to show design margins and acceptance criteria on data plots." Kept explicitly scoped as a communication skill, not a systems design topic, per the blueprint instruction. Pre-commitment to metric and threshold emphasized in slides 4 and 9 — matches course assessment philosophy from instructor_clarification_notes. Multi-sensor comparison in slide 7 connects to the W3 lab activity. Residual structure diagnostic introduced in slides 6 and 9 — seeds W3 L2 (calibration vs. validation, anomalous data patterns) without front-loading that content. AMVF comparison node made explicit in slide 10. Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — post-hoc

(title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — post-hoc metric selection); renumbered closure to 12. V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 8): Added slide 11 (Four-Part Critique Structure — opens the comparative analysis session). Old slides 11→12 (evaluator moment), 12→13 (closure). Critique structure pre-committed before in-class exercise, mirroring the pre-commitment principle already in slide 4 and 9. V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 4 — added inline definition of "post-hoc" (after the fact) per INSTRUCTOR APPROVES BUT KEEP BOTH. Slide 6 — added explicit definition of "residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point" at first use. E1_W3_L1 — Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics ASEN 3501:

ue − model prediction at each data point" at first use. E1_W3_L1 — Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics Talking Point: Weeks 1 and 2 gave you the tools to measure a quantity and express your confidence in it as an uncertainty interval. Week 3 asks the next question: now that you have a measurement with quantified uncertainty and a model prediction, how do you decide whether they agree? Not visually — not by eyeballing a plot and saying "they look close" — but with a structured, defensible, numerical argument. That shift,

t by eyeballing a plot and saying "they look close" — but with a structured, defensible, numerical argument. That shift, from visual judgment to structured comparison, is one of the most important cognitive moves in this course. This lecture gives you the tools to make it, including how to put your comparison criteria directly on the figure where anyone reading the report can see them. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem with Visual Inspection (Image: Three side-by-side plots showing identical data and model, differing only in axis scale. Plot 1: axes zoomed far out — data points and model line appear to coincide perfectly. Plot 2: axes zoomed tightly — data and model appear to diverge dramatically. Plot 3: intermediate

to coincide perfectly. Plot 2: axes zoomed tightly — data and model appear to diverge dramatically. Plot 3: intermediate scale — judgment is genuinely ambiguous. All three plots have the same axis labels and the same data values; only the scale differs. A caption below: "Same data. Same model. Three different visual impressions. Which one represents agreement?" A red annotation: "Agreement cannot depend on how you made the figure.") Talking Point: I want you to feel this problem before we solve it. I have shown you the same dataset and the same model prediction three times, with three different axis scales. In the first plot, they look identical. In the second, they look like significant disagreement. In the third, you are genuinely unsure. But the data and model have not changed — only

ok like significant disagreement. In the third, you are genuinely unsure. But the data and model have not changed — only the axis scale has. This is the fundamental problem with visual inspection as the basis for an engineering conclusion. A plot is a presentation choice, and presentation choices are not physics. If your conclusion about agreement changes depending on how you drew the figure, then your conclusion is not grounded in the data — it is grounded in aesthetics. The rest of this lecture is about replacing aesthetic judgment with a defined numerical protocol. ================================================================================ slide 3: What Does "Agreement" Mean Technically? (Image: A horizontal number line diagram. A vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction

"Agreement" Mean Technically? (Image: A horizontal number line diagram. A vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction μ_model" is shown at a fixed position, with a light shaded band around it labeled "Model Uncertainty ±U_model." A measurement point labeled "Experimental Outcome μ_exp" is shown with a horizontal two-headed arrow labeled "±U_exp." Three cases are stacked vertically below the number line. Case A: the two uncertainty bands overlap broadly — green label "Overlap — agreement plausible." Case B: the bands just barely touch — yellow label "Marginal — quantify carefully." Case C: the bands are fully separated — red label "No overlap — genuine discrepancy requires explanation." A note: "Agreement depends on both uncertainties, not just one.") Talking Point: Agreement in the

requires explanation." A note: "Agreement depends on both uncertainties, not just one.") Talking Point: Agreement in the engineering sense means that the difference between your measurement and your model prediction is not larger than what can be explained by the combined uncertainty of both quantities. Not just the measurement uncertainty — both. A model prediction carries uncertainty too, from approximations, parameter assumptions, and model-form limitations. The question is not "is the measurement equal to the prediction?" but "is the difference between measurement and prediction consistent with zero, given the combined uncertainty of both?" When uncertainty intervals overlap, agreement is plausible. When they do not overlap at all, you have a genuine discrepancy that requires

intervals overlap, agreement is plausible. When they do not overlap at all, you have a genuine discrepancy that requires explanation — either the model is wrong, the measurement is wrong, or your uncertainty estimates are wrong. And "plausible" is not enough — you still need to quantify it with a specific metric. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation Metrics — Name Your Criterion Before You Look (Image: Three labeled formula boxes arranged vertically on a clean white background. Box 1: "Relative Difference: d_rel = |Q_exp − Q_model| / Q_model × 100%. Interprets as: how far apart are the values as a percentage?" Box 2: "Within Combined Uncertainty: |Q_exp − Q_model| < √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: is the difference

entage?" Box 2: "Within Combined Uncertainty: |Q_exp − Q_model| < √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: is the difference smaller than the combined uncertainty at the stated confidence level?" Box 3: "Normalized Agreement Index: NAI = |Q_exp − Q_model| / √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: how many combined standard deviations apart are the two values? NAI < 1 suggests agreement." A red text box at the bottom: "REQUIRED: state your metric and acceptance threshold BEFORE computing the comparison. Post-hoc metric selection is not defensible.") Talking Point: A validation metric is a defined, reproducible procedure for quantifying the distance between a measurement and a model prediction in units that account for the relevant uncertainties. Three representative forms are shown here. Relative

del prediction in units that account for the relevant uncertainties. Three representative forms are shown here. Relative difference is the simplest — it gives you the percentage gap — but it does not account for uncertainty. The combined-uncertainty check adds the critical test: is the gap smaller than what your uncertainties can explain? The normalized agreement index generalizes this by expressing disagreement in units of combined standard uncertainty — values below one suggest agreement. The critical rule is at the bottom: you must state your metric and acceptance threshold before you look at the result. If you choose your metric after seeing the data — post-hoc, meaning after the fact — you can always find one that confirms what you hoped to find. That is a well-documented bias in

meaning after the fact — you can always find one that confirms what you hoped to find. That is a well-documented bias in experimental reporting, and it is why pre-commitment is required in every deliverable in this course. The term "post-hoc" is worth knowing: it appears in academic literature, review comments, and professional engineering standards, and recognizing it will help you identify this specific failure mode when you see it in your own work or someone else's. ================================================================================ slide 5: A Worked Comparison — Full Numerical Example (Image: A structured step-by-step worked example box. Measurement: "Q_exp = 14.7 N, U_exp = ±0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Model prediction: "Q_model = 15.3 N, U_model = ±0.6 N

exp = 14.7 N, U_exp = ±0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Model prediction: "Q_model = 15.3 N, U_model = ±0.6 N (from model parameter uncertainty)." Step 1: "Relative difference: |14.7 − 15.3| / 15.3 × 100% = 3.9%." Step 2: "Combined uncertainty: U_comb = √(0.4² + 0.6²) = 0.72 N = 4.7% of model prediction." Step 3: "Comparison: 3.9% < 4.7% — the difference is within combined uncertainty." Step 4 (highlighted in gold): "Conclusion: Agreement is supported at 95% confidence using the relative-difference-within-combined-uncertainty metric, pre-committed before this calculation." A note at the bottom: "All steps must appear in your report in this order.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a complete worked example so you see exactly what the report section looks like. I have a force

int: Let me walk through a complete worked example so you see exactly what the report section looks like. I have a force measurement of 14.7 N with a 95% uncertainty interval of plus or minus 0.4 N, and a model prediction of 15.3 N with an estimated model uncertainty of plus or minus 0.6 N. Step one: compute the relative difference — 3.9%. Step two: compute the combined uncertainty as the quadrature sum of the two individual uncertainties — 0.72 N, or 4.7% of the model value. Step three: compare — 3.9% is less than 4.7%, so the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. Step four: state the conclusion, tied to the metric and the confidence level. Every step must appear in your report, in this order. Skipping steps, or stating the conclusion without showing the calculation, is

must appear in your report, in this order. Skipping steps, or stating the conclusion without showing the calculation, is insufficient documentation for an engineering comparison. ================================================================================ slide 6: When Agreement Fails — Disagreement Is Diagnostic (Image: A two-branch flowchart starting from "Comparison Metric Exceeds Threshold." Branch 1: "Measurement problem?" — sub-nodes: "Calibration error? Installation effect? Uncontrolled confounder?" Branch 2: "Model problem?" — sub-nodes: "Wrong equation form? Invalid assumption? Missing physical effect?" Both branches converge at "Investigate root cause — then classify the discrepancy type." Below: two residual plots. Left: residuals randomly scattered around zero — labeled

then classify the discrepancy type." Below: two residual plots. Left: residuals randomly scattered around zero — labeled "Random residuals: consistent with measurement noise." Right: residuals trending upward with the independent variable — labeled "Structured residuals: evidence of missing physics, not noise.") Talking Point: When your validation metric tells you that measurement and model do not agree within your defined criterion, that is not a failed experiment — it is a finding. The disagreement is diagnostic: it tells you that either your measurement has an unaccounted-for problem or your model is missing something. Your job is to diagnose which. The residuals — defined as: residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point — carry that diagnostic information. Random

defined as: residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point — carry that diagnostic information. Random residuals that scatter around zero with no pattern suggest measurement noise. Residuals that show a systematic trend — always positive, or increasing with the independent variable — suggest a model-form limitation: the model is missing a physical effect that grows with that variable. Structured disagreement is more useful than agreement, because it tells you something specific about what the model is missing. ================================================================================ slide 7: Comparing Two Measurements — The Multi-Sensor Case (Image: Two side-by-side numerical scenarios. Scenario A: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 14.2 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 14.2| /

mage: Two side-by-side numerical scenarios. Scenario A: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 14.2 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 14.2| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 0.5 / 0.64 = 0.78 — within 1 combined σ. Agreement supported." Below: green checkmark. Scenario B: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 16.1 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 16.1| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 1.4 / 0.64 = 2.19 — exceeds 2 combined σ. Disagreement: investigate sensor calibration or installation." Below: red annotation. A caption: "The same metric applies whether you are comparing measurement to model or measurement to measurement.") Talking Point: The same validation metric framework applies when you are comparing two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors — which is exactly what the lab activity this week requires. The calculation is identical:

ntity using different sensors — which is exactly what the lab activity this week requires. The calculation is identical: compute the difference between the two sensor readings, compute the combined uncertainty, ask whether the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. If yes, the two sensors are internally consistent — you have cross-validated one against the other. If no, at least one sensor has a problem: calibration error, installation effect, or a systematic difference in what the two sensors are physically responding to. Using two independent sensors to measure the same quantity is one of the most powerful tools in experimental practice, because independent evidence is more convincing than a single instrument, and this lab is designed specifically around that.

use independent evidence is more convincing than a single instrument, and this lab is designed specifically around that. ================================================================================ slide 8: Showing Margins and Requirements on Plots (Image: A clean figure showing experimental data (blue dots with error bars) and a model prediction (solid red curve) on a plot with labeled axes. In addition to the data and model, three horizontal reference lines are added: a solid gold line labeled "Requirement: P ≤ 35 W," a dashed green line labeled "Design Margin: P ≤ 30 W," and a light gray shaded band between 30 and 35 W labeled "Margin Zone." Several data points fall below 30 W (in the "pass" region), one falls in the margin zone, and none exceed 35 W. A caption: "The plot itself

fall below 30 W (in the "pass" region), one falls in the margin zone, and none exceed 35 W. A caption: "The plot itself communicates whether the result passes or fails — no text narrative required.") Talking Point: A brief but important practical skill: when your experiment is connected to a design requirement — a load limit, a temperature bound, an efficiency threshold — you can and should annotate your figure with that requirement directly. Put a horizontal line on the plot at the requirement value. Add a second line at the design margin if one exists. Now the figure communicates the conclusion without requiring the reader to cross-reference a separate document: every data point is visually classified as passing or failing. This technique connects your experimental result to the

: every data point is visually classified as passing or failing. This technique connects your experimental result to the engineering decision the experiment was designed to inform. It is not a deep dive into systems design — that is for other courses — but it is a practical communication skill that will appear in every technical report you produce in industry. In this course, when your experiment has a defined acceptance criterion, that criterion should appear on the figure. ================================================================================ slide 9: Building a Defensible Comparison Argument — The Required Structure (Image: A structured six-step outline formatted as a numbered list, styled like a report section template. "(1) State validation metric — name it, explain why it

matted as a numbered list, styled like a report section template. "(1) State validation metric — name it, explain why it is appropriate for this data type." "(2) State acceptance threshold — defined before the comparison." "(3) Apply metric numerically — show all intermediate calculations." "(4) State conclusion — agreement or disagreement — tied to the confidence level and the stated metric." "(5) If disagreement: describe residual structure — random or systematic?" "(6) If agreement: note the conditions under which agreement holds and any caveats about range or extrapolation." A red annotation at steps 1 and 2: "Pre-commitment required — these two steps must precede step 3.") Talking Point: I want to give you the required structure for a defensible comparison argument, because this

de step 3.") Talking Point: I want to give you the required structure for a defensible comparison argument, because this structure will appear in every deliverable from this point forward. First: name your validation metric and justify why it is appropriate. Different data types and different modeling claims call for different metrics. Second: state your acceptance threshold before computing. Third: show the full numerical calculation with all intermediate steps. Fourth: state the conclusion explicitly — agreement or disagreement — tied to the metric and the confidence level. Fifth: if the comparison reveals disagreement, classify the residual structure. And sixth: if the comparison supports agreement, note the conditions and limits of that agreement. This is the argument structure for

the comparison supports agreement, note the conditions and limits of that agreement. This is the argument structure for the Comparative Analysis Memo due this week, and it is the same structure I will look for in every subsequent lab report. ================================================================================ slide 10: The AMVF Comparison Node — What You Are Doing (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the "Quantitative Comparison" node highlighted prominently in gold. Arrows entering the node from the left are labeled "Simulation Outcomes (with model uncertainty)" and from the right "Experimental Outcomes (with measurement uncertainty)." Arrows leaving the node: one labeled "Agreement within criterion → Validation supported" pointing upward-left toward the Mathematical Model node,

one labeled "Agreement within criterion → Validation supported" pointing upward-left toward the Mathematical Model node, and one labeled "Disagreement → Investigate model-form or measurement problem" pointing downward. A bracket below the node reads "This is the activity you are executing in the Week 3 lab and memo. This is where the two branches of the AMVF meet.") Talking Point: I want to locate everything in this lecture within the AMVF, because that connection is important. The quantitative comparison node is where the two branches of the AMVF meet — where the simulation output from the mathematical branch and the experimental outcome from the physical branch are evaluated against each other. Everything you have learned in the last two weeks — measurement, uncertainty propagation,

evaluated against each other. Everything you have learned in the last two weeks — measurement, uncertainty propagation, confidence intervals — has been preparation for this node. The structured comparison you are now able to execute is not a report-writing exercise. It is the central activity of the validation branch of the AMVF. When you apply your validation metric in the lab this week, you are executing a step that professional engineers perform every time they need to argue that a model is credible for a specific use case and a specific set of conditions. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluating Another's Work — The Four-Part Critique Structure (Image: A clean four-box layout on a white background, each box color-coded. Box

Work — The Four-Part Critique Structure (Image: A clean four-box layout on a white background, each box color-coded. Box 1 (green): "(1) What is correct and well-supported? — Name specific evidence. Identify where the report does what it claims." Box 2 (red): "(2) What is incorrect or unsupported? — Identify claims that are wrong, or conclusions that go beyond what the data shows." Box 3 (gold): "(3) What is missing? — Identify required elements that are absent: uncertainty quantification, pre-committed metric, stated confidence level, or required calculation steps." Box 4 (blue): "(4) What specific improvements would most strengthen this report? — Be actionable. Not 'add more analysis' but 'state the validation metric before applying it and show the intermediate calculation.'" A red

t 'add more analysis' but 'state the validation metric before applying it and show the intermediate calculation.'" A red caption below: "Name the criteria before you read — for the same reason you commit to a metric before you compute." A note: "This structure applies to the in-class comparative analysis exercise and to every peer evaluation in this course.") Talking Point: In a moment, we are going to apply everything from today's lecture to a sample lab report. Before you open it, I want to give you the four questions that organize a defensible critique — the same way I asked you to commit to a validation metric before computing. First: what is correct and well-supported? Identify what the report does right, with specific evidence. This matters because vague praise is useless — if you

rted? Identify what the report does right, with specific evidence. This matters because vague praise is useless — if you cannot say why something is correct, you have not evaluated it. Second: what is incorrect or unsupported? Find claims that are wrong, or conclusions that extend beyond what the data actually shows. Third: what is missing? This is the category most students underuse. Missing elements — no uncertainty quantification, no pre-committed metric, no confidence level stated — are not vague weaknesses; they are specific absences that a checklist can identify. Fourth: what specific improvements would most strengthen the report? Actionable, specific, tied to the assignment requirements. "Add more analysis" is not a critique — "state the validation metric before applying it and

the assignment requirements. "Add more analysis" is not a critique — "state the validation metric before applying it and show the propagation calculation" is. You are committing to these four criteria now, before you read — for the same reason we commit to a metric before we compute. (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's lab report excerpt: "After computing the data, I tried both the relative difference metric and the combined-uncertainty metric. The relative difference showed agreement (4.2% < 5.0% threshold). I used

nce metric and the combined-uncertainty metric. The relative difference showed agreement (4.2% < 5.0% threshold). I used that metric for my conclusion." ic and acceptance threshold must be stated before the comparison is computed. If the student genuinely believes relative difference is the right metric for this data type, they should be able to explain why on first principles, independent of which metric produces the more favorable answer. The fact that they tried both and chose the favorable one is the problem — not the metric itself. Real credibility requires that the comparison could have shown disagreement, and the student was committed to reporting it regardless. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure Structured comparison is not complicated, but it requires discipline at a moment when most people are tempted to eyeball the result and call it good. pages.title: Calibration vs. Validation — A Distinction That Matters
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Lecture Calibration vs. Validation — A Distinction That Matters Introduces the calibration-vs-validation distinction at the conceptual level: calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference (it is a measurement activity), while validation tests whether a model makes accurate predictions (it is a modeling activity). Conflating the two is a common and consequential error. This distinction is introduced in Week 3, deepened in E2 Week 8, and operationalized in the E3 V&V plan. Students encounter a case study where a "calibrated" result was incorrectly treated as a "validated" model. This lecture covers the conceptual distinction and the error pattern only (slides 2–5 in the lecture outline). Anomalous data prediction content continues in Lecture 3. See `lectures/E1_week_3/E1_W3_L2_presentation_outline.txt`. *(Note: shortened by V&V Executor 2026-05-19 per instructor approval — anomalous data content moved to W3 L3.)* Apply a named validation metric to compare a measured outcome to a model prediction within defined uncertainty bounds — CLO 2, CLO 3 Explain the distinction between calibration and validation and articulate why conflating them undermines experimental credibility — CLO 3 Evaluate agreement between two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors or methods — CLO 1, CLO 2 Identify at least one model-form limitation from residual inspection and articulate its implication — CLO 3 E1_W3_L2 — Calibration vs. Validation: A Distinction That Matters ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 Revised by V&V Executor — 2026-05-19 (split: anomalous data content moved to new E1_W3_L3; this lecture now covers calibration vs. validation only, slides 1-5 + closure) ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Calibration vs. Validation — A Distinction That Matters Talking Point: I want to discuss two words that get conflated constantly in engineering practice — calibration and validation. Both sound like they are about making sure something is right. Both involve comparing a result to some reference. But they are asking completely different questions about completely different things, and conflating them can produce conclusions that are not just imprecise but genuinely wrong. By the end of this lecture I want these two words to mean something precise and distinct to you. The second part of this conceptual thread — predicting what anomalous data looks like before you run the experiment — continues in Lecture 3. ================================================================================ slide 2: Two Words — Two Different Questions (Image: A split-panel diagram with a bold vertical divider. Left panel header: "Calibration" — wrench icon. Large centered text: "Is the sensor reporting the right value?" Sub-items: "Activity: adjust sensor output to match a known reference." "Domain: the measurement instrument." "Output: a corrected sensor reading." Right panel header: "Validation" — target-and-arrow icon. Large centered text: "Is the model making accurate predictions?" Sub-items: "Activity: compare model predictions to independent experimental outcomes." "Domain: the mathematical model." "Output: a defensible claim about model fidelity." Below the divider: bold text "Calibration is a measurement activity. Validation is a modeling activity. Neither is a substitute for the other.") Talking Point: Here is the cleanest statement of the distinction. Calibration asks whether the sensor is reporting the right value — it is a question about the instrument. Validation asks whether the model is making accurate predictions — it is a question about the model. Calibration involves comparing sensor output to a known reference standard and correcting for any systematic offset. Validation involves comparing model predictions to experimental measurements that were taken independently — not used to fit the model. The two activities are structurally similar: both involve comparing two quantities. But the things being evaluated are completely different. Calibrating your sensor improves your measurement. It tells you nothing about whether your model of the physical phenomenon is correct. Calibration is a prerequisite for credible validation — you need to trust your measurement before you use it to test a model — but it is not a substitute for validation. ================================================================================ slide 3: Calibration — What It Is (Image: A laboratory setup diagram. A known standard mass is hanging from a load cell on a rigid test stand. The mass is labeled "Reference Force: 9.81 N (traceable to NIST)." A digital readout shows the sensor voltage. To the right, a calibration curve plot: X-axis "Reference Force (N)," Y-axis "Sensor Voltage (V)." Data points from multiple known loads are shown, with a best-fit line and slight scatter. The correction equation is displayed: "Force (N) = 8.42 × Voltage + 0.23." Residuals are shown in a small inset below the calibration curve. Caption: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known standard. The calibration curve is itself a model with its own uncertainty.") Talking Point: Calibration is the process of characterizing a sensor's response to known inputs and generating a correction equation. To calibrate a load cell, you apply known reference forces traceable to a national standard — NIST-traceable weights, for example — record the sensor's voltage output at each force level, and fit a calibration curve to map voltage to force. After calibration, you apply that equation to every future measurement. The calibration residuals — the differences between your fitted curve and the actual data points — characterize the sensor's nonlinearity and the quality of your calibration fit. An important nuance: the calibration curve is itself a model. It is an approximation of the sensor's true response. It has uncertainty — both from scatter in the reference measurements and from the adequacy of the functional form you chose. That calibration uncertainty propagates into every subsequent measurement you make, and it is part of your total uncertainty budget. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation — What It Is (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the two-branch structure shown clearly. Left branch labeled "Mathematical Modeling Branch — drag coefficient prediction from aerodynamic model: Cd = f(α, Re)." Right branch labeled "Physical Modeling Branch — drag coefficient measured independently in wind tunnel at multiple angles of attack." At the Quantitative Comparison node, both branches meet: a graph shows the model prediction curve (dashed line) and experimental data points with uncertainty bars, overlaid. The comparison is annotated: "Validation: does the model predict Cd correctly within uncertainty bounds, across the tested range of α?" A gold annotation arrow points to "independent" with emphasis — "measurements not used to tune model parameters." A red X through a separate small diagram showing "model parameters tuned to match these same data — NOT validation.") Talking Point: Validation asks whether your mathematical model correctly predicts the behavior of the physical system across the domain of interest. The key structural requirement is independence: the experimental measurements used for validation must not have been used to fit the model's parameters. If you tuned your model to match your data, and then compare the model to that same data and report agreement, you have not validated — you have fit. Fitting a model to data and then checking how well the model fits that same data is circular, not validating. Validation requires a hold-out: data that the model has never seen, from conditions the model was not tuned to reproduce. Only then does agreement between prediction and measurement constitute evidence of model predictive validity. The analogy is direct: studying for a test using the test answers, then scoring well on that test, does not prove you understand the material — you just memorized the specific answers. Using the same data to fit and then evaluate a model is exactly the same failure. This is not a technicality — it is the logical structure that makes validation meaningful. ================================================================================ slide 5: The Error Pattern — Treating Calibration as Validation (Image: A two-step scenario diagram. Step 1: "Engineer calibrates load cell against reference conditions. Calibrated measurements now agree with model predictions at those conditions." A checkmark: "Sensor is more accurate." Step 2: "Engineer reports: 'Model is validated — calibrated measurements agree with model predictions.'" A large red X with the annotation: "This is not validation. The model was never tested against independent data. The calibration and model tuning may both be internally consistent with each other — this is not evidence that either correctly represents the physics outside the calibrated range." A final note: "The pattern appears constantly. Name it when you see it.") Talking Point: Here is the error pattern I want you to recognize. An engineer calibrates their sensor against a set of reference conditions. The calibrated measurements agree with the model predictions at those conditions. The engineer concludes: the model is validated. This is wrong. The calibration adjusted the sensor to match the reference values. The model predictions may have been used to select or tune the calibration conditions. Whether intentionally or not, the calibration and the model tuning are not independent — they may both be consistent with each other without either one correctly representing the physics at conditions that were not tested. When you see the phrase "our calibrated results validate our model" in a report, ask: were the measurements used for calibration the same measurements used to fit the model? If yes, nothing has been validated. ================================================================================ slide 6: Closure Calibration and validation are both essential, and they are not the same. Calibration improves the accuracy of your measurement by correcting for sensor bias — it is a measurement activity. Validation tests the predictive fidelity of your model by comparing independent measurements to model predictions — it is a modeling activity. Conflating them produces false confidence in models that have not actually been tested. In Lecture 3, we build on this distinction and answer a connected question: if you understand what your model assumes, what specific data signatures would tell you the model is wrong — before you see the data? That predictive discipline is what transforms an experiment from a data-collection exercise into a hypothesis test. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). chunks.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Lecture Calibration vs. Validation — A Distinction That Matters Introduces the calibration-vs-validation distinction at the conceptual level: calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference (it is a measurement activity), while validation tests whether a model makes accurate predictions (it is a modeling activity). Conflating the two is a common and consequential error. This distinction is introduced in Week 3, deepened in E2 Week 8, and operationalized in the E3 V&V plan. Students encounter a case study where a "calibrated" result was incorrectly treated as a "validated" model. This lecture covers the conceptual distinction and the error pattern only (slides 2–5 in the lecture outline). Anomalous data prediction content continues in Lecture 3.

That calibration uncertainty propagates into every subsequent measurement you make, and it is part of your total uncertainty budget. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation — What It Is (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the two-branch structure shown

======================== slide 4: Validation — What It Is (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the two-branch structure shown clearly. Left branch labeled "Mathematical Modeling Branch — drag coefficient prediction from aerodynamic model: Cd = f(α, Re)." Right branch labeled "Physical Modeling Branch — drag coefficient measured independently in wind tunnel at multiple angles of attack." At the Quantitative Comparison node, both branches meet: a graph shows the model prediction curve (dashed line) and experimental data points with uncertainty bars, overlaid. The comparison is annotated: "Validation: does the model predict Cd correctly within uncertainty bounds, across the tested range of α?" A gold annotation arrow points to "independent" with emphasis — "measurements not used to tune model

tested range of α?" A gold annotation arrow points to "independent" with emphasis — "measurements not used to tune model parameters." A red X through a separate small diagram showing "model parameters tuned to match these same data — NOT validation.") Talking Point: Validation asks whether your mathematical model correctly predicts the behavior of the physical system across the domain of interest. The key structural requirement is independence: the experimental measurements used for validation must not have been used to fit the model's parameters. If you tuned your model to match your data, and then compare the model to that same data and report agreement, you have not validated — you have fit. Fitting a model to data and then checking how well the model fits that same data is circular,

validated — you have fit. Fitting a model to data and then checking how well the model fits that same data is circular, not validating. Validation requires a hold-out: data that the model has never seen, from conditions the model was not tuned to reproduce. Only then does agreement between prediction and measurement constitute evidence of model predictive validity. The analogy is direct: studying for a test using the test answers, then scoring well on that test, does not prove you understand the material — you just memorized the specific answers. Using the same data to fit and then evaluate a model is exactly the same failure. This is not a technicality — it is the logical structure that makes validation meaningful.

is exactly the same failure. This is not a technicality — it is the logical structure that makes validation meaningful. ================================================================================ slide 5: The Error Pattern — Treating Calibration as Validation (Image: A two-step scenario diagram. Step 1: "Engineer calibrates load cell against reference conditions. Calibrated measurements now agree with model predictions at those conditions." A checkmark: "Sensor is more accurate." Step 2: "Engineer reports: 'Model is validated — calibrated measurements agree with model predictions.'" A large red X with the annotation: "This is not validation. The model was never tested against independent data. The calibration and model tuning may both be internally consistent with each other — this

sted against independent data. The calibration and model tuning may both be internally consistent with each other — this is not evidence that either correctly represents the physics outside the calibrated range." A final note: "The pattern appears constantly. Name it when you see it.") Talking Point: Here is the error pattern I want you to recognize. An engineer calibrates their sensor against a set of reference conditions. The calibrated measurements agree with the model predictions at those conditions. The engineer concludes: the model is validated. This is wrong. The calibration adjusted the sensor to match the reference values. The model predictions may have been used to select or tune the calibration conditions. Whether intentionally or not, the calibration and the model tuning are

sed to select or tune the calibration conditions. Whether intentionally or not, the calibration and the model tuning are not independent — they may both be consistent with each other without either one correctly representing the physics at conditions that were not tested. When you see the phrase "our calibrated results validate our model" in a report, ask: were the measurements used for calibration the same measurements used to fit the model? If yes, nothing has been validated. ================================================================================ slide 6: Closure Calibration and validation are both essential, and they are not the same. Calibration improves the accuracy of your measurement by correcting for sensor bias — it is a measurement activity. Validation tests the

oves the accuracy of your measurement by correcting for sensor bias — it is a measurement activity. Validation tests the predictive fidelity of your model by comparing independent measurements to model predictions — it is a modeling activity. Conflating them produces false confidence in models that have not actually been tested. In Lecture 3, we build on this distinction and answer a connected question: if you understand what your model assumes, what specific data signatures would tell you the model is wrong — before you see the data? That predictive discipline is what transforms an experiment from a data-collection exercise into a hypothesis test. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint

So Week 3 has three lectures. W3 L2 and W3 L3 can be on the shorter side of a typical lecture." - Slide count: 6 (title + 4 content + closure). tor instruction. - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 4 talking point — added hold-out principle analogy: "studying for a test using the test answers and then claiming the test proves your understanding." - Blueprint updated: E1_week_by_week.md revised to add W3 L3 as a third lecture in Week 3.

pages.title: Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Lecture Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures Extends the calibration-vs-validation distinction into predictive discipline: if you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict not just the expected data signature but the specific anomalous patterns that would indicate model-form failure. Covers the spring-mass hysteresis example (loading vs. unloading differ because energy is dissipated — linear model cannot produce this), scatter vs. systematic anomaly as a diagnostic distinction (uncertainty band as null hypothesis), and connects directly to the Tier 1 Phase 2 Prediction Memo risk assessment section. Students leave with the skill of naming, before touching hardware, what "wrong" would look like and what physical effect it would indicate. Closes with the arc diagram connecting W3 to W5 Phase 2, E2 W8, and E3 V&V plan. See `lectures/E1_week_3/E1_W3_L3_presentation_outline.txt`. *(New lecture, V&V Executor 2026-05-19, created from content of original W3 L2 slides 6–10.)* Apply a named validation metric to compare a measured outcome to a model prediction within defined uncertainty bounds — CLO 2, CLO 3 Explain the distinction between calibration and validation and articulate why conflating them undermines experimental credibility — CLO 3 Evaluate agreement between two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors or methods — CLO 1, CLO 2 Identify at least one model-form limitation from residual inspection and articulate its implication — CLO 3 E1_W3_L3 — Predicting What Wrong Looks Like: Anomalous Data Signatures ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 3 Created by V&V Executor — 2026-05-19 (split from E1_W3_L2; this lecture covers anomalous data prediction content from original W3 L2 slides 6-10, now renumbered 2-6) ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures Talking Point: In Lecture 2 we established that calibration and validation are different activities — calibration is about the sensor, validation is about the model. Now I want to extend that idea in a direction that has real professional consequences. If you understand what your model assumes, you should be able to describe not just what the expected data looks like — but what specific patterns in the data would tell you the model is fundamentally wrong. This lecture is about developing that predictive habit before you run an experiment. By the end, you should be able to look at a model, name its assumptions, and predict the specific anomalous signatures that would indicate model-form failure — not measurement noise, but a signal that the physics are telling you the model is missing something. ================================================================================ slide 2: Predicting What Right Looks Like (Image: A simple force-displacement graph for a spring-mass system. X-axis: "Displacement (mm)." Y-axis: "Force (N)." A solid straight diagonal line labeled "Expected model response: F = kx (linear)." Two uncertainty bands flank the line — one for measurement uncertainty (gray shading) and one for model parameter uncertainty (lighter outer shading). Annotations: "Slope = k (spring constant)" and "Expected scatter: within these bounds for any individual measurement." A caption: "Before touching hardware: commit to what the expected data signature looks like — the slope, the linearity, the magnitude, and the noise floor.") Talking Point: One of the habits I want you to develop before the end of this course is this: before you run an experiment, commit to what the right result looks like — specifically. Not "force increases with displacement," but "force increases linearly with displacement at a slope equal to k, with scatter bounded by the propagated uncertainty from my sensor spec." The sketch on this slide is what that commitment looks like. You have drawn the expected data signature, including the expected noise floor, before any hardware has been touched. This sketch is worth something — it is a commitment that can be evaluated after the fact. And it has a corollary that is just as important: if you can describe what right looks like, you should also be able to describe what wrong looks like. ================================================================================ slide 3: Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures (Image: Three side-by-side force-displacement plots for the same spring-mass system. Plot A (left): "Expected — scatter around a straight line. Scatter is symmetric, consistent with measurement noise." Dots scattered around the model line within the uncertainty band. Plot B (center): "Anomalous — hysteresis loop. Loading and unloading traces follow different paths, forming a closed loop." A label box reads: "Hysteresis: output depends on history — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated. This is not noise — it is a systematic pattern that the linear model cannot explain. Evidence of damping or friction not in the model." Plot C (right): "Anomalous — nonlinearity at large displacement. Slope increases at large displacement." Labeled: "Model assumes constant k. Data shows k is displacement-dependent at large deflection — geometric nonlinearity or material yielding." Caption: "You can predict which anomalous signatures are possible — if you understand your model's assumptions.") Talking Point: Here is the key insight of this lecture. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure — failure that is not measurement noise, but a sign that the model is fundamentally wrong for these conditions. For a linear spring model, the expected signature is scatter around a straight line within the propagated uncertainty. But there are specific signatures that would tell you the model is wrong. Hysteresis — different loading and unloading paths — is evidence of energy dissipation: the model does not include damping or friction, so it cannot produce a loop. Hysteresis is a specific term worth knowing: it describes a system whose output depends not just on the current input, but on the history of how it got there — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated along the way. Nonlinearity at large displacement is evidence of material or geometric effects the model treats as negligible. These anomalous signatures are predictable in advance if you understand what assumptions your model is making. Commit to them before you run the experiment. Then, if you see them in your data, you will know exactly what physical effect they represent — and you will not mistake them for measurement noise. ================================================================================ slide 4: Scatter vs. Anomaly — The Diagnostic Distinction (Image: Two plots shown side by side, both using the same force-displacement setup. Left plot: "Measurement Noise — scatter." Multiple data points scatter around the model line, some above and some below, with no systematic pattern. The scatter is bounded by the propagated uncertainty band (gray shading). Label: "This is consistent with random measurement error. No model revision required." Right plot: "Model-Form Error — hysteresis." The data forms a distinct loading and unloading loop that is clearly outside the uncertainty band and clearly systematic. Label: "This is NOT measurement noise. The uncertainty band does not explain this pattern. The model is missing an energy dissipation term." A central annotation: "The distinguishing feature is structure — systematic patterns are model-form errors. Random scatter within the uncertainty band is measurement noise.") Talking Point: The distinction between scatter and anomaly is the practical form of the calibration-versus-validation distinction. Scatter that is bounded by your propagated uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise — random, symmetric, not systematic. It tells you your measurement process is working as expected. A pattern that is systematic — like hysteresis, or a trending residual, or data that falls consistently above or below the model across a range of conditions — is not measurement noise. It cannot be explained by your sensor spec. It is evidence that the model is missing a physical effect. The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis: data within it is consistent with your model being adequate. Data outside it, in a systematic way, is evidence that the model form is wrong. Commit to this diagnostic before the experiment, and you will recognize a model-form failure immediately when you see it in your data. ================================================================================ slide 5: Connecting to Phase 2 — Your Job Before Data Is Released (Image: A labeled timeline for the Tier 1 assignment. Three phases shown horizontally: "Phase 1: Forward Analysis" (Weeks 1–4, grayed out), "Phase 2: Reverse Prediction" (Week 5, highlighted in gold), "Phase 3: Synthesis" (Week 5, lighter shade). Under Phase 2, two sub-items: "(1) Before data release: Prediction Memo — commit to expected data signature, uncertainty estimate, and at least one anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure." "(2) After data release: compare prediction to measured outcome, classify discrepancy type (noise vs. model-form)." A callout box: "The risk assessment section of the Prediction Memo asks you to name what wrong looks like — not just what right looks like. That is what today's lecture is preparing you for.") Talking Point: I want to connect this lecture explicitly to what is coming in Week 5. In Tier 1 Phase 2, you will be given the experiment packet for an experiment you did not run — theory primer, sensor specs, physical setup description — and before any data is released, you will submit a Prediction Memo. One of the required sections of that memo is a risk assessment: you must identify at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation, as distinct from measurement noise. That is exactly the skill this lecture is building. For the spring-mass experiment, the risk assessment might say: if the force-displacement data shows hysteresis, the model is missing an energy dissipation term; if the data shows nonlinearity at large displacement, the linear stiffness assumption breaks down. You are committing, before seeing any data, to what wrong looks like — and why. That commitment is what makes your subsequent interpretation defensible. ================================================================================ slide 6: The Arc of This Concept — Where It Appears Again (Image: A horizontal course arc diagram with five labeled milestones. Milestone 1 (Week 3): "Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced. Anomalous data signatures introduced." Milestone 2 (Week 4): "Calibration uncertainty propagated into integration report." Milestone 3 (Week 5): "Phase 2 Prediction Memo — risk assessment section names anomalous signatures." Milestone 4 (E2 Week 8): "Distinction deepened in complex aerospace systems — residual analysis and discrepancy as diagnostic." Milestone 5 (E3 Week 11): "Calibration and validation as separate sections of a formal V&V plan." A curved arc spanning all five milestones labeled "Increasing depth and stakes." The current milestone is highlighted in gold.) Talking Point: I want to give you the arc. Today is the seed — you have the distinction conceptually. In Week 4, calibration becomes procedurally concrete when you run a calibration exercise in the lab and propagate calibration uncertainty into your integration report. In Week 5, the Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires you to use the anomalous-signature framework in your risk assessment. In E2 Week 8, we return to this distinction in the context of more complex aerospace systems, where residual analysis formalizes the diagnostic approach we introduced today. And in E3, when you write a V&V plan, calibration activities and validation activities appear as explicitly separate sections with separate purposes and separate evidence requirements. The concept does not disappear after this week — it gets heavier and more consequential at every stage. Build the habit now. ================================================================================ slide 7: Closure The extension of the calibration-versus-validation distinction — committing before an experiment to what both the expected and the anomalous results look like — is the discipline that transforms experiment design from observation to hypothesis testing. When you know what wrong looks like before you start, you will recognize it when you see it, and you will be able to name what it means physically. The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis. Data outside it, in a systematic way, is your evidence of model-form failure. Pre-committing to the specific signature — hysteresis, nonlinearity, a consistent offset, an amplitude-dependent period — is what makes the Phase 2 Prediction Memo's risk assessment section more than a formality. That is the skill you need for every validation activity in the rest of this course. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Created by V&V Executor (2026-05-19) as part of the split of original E1_W3_L2. Content source: original E1_W3_L2 slides 6-10 (Predicting What Right Looks Like; Predicting What Wrong Looks Like; Scatter vs. Anomaly; Connecting to Phase 2; The Arc). Now renumbered as slides 2-6. So Week 3 has three lectures. W3 L2 and W3 L3 can be on the shorter side of a typical lecture." Slide 3 (Anomalous Data Signatures) updated from original W3 L2 slide 7: added explicit definition of "hysteresis" in the talking point and image description ("output depends on history — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated"), per not-consolidated approved item: E1_W3_L2 slide 7 — define hysteresis before spring-mass example. Slide count: 7 (title + 5 content + closure). Intentionally shorter per instructor instruction. Blueprint updated: E1_week_by_week.md revised to include this lecture as W3 L3. The original W3 L2 Agent Note about the course arc diagram (slide 10) is preserved here as slide 6 — the arc diagram remains with the anomalous data content rather than being moved to W4 L1. Two uncertainty bands flank the line — one for measurement uncertainty (gray shading) and one for model parameter uncertainty (lighter outer shading). Annotations: "Slope = k (spring constant)" and "Expected scatter: within these bounds for any individual measurement." A caption: "Before touching hardware: commit to what the expected data signature

bounds for any individual measurement." A caption: "Before touching hardware: commit to what the expected data signature looks like — the slope, the linearity, the magnitude, and the noise floor.") Talking Point: One of the habits I want you to develop before the end of this course is this: before you run an experiment, commit to what the right result looks like — specifically. Not "force increases with displacement," but "force increases linearly with displacement at a slope equal to k, with scatter bounded by the propagated uncertainty from my sensor spec." The sketch on this slide is what that commitment looks like. You have drawn the expected data signature, including the expected noise floor, before any hardware has been touched. This sketch is worth something — it is a commitment

ding the expected noise floor, before any hardware has been touched. This sketch is worth something — it is a commitment that can be evaluated after the fact. And it has a corollary that is just as important: if you can describe what right looks like, you should also be able to describe what wrong looks like. ================================================================================ slide 3: Predicting What Wrong Looks Like — Anomalous Data Signatures (Image: Three side-by-side force-displacement plots for the same spring-mass system. Plot A (left): "Expected — scatter around a straight line. Scatter is symmetric, consistent with measurement noise." Dots scattered around the model line within the uncertainty band. Plot B (center): "Anomalous — hysteresis loop. Loading and unloading

around the model line within the uncertainty band. Plot B (center): "Anomalous — hysteresis loop. Loading and unloading traces follow different paths, forming a closed loop." A label box reads: "Hysteresis: output depends on history — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated. This is not noise — it is a systematic pattern that the linear model cannot explain. Evidence of damping or friction not in the model." Plot C (right): "Anomalous — nonlinearity at large displacement. Slope increases at large displacement." Labeled: "Model assumes constant k. Data shows k is displacement-dependent at large deflection — geometric nonlinearity or material yielding." Caption: "You can predict which anomalous signatures are possible — if you understand your model's

r material yielding." Caption: "You can predict which anomalous signatures are possible — if you understand your model's assumptions.") Talking Point: Here is the key insight of this lecture. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure — failure that is not measurement noise, but a sign that the model is fundamentally wrong for these conditions. For a linear spring model, the expected signature is scatter around a straight line within the propagated uncertainty. But there are specific signatures that would tell you the model is wrong. Hysteresis — different loading and unloading paths — is evidence of energy dissipation: the model does not include damping or

resis — different loading and unloading paths — is evidence of energy dissipation: the model does not include damping or friction, so it cannot produce a loop. Hysteresis is a specific term worth knowing: it describes a system whose output depends not just on the current input, but on the history of how it got there — loading and unloading follow different paths because energy is dissipated along the way. Nonlinearity at large displacement is evidence of material or geometric effects the model treats as negligible. These anomalous signatures are predictable in advance if you understand what assumptions your model is making. Commit to them before you run the experiment. Then, if you see them in your data, you will know exactly what physical effect they represent — and you will not mistake

Then, if you see them in your data, you will know exactly what physical effect they represent — and you will not mistake them for measurement noise. ================================================================================ slide 4: Scatter vs. Anomaly — The Diagnostic Distinction (Image: Two plots shown side by side, both using the same force-displacement setup. Left plot: "Measurement Noise — scatter." Multiple data points scatter around the model line, some above and some below, with no systematic pattern. The scatter is bounded by the propagated uncertainty band (gray shading). Label: "This is consistent with random measurement error. No model revision required." Right plot: "Model-Form Error — hysteresis." The data forms a distinct loading and unloading loop that is clearly

ired." Right plot: "Model-Form Error — hysteresis." The data forms a distinct loading and unloading loop that is clearly outside the uncertainty band and clearly systematic. Label: "This is NOT measurement noise. The uncertainty band does not explain this pattern. The model is missing an energy dissipation term." A central annotation: "The distinguishing feature is structure — systematic patterns are model-form errors. Random scatter within the uncertainty band is measurement noise.") Talking Point: The distinction between scatter and anomaly is the practical form of the calibration-versus-validation distinction. Scatter that is bounded by your propagated uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise — random, symmetric, not systematic. It tells you your measurement process is

band is consistent with measurement noise — random, symmetric, not systematic. It tells you your measurement process is working as expected. A pattern that is systematic — like hysteresis, or a trending residual, or data that falls consistently above or below the model across a range of conditions — is not measurement noise. It cannot be explained by your sensor spec. It is evidence that the model is missing a physical effect. The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis: data within it is consistent with your model being adequate. Data outside it, in a systematic way, is evidence that the model form is wrong. Commit to this diagnostic before the experiment, and you will recognize a model-form failure immediately when you see it in your data.

diagnostic before the experiment, and you will recognize a model-form failure immediately when you see it in your data. ================================================================================ slide 5: Connecting to Phase 2 — Your Job Before Data Is Released (Image: A labeled timeline for the Tier 1 assignment. Three phases shown horizontally: "Phase 1: Forward Analysis" (Weeks 1–4, grayed out), "Phase 2: Reverse Prediction" (Week 5, highlighted in gold), "Phase 3: Synthesis" (Week 5, lighter shade). Under Phase 2, two sub-items: "(1) Before data release: Prediction Memo — commit to expected data signature, uncertainty estimate, and at least one anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure." "(2) After data release: compare prediction to measured outcome, classify

ature that would indicate model-form failure." "(2) After data release: compare prediction to measured outcome, classify discrepancy type (noise vs. model-form)." A callout box: "The risk assessment section of the Prediction Memo asks you to name what wrong looks like — not just what right looks like. That is what today's lecture is preparing you for.") Talking Point: I want to connect this lecture explicitly to what is coming in Week 5. In Tier 1 Phase 2, you will be given the experiment packet for an experiment you did not run — theory primer, sensor specs, physical setup description — and before any data is released, you will submit a Prediction Memo. One of the required sections of that memo is a risk assessment: you must identify at least one anomalous data pattern that would

he required sections of that memo is a risk assessment: you must identify at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation, as distinct from measurement noise. That is exactly the skill this lecture is building. For the spring-mass experiment, the risk assessment might say: if the force-displacement data shows hysteresis, the model is missing an energy dissipation term; if the data shows nonlinearity at large displacement, the linear stiffness assumption breaks down. You are committing, before seeing any data, to what wrong looks like — and why. That commitment is what makes your subsequent interpretation defensible. ================================================================================ slide 6: The Arc of This Concept — Where It Appears Again

============================================================== slide 6: The Arc of This Concept — Where It Appears Again (Image: A horizontal course arc diagram with five labeled milestones. Milestone 1 (Week 3): "Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced. Anomalous data signatures introduced." Milestone 2 (Week 4): "Calibration uncertainty propagated into integration report." Milestone 3 (Week 5): "Phase 2 Prediction Memo — risk assessment section names anomalous signatures." Milestone 4 (E2 Week 8): "Distinction deepened in complex aerospace systems — residual analysis and discrepancy as diagnostic." Milestone 5 (E3 Week 11): "Calibration and validation as separate sections of a formal V&V plan." A curved arc spanning all five milestones labeled "Increasing depth and stakes."

eparate sections of a formal V&V plan." A curved arc spanning all five milestones labeled "Increasing depth and stakes." The current milestone is highlighted in gold.) Talking Point: I want to give you the arc. Today is the seed — you have the distinction conceptually. In Week 4, calibration becomes procedurally concrete when you run a calibration exercise in the lab and propagate calibration uncertainty into your integration report. In Week 5, the Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires you to use the anomalous-signature framework in your risk assessment. In E2 Week 8, we return to this distinction in the context of more complex aerospace systems, where residual analysis formalizes the diagnostic approach we introduced today. And in E3, when you write a V&V plan, calibration activities and

formalizes the diagnostic approach we introduced today. And in E3, when you write a V&V plan, calibration activities and validation activities appear as explicitly separate sections with separate purposes and separate evidence requirements. The concept does not disappear after this week — it gets heavier and more consequential at every stage. Build the habit now. ================================================================================ slide 7: Closure The extension of the calibration-versus-validation distinction — committing before an experiment to what both the expected and the anomalous results look like — is the discipline that transforms experiment design from observation to hypothesis testing. When you know what wrong looks like before you start, you will recognize it when

from observation to hypothesis testing. When you know what wrong looks like before you start, you will recognize it when you see it, and you will be able to name what it means physically. The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis. Data outside it, in a systematic way, is your evidence of model-form failure. Pre-committing to the specific signature — hysteresis, nonlinearity, a consistent offset, an amplitude-dependent period — is what makes the Phase 2 Prediction Memo's risk assessment section more than a formality. That is the skill you need for every validation activity in the rest of this course. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Created by V&V Executor (2026-05-19) as part of the split of original E1_W3_L2. pages.title: How to Present a Graph
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Reading How to Present a Graph A short handout capturing the sequential graph presentation framework introduced in E1 W2 Lecture 2. The checklist: (1) motivation — why does this graph exist; (2) axes — what is plotted, units, scale; (3) data source — where the data came from; (4) uncertainties — error bars or bounds shown and labeled; (5) trend — what pattern the data shows; (6) model comparison — how the trend compares to the predictive model; (7) discrepancy — possible causes for any gap; (8) system-level implication — what the graph tells you about the overall system. Students should apply this framework to every MATLAB figure they produce in the course. This reading reinforces the framework introduced in W2 and gives students a durable reference they can use in all subsequent deliverables. Apply a named validation metric to compare a measured outcome to a model prediction within defined uncertainty bounds — CLO 2, CLO 3 Explain the distinction between calibration and validation and articulate why conflating them undermines experimental credibility — CLO 3 Evaluate agreement between two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors or methods — CLO 1, CLO 2 Identify at least one model-form limitation from residual inspection and articulate its implication — CLO 3 How to Present a Graph Eight Questions Every Engineering Figure Must Answer A graph that requires a verbal explanation has already failed. A figure in an engineering report must stand alone, answering every question a critical reader could raise without requiring the author's interpretation. Engineering figures are the primary medium for arguing from data. A well-constructed figure makes a claim, shows the evidence for that claim, and gives the reader the tools to evaluate both, without assistance from the surrounding text. A poorly constructed figure forces the reader to work to extract meaning, often arriving at the wrong conclusion or no conclusion at all. The gap between these two outcomes is not artistic talent; it is a checklist. This mindset matters beyond ASEN 3501. In capstone and in professional engineering work, a figure often has to defend a design decision before the author is in the room to explain it. The following eight-question framework provides a sequential checklist for evaluating any engineering figure you produce, or any figure you encounter in someone else's report. Apply these questions in order: each one checks a necessary element of a complete engineering argument from data. A figure that passes all eight can stand alone. A figure that fails any one of them is incomplete. *This image gives you a fully worked example of what a figure looks like when it carries evidence, interpretation, and model comparison all at once. 1. Motivation — Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer? The figure caption or title should state this explicitly in one sentence. A graph without a stated purpose forces the reader to guess, and readers guess wrong. Example: "Figure 3. Measured vs. predicted oscillation amplitude — evaluating whether the undamped model agrees with measured decay within propagated uncertainty bounds." If you cannot write this sentence, the graph does not belong in the report yet. 2. Axes — What is plotted on each axis, with units, at an appropriate scale? Axis labels without units are incomplete. Scale choices that compress variation make disagreement invisible; scale choices that amplify noise make agreement look worse than it is. Both axes should span the physically meaningful range of the quantity, not the range of convenience. 3. Data source — Where did the data come from, and how was it collected? The reader should be able to identify the sensor, the acquisition method, and the conditions under which the data was taken without reading the body of the report. This information belongs in the caption or the legend. If your figure uses data from two different sensors or collection sessions, both must be identified. 4. Uncertainties — Are error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and explicitly labeled? A data point without uncertainty bounds isn't an engineering claim; it is a raw observation. Uncertainty bounds must appear on the figure, be derived by the method described in the report, and be labeled with their basis (for example: "95% CI, partial derivative propagation from manufacturer spec"). Unless otherwise specified, error bars in this course represent ±1 standard uncertainty (k=1), not a 95% confidence interval; if you use a different coverage factor, state it explicitly. A figure with no error bars says only "here is a number." A figure with labeled uncertainty bounds says "here is a defensible claim." 5. Trend — What pattern does the data show? Is the relationship linear, exponential, oscillatory, saturating? The trend is the signal your measurement is characterizing. State it explicitly, either in the caption or as an annotated line on the figure. A reader should be able to identify the pattern from the figure alone, without searching the prose. 6. Model comparison — How does the measured trend compare to the predictive model? The model should appear on the same figure as the data, as a line, band, or point estimate, with its own label. Placing model and data on the same axes makes the comparison visible and makes the argument spatial rather than verbal. A figure that shows only data, with the model discussed in the text, requires the reader to perform the comparison in their head. Do not outsource the argument. 7. Discrepancy — If there is a meaningful gap between measured data and model prediction, what are the possible causes? A gap that falls within the uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise; note that briefly when it matters to the claim. A gap with systematic structure (increasing with the independent variable, or consistently signed) suggests a model-form limitation; name the physical effect that is likely missing. If the gap is localized to a specific part of the figure, mark that region clearly, then explain it in the caption or surrounding text. Long explanations inside the plot usually make the figure harder to read. 8. System-level implication — What does this graph tell you about the overall system? The figure is evidence for a conclusion about the system under study. State that conclusion. A figure that ends at "the data shows X" without connecting X to a claim about the system has done half the work. The closing sentence of the caption should complete the argument: "Result implies that the measured damping ratio (ζ = 0.12 ± 0.01) is 20% higher than the undamped model predicts, suggesting an aerodynamic drag contribution not captured by the model." *This image converts the example above into a repeatable review tool you can apply to your own figures before anyone else has to. Building a figure from scratch: Before opening MATLAB, start by answering question 1: what question does this figure answer? If you cannot state the question, you do not have a figure yet; you have a data-plotting exercise. Once the question is stated, the remaining seven elements follow naturally: the question determines what belongs on the axes, what comparison is needed, and what the closing sentence of the caption must say. This sequence (question first, figure second) is the difference between a figure that makes an argument and one that merely displays data. The Takeaway: Apply this eight-question checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course. It is the minimum standard for an engineering argument from data. The quality of your figures against this checklist will be evaluated in every subsequent deliverable, beginning with the comparative analysis memo due this week.
