Students should apply this framework to every MATLAB figure they produce in the course. This reading reinforces the framework introduced in W2 and gives students a durable reference they can use

n the course. This reading reinforces the framework introduced in W2 and gives students a durable reference they can use in all subsequent deliverables. Apply a named validation metric to compare a measured outcome to a model prediction within defined uncertainty bounds — CLO 2, CLO 3 Explain the distinction between calibration and validation and articulate why conflating them undermines experimental credibility — CLO 3 Evaluate agreement between two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors or methods — CLO 1, CLO 2 Identify at least one model-form limitation from residual inspection and articulate its implication — CLO 3 How to Present a Graph Eight Questions Every Engineering Figure Must Answer A graph that requires a verbal explanation has already failed. A figure in

uestions Every Engineering Figure Must Answer A graph that requires a verbal explanation has already failed. A figure in an engineering report must stand alone, answering every question a critical reader could raise without requiring the author's interpretation. Engineering figures are the primary medium for arguing from data. A well-constructed figure makes a claim, shows the evidence for that claim, and gives the reader the tools to evaluate both, without assistance from the surrounding text. A poorly constructed figure forces the reader to work to extract meaning, often arriving at the wrong conclusion or no conclusion at all. The gap between these two outcomes is not artistic talent; it is a checklist. This mindset matters beyond ASEN 3501. In capstone and in professional engineering

t artistic talent; it is a checklist. This mindset matters beyond ASEN 3501. In capstone and in professional engineering work, a figure often has to defend a design decision before the author is in the room to explain it. The following eight-question framework provides a sequential checklist for evaluating any engineering figure you produce, or any figure you encounter in someone else's report. Apply these questions in order: each one checks a necessary element of a complete engineering argument from data. A figure that passes all eight can stand alone. A figure that fails any one of them is incomplete. *This image gives you a fully worked example of what a figure looks like when it carries evidence, interpretation, and model comparison all at once. 1. Motivation — Why does this graph

oks like when it carries evidence, interpretation, and model comparison all at once. 1. Motivation — Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer? The figure caption or title should state this explicitly in one sentence. A graph without a stated purpose forces the reader to guess, and readers guess wrong. Example: "Figure 3. Measured vs. predicted oscillation amplitude — evaluating whether the undamped model agrees with measured decay within propagated uncertainty bounds." If you cannot write this sentence, the graph does not belong in the report yet. 2. Axes — What is plotted on each axis, with units, at an appropriate scale? Axis labels without units are incomplete. Scale choices that compress variation make disagreement invisible; scale choices that amplify noise make

are incomplete. Scale choices that compress variation make disagreement invisible; scale choices that amplify noise make agreement look worse than it is. Both axes should span the physically meaningful range of the quantity, not the range of convenience. 3. Data source — Where did the data come from, and how was it collected? The reader should be able to identify the sensor, the acquisition method, and the conditions under which the data was taken without reading the body of the report. This information belongs in the caption or the legend. If your figure uses data from two different sensors or collection sessions, both must be identified. 4. Uncertainties — Are error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and explicitly labeled? A data point without uncertainty bounds isn't an engineering

or bars or uncertainty bounds shown and explicitly labeled? A data point without uncertainty bounds isn't an engineering claim; it is a raw observation. Uncertainty bounds must appear on the figure, be derived by the method described in the report, and be labeled with their basis (for example: "95% CI, partial derivative propagation from manufacturer spec"). Unless otherwise specified, error bars in this course represent ±1 standard uncertainty (k=1), not a 95% confidence interval; if you use a different coverage factor, state it explicitly. A figure with no error bars says only "here is a number." A figure with labeled uncertainty bounds says "here is a defensible claim." 5. Trend — What pattern does the data show? Is the relationship linear, exponential, oscillatory, saturating? The

laim." 5. Trend — What pattern does the data show? Is the relationship linear, exponential, oscillatory, saturating? The trend is the signal your measurement is characterizing. State it explicitly, either in the caption or as an annotated line on the figure. A reader should be able to identify the pattern from the figure alone, without searching the prose. 6. Model comparison — How does the measured trend compare to the predictive model? The model should appear on the same figure as the data, as a line, band, or point estimate, with its own label. Placing model and data on the same axes makes the comparison visible and makes the argument spatial rather than verbal. A figure that shows only data, with the model discussed in the text, requires the reader to perform the comparison in their

igure that shows only data, with the model discussed in the text, requires the reader to perform the comparison in their head. Do not outsource the argument. 7. Discrepancy — If there is a meaningful gap between measured data and model prediction, what are the possible causes? A gap that falls within the uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise; note that briefly when it matters to the claim. A gap with systematic structure (increasing with the independent variable, or consistently signed) suggests a model-form limitation; name the physical effect that is likely missing. If the gap is localized to a specific part of the figure, mark that region clearly, then explain it in the caption or surrounding text. Long explanations inside the plot usually make the figure harder to

then explain it in the caption or surrounding text. Long explanations inside the plot usually make the figure harder to read. 8. System-level implication — What does this graph tell you about the overall system? The figure is evidence for a conclusion about the system under study. State that conclusion. A figure that ends at "the data shows X" without connecting X to a claim about the system has done half the work. The closing sentence of the caption should complete the argument: "Result implies that the measured damping ratio (ζ = 0.12 ± 0.01) is 20% higher than the undamped model predicts, suggesting an aerodynamic drag contribution not captured by the model." *This image converts the example above into a repeatable review tool you can apply to your own figures before anyone else has

image converts the example above into a repeatable review tool you can apply to your own figures before anyone else has to. Building a figure from scratch: Before opening MATLAB, start by answering question 1: what question does this figure answer? If you cannot state the question, you do not have a figure yet; you have a data-plotting exercise. Once the question is stated, the remaining seven elements follow naturally: the question determines what belongs on the axes, what comparison is needed, and what the closing sentence of the caption must say. This sequence (question first, figure second) is the difference between a figure that makes an argument and one that merely displays data. The Takeaway: Apply this eight-question checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course. It

y displays data. The Takeaway: Apply this eight-question checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course. It is the minimum standard for an engineering argument from data. The quality of your figures against this checklist will be evaluated in every subsequent deliverable, beginning with the comparative analysis memo due this week.

pages.title: Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Reading Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work Introduces and elaborates the four-part critique structure that students use in the W3 in-class engineering critique exercise and in all subsequent peer evaluations (E2 W9, E3 W14–15): (1) what is correct and well-supported; (2) what is incorrect or unsupported; (3) what is missing that should be present; (4) what specific improvements would most strengthen the report. The reading frames engineering critique as a technical skill in its own right — not a softer version of writing — and explains why specificity in all four parts is required. Connects the pre-commitment principle from W3 L1 (name the criteria before you read, for the same reason you commit to a metric before you compute) to the critique context. Seeds the critique skill that recurs as a graded mechanism throughout E2 and E3. See `readings/E1_week_3/E1_W3_R2_reading.md`. *(New reading, V&V Executor 2026-05-19 per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS — item 8.)* ## 3. Bridge to Next Week Students have now walked the individual steps of the Phase I pipeline and practiced evaluating the quality of another's work. Week 4 completes Tier 1 Phase 1 data collection and asks students to execute the full pipeline — from hypothesis to AMVF annotation — in the Phase 1 report. **AMVF reminder (W3):** The AMVF is the organizing spine of the holistic curriculum — experimental methods is the physical modeling branch that connects mathematical models to the real world. Students should be reminded at this point that each experiment they design and execute is not an isolated activity: the question is always "what does this experiment tell you about the system, and why does it matter to the project?" This connection to senior projects and the broader curriculum should be made explicit here and reinforced in E2 W6 and E3 W11. Apply a named validation metric to compare a measured outcome to a model prediction within defined uncertainty bounds — CLO 2, CLO 3 Explain the distinction between calibration and validation and articulate why conflating them undermines experimental credibility — CLO 3 Evaluate agreement between two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors or methods — CLO 1, CLO 2 Identify at least one model-form limitation from residual inspection and articulate its implication — CLO 3 Engineering Critique — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work Why the ability to evaluate a report matters as much as the ability to write one The most important thing a reviewer can tell you is not what you got wrong. It's what you assumed was obvious but left out. Engineers spend years learning to produce: to design experiments, collect data, propagate uncertainty, and write the result. Far less time is spent learning the complementary skill: reading another engineer's work and determining, precisely and charitably, whether the argument holds. Yet engineering critique is not a peripheral academic exercise. It is the mechanism by which engineering knowledge is verified before it is trusted. Every technical report that crosses a program manager's desk, every analysis that feeds a design decision, passes through someone's judgment of: does this argument hold, and what is missing? *This image makes the role shift visible so you can treat peer review as a different engineering task from drafting, not just a second pass at your own writing. Engineering critique is the practice of evaluating another person's engineering argument, not for aesthetic quality or writing style, but for structural validity: are the claims correct, are they supported by the evidence shown, does the work meet the requirements of the assignment, and what structured feedback would help the author improve it? The four-part framework below is the structure that makes this evaluation rigorous rather than impressionistic. You are committing to it before you read, for exactly the same reason you commit to a validation metric before you compute: naming your evaluation criteria in advance prevents you from finding what you hoped to find rather than what is actually there. Part 1 — What is correct and well-supported. Every critique must begin here. Identifying what is done well is not a formality or a softener; it's a calibration exercise. If you cannot point to specific evidence that supports a claim, you have not evaluated it; you have reacted to it. A claim is well-supported when the calculation is shown, the intermediate steps are traceable, and the conclusion follows from the numbers. "The uncertainty analysis is complete" is not a valid observation unless you can cite the specific propagation steps and confirm they are present. Part 2 — What is incorrect or unsupported. A claim is incorrect when the calculation is wrong or the conclusion contradicts the data shown. A claim is unsupported when the conclusion is present but the calculation that would support it is absent. These are different failure modes and require different responses. An incorrect claim needs correction; an unsupported claim needs the missing work to be shown. A common unsupported claim in validation work: "The measurement agrees with the model," stated without showing the numerical comparison at all. Agreement is a conclusion that requires an argument, not just an assertion. Part 3 — What is missing. This is the category most evaluators underuse, and it is where the deepest value in a critique lies. Missing elements are structural absences: the required calculation is not there, the confidence level is not stated, the validation metric is not pre-committed, the residual analysis is not shown. Unlike an incorrect claim, a missing element cannot be corrected by revising what is written; it requires adding work that was never done. Identifying missing elements requires knowing the assignment requirements well enough to notice their absence. This is why the course requires pre-committed criteria in every deliverable: both for the student writing and for the student evaluating. Part 4 — What specific improvements would most strengthen the report. Critique that ends at diagnosis is incomplete. The fourth part of a defensible evaluation asks: given the errors and absences identified, what specific actions would most improve this work? Not "add more analysis," but "state the validation metric and acceptance threshold before presenting the numerical comparison, and show the intermediate calculation steps." The test of a specific improvement is whether the author could act on it without asking a clarifying question. Vague feedback is not useful feedback; it is a signal that the reviewer stopped before finishing the evaluation. *This image shows the level of structure a useful critique needs so feedback stays tied to evidence and requirements rather than drifting into vague impressions. Peer evaluation is engineering critique of a live presentation. The Week 3 exercise asks you to evaluate a written report; the Phase 3 lightning talk asks you to evaluate a peer's spoken presentation. The same four-part structure applies, with one adaptation: the evidence you cite must be specific to what was said, shown, or omitted in the talk, not a general impression. "The uncertainty analysis was unclear" is not an observation; "the presenter stated a final result without showing how the sensor spec uncertainty was propagated into the derived quantity, which the rubric requires" is one. Vague praise ("the talk was well-organized") does not meet the standard; specific praise does ("the risk assessment named two physically motivated anomalous patterns before describing the outcome, which is the required structure"). The grading standard for a peer evaluation is the same as for any other deliverable: a claim without specific evidence is not a defensible claim. The Takeaway: Engineering critique is a skill, and like all skills it must be practiced against defined criteria. Name the four questions before you read or listen, apply them specifically, and your structured feedback becomes the kind of evidence-based argument the course requires of every deliverable. The engineering critique exercise in Week 3 is your first graded practice, and the same four-part structure will appear in every peer evaluation and segment debrief through Phase III.

pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Questions to Think Through Before Class Validation metrics, calibration vs. validation, multi-sensor comparison, residuals, and critique quality. Why is visual overlap on a plot alone not enough to support an engineering comparison claim? Which statement best captures the Week 3 distinction between calibration and validation? If two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree outside the accepted comparison bound, what is the best Week 3 conclusion? What does a residual pattern tell you that a single overall error number might not tell you? If you were critiquing a sample lab report in the Week 3 style, what four things should your critique try to identify?

pages.title: E1 Week 4: Integration Lab: Full Phase I Pipeline
pages.text: E1 Week 4: Integration Lab: Full Phase I Pipeline Week 4 is the integration week. No new concepts are introduced. Students execute the complete Phase I pipeline — hypothesis, sensor selection, installation, measurement, MATLAB-based uncertainty propagation, and quantitative model comparison — as a single uninterrupted, documented workflow. Every analytical decision connects to the AMVF. The integration difficulty is intentional: students discover which steps they... Read a physical model's equations to identify what must be measured, how precisely, and under what conditions — connecting model structure to experimental design requirements — CLO 1, CLO 3 Rank the contributions of multiple error sources in a measurement chain and articulate where improving one input would most reduce total uncertainty in the derived output — CLO 2 Execute a complete Phase I experimental pipeline — hypothesis, sensor selection, installation, MATLAB-based data collection, uncertainty propagation, and quantitative model comparison — as a single traceable workflow — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3 Write explicit sensor selection rationale connected to accuracy requirements and intended use — CLO 1 Perform a structured calibration procedure and propagate calibration uncertainty into the final experimental outcome — CLO 1 Produce a complete Tier 1 Phase 1 Individual Report with all required sections, AMVF annotation, and properly captioned MATLAB figures — CLO 5 From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test Establishes the connection between a physical model and the experimental design required to test it — and the connection between that design and the uncertainty budget. The lecture opens with a direct question: given a model equation, what does it tell you about what to measure, how precisely, and under what conditions? The answer lives in the partial derivatives. Each term in an error propagation expression is a sensitivity — it quantifies how much the derived output changes per unit of error in that input. A large sensitivity on a poorly characterized input is the dominant error source; a small sensitivity on a noisy input may be negligible. This is exactly the P=IV lesson from Lab 1, generalized: P=I²R is more sensitive to current than to resistance, so a current-measurement error has greater consequence than the same proportional error in resistance. That logic applies to any model. **Worked example (model-driven design):** Walk through a simple dynamics model from the Tier 1 experiment pool — e.g., an angular velocity expression for an unbalanced wheel or a damped oscillation equation — and use the partial derivatives to identify: (1) which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget, (2) what that implies about sensor selection (which input needs the tightest spec?), (3) what range of the independent variable would make the model comparison most informative (where is the model most sensitive to the parameter being estimated?), and (4) what anomalous behavior would look like if a model assumption breaks down. The key message: the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document for the experiment itself. Students who read their model before they set up the hardware make better instrumentation choices and write more defensible reports. Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree A short reference reading covering what to do when two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree — and, more broadly, how to think about multiple sources of uncertainty in the same measurement chain. Three cases are distinguished: (1) one sensor is biased (systematic difference — the gap is consistent across repeated trials), (2) both sensors have random noise but no systematic offset (the distributions overlap but their centers agree), (3) the two sensors are not measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or time (a spatial or temporal mismatch that is not a sensor error at all, but a design flaw). Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy. The reading also covers the taxonomy of error sources students encounter in the Tier 1 experiment: random noise (scatter across repeated trials), systematic bias (consistent offset from truth, bounded by the instrument spec), and structured error (a repeatable pattern that is neither random nor a constant bias — often a sign of model-form limitation or confounding factor). The reading frames how these three categories should be handled differently in a measurement report: random error is characterized statistically, systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet and propagated, and structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated. This reading complements the W4 lecture (dominant error source ranking) and directly supports the Tier 1 Phase 1 report's uncertainty propagation and multi-sensor comparison sections. It also bridges forward to E2 W8's treatment of discrepancy as diagnostic information and the Tier 2 improvement proposal. Questions to Think Through Before Class
