Talking Point: Here is the reverse problem, and it is the one that shows up constantly in actual test design. Suppose I have a requirement: the uncertainty in my centripetal force estimate must be less than 5% at 95% confidence. I can compute, from the partial derivative structure, what that requirement implies for each of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the

sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the angular velocity term. Inverting the propagation equation tells me the maximum allowable angular velocity uncertainty — say, 0.8 radians per second at 120 radians per second — which translates to a tachometer precision requirement of less than 0.67% of full scale. Now I can go to an instrument catalog with a specific requirement, not just a vague desire for a "good" tachometer. This is what sensor selection looks like when it is grounded in the model rather than in habit or convenience. ================================================================================ slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative? Talking Point: From the model, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would tell you the model is wrong. For the centripetal force model, if the data shows scatter within the uncertainty band, the model is adequate for this experiment. But if the data shows a systematic deviation that grows with omega — falling below the model at high rotation rates — that is a signature of an additional velocity-dependent force, like aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass, that the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. t the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega values, that is not a model-form error — it is a systematic measurement bias in mass or radius, which is a calibration issue, not a physics issue. These three patterns are distinguishable, and you can predict which ones are physically plausible before running a single trial. propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total uncertainty. This is because our lab scale has the largest absolute uncertainty of all three inputs at ±0.5 kg. Therefore, improving the scale would most reduce total uncertainty in F_c." A bold prompt below: "Is this ranking correct? If not, what is the error in the reasoning?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause.] The ranking is wrong. The student identified the input with the largest absolute uncertainty but forgot to multiply by the sensitivity coefficient. Dominant error source is determined by the product |∂F_c/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity

ᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity coefficient is orders of magnitude larger than ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω². Even if the tachometer has a smaller absolute uncertainty than the scale, multiplying by the large sensitivity coefficient may still give a larger contribution to total force uncertainty. The correct procedure is to compute all three products and rank them. The student skipped the sensitivity analysis and went straight to comparing raw uncertainties — which is exactly the error the partial derivative method is designed to prevent. If you are nodding right now, you understand why this lecture exists. ================================================================================ slide 15: Closure The

s lecture exists. ================================================================================ slide 15: Closure The physical modeling branch of the AMVF is not a passive receiver of whatever data a measurement produces — it is an active design space that the mathematical model constrains from the beginning. The partial derivative sensitivity analysis you have practiced since Week 2 is not just a bookkeeping tool for uncertainty propagation — it is a design tool that tells you which sensors matter most, what precision they need, where to run the experiment for maximum information, and what anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure. The Phase 1 report asks you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline

you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline — from hypothesis through sensor selection through calibration through comparison — as a single traceable workflow grounded in the model you were given. That traceable workflow is what test engineering looks like. That is what this week asks you to demonstrate. ================================================================================ Agent Notes New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Section 4 is your multi-sensor comparison — do your independent measurement approaches agree within their mutual uncertainty bounds? Section 5 is uncertainty propagation — partial derivative sensitivity analysis leading to a ranked list of error sources. Section 6 is model comparison — does the data agree with the prediction, and what does the residual structure tell you? Section 7 is the experimentally informed model — given the comparison, how would you update the model? Section 8 is your MATLAB figures. Section 9 is your plain-language summary — the same result, stated for a non-engineer. And Section 10 is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, which you will execute today during the

stated for a non-engineer. And Section 10 is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, which you will execute today during the lab session using the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ information for your experiment's primary sensor. Sections 1 through 9 form a single analytical thread from setup to conclusion. Section 10 is a separate procedure that previews Phase 2 — I am going to explain it explicitly in a moment. ================================================================================ slide 3: Calibration Uncertainty Propagation — A Worked Step (Image: A four-step workflow diagram labeled "How Calibration Uncertainty Enters Your Total Error Budget." Step 1: "Record sensor output at known reference values" — a small table showing reference input values (e.g., known masses) in one

cord sensor output at known reference values" — a small table showing reference input values (e.g., known masses) in one column and measured sensor outputs in a second column, with the label "known standards → recorded sensor response." Step 2: "Fit calibration curve" — a scatter plot of the reference data with a linear fit line through it, slope and intercept labeled. Step 3: "Compute calibration residuals" — a residual plot showing (measured output − curve prediction) at each reference point, with the spread of residuals annotated as "calibration uncertainty u_cal." Step 4: "Propagate as one term in the quadrature sum" — the full quadrature propagation equation displayed: u_total = √[(∂Q/∂x₁ · u₁)² + (∂Q/∂x₂ · u₂)² + u_cal²], with u_cal labeled in gold as "calibration uncertainty enters

ed: u_total = √[(∂Q/∂x₁ · u₁)² + (∂Q/∂x₂ · u₂)² + u_cal²], with u_cal labeled in gold as "calibration uncertainty enters here." A gold annotation: "Calibration uncertainty is not separate from your error budget — it enters through the same propagation machinery you have been using since Week 2." A caption below: "For the centripetal force experiment: the calibration residuals of your force transducer are the u_cal term. They propagate exactly like any other input uncertainty.") Talking Point: One required component of your Phase 1 report — Section 5 — is a calibration uncertainty term in your total error budget. I want to make the procedure explicit because it is not always obvious how calibration connects to propagation. Here is the four-step chain. Step 1: record the sensor output at a

ways obvious how calibration connects to propagation. Here is the four-step chain. Step 1: record the sensor output at a set of known reference values. For a force transducer, these might be known dead-weight standards. For a temperature sensor, a precision bath. These are the calibration data points. Step 2: fit a calibration curve to those reference points — typically a linear regression, giving you a slope and an intercept. This curve is how you convert sensor voltage or count to physical units. Step 3: compute the calibration residuals — the difference between the curve's prediction and the actual measured output at each reference point. The spread of those residuals is your calibration uncertainty, u_cal. Step 4: propagate u_cal through the partial derivative framework as one

esiduals is your calibration uncertainty, u_cal. Step 4: propagate u_cal through the partial derivative framework as one additive term inside the quadrature sum, alongside your random measurement uncertainty and any systematic instrument uncertainty from the spec sheet. It enters through the same machinery you have been using since Week 2 — it is not a separate accounting system. For your Tier 1 experiment, the calibration documentation for each sensor you used tells you whether calibration was performed recently and with what precision. That documentation feeds directly into this step. If calibration records are not available, that absence is itself a reportable uncertainty. ================================================================================ slide 4: Predict-from-Specs — A

tainty. ================================================================================ slide 4: Predict-from-Specs — A Labeled Procedure (Image: A three-step labeled workflow box titled "Predict-from-Specs — Section 10 Procedure." Step 1: "Read the supplied lab sensor specifications" — icon of a one-page document labeled "From your experiment-specific lab materials." Step 2: "Propagate specs forward" — the uncertainty propagation equation with the sensor's specified accuracy values substituted in; the output is labeled "predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for this sensor configuration." Step 3: "Sketch the prediction" — a hand-drawn-style sketch of the primary derived quantity plotted against the independent variable, with a shaded uncertainty band. Required

ketch of the primary derived quantity plotted against the independent variable, with a shaded uncertainty band. Required annotation labels point to: x-axis (independent variable name and units), y-axis (derived quantity name and units), shaded band (±u at 95% CI), and a legend entry for the prediction curve. A gold annotation: "This is Phase 2 in miniature — predict, without data, what the designated sensor configuration should produce." A note at the bottom: "No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs.") Talking Point: Section 10 of the Phase 1 report is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, and I want to name it explicitly as a procedure so you know exactly what to do during today's lab session. Here are the three steps. Step 1: return to the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ

to do during today's lab session. Here are the three steps. Step 1: return to the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ details for the primary sensor identified in your experiment-specific lab materials. Step 2: using those specifications, propagate the stated sensor accuracies through the partial derivative framework from Section 5 before you rely on any measured data. The output is a predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for that designated sensor configuration. Step 3: sketch that prediction — a plot of the derived quantity versus the independent variable with labeled axes and an uncertainty band shown as a shaded region or error bars. I want the sketch to include labeled axes with units, the uncertainty bound quantified numerically, and a clear indication that

the sketch to include labeled axes with units, the uncertainty bound quantified numerically, and a clear indication that this is a prediction, not a measurement. No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs. This procedure is Phase 2 in miniature — you are predicting, from specifications alone, what the selected sensor configuration should produce. Today is your first practice run at it. ================================================================================ slide 5: The Model as a Design Document (Image: A simple schematic showing a mathematical model equation on the left — for example, a damped oscillation equation "x(t) = A·e^(-ζωₙt)·cos(ωd·t + φ)" — with labeled parameters: A (initial amplitude), ζ (damping ratio), ωₙ (natural frequency), ωd (damped frequency). On the

with labeled parameters: A (initial amplitude), ζ (damping ratio), ωₙ (natural frequency), ωd (damped frequency). On the right, a test article diagram showing a spring-mass-damper on a shaker platform with accelerometers attached. Bidirectional arrows labeled with three questions: "What must I measure?" pointing from equation to hardware; "How precisely?" pointing from equation to hardware; "Under what conditions?" pointing from equation to hardware. A gold banner across the bottom: "Every term in the model is an experimental design requirement — if you know how to read it.") Talking Point: The connection I want you to see is this: every term in a physical model is a measurement requirement in disguise. Look at the damped oscillation equation. It has four parameters — amplitude, damping

easurement requirement in disguise. Look at the damped oscillation equation. It has four parameters — amplitude, damping ratio, natural frequency, and phase. Each of those parameters needs to be either measured, inferred, or held constant in the experiment. The natural frequency can be estimated from the mass and stiffness — but both mass and stiffness must be measured, and if either has uncertainty, that uncertainty appears in the natural frequency estimate. The damping ratio requires the decay envelope to be resolved — which means your time resolution must be fine enough to track the amplitude decrease over several cycles. The model tells you all of this before you touch hardware — if you know how to read it. The model is the first instrumentation document. Whatever your Tier 1

e you touch hardware — if you know how to read it. The model is the first instrumentation document. Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — whether it is centripetal force, pendulum period, or electrical power — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor. ================================================================================ slide 6: The P=IV Lesson Generalized (Image: A two-panel figure. Left panel shows the P=IV power equation example from Week 2 with the sensitivity breakdown: "P = I²R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error because current appears squared." Annotation: "You learned this pattern in Week 2." Right panel generalizes: a generic derived quantity equation Q = f(x₁, x₂, ...,

n: "You learned this pattern in Week 2." Right panel generalizes: a generic derived quantity equation Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) with a table showing three columns: "Input," "Partial Derivative ∂Q/∂xᵢ," and "Sensitivity Magnitude |∂Q/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ|." The table has three filled rows with placeholder values showing one input dominating the sensitivity. A gold arrow connects the two panels with the label "Same logic — any model, any derived quantity." Caption: "The power equation was the training problem. Now you apply the same logic to your actual experiment.") Talking Point: You learned this pattern in Week 2 with the power equations. P equals I-squared times R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error, because current appears squared and the partial derivative amplifies its

tive to current error than to resistance error, because current appears squared and the partial derivative amplifies its contribution. That was not a special property of the power equation — it is a property of any derived quantity that depends on measured inputs. For any model Q equals f of x₁, x₂, and so on, the partial derivatives are the sensitivity coefficients, and the product of a large sensitivity coefficient and a large input uncertainty is the signature of a dominant error source. The power equation was the training example. Your Tier 1 canned experiment is the real problem. Right now, you should be taking the governing equations from your experiment — whatever physical model connects your inputs to your derived output — and computing the partial derivatives to see which

whatever physical model connects your inputs to your derived output — and computing the partial derivatives to see which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget. That calculation should have been in your Phase 1 report. If it was not, it will be. ================================================================================ slide 7: Worked Example — An Unbalanced Wheel (Tier 1 Pool) (Image: A diagram of a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass attached at radius r from the center. The disk rotates at angular velocity ω, and the centripetal force on the eccentric mass is labeled F_c = m·r·ω². Below the diagram, a partial derivative sensitivity analysis table. Column 1: "Parameter." Column 2: "Partial Derivative of F_c." Column 3: "Value (for m = 0.05 kg, r = 0.08 m, ω = 120

le. Column 1: "Parameter." Column 2: "Partial Derivative of F_c." Column 3: "Value (for m = 0.05 kg, r = 0.08 m, ω = 120 rad/s, typical sensor spec uncertainties)." Rows: m — ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²; r — ∂F_c/∂r = m·ω²; ω — ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. The sensitivity table shows that angular velocity dominates — the factor of 2m·r·ω, evaluated at 120 rad/s, is much larger than the others. A gold box: "Dominant uncertainty source: angular velocity measurement." This example is from the Tier 1 pool and is distinct from any experiment a student in the room is running.) Talking Point: Let me use an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that is not your assigned experiment — to walk through this analysis concretely. Consider a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass at radius r. The centripetal force is F_c

this analysis concretely. Consider a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass at radius r. The centripetal force is F_c equals m times r times omega squared. Taking partial derivatives: the sensitivity to mass is r times omega squared, to radius is m times omega squared, and to angular velocity is 2 times m times r times omega. At typical lab conditions — 120 radians per second, for example — the angular velocity term is much larger than the others. And there is an additional amplification: angular velocity appears squared in the model, so a 2% uncertainty in omega contributes 4% to the uncertainty in force. This immediately tells you three things: first, angular velocity measurement is the highest-priority sensor selection. Second, if your tachometer has poor precision, improve it

velocity measurement is the highest-priority sensor selection. Second, if your tachometer has poor precision, improve it before improving the mass or ruler. Third, the most informative test conditions are those where omega variation is detectable and measurable, not just those where force is conveniently large. ================================================================================ slide 8: Four Questions the Model Answers (Image: A clean four-question framework formatted as a 2x2 grid of boxes. Box 1 (top-left): "Which input dominates the uncertainty budget?" — icon of a ranked bar chart. Box 2 (top-right): "What sensor spec do I need?" — icon of a sensor with a tolerance specification label. Box 3 (bottom-left): "What test conditions are most informative?" — icon of a plot with

a tolerance specification label. Box 3 (bottom-left): "What test conditions are most informative?" — icon of a plot with a region highlighted. Box 4 (bottom-right): "What anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong?" — icon of a plot with a systematic deviation from the expected curve. A gold border surrounds all four boxes with the label: "Read these from the partial derivative structure — before you touch hardware.") Talking Point: Every physical model, when you apply partial derivative sensitivity analysis, answers four questions that directly inform your experimental design. First: which input dominates the uncertainty budget? The answer tells you which sensor needs the tightest spec. Second: what sensor precision do I need? The answer comes from working backward from the

sensor needs the tightest spec. Second: what sensor precision do I need? The answer comes from working backward from the acceptable output uncertainty — what maximum input uncertainty is tolerable given the sensitivity coefficients? Third: what test conditions are most informative? Models are often most sensitive to the parameter of interest — the one you are trying to estimate — at specific values of the independent variable. Testing there maximizes the information you extract. Fourth: what anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong? If the model assumes linearity, the anomalous signature is curvature or hysteresis. If the model ignores a coupling term, the anomalous signature is a dependence on a variable you held constant. Commit to all four answers before you run the

, the anomalous signature is a dependence on a variable you held constant. Commit to all four answers before you run the experiment. ================================================================================ slide 9: Working Backward — What Precision Do I Need? (Image: A worked example diagram. Top: "Acceptable output uncertainty goal: δF_c < 5% at 95% CI." Below: the F_c = m·r·ω² equation with partial derivative terms shown. A table computes: "Given δm from a lab scale (±0.1 g), δr from a caliper (±0.5 mm), what δω is acceptable?" Using the quadrature sum requirement and the dominant sensitivity coefficient for ω, the calculation shows that δω must be less than approximately 0.8 rad/s to meet the 5% goal. Below the table: "This means your tachometer or speed controller must have

approximately 0.8 rad/s to meet the 5% goal. Below the table: "This means your tachometer or speed controller must have precision better than 0.8 rad/s at 120 rad/s — about 0.67% of full scale." A gold annotation: "Working backward from an output requirement to an input spec is called an accuracy requirement — it is the sensor selection criterion.") Talking Point: Here is the reverse problem, and it is the one that shows up constantly in actual test design. Suppose I have a requirement: the uncertainty in my centripetal force estimate must be less than 5% at 95% confidence. I can compute, from the partial derivative structure, what that requirement implies for each of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows

of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the angular velocity term. Inverting the propagation equation tells me the maximum allowable angular velocity uncertainty — say, 0.8 radians per second at 120 radians per second — which translates to a tachometer precision requirement of less than 0.67% of full scale. Now I can go to an instrument catalog with a specific requirement, not just a vague desire for a "good" tachometer. This is what sensor selection looks like when it is grounded in the model rather than in habit or convenience. ================================================================================ slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative? (Image: A sensitivity versus independent

========================== slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative? (Image: A sensitivity versus independent variable plot. X-axis: "Angular Velocity ω (rad/s)" ranging from 0 to 200. Y-axis: "Sensitivity of F_c to mass uncertainty ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²." The curve rises steeply with ω, labeled "Sensitivity increases as ω²." Three vertical dashed lines mark: "Low ω region — small sensitivity, hard to distinguish F_c from noise." "Mid ω region — moderate sensitivity, informative but not extreme." "High ω region — large sensitivity, best signal-to-noise for model comparison, but hardware constraints apply." A shaded green band marks the "most informative region" around mid-to-high ω. A note: "The model tells you where the comparison is most discriminating — test there.") Talking Point:

d mid-to-high ω. A note: "The model tells you where the comparison is most discriminating — test there.") Talking Point: The model also tells you where to run the experiment for maximum information. In the centripetal force example, the force depends on omega squared — which means the sensitivity of the comparison between model and data is much higher at large omega. At low rotation rates, the forces are small and hard to measure above the noise floor. At high rotation rates, the forces are large and the comparison between model and data is highly discriminating — small disagreements are detectable. Of course, hardware constraints — bearing loads, motor speed limits, safety margins — set the upper bound. But within those constraints, the model tells you that testing at moderate-to-high

safety margins — set the upper bound. But within those constraints, the model tells you that testing at moderate-to-high omega gives you the most informative comparison. This is not just intuition — it comes directly from the partial derivative structure. Testing at conditions where the model is most sensitive to the quantity of interest is how you design a meaningful experiment rather than one that could agree or disagree for no informative reason. ================================================================================ slide 11: What Anomalous Behavior Would Tell Me the Model Is Wrong? (Image: Three side-by-side plots, each showing the centripetal force model prediction (dashed line, F_c = m·r·ω²) against possible data patterns. Plot A: "Expected — scatter around the model curve

rediction (dashed line, F_c = m·r·ω²) against possible data patterns. Plot A: "Expected — scatter around the model curve within the uncertainty band." Dots scatter around the curve within gray bounds. Label: "Consistent with measurement noise — model is adequate." Plot B: "Anomalous — nonlinear deviation at high ω." Data follows the model at low ω but falls below it at high ω. Label: "Possible aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass — not in the model. Model is missing a velocity-dependent force." Plot C: "Anomalous — constant offset across all ω." Data runs parallel to the model but consistently below. Label: "Possible systematic bias in mass or radius measurement — calibration issue, not model-form error." Caption: "Anomalous signatures are predictable from model assumptions — commit to

ibration issue, not model-form error." Caption: "Anomalous signatures are predictable from model assumptions — commit to them before the experiment.") Talking Point: From the model, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would tell you the model is wrong. For the centripetal force model, if the data shows scatter within the uncertainty band, the model is adequate for this experiment. But if the data shows a systematic deviation that grows with omega — falling below the model at high rotation rates — that is a signature of an additional velocity-dependent force, like aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass, that the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega

t the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega values, that is not a model-form error — it is a systematic measurement bias in mass or radius, which is a calibration issue, not a physics issue. These three patterns are distinguishable, and you can predict which ones are physically plausible before running a single trial. That predictive commitment is what I want to see in the Phase 1 report's model comparison section. ================================================================================ slide 12: The Phase 1 Report — Connecting This Lecture to the Deliverable (Image: A structured outline of the Phase 1 report's required sections, with specific sections highlighted. The full list: (1) Experimental design,

e of the Phase 1 report's required sections, with specific sections highlighted. The full list: (1) Experimental design, (2) AMVF annotation, (3) Measurement record, (4) Multi-sensor comparison, (5) Uncertainty propagation, (6) Model comparison, (7) Experimentally informed model, (8) MATLAB figures, (9) Plain-language summary, (10) Predict-from-specs exercise. Sections 5 and 6 are highlighted in gold with annotations: Section 5 — "Dominant error source ranking: use partial derivative sensitivity analysis — which input contributes most to output uncertainty?" Section 6 — "Model comparison: validation metric + residual analysis — is disagreement random scatter or structured pattern?" A note: "Sections 5 and 6 are where this lecture lives.") Talking Point: Let me connect this lecture to what

d pattern?" A note: "Sections 5 and 6 are where this lecture lives.") Talking Point: Let me connect this lecture to what you are producing for the Phase 1 report, which is due at the end of this week. Section five — uncertainty propagation — requires dominant error source ranking. That ranking comes from the partial derivative sensitivity analysis you now know how to do: compute the sensitivity coefficients, multiply by the input uncertainties, and rank the contributions. The dominant source is the one with the largest product. Section six — model comparison — requires not just a validation metric but also a classification of the residual structure. Is the disagreement between your data and your model random scatter within the uncertainty band, or is it structured — showing a trend, a

nt between your data and your model random scatter within the uncertainty band, or is it structured — showing a trend, a curvature, a consistent offset? That classification comes from examining the residuals the way we discussed in Week 3 Lecture 2. Sections five and six are where everything in this lecture appears in your deliverable. Use the analysis, not just the vocabulary. ================================================================================ slide 13: The Model Is the First Instrumentation Document (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column header: "Intuitive Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "I'll use the sensor we have in the lab. I'll measure everything I can reach. I'll run as many trials as time allows." A red annotation: "Decisions not connected to model structure

hing I can reach. I'll run as many trials as time allows." A red annotation: "Decisions not connected to model structure — no basis for evaluating if they are sufficient." Right column header: "Model-Driven Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "Angular velocity measurement is the dominant source: spec the tachometer first. Test at high omega where comparison is discriminating. Take enough angular velocity readings to reduce the dominant uncertainty to below 1%." A green annotation: "Decisions derived from partial derivative analysis — directly connected to output uncertainty requirement." A gold banner: "The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you touch hardware.") Talking Point: I want to close with a contrast that I hope makes the central point of this lecture

you touch hardware.") Talking Point: I want to close with a contrast that I hope makes the central point of this lecture vivid. The left column is how instrumentation decisions get made when they are not connected to the model — you use what's available, measure what's accessible, and run as many trials as time allows. These are not bad choices necessarily, but they have no principled basis. You do not know if they are sufficient. The right column is what model-driven instrumentation design looks like — specific sensor choices with specific precision requirements derived from specific sensitivity coefficients, test conditions selected because the model is most discriminating there, trial count determined by the output uncertainty requirement. The model is the first instrumentation

scriminating there, trial count determined by the output uncertainty requirement. The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you write a sensor list. Read it before you design the test matrix. Read it before you finalize your lab setup. If you do, your instrumentation choices become defensible, your comparison becomes meaningful, and your report becomes an engineering argument rather than a data summary. ================================================================================ slide 14: Evaluator Moment — Find the Error in This Ranking (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's Phase 1 report excerpt: "Uncertainty propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total

propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total uncertainty. This is because our lab scale has the largest absolute uncertainty of all three inputs at ±0.5 kg. Therefore, improving the scale would most reduce total uncertainty in F_c." A bold prompt below: "Is this ranking correct? If not, what is the error in the reasoning?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause.] The ranking is wrong. The student identified the input with the largest absolute uncertainty but forgot to multiply by the sensitivity coefficient. Dominant error source is determined by the product |∂F_c/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity

ᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity coefficient is orders of magnitude larger than ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω². Even if the tachometer has a smaller absolute uncertainty than the scale, multiplying by the large sensitivity coefficient may still give a larger contribution to total force uncertainty. The correct procedure is to compute all three products and rank them. The student skipped the sensitivity analysis and went straight to comparing raw uncertainties — which is exactly the error the partial derivative method is designed to prevent. If you are nodding right now, you understand why this lecture exists. ================================================================================ slide 15: Closure The

s lecture exists. ================================================================================ slide 15: Closure The physical modeling branch of the AMVF is not a passive receiver of whatever data a measurement produces — it is an active design space that the mathematical model constrains from the beginning. The partial derivative sensitivity analysis you have practiced since Week 2 is not just a bookkeeping tool for uncertainty propagation — it is a design tool that tells you which sensors matter most, what precision they need, where to run the experiment for maximum information, and what anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure. The Phase 1 report asks you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline

you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline — from hypothesis through sensor selection through calibration through comparison — as a single traceable workflow grounded in the model you were given. That traceable workflow is what test engineering looks like. That is what this week asks you to demonstrate. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W4 Lecture 1: "From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test" (CLO 1, 2, 3). - Combines the blueprint's two options: dominant error source ranking (Option A) and model-to-experiment reading

2, 3). - Combines the blueprint's two options: dominant error source ranking (Option A) and model-to-experiment reading (Option B). Opens with partial derivative sensitivity as the bridge between model structure and measurement design. - Worked example uses centripetal force for an unbalanced wheel — an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that is distinct from what students in the room would be running (per blueprint instruction). Not a pendulum. Not P=IV (those are already used). - Four-question framework (slide 8) captures the blueprint's stated scope: which quantity dominates, sensor selection implications, most informative test conditions, anomalous behavior signature. - Backward propagation (sensor spec from output requirement) is in slide 9 — a practical skill that supports the Phase 1

- Backward propagation (sensor spec from output requirement) is in slide 9 — a practical skill that supports the Phase 1 report's sensor selection rationale section. - Phase 1 report connection made explicit in slides 2 and 12: slide 2 orients students to all 10 sections as an integrated argument; slide 12 drills into sections 5 and 6 specifically. - V&V Executor revision (2026-05-19): Added slides 2, 3, 4 as an opening sequence per approved action item [HIGH] W4 L1 — Three critical content gaps. Slides 2–11 (original) renumbered to 5–14. New slides cover: (a) Phase 1 report as 10-section integrated argument; (b) calibration uncertainty propagation 4-step procedure using centripetal force example; (c) predict-from-specs workflow as a labeled 3-step procedure for Section 10 using the

using centripetal force example; (c) predict-from-specs workflow as a labeled 3-step procedure for Section 10 using the supplied lab sensor specifications. - No E2 or E3 concepts introduced. W3 L2 calibration vs. validation distinction referenced but not re-taught. - Slide count: 15 (title + 12 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 14 (Evaluator Moment — wrong partial derivative ranking). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 5 talking point — added one-sentence bridge to student's own experiment: "Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor."

pages.title: Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree
pages.text: E1 Week 4 (Course Week 4) Reading Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree A short reference reading covering what to do when two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree — and, more broadly, how to think about multiple sources of uncertainty in the same measurement chain. Three cases are distinguished: (1) one sensor is biased (systematic difference — the gap is consistent across repeated trials), (2) both sensors have random noise but no systematic offset (the distributions overlap but their centers agree), (3) the two sensors are not measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or time (a spatial or temporal mismatch that is not a sensor error at all, but a design flaw). Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy. The reading also covers the taxonomy of error sources students encounter in the Tier 1 experiment: random noise (scatter across repeated trials), systematic bias (consistent offset from truth, bounded by the instrument spec), and structured error (a repeatable pattern that is neither random nor a constant bias — often a sign of model-form limitation or confounding factor). The reading frames how these three categories should be handled differently in a measurement report: random error is characterized statistically, systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet and propagated, and structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated. This reading complements the W4 lecture (dominant error source ranking) and directly supports the Tier 1 Phase 1 report's uncertainty propagation and multi-sensor comparison sections. It also bridges forward to E2 W8's treatment of discrepancy as diagnostic information and the Tier 2 improvement proposal. Read a physical model's equations to identify what must be measured, how precisely, and under what conditions — connecting model structure to experimental design requirements — CLO 1, CLO 3 Rank the contributions of multiple error sources in a measurement chain and articulate where improving one input would most reduce total uncertainty in the derived output — CLO 2 Execute a complete Phase I experimental pipeline — hypothesis, sensor selection, installation, MATLAB-based data collection, uncertainty propagation, and quantitative model comparison — as a single traceable workflow — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3 Write explicit sensor selection rationale connected to accuracy requirements and intended use — CLO 1 Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree Three Diagnostic Cases and What Each One Tells You About Your Measurement Two sensors measuring the same physical quantity should agree. When they don't, the disagreement isn't a problem to explain away; it is a diagnostic that tells you something about your measurement that neither sensor could reveal alone. Using multiple sensors to measure the same quantity is standard practice in professional aerospace testing. A single sensor gives you a number; two sensors give you a check. If they agree within their specified uncertainties, you have corroboration: independent evidence that your measurement is not a sensor artifact. If they disagree, you have information. But extracting that information requires knowing which of three fundamentally different cases produced the gap. Defaulting to "sensor error" as a catch-all explanation when sensors disagree is one of the most common errors in student lab reports, and one of the most professionally costly. Each case has a different cause, a different diagnostic signature, and a different remedy. Confusing them leads to wrong corrections and wrong conclusions. Case 1: Systematic bias. One sensor consistently reads higher or lower than the other across all trials. The gap is approximately constant: it does not grow with the measured value, does not fluctuate randomly with time, and does not disappear when conditions change. This is a systematic error (also called a bias ), and it has a specific physical source: a calibration offset, a manufacturing tolerance that shifts the sensor's output, or an installation condition that introduces a consistent reading error. A thermocouple not fully embedded in the material it is measuring, a pressure tap with a small leak, or a strain gauge bonded with an adhesive layer of inconsistent thickness can all produce a systematic offset. The diagnostic signature is a constant gap in a time-series overlay or a consistent offset in a scatter plot of one sensor's output against the other. The remedy is calibration: compare both sensors to a known reference and determine which, if either, is tracking truth. *This image gives you an immediate visual contrast between a persistent offset and ordinary measurement scatter, which is the key distinction this reading keeps returning to. Case 2: Statistical agreement within noise. Both sensors scatter around the same central value, but neither gives exactly the same reading on repeated measurements. The distributions overlap; the 95% confidence intervals from both sensors include a common range of values. This isn't disagreement at all; it is random variability , and the appropriate comparison is not "which sensor is right?" but "do the distributions agree within the propagated uncertainty?" If the confidence intervals overlap, the sensors are statistically consistent. The remedy is not to pick one sensor and discard the other; it is to report the comparison explicitly as evidence of consistency, and to consider averaging the two as a way to reduce the random component of the combined measurement. One important qualifier: averaging reduces the random component only when the two sensors' errors are uncorrelated. If both sensors share a common systematic bias (for example, both are mounted on a surface with the same thermal gradient), averaging will not reduce that component. Case 3: Spatial or temporal mismatch. The two sensors appear to disagree, but they are not actually measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or the same moment in time. This is the most frequently misdiagnosed case. A temperature sensor in the center of a flow field and a temperature sensor at the wall will read differently because the fluid temperature genuinely varies across the cross-section; this is not sensor error, it is spatial structure in the physical quantity being measured. Similarly, two sensors sampling at different rates will produce time-series that appear mismatched because the measurement bandwidth differs. The diagnostic signature is that the disagreement has spatial or temporal structure: it is not random scatter and not a constant offset, but a pattern that maps to a physical gradient or a timing difference. The remedy is experimental redesign: co-locate the sensors or explicitly account for the spatial or temporal difference in your uncertainty analysis. These three cases map onto a broader taxonomy of error sources that applies to any measurement chain. Random error is the scatter you observe across repeated trials. It is a property of the measurement process, characterized statistically (standard deviation, confidence interval). It cannot be reduced by taking one more careful measurement, but it can be reduced by taking more measurements and averaging. Systematic bias is a consistent offset from truth that repeats across trials; it is bounded using the instrument's datasheet specification and propagated through partial derivative error analysis. Structured error is the most informative category: a repeatable pattern in the residuals that is neither random scatter nor a constant offset. Structured error appears as a trend: residuals that increase with the independent variable, or that switch sign at a specific condition. This is not measurement noise; it is evidence that the model underlying your measurement or data reduction is missing a physical effect. *This image translates the vocabulary of random error, bias, and model-form limitation into residual shapes you can actually recognize on a plot. In a lab report, these three categories are handled differently. Random error is characterized statistically and reported as a confidence interval. Systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet specification and propagated using partial derivatives. Structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated; "the residuals show a systematic positive trend above 50 rpm. One plausible explanation is aerodynamic drag that was neglected because the initial model assumed drag would be small; follow-up analysis or testing is needed before treating drag as the confirmed cause" is the kind of statement that distinguishes an analytical engineer from one who simply fitted a trendline and moved on. The three cases in this reading map directly to the discrepancy taxonomy covered later in Week 5: Case 1 (systematic bias) = Category 1 in the taxonomy; Case 2 (statistical agreement within noise) = Category 2; Case 3 (structured error in residuals) = Category 3. When you reach the Week 5 reading on discrepancy classification, you'll find the same framework applied to model-experiment comparisons; the diagnostic logic carries over exactly. The Takeaway: When two sensors disagree, your first question should be which of the three diagnostic cases applies (systematic bias, statistical agreement, or spatial/temporal mismatch) because each has a different cause, a different remedy, and a different implication for what you can and cannot conclude from your measurement.

chunks.text: E1 Week 4 (Course Week 4) Reading Multiple Sensors, Multiple Sources — When Measurements Disagree A short reference reading covering what to do when two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree — and, more broadly, how to think about multiple sources of uncertainty in the same measurement chain. Three cases are distinguished: (1) one sensor is biased (systematic difference — the gap is consistent across repeated trials), (2) both sensors have random noise but no systematic offset (the distributions overlap but their centers agree), (3) the two sensors are not measuring the same physical quantity at the same point in space or time (a spatial or temporal mismatch that is not a sensor error at all, but a design flaw). Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy.

is not a sensor error at all, but a design flaw). Each case has a different diagnostic signature and a different remedy. The reading also covers the taxonomy of error sources students encounter in the Tier 1 experiment: random noise (scatter across repeated trials), systematic bias (consistent offset from truth, bounded by the instrument spec), and structured error (a repeatable pattern that is neither random nor a constant bias — often a sign of model-form limitation or confounding factor). The reading frames how these three categories should be handled differently in a measurement report: random error is characterized statistically, systematic bias is bounded from the datasheet and propagated, and structured error is identified, named, and its physical cause investigated. It also bridges forward to E2 W8's treatment of discrepancy as diagnostic information and the Tier 2 improvement proposal. pages.title: Questions
pages.text: E1 Week 4 (Course Week 4) Questions to Think Through Before Class Reading models to design tests, ranking error sources, sensor selection logic, and integrating the full Phase I pipeline. If a model output has to be known within a certain tolerance, what is the most useful Week 4 design move? Why is ranking dominant error sources useful? Which statement best reflects the Week 4 full-pipeline mindset? Suppose one error term dominates your uncertainty budget by a wide margin. What does Week 4 suggest you should do with that information? In your own words, what does it mean to say that the Phase I report is a “single integrated argument”?

pages.title: E1 Week 5: Transition 1: Cross-Prediction, Lightning Talks & Peer Assessment
pages.text: E1 Week 5: Transition 1: Cross-Prediction, Lightning Talks & Peer Assessment Week 5 is a transition, not a content week. Students are assigned an experiment they did not run and must construct a predictive model — with committed hypothesis, dominant uncertainty sources, and a defined acceptance criterion — before any data is released. They then compare their prior prediction to measured outcomes from a peer group and reverse roles. The week culminates in lightning talks where students pres... Identify the five elements that make a prediction defensible — system response features, propagated uncertainty, a sketch with committed bounds, a ranked uncertainty source list, and a named acceptance criterion — and produce all five for an unfamiliar experiment — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3 Distinguish between a vague prediction ("it will go up") and a committed prediction ("I expect the measured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for engineering credibility — CLO 3 Construct a predictive model with committed system response features and defined acceptance criterion for an experiment they did not run — CLO 1, CLO 3 Identify dominant uncertainty sources in an unfamiliar experimental setup and incorporate them into a prediction memo — CLO 2 Interpret discrepancy between a prior prediction and measured outcomes using model-form reasoning rather than measurement error attribution — CLO 3 Deliver a concise technical presentation (lightning talk) summarizing a prediction and its comparison to data — CLO 5 Evaluate another group's experiment and provide specific, evidence-based constructive feedback on strengths and weaknesses — CLO 5, CLO 6 What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look Directly primes students for the Tier 1 Phase 2 prediction memo, which they will begin in this week's lab session. The lecture opens with the distinction that separates a prediction from a guess: a prediction is falsifiable, committed in writing before data is seen, and derived from reasoning that can be evaluated independently of whether the prediction turned out to be correct. The lecture walks through the five elements that every defensible prediction must contain — (1) system response features with committed specific values or ranges (not vague directional claims), (2) an uncertainty estimate derived from the available sensor specs using the propagation methods from E1 W2, (3) a sketch of the expected output with axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before any data is seen, (4) a ranked list of dominant uncertainty sources with estimated magnitudes, (5) a named acceptance criterion stated as a numerical threshold. Each element is illustrated using a worked example from an experiment the class has not used — the instructor walks through how to extract a prediction from theory and specs alone, without touching hardware. **The grading philosophy:** This lecture must communicate the Phase 2 grading standard explicitly: a prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and complete committed analysis is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The goal of pre-commitment is not to be correct — it is to be honest about what you knew before you saw the data, so the discrepancy teaches you something. This philosophy echoes through E2 and E3: it is the professional norm for test engineers, and students should hear it named clearly before Phase 2 begins. The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough The instructor walks through a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that no student in the room ran as their Phase 1. This is not a polished demonstration — it is a transparent modeling exercise, done in real time, with the instructor narrating every decision. The sequence mirrors exactly what students must do in Phase 2 and Phase 3: read the theory primer and sensor specs → identify the primary output and its expected range → apply partial derivative propagation to estimate uncertainty → sketch the expected time-series or distribution with committed bounds → define an acceptance criterion → then reveal the actual data and compare. The comparison includes explicit narration of what the discrepancy reveals: is the gap within the propagated uncertainty band (consistent with measurement noise), or does the residual show a systematic trend (evidence of a model-form limitation)? The instructor names which category applies and explains the physical interpretation. **Bridge to E2:** This lecture closes E1 by making explicit what E2 will demand. In E1, students ran their own experiments and separately predicted someone else's. In E2, prediction before exposure is not an exercise — it is the required gate before any hardware access. The canned experiments in E2 are more complex, the models have more parameters, and the consequences of a vague pre-commitment are more visible. What students practiced in Tier 1 Phase 2 is the same skill, now applied under higher stakes. The instructor should close this lecture — and E1 — with the question that opens E2: *what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?* Discrepancy Is Data — Interpreting What the Gap Tells You A short reading that frames discrepancy between a committed prediction and measured data not as failure but as information. Three categories of discrepancy are described and distinguished: (1) the gap falls within the propagated uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise, no model revision required; (2) the gap is larger than the uncertainty band but has no systematic structure — suggests the uncertainty was underestimated, prompting a review of which error sources were omitted; (3) the gap shows a systematic trend (residuals increase with the independent variable, or have a consistent sign) — evidence of a model-form limitation, where the model is missing a physical effect. The reading gives brief examples of each: a bounce ball trajectory where air resistance is ignored produces a systematically optimistic prediction at long times (category 3); a pendulum period measurement with an over-optimistic stopwatch uncertainty bound produces a gap that looks large but may simply reflect an underestimated error source (category 2); and a spring-mass natural frequency estimate where scatter is within the uncertainty band (category 1). Students are expected to apply this taxonomy in both the Tier 1 Phase 2 comparison and the Phase 3 synthesis segment. This reading bridges directly to E2 W8, where the same taxonomy is formalized using residual plots and applied to the more complex Tier 2 canned experiments. ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** Course Weeks 5–8 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO... Tier 1 Phase 2 — Prediction Memo (submitted before data release) The pre-data-release commitment document. See `TIER1_assignment.md` for the full student-facing specification. Required sections: (1) predicted system response — specific values and ranges, expected trend shape committed in writing; (2) predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — partial derivatives applied to the packet's sensor specs, result stated as estimate ± uncertainty; (3) predicted plot sketch — hand-drawn or MATLAB, with uncertainty bounds, labeled axes; (4) dominant uncertainty sources ranked by contribution; (5) acceptance criterion defined as a numerical threshold before data is seen; (6) risk assessment — at least one anomalous data pattern identified that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. Graded on quality of reasoning and completeness of commitment — not on whether the prediction turned out to be correct. Right prediction from wrong reasoning scores lower than wrong prediction from sound reasoning — this grading philosophy must be communicated explicitly in the rubric and in class before Phase 2 begins. Questions to Think Through Before Class
