pages.title: ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment
pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Assessment ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** Course Weeks 5–8 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO... Identify the five elements that make a prediction defensible — system response features, propagated uncertainty, a sketch with committed bounds, a ranked uncertainty source list, and a named acceptance criterion — and produce all five for an unfamiliar experiment — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3 Distinguish between a vague prediction ("it will go up") and a committed prediction ("I expect the measured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for engineering credibility — CLO 3 Construct a predictive model with committed system response features and defined acceptance criterion for an experiment they did not run — CLO 1, CLO 3 Identify dominant uncertainty sources in an unfamiliar experimental setup and incorporate them into a prediction memo — CLO 2 ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Weeks: Course Weeks 5–8 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 Structure: Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4) What This Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical experiments differ: you'll be assigned a specific experiment for each phase. But the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are identical for all students, regardless of which experiment they're assigned. Throughout all phases, use the course uncertainty convention in E1_W2_R3_reading.md : put inputs on a standard-uncertainty basis before propagation and label every reported or plotted interval by type, method, and coverage. Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional, and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive. Learning Goals By the end of this assignment and its associated activities, you should be able to: Execute an assigned experiment, record the relevant measurements in the required engineering format, and state a defensible uncertainty basis for each primary quantity. Compare two sensor types or measurement methods for the same physical quantity and argue, with a named validation metric, whether the results are consistent enough for the intended use. Build a blind prediction from theory, sensor specifications, and DAQ information before seeing data, then state what agreement threshold and anomalous pattern would matter. Interpret discrepancies between prediction and measurement without post-hoc guesswork, using the comparison to identify model limitations, uncertainty drivers, and at least one experimentally informed improvement. Synthesize evidence across two related experiments in a short technical presentation and explain what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Why Three Phases? Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1. It's the natural order. It's also a good starting point, but that's only the beginning. A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it on top of a prediction. Before a test ever runs, a test engineer asks: what should the data look like? They build a model of the experiment itself, accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, and the signal chain, then commit to a prediction. That prediction isn't decoration. It's used to design the experiment. It determines whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of interest, anticipates failure modes, and defines in advance what a meaningful result looks like. The ability to watch data come in during a live test, immediately know whether it matches expectation, and act on that in real time is one of the most important skills a test engineer develops. This course will get there. The full experimental design workflow, where your prediction shapes the design of the test, is the focus of E3. For now, in E1 and E2, you are building the foundational mindset: the habit of pre-committing to a prediction before you see the data. This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model before you saw the data, you can't dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is real. You have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one experiment. Your partner ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data: an experiment you never ran, using only the setup description and sensor specs. This is deliberate. The goal is not to mimic the full test engineer workflow, but to develop the prediction-first mindset by working through it in the most challenging form: predicting an experiment you have no direct experience with. Think of Phase 3 as reverse-engineering the experiment. You are reconstructing what the data should look like from the physics and the instrument chain alone, without the shortcut of having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset, building a forward model before you have the answer, is exactly what you'll need when you design your own experiment from scratch in E3. The three-phase structure is not the actual workflow of a test engineer. It is a scaffold for developing the mindset of one. Assignment Overview Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual) What you are assigned: One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool. What you do: Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Course Weeks 5–7 (Sep 14 through Sep 30; see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates). Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind) What you are assigned: A different experiment from the Tier 1 pool: one you did not run in Phase 1. What you do: Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup description for this unfamiliar experiment, predict what the data should look like. You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware. What you produce: A Phase 2 Prediction Memo, submitted before data is released. When: Course Week 7 (prediction submitted before data release). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You'll be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment. They ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. What you do: Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments. What you produce: A combined oral presentation (lightning talk). You and your partner present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4 presentation. When: Course Week 8, during the Tier 1 interview window on Monday, Oct 5 and Wednesday, Oct 7 (see Canvas for sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model. Before the lab session and during the lab session Complete items 1–4 before you arrive; items 5–8 are in-lab steps. Items 1–4 aren't graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable. They're required inputs to your Phase 1 report, and students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab. Read the experiment-specific lab document for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document. Write a hypothesis : a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]." Sketch an AMVF diagram for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect. You'll refine this after the lab. Identify your independent and dependent variables. Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor. Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis. Record your measurements using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound). Use MATLAB for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately: when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence. Phase 1 report — required sections 1. Experimental design Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least one confounding factor identified and addressed. 2. AMVF annotation Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one) to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan. 3. Theory — confirm the model and understand it The experiment-specific lab document provides a theoretical model for the physical phenomenon you are measuring. You are not expected to re-derive it from scratch, but you are expected to demonstrate that you understand it well enough to use it. This means identifying the physical principle(s) the model applies and confirming that the dimensions are consistent. Name at least one simplifying assumption the model makes, and describe the condition under which that assumption would break down. Then show the working equation with your specific experimental values substituted in. This is the prediction your model makes for your particular setup. If you cannot do this, you are not ready to run the analysis in the sections that follow. 4. Measurement record Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each. Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table. 5. Multi-sensor comparison Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold, and the conclusion numerically, not just visually. 6. Uncertainty propagation For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error sources. Show your work. 7. Model comparison Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance criterion. Interpret any discrepancy: is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model form limitations, or both? 8. Experimentally informed model Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be updated or refined. This isn't about accepting or rejecting the model. It's about using the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what physical reasoning supports that change. 9. MATLAB figures All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the graph presentation framework from E1 W2. 10. Plain-language summary A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer. This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix. 11. Predict-from-specs Using only the sensor specifications and DAQ configuration provided in your experiment-specific lab document for the primary sensor identified by the instructional team, estimate what the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity should be before you use any measured data. Propagate the uncertainty from the stated spec-sheet parameters. Sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and approximate uncertainty bounds. This workflow is the conceptual foundation for Phase 2, where you will apply the same predict-from-specs procedure to a complete experiment you have never run. Phase 1 report — format and submission Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections. Format: LaTeX. Submit a single .zip archive containing your LaTeX source ( .tex file(s) and any figure files) and the compiled PDF. Figures should be embedded in the compiled PDF. See E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX if you haven't used LaTeX before. Submission: See Canvas for due date. Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you've never run: predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit it before you see any real data. What "reverse" means In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it. In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not just of the physical phenomenon. You are essentially reverse-engineering the experiment. Note that in Phase 2 you are predicting the output of a single, specified sensor: the one described in your experiment packet. Phase 1 required you to use two sensors and compare them; Phase 2 is a prediction exercise, and your packet will identify which sensor and configuration to base your analysis on. Use only that sensor's specifications for your uncertainty propagation. This requires you to think about: What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup? What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs? How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity? What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs? Where are the likely failure modes, and what could produce data that looks anomalous? A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate whether an experiment is worth running and whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of interest. It's also how you determine whether you'll be able to detect anomalies in real time during the test. What you receive for Phase 2 You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment. The theory primer in that packet is the same document the students who ran the experiment received. It contains: The theory primer for the experiment The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent) The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments) A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab You do not receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet. Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections 1. Predicted system response What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key features. For example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s during the first 5 s, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions. 2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet. Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result as: [quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units]. 3. Predicted plot sketch Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs. time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included. Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction. 4. Dominant uncertainty sources List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution. Justify the ranking using your propagation results. 5. Acceptance criterion Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of your predicted value, you will consider the prediction reasonably supported. State this threshold before you see the data. 6. Risk assessment Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data, not because of a faulty experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Use the Week 3 anomalous-data-signature framework here: name what “wrong” would look like physically, not a generic equipment-failure scenario. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you would investigate first. Submission: The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release date. Once submitted, the version you turn in is frozen for the blind comparison and is the version that will be graded as your original commitment. After data is released, you are encouraged to keep iterating on your thinking, but preserve the original memo and track how your interpretation changes so the evolution of your reasoning remains traceable. Grading philosophy for Phase 2 A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning and committed uncertainty analysis, is more valuable than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This isn't arbitrary. It's the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy and demonstrates a deeper attempt at understanding. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing durable from the result. This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have questions about it, ask before you submit. Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions The pairing You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. This means: You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared. The presentation structure Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately 8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time). Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1) Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment: what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then what the actual data showed. Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly) The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair. One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make that the other experiment’s model also makes, and where do those assumptions diverge? The most interesting synthesis often lives in that gap. Group of 4 (if applicable) If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly. Each pair presents Segments 1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together. This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments. The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section before the Course Week 8 interview window. Peer evaluation Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for each presentation. Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations. A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation. Vague evaluations ("good job, maybe add more data") receive no credit. See the Tier 1 student rubric in this assignment package for the presentation rubric and peer evaluation criteria. Assignment Timeline Summary Phase Activity Format When Release Tier 1 assignment released Canvas / course site Course Week 5, Monday Sep 14 Phase 1 Run experiment, analyze data Lab sessions Course Weeks 5–7 Phase 1 Individual report Written, Canvas Due before the Course Week 8 interview window (see Canvas) Phase 2 Receive experiment packet — Course Week 7 release date Phase 2 Prediction memo (submitted before data release) Written, Canvas Course Week 7 deadline (before data release) Phase 3 Paired presentation / interview In-person, in lab Course Week 8: Mon Oct 5 and Wed Oct 7 Phase 3 Peer evaluations Written, submitted same day Course Week 8: Mon Oct 5 and Wed Oct 7 Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 3 Lecture: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics E1 Week 3 Reading: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework E1 Week 4 Lab: Full Phase I Pipeline (this is Phase 1 of the Tier 1 assignment) E1 Week 5 Discussion: Lightning Talks & Peer Assessment E0 Supplemental: Technical Writing Basics and Presentation Best Practices E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice E0 Supplemental: Getting Started with Overleaf and LaTeX Experiment-specific lab document (provided separately for your assigned experiment) Coleman & Steele, 4th ed. — reference for uncertainty propagation Student Package Note Use this assignment together with the Tier 1 student rubric included in the student-facing assignment package. The rubric gives the pass criteria and evidence standards for the written report, prediction memo, presentation, and peer evaluations. ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment | Course Weeks 5–8 Evergreen — structure, phases, analytical requirements Update-friendly — experiment names, Canvas due dates, group of 4 logistics, experiment packet contents

chunks.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Assessment ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** Course Weeks 5–8 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO... Identify the five elements that make a prediction defensible — system response features, propagated uncertainty, a sketch with committed bounds, a ranked uncertainty source list, and a named acceptance criterion — and produce all five for an unfamiliar experiment — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3 Distinguish between a vague prediction ("it will go up") and a committed prediction ("I expect the measured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for

easured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for engineering credibility — CLO 3 Construct a predictive model with committed system response features and defined acceptance criterion for an experiment they did not run — CLO 1, CLO 3 Identify dominant uncertainty sources in an unfamiliar experimental setup and incorporate them into a prediction memo — CLO 2 ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Weeks: Course Weeks 5–8 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 Structure: Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3

ividual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4) What This Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical experiments differ: you'll be assigned a specific experiment for each phase. But the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are identical for all students, regardless of which experiment they're assigned. Throughout all phases, use the course uncertainty convention in E1_W2_R3_reading.md : put inputs on a standard-uncertainty basis before propagation and label every reported or plotted interval by type, method, and coverage. Read this document carefully before you touch

nd label every reported or plotted interval by type, method, and coverage. Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional, and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive. Learning Goals By the end of this assignment and its associated activities, you should be able to: Execute an assigned experiment, record the relevant measurements in the required engineering format, and state a defensible uncertainty basis for each primary quantity. Compare two sensor types or measurement methods for the same physical quantity and argue, with a named validation metric, whether the results are consistent enough for the intended use. Build a blind prediction from theory, sensor specifications, and DAQ information before seeing data,

r the intended use. Build a blind prediction from theory, sensor specifications, and DAQ information before seeing data, then state what agreement threshold and anomalous pattern would matter. Interpret discrepancies between prediction and measurement without post-hoc guesswork, using the comparison to identify model limitations, uncertainty drivers, and at least one experimentally informed improvement. Synthesize evidence across two related experiments in a short technical presentation and explain what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Why Three Phases? Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1. It's the natural order. It's also a good starting point, but that's only the beginning. A test engineer's job is much more

's the natural order. It's also a good starting point, but that's only the beginning. A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it on top of a prediction. Before a test ever runs, a test engineer asks: what should the data look like? They build a model of the experiment itself, accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, and the signal chain, then commit to a prediction. That prediction isn't decoration. It's used to design the experiment. It determines whether the sensors can resolve the quantity of interest, anticipates failure modes, and defines in advance what a meaningful result looks like. The ability to watch data come in during a live test, immediately know whether it matches expectation, and act on that in real time is one of the most important

a live test, immediately know whether it matches expectation, and act on that in real time is one of the most important skills a test engineer develops. This course will get there. The full experimental design workflow, where your prediction shapes the design of the test, is the focus of E3. For now, in E1 and E2, you are building the foundational mindset: the habit of pre-committing to a prediction before you see the data. This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model before you saw the data, you can't dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is real. You have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You

ct. The gap is real. You have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one experiment. Your partner ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data: an experiment you never ran, using only the setup description and sensor specs. This is deliberate. The goal is not to mimic the full test engineer workflow, but to develop the prediction-first mindset by working through it in the most challenging form: predicting an experiment you have no direct experience with. Think of Phase 3 as reverse-engineering the experiment. You are reconstructing what the data should look like from the physics and the instrument chain alone, without the shortcut of having been in the room when the data was collected. This

hysics and the instrument chain alone, without the shortcut of having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset, building a forward model before you have the answer, is exactly what you'll need when you design your own experiment from scratch in E3. The three-phase structure is not the actual workflow of a test engineer. It is a scaffold for developing the mindset of one. What you do: Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Course Weeks 5–7 (Sep 14 through Sep 30; see Canvas

el improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Course Weeks 5–7 (Sep 14 through Sep 30; see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates). Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind) What you are assigned: A different experiment from the Tier 1 pool: one you did not run in Phase 1. What you do: Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup description for this unfamiliar experiment, predict what the data should look like. You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware. What you produce: A Phase 2 Prediction Memo, submitted before data is released. When: Course Week 7 (prediction submitted before data release). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You'll be

itted before data release). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You'll be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment. They ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. What you do: Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments. What you produce: A combined oral presentation (lightning talk). You and your partner present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4 presentation. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment: what they expected, what the

er's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment: what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then what the actual data showed. Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly) The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair. One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make

hy? 2–3 minutes for the pair. One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make that the other experiment’s model also makes, and where do those assumptions diverge? The most interesting synthesis often lives in that gap. Group of 4 (if applicable) If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly. Each pair presents Segments 1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together. This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments. The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section before the Course Week 8 interview window. Peer evaluation

ether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section before the Course Week 8 interview window. Peer evaluation Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for each presentation. Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations. A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation. Vague evaluations ("good job, maybe add more data") receive no credit. See the Tier 1 student rubric in this assignment package for the presentation rubric and peer evaluation criteria. ine Summary Phase Activity Format When Release Tier 1 assignment released Canvas / course site Course Week 5, Monday Sep 14 Phase 1 Run experiment, analyze data Lab sessions Course Weeks 5–7 Phase 1 Individual report Written, Canvas Due before the Course Week 8 interview window (see Canvas) Phase 2 Receive experiment packet — Course Week 7 release date Phase 2 Prediction memo (submitted before data release) Written, Canvas Course Week 7 deadline (before data release) Phase 3 Paired presentation / interview In-person, in lab Course Week 8: Mon Oct 5 and Wed Oct 7 Phase 3 Peer evaluations Written, submitted same day Course Week 8: Mon Oct 5 and Wed Oct 7 Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 3 Lecture: Structured Comparison & Validation

se this assignment together with the Tier 1 student rubric included in the student-facing assignment package. The rubric gives the pass criteria and evidence standards for the written report, prediction memo, presentation, and peer evaluations. pages.title: Tier 1 Phase 2 — Prediction Memo (submitted before data release)
pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Assignment Tier 1 Phase 2 — Prediction Memo (submitted before data release) The pre-data-release commitment document. See `TIER1_assignment.md` for the full student-facing specification. Required sections: (1) predicted system response — specific values and ranges, expected trend shape committed in writing; (2) predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — partial derivatives applied to the packet's sensor specs, result stated as estimate ± uncertainty; (3) predicted plot sketch — hand-drawn or MATLAB, with uncertainty bounds, labeled axes; (4) dominant uncertainty sources ranked by contribution; (5) acceptance criterion defined as a numerical threshold before data is seen; (6) risk assessment — at least one anomalous data pattern identified that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. Graded on quality of reasoning and completeness of commitment — not on whether the prediction turned out to be correct. Right prediction from wrong reasoning scores lower than wrong prediction from sound reasoning — this grading philosophy must be communicated explicitly in the rubric and in class before Phase 2 begins. Identify the five elements that make a prediction defensible — system response features, propagated uncertainty, a sketch with committed bounds, a ranked uncertainty source list, and a named acceptance criterion — and produce all five for an unfamiliar experiment — CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3 Distinguish between a vague prediction ("it will go up") and a committed prediction ("I expect the measured value to reach X ± Y by time T, because of physical reasoning Z") and articulate why the distinction matters for engineering credibility — CLO 3 Construct a predictive model with committed system response features and defined acceptance criterion for an experiment they did not run — CLO 1, CLO 3 Identify dominant uncertainty sources in an unfamiliar experimental setup and incorporate them into a prediction memo — CLO 2 Tier 1 Lab Assignment — Frequently Asked Questions 1. Is Tier 1 one lab, or several connected deliverables? Tier 1 is one assignment sequence with three connected phases. Phase 1 is your own experiment and report. Phase 2 is your blind prediction for a different experiment. Phase 3 is the paired comparison and synthesis presentation. The phases overlap in time, so do not wait to finish one completely before reading the next. 2. Do I use the same experiment in all three phases? No. In Phase 1, you run one assigned experiment from the Tier 1 pool. In Phase 2, you receive a different experiment packet and predict that experiment before seeing any data. In Phase 3, you compare your Phase 2 prediction to the real data from that second experiment, and your partner does the same for yours. 3. What exactly do I need before I arrive for the Phase 1 lab session? Bring the Phase 1 pre-lab work listed in the assignment: your one-sentence hypothesis your initial AMVF sketch your independent and dependent variables at least one control condition and one possible confounding factor These are not graded as a separate pre-lab submission, but they are required inputs to the Phase 1 report. Students who arrive without them usually lose time during the lab. 4. Do I really need two sensors or two measurement methods in Phase 1? Yes. That comparison is required, not optional. Tier 1 is designed to make you compare independent ways of measuring the same quantity, then argue numerically whether they agree. A visual overlay by itself is not enough; your report must apply a named validation metric and state the threshold and conclusion clearly. 5. What is the predict-from-specs task in Phase 1? Phase 1 includes one predict-from-specs exercise. Use the supplied sensor specifications and DAQ information for the primary sensor identified in your experiment-specific lab materials. Before relying on measured data, propagate those specification-based uncertainties forward to estimate the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity, then sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and uncertainty bounds. This is practice for the larger Phase 2 prediction workflow. 6. What is in the Phase 2 Experiment Packet? The packet includes: the theory primer for the experiment the sensor specifications the DAQ configuration the physical setup description and nominal operating conditions the pre-lab hypothesis submitted by the student who ran that experiment It does not include the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report. Your prediction must come entirely from the packet. 7. What does “blind” mean in Phase 2? It means you commit to the prediction before you see the data. That commitment is the point of the exercise. If the comparison is made after the data is already known, the prediction loses its evidentiary value. 8. If my prediction memo is frozen, am I allowed to change my mind later? You may continue refining your thinking after the data is released, but the version you submit before data release is the original commitment used for the blind comparison. Keep that original memo intact. If your interpretation evolves later, track the change clearly so the evolution of your reasoning is traceable. 9. Is Phase 2 graded on whether my prediction is correct? No. Phase 2 is graded on the quality of your reasoning, not on raw prediction accuracy. A prediction built from sound physical reasoning, explicit uncertainty analysis, and a clear acceptance criterion is more valuable than a lucky right answer with vague logic behind it. The memo should show how you thought, not just what number you guessed. 10. What counts as a good risk assessment in Phase 2? A good risk assessment names what “wrong” would look like physically. It should identify an anomalous data pattern that would suggest a model limitation or physics edge case, not a generic equipment failure. Use the Week 3 anomalous-data-signature framework here. For example, a useful risk statement might name hysteresis, a systematic trend, or a shape change in the response that the model cannot explain. 11. What should the Phase 2 prediction memo include? The memo must include all six required elements: predicted system response predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity predicted plot sketch with units and uncertainty bounds dominant uncertainty sources acceptance criterion risk assessment If one of these is missing, the prediction is incomplete. 12. How strict is the acceptance criterion requirement? It must be numerical and stated before data release. “Close to the prediction” is not enough. You need to define what counts as adequate agreement before you compare the prediction to the actual data. 13. What happens in Phase 3? Phase 3 is the paired comparison and synthesis presentation. You and your partner present: your own Phase 1 result your own Phase 2 prediction of the other experiment a joint synthesis across both experiments If logistics allow, two pairs may be combined into a group of 4 for the presentation, but the core comparison structure stays the same. 14. Are peer evaluations really graded? Yes. Peer evaluation is a required part of Phase 3. A strong peer evaluation names a specific strength, a specific weakness, and a concrete improvement recommendation, each supported by evidence from the presentation. Generic praise or vague criticism does not satisfy the requirement. 15. Where should I look if I want the exact pass criteria? Use TIER1_rubric_student.md alongside the assignment. The assignment tells you what to produce. The student rubric tells you what a passing response looks like in each phase. You should read both before you begin data collection. ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 FAQ | Companion to TIER1_assignment.md and TIER1_rubric_student.md Evergreen — structure, expectations, and grading logic are stable across experiment assignments

pages.title: Questions
pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Questions to Think Through Before Class Prediction before data release, committed reasoning, discrepancy interpretation, and cross-prediction. Why does the course require a prediction memo before students see the data? Which of the following is the strongest prediction in the Week 5 sense? If a prediction is wrong but the reasoning was careful and committed in advance, what is the best Week 5 interpretation? Why is predicting someone else’s experiment from specs a useful training exercise, even though it is not the same as running the experiment yourself? A team says, “Our prediction was off, but that just means the experiment was bad.” What would Week 5 push you to ask next?

pages.title: E2 Week 6: Prediction Before Exposure & Formal Hypothesis Framing
pages.text: E2 Week 6: Prediction Before Exposure & Formal Hypothesis Framing The defining epistemic move of E2 is introduced here: prediction must precede measurement, and that sequence is not arbitrary. Week 6 builds directly on the cross-prediction exercise of Week 5 and formalizes the idea that committing to a hypothesis before encountering data is not just good scientific practice — it is the standard of engineering validation. Students learn to specify system response features, define... Explain why postdiction — fitting a model to data already collected — is fundamentally different from genuine validation, and identify cognitive biases that make postdiction feel like validation — CLO 3 Formulate a formal hypothesis for a complex aerospace system, specifying expected system response features and acceptable agreement criteria before any data is released — CLO 1, CLO 3 Define intended use as the primary framing constraint on all predictive modeling decisions — CLO 3 Submit a pre-experiment prediction document for a canned aerospace experiment with reasoning that is graded independently of whether the prediction is correct — CLO 1, CLO 5 Connect the hypothesis-first discipline of E1's simple experiments to the prediction-first discipline of E2's complex system experiments — CLO 1, CLO 3 Articulate how experimental results can be used to inform and update a predictive model — not merely validate it — as the stated purpose of every E2 canned experiment — CLO 3 Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement Examines the psychological and epistemic reasons why prediction must precede measurement in a credible validation effort. **Opening consolidation beat (10–15 min) — Where we are and what you should have solid before W7:** Before introducing E2's new material, this lecture opens with an explicit inventory of the two foundational nodes that E1 developed but did not fully consolidate, and which W7 will depend on heavily. (1) *Transducer and sensor principles (N001):* Students have been introduced to the idea that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally — but this node was activated only once in E1 (W1 L2) and was never reinforced. W7 will build the entire signal conditioning chain (noise, filtering, DAQ) directly on top of this foundation. Students who are shaky on what a transducer actually does, or why sensor non-ideality matters at the signal level, will find W7 harder than it needs to be. A brief in-class check: "What does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity? Give me an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error." (2) *Statistical properties and distributions (N015):* Students have applied uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo throughout E1, but the underlying distribution theory — what a Gaussian distribution is and why it models measurement scatter, what variance means and where it comes from — was introduced at introductory depth in W2 L1 and not revisited. W9 will extend Monte Carlo to multi-variable systems and introduce regression and confidence interval comparison. Students who are computing correctly but do not understand *why* the distributions behave as they do will struggle with the interpretation demands of those tools. A brief prompt: "If I repeat a measurement 100 times and compute the mean, why does the uncertainty in the mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason?" These are not graded checks — they are calibration signals for students and the instructor. Students who cannot answer should revisit E1 W2 L1 and the Coleman & Steele reference before W7. The opening consolidation beat should take no more than 15 minutes and transition naturally into the postdiction framing that follows. Covers the cognitive bias of postdiction — the tendency to construct a model after seeing data and treat the fit as evidence of predictive validity — and why this is structurally indistinguishable from curve fitting. Students see the AMVF flowchart from Week 1 again: it runs left-to-right because model development logically precedes experimental data collection, not the other way around. The Tier 1 Phase 2 cross-prediction experience is revisited explicitly — what students felt when predicting before data release is now formalized as a required discipline. **EAC motivational framing + test engineer identity:** This lecture should open with the professional context for the E2 lab activities: the skill of understanding how experiments were done in the past, identifying their limitations, and proposing improvements using current technology mirrors exactly what new engineers do when they join an organization. The Engineering Advisory Council (EAC) has identified this as a priority professional competency — the ability to walk into a legacy lab or experimental workflow, understand what was done and why, and recommend upgrades using current sensors, analysis methods, or understanding. E2's canned experiments are deliberately structured to give students practice with exactly this skill before they design from scratch in E3. **Test engineer framing (E2):** At the opening of E2, remind students explicitly that these competencies — evaluating legacy experiments, identifying limitations, proposing improvements — are core test engineer competencies. The EAC has flagged this as what new hires are expected to be able to do. The canned experiments in E2 are structured to be recognizable test engineer work, not academic exercises. Formal Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems Extends E1's hypothesis formulation vocabulary to more complex systems where the model has more parameters, more potential failure modes, and less obvious predictions. Covers: intended use as the framing constraint (the model is only validated for the purpose it is designed for), system response features as the specific quantities the hypothesis makes claims about, and defining acceptable agreement as a numerical criterion before any experiment begins. The connection to E1's Week 1 hypothesis work is made explicit — the vocabulary is the same, but the stakes and complexity are higher. **AMVF framing (W6):** This lecture should explicitly reconnect to the AMVF — the experiment is not an end in itself. The question students should carry into every E2 lab is "what does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter?" This framing connects course activities to senior design projects and to the broader curriculum: experimental methods is the physical modeling branch that bridges mathematical models and the real world. A brief reminder of the AMVF diagram at the opening of this lecture reinforces the learning spine. The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First Conceptual companion to W6 L1, approaching the postdiction problem from an epistemic and psychological angle rather than a procedural one. Covers the cognitive mechanisms that make postdiction feel like validation — hindsight bias, confirmation bias, and the "I knew it all along" effect — and why these biases are structurally invisible to the person experiencing them. Uses historical examples from engineering and science where post-hoc model fitting was mistaken for genuine predictive validity, with consequences. The reading establishes the core E2 discipline at the level of *why it matters*, leaving the formal mechanics (hypothesis framing, acceptance criteria) to the lecture. Students who understand the psychological stakes will engage with the formal requirements as protection against a real failure mode, not as bureaucratic compliance. Closes with the connection to professional practice: in test engineering, prediction-before-exposure is not a preference — it is the line between a credible validation report and an anecdote. Questions to Think Through Before Class

. Students who understand the psychological stakes will engage with the formal requirements as protection against a real failure mode, not as bureaucratic compliance. Closes with the connection to professional practice: in test engineering, prediction-before-exposure is not a preference — it is the line between a credible validation report and an anecdote. Questions to Think Through Before Class

pages.title: Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement
pages.text: E2 Week 1 (Course Week 6) Lecture Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement Examines the psychological and epistemic reasons why prediction must precede measurement in a credible validation effort. **Opening consolidation beat (10–15 min) — Where we are and what you should have solid before W7:** Before introducing E2's new material, this lecture opens with an explicit inventory of the two foundational nodes that E1 developed but did not fully consolidate, and which W7 will depend on heavily. (1) *Transducer and sensor principles (N001):* Students have been introduced to the idea that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally — but this node was activated only once in E1 (W1 L2) and was never reinforced. W7 will build the entire signal conditioning chain (noise, filtering, DAQ) directly on top of this foundation. Students who are shaky on what a transducer actually does, or why sensor non-ideality matters at the signal level, will find W7 harder than it needs to be. A brief in-class check: "What does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity? Give me an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error." (2) *Statistical properties and distributions (N015):* Students have applied uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo throughout E1, but the underlying distribution theory — what a Gaussian distribution is and why it models measurement scatter, what variance means and where it comes from — was introduced at introductory depth in W2 L1 and not revisited. W9 will extend Monte Carlo to multi-variable systems and introduce regression and confidence interval comparison. Students who are computing correctly but do not understand *why* the distributions behave as they do will struggle with the interpretation demands of those tools. A brief prompt: "If I repeat a measurement 100 times and compute the mean, why does the uncertainty in the mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason?" These are not graded checks — they are calibration signals for students and the instructor. Students who cannot answer should revisit E1 W2 L1 and the Coleman & Steele reference before W7. The opening consolidation beat should take no more than 15 minutes and transition naturally into the postdiction framing that follows. Covers the cognitive bias of postdiction — the tendency to construct a model after seeing data and treat the fit as evidence of predictive validity — and why this is structurally indistinguishable from curve fitting. Students see the AMVF flowchart from Week 1 again: it runs left-to-right because model development logically precedes experimental data collection, not the other way around. The Tier 1 Phase 2 cross-prediction experience is revisited explicitly — what students felt when predicting before data release is now formalized as a required discipline. **EAC motivational framing + test engineer identity:** This lecture should open with the professional context for the E2 lab activities: the skill of understanding how experiments were done in the past, identifying their limitations, and proposing improvements using current technology mirrors exactly what new engineers do when they join an organization. The Engineering Advisory Council (EAC) has identified this as a priority professional competency — the ability to walk into a legacy lab or experimental workflow, understand what was done and why, and recommend upgrades using current sensors, analysis methods, or understanding. E2's canned experiments are deliberately structured to give students practice with exactly this skill before they design from scratch in E3. **Test engineer framing (E2):** At the opening of E2, remind students explicitly that these competencies — evaluating legacy experiments, identifying limitations, proposing improvements — are core test engineer competencies. The EAC has flagged this as what new hires are expected to be able to do. The canned experiments in E2 are structured to be recognizable test engineer work, not academic exercises. Explain why postdiction — fitting a model to data already collected — is fundamentally different from genuine validation, and identify cognitive biases that make postdiction feel like validation — CLO 3 Formulate a formal hypothesis for a complex aerospace system, specifying expected system response features and acceptable agreement criteria before any data is released — CLO 1, CLO 3 Define intended use as the primary framing constraint on all predictive modeling decisions — CLO 3 Submit a pre-experiment prediction document for a canned aerospace experiment with reasoning that is graded independently of whether the prediction is correct — CLO 1, CLO 5 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement Talking Point: Welcome to Phase II. Everything we did in E1 — measuring, propagating uncertainty, comparing to a model — was preparation for the discipline we are about to practice: prediction before exposure. In Phase I you were reactive analysts. In Phase II you become disciplined modelers, and the single most important habit of a disciplined modeler is this — you commit to what you expect before you see what happened. That is what this lecture is about, and it is the governing principle of everything we do in Weeks 6 through 10. slide 2: Two Scenarios, Same Data (Image: A split panel illustration — left side labeled "Scenario A: Postdiction" shows a scatter plot with a smooth curve clearly fitted through the data points, with an annotation reading "model adjusted after data collected." Right side labeled "Scenario B: Prediction" shows the same scatter plot with an identical-looking curve, but the curve was drawn before the data points were revealed, annotated "model committed before data released." Both panels look identical at a glance, but only Scenario B has an arrow pointing to a lock icon labeled "Pre-commitment document." Background tone is dark with gold text to match course theme.) Talking Point: I want you to look at these two panels and tell me which one represents valid engineering. At first glance, they look identical — the model matches the data in both cases. But there is a fundamental difference. In Scenario A, someone built a model after they already saw the data, and they adjusted the parameters until the curve fit well. In Scenario B, someone committed to the model before the data was released, and the match — or mismatch — tells us something true about how well the model captures reality. These two scenarios are structurally indistinguishable without documentation. That is why the pre-commitment document is not paperwork — it is the evidence that separates validation from curve fitting. slide 3: Opening Consolidation — What You Should Have Solid Before Week 7 (Image: A two-node diagram on a plain background. Node 1 is labeled "Transducer & Sensor Principles" with a small sensor-to-signal arrow icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W1 L2" tag. Node 2 is labeled "Statistical Properties & Distributions" with a small bell curve icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W2 L1" tag. Both nodes have a dashed orange border labeled "W7 will build on these." The visual emphasizes that these are foundation nodes — not new material, but material that will be load-bearing in W7.) Talking Point: Before we go any further into the new E2 content, I need to do a quick calibration check with you — not for a grade, but because two things from E1 are going to be load-bearing in Week 7, and I want to make sure they are solid. Here is the first: transducers and sensor principles. You were introduced to the idea in Week 1 Lecture 2 that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally. Week 7 builds the entire signal conditioning chain — noise, filtering, DAQ — directly on top of that foundation. Quick check for yourself: what does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity, and can you name an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error? If you are shaky on that, revisit Week 1 before next week. Here is the second: statistical distributions. You have been applying uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo since Week 2, but we are going to extend those tools to multi-variable systems in Week 9, and the interpretation demands go up significantly. Ask yourself: why does the uncertainty in a mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason? If you cannot articulate that, go back to Week 2 Lecture 1 and the Coleman and Steele reference on Canvas — it has a clear treatment of standard error and sampling distributions. I am not checking these now for a grade — I am giving you a signal about where to put your attention before Week 7. slide 4: The Cognitive Bias That Makes Postdiction Feel Like Validation (Image: A brain-shaped diagram divided into two halves. The left half is labeled "Before Data" and contains a simple idealized sine wave with the label "Mental model: clean, complete." The right half is labeled "After Data" and shows the same sine wave overlaid with noisy scatter data — but with a note bubble pointing to the brain saying "I knew it all along." Below the diagram is a short axis arrow labeled "Time →" with a lock icon at the "Before Data" end and an X-circle icon at the "After Data" end, illustrating that the window for honest prediction closes once data is seen. The tone is conceptual and slightly cautionary — this is a cognitive trap.) Talking Point: There is a well-documented cognitive bias that makes postdiction feel exactly like genuine prediction — it is called hindsight bias, and it affects trained engineers just as much as anyone else. Once you have seen the data, you cannot un-see it. Your brain retroactively adjusts your memory of what you expected, so the model you build after the fact feels like the model you would have built before. The insidious thing is that this happens without conscious intent — you are not trying to deceive anyone, including yourself. The result is a model that fits the data you have but may fail completely on data you have not collected yet. Postdiction produces models that describe observations rather than predict behavior, and in aerospace engineering, the difference between those two things has consequences. The pre-commitment document is the structural solution: it creates a record that cannot be retroactively adjusted. slide 5: The AMVF Runs Left to Right — And That Direction Is Not Arbitrary (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram reproduced in the course color scheme — gold nodes on dark background. The flowchart shows the mathematical modeling branch on the left and the physical modeling branch on the right, with the model node at top-left, the experimental data node at bottom-right, and validation as the comparison step in the center. A bold left-to-right arrow runs across the top of the diagram labeled "Model development logically precedes data collection." A second arrow in red, pointing right-to-left, is labeled with an X: "Postdiction: building the model after seeing data." The physical modeling branch nodes are highlighted to show they are the focus of E2.) Talking Point: You saw this diagram in Week 1 Lecture 1, and I told you then that the left-to-right flow is not arbitrary. Now I want to make that concrete. The AMVF runs from model development on the left to experimental data on the right because that is the logical sequence: you build a model, you make a prediction based on it, and then you measure to test whether the prediction holds. If you run the flowchart backwards — if you collect data first and build a model to match it — you are not using experimental data to validate a model. You are using experimental data to construct a model, and those are fundamentally different activities. In E2, we are going to hold ourselves to the forward direction, every time. Your prediction document is your evidence that you ran the flowchart in the right direction. slide 6: What You Felt in Tier 1 Phase 2 — Now Formalized as a Requirement (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two documents. On the left: a hand-written or typed one-page "Prediction Memo" with a timestamp in the upper right corner labeled "Submitted before data release." The memo has visible sections labeled "Expected Response," "Estimated Uncertainty," and "Acceptance Criterion." On the right: the same experiment's data plotted in MATLAB with the predicted curve overlaid — the prediction does not perfectly match the data, but is within the uncertainty band. A bracket connects the two documents with the label "Pre-commitment preserved: this is what makes the comparison meaningful." Warm gold accent color on the connecting bracket.) Talking Point: Many of you remember what it felt like in Week 5 when you had to commit to a prediction for an experiment you did not run, before seeing any data. Some of you found it uncomfortable — you wanted to see the data first and then decide what to say about it. That discomfort is exactly the right signal. What you felt is the gap between knowing something and predicting something, and in Phase II that gap is what we are asking you to cross every time, for every experiment. The Tier 1 Phase 2 prediction memo was practice. The Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document is the real thing, and it is a gate — not a suggestion. You do not get equipment access until your prediction is documented and reviewed. The quality of your reasoning in that document is what you are being evaluated on, not whether your prediction turned out to be correct. slide 7: The Test Engineer Identity — Why This Competency Matters Professionally (Image: A horizontal timeline illustration showing three career stages. Stage 1 (left) shows a new hire engineer reading a lab notebook and a legacy instrument schematic labeled "Understand what was done." Stage 2 (center) shows the same engineer with a laptop and a red-pencil annotation on a graph labeled "Identify limitations." Stage 3 (right) shows the engineer at a whiteboard with a design schematic and a sensor labeled "Propose improvements using current technology." An EAC logo badge is attached to Stage 2 and 3, labeled "EAC-identified priority competency." The background has a faint aerospace laboratory setting — wind tunnel, test stand — sketched in light gray.) Talking Point: The Engineering Advisory Council — the external board of working aerospace engineers who give us feedback on this curriculum — has told us explicitly that what E2 is teaching is exactly what new hires are expected to do when they join an organization. You walk into a legacy lab or an existing test program. Someone hands you documentation for an experiment that was designed ten years ago. Your job is to understand what was done and why, identify the limitations of that approach, and propose improvements using better sensors, better analysis methods, or better understanding. That is precisely what the Tier 2 canned experiments are structured to give you practice with. These are not academic exercises where you run a script and fill in blanks. They are recognizable test engineer work — and the skill of walking up to someone else's experimental infrastructure and knowing how to evaluate it is one that gets you hired and keeps you employed. slide 8: Closure This lecture opened E2 with the discipline that defines Phase II: prediction must precede measurement, and that sequence must be documented. Postdiction feels like validation because hindsight bias is invisible from the inside — but it is structurally indistinguishable from curve fitting and produces models that describe the past rather than predict the future. The AMVF flowchart runs left to right because model development is logically prior to data collection, and your prediction document is the evidence that you followed that sequence. In Lecture 2 this week, we will move from the epistemics of why prediction matters to the mechanics of how to build a defensible one: the five-element anatomy of the prediction document, the formal vocabulary for system response features and acceptance criteria, and the gate procedure before equipment access. Come ready to produce the real thing. Node 1 is labeled "Transducer & Sensor Principles" with a small sensor-to-signal arrow icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W1 L2" tag. Node 2 is labeled "Statistical Properties & Distributions" with a small bell curve icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W2 L1" tag. Both nodes have a dashed orange border labeled "W7 will build on these." The visual emphasizes that these are foundation nodes — not new material, but material that will be load-bearing in W7.) Talking Point: Before we go any further into the new E2 content, I need to do a quick calibration check with you — not for a grade, but because two things from E1 are going to be load-bearing in Week 7, and I want to

n check with you — not for a grade, but because two things from E1 are going to be load-bearing in Week 7, and I want to make sure they are solid. Here is the first: transducers and sensor principles. You were introduced to the idea in Week 1 Lecture 2 that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally. Week 7 builds the entire signal conditioning chain — noise, filtering, DAQ — directly on top of that foundation. Quick check for yourself: what does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity, and can you name an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error? If you are shaky on that, revisit Week 1 before next week. Here is the second: statistical distributions. You have been applying uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo

next week. Here is the second: statistical distributions. You have been applying uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo since Week 2, but we are going to extend those tools to multi-variable systems in Week 9, and the interpretation demands go up significantly. Ask yourself: why does the uncertainty in a mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason? If you cannot articulate that, go back to Week 2 Lecture 1 and the Coleman and Steele reference on Canvas — it has a clear treatment of standard error and sampling distributions. I am not checking these now for a grade — I am giving you a signal about where to put your attention before Week 7. slide 4: The Cognitive Bias That Makes Postdiction Feel Like Validation (Image: A brain-shaped diagram divided into two

slide 4: The Cognitive Bias That Makes Postdiction Feel Like Validation (Image: A brain-shaped diagram divided into two halves. The left half is labeled "Before Data" and contains a simple idealized sine wave with the label "Mental model: clean, complete." The right half is labeled "After Data" and shows the same sine wave overlaid with noisy scatter data — but with a note bubble pointing to the brain saying "I knew it all along." Below the diagram is a short axis arrow labeled "Time →" with a lock icon at the "Before Data" end and an X-circle icon at the "After Data" end, illustrating that the window for honest prediction closes once data is seen. The tone is conceptual and slightly cautionary — this is a cognitive trap.) Talking Point: There is a well-documented cognitive bias that

ptual and slightly cautionary — this is a cognitive trap.) Talking Point: There is a well-documented cognitive bias that makes postdiction feel exactly like genuine prediction — it is called hindsight bias, and it affects trained engineers just as much as anyone else. Once you have seen the data, you cannot un-see it. Your brain retroactively adjusts your memory of what you expected, so the model you build after the fact feels like the model you would have built before. The insidious thing is that this happens without conscious intent — you are not trying to deceive anyone, including yourself. The result is a model that fits the data you have but may fail completely on data you have not collected yet. Postdiction produces models that describe observations rather than predict behavior, and

ata you have not collected yet. Postdiction produces models that describe observations rather than predict behavior, and in aerospace engineering, the difference between those two things has consequences. The pre-commitment document is the structural solution: it creates a record that cannot be retroactively adjusted. slide 5: The AMVF Runs Left to Right — And That Direction Is Not Arbitrary (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram reproduced in the course color scheme — gold nodes on dark background. The flowchart shows the mathematical modeling branch on the left and the physical modeling branch on the right, with the model node at top-left, the experimental data node at bottom-right, and validation as the comparison step in the center. A bold left-to-right arrow runs across the top of the

bottom-right, and validation as the comparison step in the center. A bold left-to-right arrow runs across the top of the diagram labeled "Model development logically precedes data collection." A second arrow in red, pointing right-to-left, is labeled with an X: "Postdiction: building the model after seeing data." The physical modeling branch nodes are highlighted to show they are the focus of E2.) Talking Point: You saw this diagram in Week 1 Lecture 1, and I told you then that the left-to-right flow is not arbitrary. Now I want to make that concrete. The AMVF runs from model development on the left to experimental data on the right because that is the logical sequence: you build a model, you make a prediction based on it, and then you measure to test whether the prediction holds. If you

you build a model, you make a prediction based on it, and then you measure to test whether the prediction holds. If you run the flowchart backwards — if you collect data first and build a model to match it — you are not using experimental data to validate a model. You are using experimental data to construct a model, and those are fundamentally different activities. In E2, we are going to hold ourselves to the forward direction, every time. Your prediction document is your evidence that you ran the flowchart in the right direction. slide 6: What You Felt in Tier 1 Phase 2 — Now Formalized as a Requirement (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two documents. On the left: a hand-written or typed one-page "Prediction Memo" with a timestamp in the upper right corner labeled "Submitted before

a hand-written or typed one-page "Prediction Memo" with a timestamp in the upper right corner labeled "Submitted before data release." The memo has visible sections labeled "Expected Response," "Estimated Uncertainty," and "Acceptance Criterion." On the right: the same experiment's data plotted in MATLAB with the predicted curve overlaid — the prediction does not perfectly match the data, but is within the uncertainty band. A bracket connects the two documents with the label "Pre-commitment preserved: this is what makes the comparison meaningful." Warm gold accent color on the connecting bracket.) Talking Point: Many of you remember what it felt like in Week 5 when you had to commit to a prediction for an experiment you did not run, before seeing any data. Some of you found it

5 when you had to commit to a prediction for an experiment you did not run, before seeing any data. Some of you found it uncomfortable — you wanted to see the data first and then decide what to say about it. That discomfort is exactly the right signal. What you felt is the gap between knowing something and predicting something, and in Phase II that gap is what we are asking you to cross every time, for every experiment. The Tier 1 Phase 2 prediction memo was practice. The Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document is the real thing, and it is a gate — not a suggestion. You do not get equipment access until your prediction is documented and reviewed. The quality of your reasoning in that document is what you are being evaluated on, not whether your prediction turned out to be correct. slide 7: The

in that document is what you are being evaluated on, not whether your prediction turned out to be correct. slide 7: The Test Engineer Identity — Why This Competency Matters Professionally (Image: A horizontal timeline illustration showing three career stages. Stage 1 (left) shows a new hire engineer reading a lab notebook and a legacy instrument schematic labeled "Understand what was done." Stage 2 (center) shows the same engineer with a laptop and a red-pencil annotation on a graph labeled "Identify limitations." Stage 3 (right) shows the engineer at a whiteboard with a design schematic and a sensor labeled "Propose improvements using current technology." o badge is attached to Stage 2 and 3, labeled "EAC-identified priority competency." The background has a faint aerospace laboratory setting — wind tunnel, test stand — sketched in light gray.) You walk into a legacy lab or an existing test program. Someone hands you documentation for an experiment that was designed ten years ago. Your job is to understand what was done and why, identify the limitations of that approach, and propose improvements using better sensors, better analysis methods, or better understanding. That is

that approach, and propose improvements using better sensors, better analysis methods, or better understanding. That is precisely what the Tier 2 canned experiments are structured to give you practice with. These are not academic exercises where you run a script and fill in blanks. They are recognizable test engineer work — and the skill of walking up to someone else's experimental infrastructure and knowing how to evaluate it is one that gets you hired and keeps you employed. slide 8: Closure This lecture opened E2 with the discipline that defines Phase II: prediction must precede measurement, and that sequence must be documented.
