Tone throughout: spoken-word instructor voice, not lecture notes. slide 1:

iverable students are building across W6–W10. Tone throughout: spoken-word instructor voice, not lecture notes. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Formal Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems Talking Point: In Week 1, you wrote a hypothesis for a simple canned experiment — one primary output, a clean physical model, and a straightforward comparison. This lecture extends that vocabulary to the systems you are going to work with in E2: more parameters, more potential failure modes, and less obvious predictions. The formal hypothesis framing tools are the same ones you already know, but the complexity of applying them has gone up, and the consequences of being vague — vague system response features, vague acceptance criteria — are more visible when the system is more complex. By the end of this

stem response features, vague acceptance criteria — are more visible when the system is more complex. By the end of this lecture, you will have the full vocabulary for committing to a prediction that is worth something. slide 2: Revisiting the AMVF — The Experiment Is Not the End (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram with the mathematical modeling branch on the left side and the physical modeling branch on the right, rendered in the course color scheme. A bold annotation box with a gold border is placed at the top of the diagram, reading: "The experiment is not an end in itself — it is a test of a model." An arrow runs from the "Model" node at top-left down and across to the "Experimental Data" and "Validation" nodes, with the label "The question: does reality behave as the model predicts?"

he "Experimental Data" and "Validation" nodes, with the label "The question: does reality behave as the model predicts?" A second annotation at the bottom of the diagram reads: "What does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter?" The diagram is clean and uncluttered — two annotations maximum.) Talking Point: Before we get into the vocabulary of formal hypothesis framing, I want to anchor this lecture in the AMVF. The experiment you are running in E2 is not an end in itself — it is a test of a model. The question you should be asking about every E2 lab activity is: what does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter? This framing connects everything we do in this course to the broader curriculum and to your senior design projects. In ASEN

s framing connects everything we do in this course to the broader curriculum and to your senior design projects. In ASEN 3501, experimental methods is the physical modeling branch — it is the part of the framework that bridges mathematical models and the real world. When you develop a predictive model, run an experiment, and compare the outcomes, you are contributing to the credibility of an engineering claim. Keeping that purpose in mind is what separates disciplined modeling from going through the motions. slide 3: What "Intended Use" Means and Why It Is the First Constraint (Image: A three-panel illustration showing the same physical system — a vibrating beam — at three different intended uses. Panel 1: "Intended use: predict resonant frequency within ±5% for mission planning." Panel

hree different intended uses. Panel 1: "Intended use: predict resonant frequency within ±5% for mission planning." Panel 2: "Intended use: predict peak deflection under a specific load for structural margin." Panel 3: "Intended use: characterize damping ratio for control system design." Each panel shows a different set of highlighted model parameters and measurement requirements. Below the three panels, a single line reads: "The model that is adequate for one intended use may be completely inadequate for another." The panels are arranged horizontally, and each has a distinct colored border to help students visually separate the three cases.) Talking Point: Here is the first and most important framing constraint on any predictive model: intended use. A model is not validated in the

s the first and most important framing constraint on any predictive model: intended use. A model is not validated in the abstract — it is validated for a purpose. The same physical system can require three entirely different models depending on what you are trying to predict and how accurately you need to predict it. If your intended use is predicting a resonant frequency to within five percent, a simple Euler-Bernoulli beam model with a mass correction might be perfectly adequate. If your intended use is predicting the damping ratio for a control system, you need a model that includes energy dissipation mechanisms that the simple beam model ignores entirely. The intended use statement drives every downstream modeling decision: what parameters need to be measured, how precisely, under

ended use statement drives every downstream modeling decision: what parameters need to be measured, how precisely, under what conditions, and what level of discrepancy counts as acceptable agreement. It is the first sentence of a defensible validation plan, and it must be written before any modeling decisions are made. slide 4: System Response Features — What You Are Actually Committing To (Image: A two-column layout. Left column header: "Vague system response feature (not acceptable)." Below it: three examples in red italic text — "The output will increase," "There will be oscillation," "The temperature will change over time." Right column header: "Committed system response feature (required)." Below it: three examples in green regular text — "Peak amplitude will reach 8.3 mm ± 0.4 mm by

onse feature (required)." Below it: three examples in green regular text — "Peak amplitude will reach 8.3 mm ± 0.4 mm by t = 0.5 s," "Oscillation frequency will be 47 Hz ± 2 Hz at the primary resonance mode," "Temperature will reach steady state within 90 seconds at a value of 52°C ± 3°C." At the bottom of the slide, a horizontal divider, then a caption: "A system response feature is falsifiable only if it specifies what would count as disagreement — meaning there exists a specific measurable outcome that would count as disagreement, not just one that would count as confirmation." The visual contrast between the red vague examples and the green specific examples is the central visual message.) Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific, falsifiable claim about what your model

central visual message.) Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific, falsifiable claim about what your model predicts the experiment will produce. Let me be direct about what "specific" means: it means a numerical value, a range, a time, a frequency — something that can be compared to a number. "The output will increase" is not a system response feature — it is an observation. "The output will reach a peak amplitude of 8.3 millimeters at approximately 0.5 seconds" is a system response feature — I can put data on the same axes and tell you whether the prediction held. The reason specificity matters is that a vague prediction can never be falsified, which means it can never teach you anything. If you said "the output will go up" and the output goes up, you have learned nothing.

s it can never teach you anything. If you said "the output will go up" and the output goes up, you have learned nothing. If you said "the output will reach 8.3 millimeters" and the output reaches 6.1, you have learned that your model is overestimating amplitude by 26%, and now you have a specific question: which model assumption is responsible for that gap? slide 5: Acceptable Agreement — What Does "Close Enough" Mean, and Who Decides? (Image: A single graph with a model prediction line shown as a solid gold curve and experimental data scatter plotted around it. The graph shows a clear acceptance band — a shaded region around the prediction line, labeled "Acceptance criterion: agreement within ±10% relative to predicted peak, or within uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger." Below the

iterion: agreement within ±10% relative to predicted peak, or within uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger." Below the shaded region, two data points fall outside the band with arrows labeled "These points require explanation — not dismissal." The acceptance band is drawn with a dashed border to indicate it was defined before data collection. A note in the lower right corner reads: "The acceptance criterion must be stated before data is collected. It cannot be adjusted after." The graph style matches MATLAB-generated figures.) Talking Point: The acceptance criterion is the pre-stated answer to the question: how close does the data have to be to my prediction for me to say the model is adequate for the intended use? This is not a judgment you make after you see the results — it is a

e to say the model is adequate for the intended use? This is not a judgment you make after you see the results — it is a commitment you make before you collect any data. And it has to be numerical. "The model is good enough" is not an acceptance criterion. "Agreement within plus-or-minus ten percent relative to the predicted peak value, or within the propagated uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger" — that is an acceptance criterion. We use the larger criterion because claiming a meaningful disagreement when the difference is smaller than your measurement noise would be misleading — your uncertainty floor sets the minimum distinction you can honestly claim. Setting the acceptance criterion before data collection is what keeps you honest when the data comes back and you are tempted to

the acceptance criterion before data collection is what keeps you honest when the data comes back and you are tempted to judge whether it "looks good." slide 6: More Parameters, More Failure Modes — Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems (Image: A comparison between a simple system and a complex system. Left side: "E1 system — pendulum" with a single-equation model and two parameters (length, gravitational acceleration). Two labeled failure modes: "Amplitude too large (nonlinear)," "Air resistance ignored." Right side: "E2 system — wind tunnel / vibration rig" with a multi-parameter block diagram showing three input parameters, two environmental factors, and four potential model failure modes. The failure modes on the right are: "Boundary condition assumptions," "Unmodeled damping,"

otential model failure modes. The failure modes on the right are: "Boundary condition assumptions," "Unmodeled damping," "Nonlinear stiffness beyond small deflection," "Aerodynamic coupling effects." A bracket spans the right side labeled "Each failure mode corresponds to a specific anomalous data pattern." The comparison illustrates escalation of complexity without making the task seem impossible.) Talking Point: The core challenge of hypothesis framing in E2 is that the systems are more complex — more parameters, more coupling between effects, and more ways for the model to be wrong in a structured and physically meaningful way. In E1, you had a pendulum with one primary model parameter, and the dominant failure mode was clear — once the amplitude gets large, the small-angle assumption

ary model parameter, and the dominant failure mode was clear — once the amplitude gets large, the small-angle assumption breaks. In E2, you might be working with a vibrating structure where boundary conditions, nonlinear stiffness, aerodynamic coupling, and damping mechanisms can all produce model-data discrepancy independently or together. The good news is that the vocabulary is exactly the same: you specify system response features, you commit to an acceptance criterion, and you name the anomalous patterns that would indicate each type of model failure. The discipline is identical — you are just applying it to a harder problem, and the payoff for doing it correctly is higher. slide 7: The Risk Assessment — Naming What Wrong Looks Like in Advance (Image: A three-row table illustration.

s higher. slide 7: The Risk Assessment — Naming What Wrong Looks Like in Advance (Image: A three-row table illustration. Column headers: "Anomalous pattern," "Physical cause," "Implication for model." Row 1: "Residuals increase systematically with amplitude — systematic trend" | "Nonlinear stiffness — model assumes small deflection" | "Model-form error: linear model inadequate above this amplitude range." Row 2: "Measurements oscillate at a frequency not in the model" | "Unmodeled structural mode" | "Model-form error: additional degree of freedom required." Row 3: "Bias offset between prediction and data at all conditions" | "Parametric error — wrong stiffness value used" | "Parametric uncertainty: model form may be correct but input is wrong." Each row is color-coded: systematic trend in

" | "Parametric uncertainty: model form may be correct but input is wrong." Each row is color-coded: systematic trend in orange, unmodeled mode in red, parametric bias in yellow. The table is clear and readable — not crowded. Note: "model-form error" is a term we will formally define in W8. For now, think of it as: the model's structure is missing a physical effect — not just a wrong parameter value, but a wrong equation. It stems from flaws in the model's fundamental formulation.) Talking Point: The risk assessment section of your prediction document asks you to name, in advance, at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. This is not pessimism — it is predictive discipline. If you understand your model's assumptions, you

measurement noise. This is not pessimism — it is predictive discipline. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict exactly what the data would look like if one of those assumptions breaks down. A linear spring model will produce residuals that increase systematically with amplitude if nonlinear stiffness is present — because the model cannot generate amplitude-dependent behavior. An unmodeled structural mode will produce oscillations at a frequency that is not in your prediction. A wrong parameter value will produce a bias offset that is consistent across all conditions. Each of these patterns is specific and distinguishable from random measurement noise, and the tool for distinguishing them is the uncertainty band you already computed. If a data pattern is inside the

oise, and the tool for distinguishing them is the uncertainty band you already computed. If a data pattern is inside the uncertainty band, it is consistent with noise. If it shows systematic structure that your model cannot produce, it is telling you something about the model. slide 8: What "Prediction Before Exposure" Actually Requires — The Five-Element Checklist (Image: A five-element checklist diagram — each element in its own horizontal band, numbered 1 through 5. Element 1: "System response features — specific values or ranges, shape of expected output." Element 2: "Model derivation — equations with substituted numbers, not just variable names." Element 3: "Estimated uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty." Element 4: "Acceptance

ted uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty." Element 4: "Acceptance criterion — numerical threshold committed before data collection." Element 5: "Risk assessment — at least one anomalous pattern named that would indicate model-form limitation rather than noise." Each band has a small checkmark icon at the left in gold. The overall layout is clean and structured — this is the checklist students will carry into the Tier 2 pre-lab session.) Talking Point: When I say prediction before exposure, I do not mean a vague directional statement — "I think the output will go up." A defensible prediction has five specific elements, and I want you to see them now because they are the required components of your Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document.

nts, and I want you to see them now because they are the required components of your Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document. First: system response features — what specific physical quantities do you expect, at what magnitudes, and in what shape over time or across the independent variable? Second: a model derivation — show the equations with numbers substituted, not just the symbolic form. Third: an estimated uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specs you have been given and express the result as estimate plus or minus uncertainty. Fourth: an acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold that defines what "agreement" means, stated before you collect any data. Fifth: a risk assessment — name at least one anomalous data pattern that

ent" means, stated before you collect any data. Fifth: a risk assessment — name at least one anomalous data pattern that would tell you the model form is wrong rather than the measurements being noisy. Each of these elements has a specific purpose, and we cover the reasoning behind each one in the slides that follow. slide 9: The Anatomy of a Tier 2 Prediction Document (Image: A visual breakdown of the prediction document structure, laid out as a one-page annotated mockup. The document is divided into five clearly labeled sections, each with a brief description: Section 1 "System Response Features — specific values, ranges, time-series shape committed in writing" | Section 2 "Model Derivation — equation with substituted numbers, intermediate steps shown" | Section 3 "Estimated Uncertainty

on 2 "Model Derivation — equation with substituted numbers, intermediate steps shown" | Section 3 "Estimated Uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty" | Section 4 "Acceptance Criterion — numerical threshold stated before data collection" | Section 5 "Risk Assessment — at least one anomalous pattern with physical cause named." Each section has a word count indicator: "2–4 sentences each" or "one equation with substituted values." The document mockup has a visible header: "Group names, Date, Experiment: [Canned Experiment 1]" and a TA signature line at the bottom. The visual conveys that this is a real, formal document — not a worksheet.) Talking Point: Let me make this concrete. The Tier 2 prediction document has five required sections,

— not a worksheet.) Talking Point: Let me make this concrete. The Tier 2 prediction document has five required sections, and here is what each one looks like in practice. The first section is your system response features — you commit in writing to what specific physical quantities you expect, with magnitudes, units, and expected trend shape. Not "the output will increase" — "the peak deflection will reach this value at this point in the test, under this loading condition." The second section is your model derivation — you show the governing equations with numbers substituted in, not just symbolic expressions. The third section is your estimated uncertainty — you apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specifications you have been given and express the result in correct

apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specifications you have been given and express the result in correct engineering form: estimate plus or minus uncertainty with units and method stated. The fourth section is your acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold. And the fifth section is your risk assessment. That document is shown to the TA or instructor before you are cleared for equipment access, and it is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. Write it as if your professional credibility depends on it, because in a real test program, it would. slide 10: What Makes a Hypothesis Framing "Complete" vs. "Vague" (Image: A two-column side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis framings for the same experiment. Left column labeled "Incomplete (not acceptable)" in

side comparison of two hypothesis framings for the same experiment. Left column labeled "Incomplete (not acceptable)" in red — shows a multi-line hypothesis that reads: "We expect the system to respond as predicted by our model. The output should match the theoretical value within a reasonable range, and if it doesn't, there may be some error sources we haven't accounted for." Right column labeled "Complete (required)" in green — shows a structured framing with explicit entries for each of the five elements: system response feature with numbers, model equation with substituted values, uncertainty estimate with partial derivative shown, acceptance criterion as a numerical inequality, and risk assessment identifying one specific anomalous pattern. The contrast in specificity between left

cal inequality, and risk assessment identifying one specific anomalous pattern. The contrast in specificity between left and right columns is stark and intentional.) Talking Point: I want to show you the difference between a hypothesis framing that is incomplete and one that is complete, using the same experiment. The incomplete version on the left is what many groups produce when they are in a hurry or when they are unsure of the model — it says something like "we expect the output to match the theoretical value within a reasonable range." There is nothing falsifiable in that sentence. "Reasonable" is doing all the work, and it is doing it after the fact. The complete version on the right specifies exactly what values are expected, where those values came from, how uncertain those values

ersion on the right specifies exactly what values are expected, where those values came from, how uncertain those values are, what threshold defines agreement, and what specific data pattern would tell you the model has a structural problem. Notice that the complete version is not longer — it is just more specific. Specificity is not extra work — it is the minimum requirement for a prediction that can teach you something when it fails. slide 11: How the Experimentally Informed Model Changes the Task (Image: A two-stage diagram. Stage 1 is labeled "Traditional view: Validate or reject" and shows a simple binary branch — a model on the left, experimental data on the right, and two outcome arrows: one labeled "Model accepted (data agrees)" and one labeled "Model rejected (data disagrees)."

t, and two outcome arrows: one labeled "Model accepted (data agrees)" and one labeled "Model rejected (data disagrees)." Stage 2 is labeled "E2 view: Experiment informs model" and shows the same model and data with an additional feedback loop arrow pointing from "discrepancy analysis" back to the model, labeled "What does the gap reveal about what the model is missing?" The feedback loop arrow is drawn in bright gold to emphasize it is the new element. The overall layout is clean with enough contrast to read clearly on a projected slide.) Talking Point: I want to reframe what you think the canned experiments are for. You might assume the goal is to run the experiment, compare to your prediction, and conclude "the model is good" or "the model is not good." That is not the goal. The goal is

mpare to your prediction, and conclude "the model is good" or "the model is not good." That is not the goal. The goal is to use the experimental results to understand what the model is missing — and to propose, specifically, how you would improve it based on what you learned. This is the experimentally informed model concept. In E2, it is a required deliverable. Section 3D of your Tier 2 report asks you to answer specifically: what model improvement does your data suggest? That question starts here, in the prediction document — when you name in advance what would reveal a model limitation, you are already setting up the question that Section 3D must answer. slide 12: Connecting Hypothesis Framing to the Tier 2 Analysis Sections (Image: A flow diagram showing how each prediction document

onnecting Hypothesis Framing to the Tier 2 Analysis Sections (Image: A flow diagram showing how each prediction document element maps to a Tier 2 report section. The diagram has five boxes on the left labeled "Prediction Document Elements" with arrows pointing to corresponding boxes on the right labeled "Tier 2 Report Sections." Mapping: "System response features" → "Section 1: Pre-lab prediction (reproduced verbatim)." "Model derivation" → "Section 2: Experimental setup + Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome." "Uncertainty estimate" → "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis." "Acceptance criterion" → "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome comparison." "Risk assessment" → "Section 3A: Discrepancy classification." The connecting arrows are gold. Below the diagram, a small note: "The prediction

ection 3A: Discrepancy classification." The connecting arrows are gold. Below the diagram, a small note: "The prediction document is not a separate deliverable — it is the foundation of the group report." The visual shows that the prediction document is structurally integrated into the analysis, not a separate hoop to jump through.) Talking Point: I want you to see that the prediction document and the group report are not two separate tasks — the prediction document is the first section of the report and the foundation of every analysis section that follows. The system response features you commit to become the benchmark against which you measure your residuals. The model derivation becomes the basis for your prediction-versus-outcome comparison. The uncertainty estimate feeds directly

odel derivation becomes the basis for your prediction-versus-outcome comparison. The uncertainty estimate feeds directly into the uncertainty analysis section and into whether the discrepancy you observe is larger than what measurement noise can explain. The acceptance criterion is what you apply in Section 3A to conclude whether the model is adequate for the stated intended use. And the risk assessment is what you compare against the anomalous patterns you actually observe. Every element of the prediction document does real analytical work later in the report. That is why we require it — not as a bureaucratic gate, but because a report without a committed prediction has no foundation. slide 13: The Pre-Commitment Gate — What to Expect in Week 6 Lab (Image: A simple process flow diagram

as no foundation. slide 13: The Pre-Commitment Gate — What to Expect in Week 6 Lab (Image: A simple process flow diagram showing four boxes connected by arrows. Box 1 (light gold): "Review canned experiment document — understand the system and sensor suite." Box 2 (medium gold): "Develop predictive model — derive equations, substitute values, estimate uncertainty." Box 3 (bright gold): "Produce prediction document — all five required elements, shown to TA/instructor." Box 4 (red-outlined): "Gate check — approved before equipment access is granted in Week 8." A stop sign icon appears on the arrow before Box 4 to indicate that this is a hard gate, not a suggested step. Below the flow diagram, a small note reads: "The prediction document is reproduced verbatim in Section 1 of the Tier 2

. Below the flow diagram, a small note reads: "The prediction document is reproduced verbatim in Section 1 of the Tier 2 group report — it is not revised after the gate check.") Talking Point: Here is exactly what you are doing in the Week 6 lab session. Your group receives the canned experiment document for your assigned Tier 2 experiment. You read it carefully, understand the physical system, and then — before anyone touches any hardware — you develop a predictive model from physical principles. You derive the equations, substitute the system parameters you have been given, estimate uncertainty from the sensor specs, sketch the expected output, and write down an acceptance criterion and a risk assessment. You bring that document to the TA or instructor, and they review it. If the

acceptance criterion and a risk assessment. You bring that document to the TA or instructor, and they review it. If the prediction is documented and the reasoning is sound, you are cleared for equipment access in Week 8. If it is not, you are not cleared. And here is the key thing about the document itself: you do not revise it after the gate check. Whatever you commit to in Week 6 is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. The honesty of that pre-commitment is what makes the comparison in Week 8 meaningful. slide 14: Closure Formal hypothesis framing for complex systems is the same discipline as the hypothesis work you did in E1, applied under higher stakes and with more parameters to account for. Intended use is the first constraint: it determines what model you need,

stakes and with more parameters to account for. Intended use is the first constraint: it determines what model you need, how precisely you need it to be accurate, and what counts as adequate agreement. System response features must be specific enough to be falsifiable — a number, a range, a shape, something you can compare to data. The acceptance criterion defines agreement before the data exists. And the risk assessment names, in advance, what the data would look like if your model has a structural problem — which is the single best preparation for interpreting discrepancy correctly when it appears. In the Week 6 lab session, you will produce this prediction document for your Tier 2 canned experiment and present it for the pre-commitment gate check. The habits you build here —

document for your Tier 2 canned experiment and present it for the pre-commitment gate check. The habits you build here — specificity, pre-commitment, honest acknowledgment of model limitations — are the habits of a test engineer, and they are what will make your validation report credible to someone who was not in the room when you ran the experiment. ## Agent Notes - AMVF framing is incorporated in slide 2 as required by the W6 L2 blueprint purpose statement. - Intended use as framing constraint is the central thread of slides 3, 4, and 5. - Connection to E1 hypothesis work is explicit in the title slide and in slide 6. - Slides 8–10 from L1 (five-element checklist preview, experimentally informed model, gate logistics) have been integrated into L2 per the V&V consolidation restructure

pages.title: The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First
pages.text: E2 Week 1 (Course Week 6) Reading The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First Conceptual companion to W6 L1, approaching the postdiction problem from an epistemic and psychological angle rather than a procedural one. Covers the cognitive mechanisms that make postdiction feel like validation — hindsight bias, confirmation bias, and the "I knew it all along" effect — and why these biases are structurally invisible to the person experiencing them. Uses historical examples from engineering and science where post-hoc model fitting was mistaken for genuine predictive validity, with consequences. The reading establishes the core E2 discipline at the level of *why it matters*, leaving the formal mechanics (hypothesis framing, acceptance criteria) to the lecture. Students who understand the psychological stakes will engage with the formal requirements as protection against a real failure mode, not as bureaucratic compliance. Closes with the connection to professional practice: in test engineering, prediction-before-exposure is not a preference — it is the line between a credible validation report and an anecdote. Explain why postdiction — fitting a model to data already collected — is fundamentally different from genuine validation, and identify cognitive biases that make postdiction feel like validation — CLO 3 Formulate a formal hypothesis for a complex aerospace system, specifying expected system response features and acceptable agreement criteria before any data is released — CLO 1, CLO 3 Define intended use as the primary framing constraint on all predictive modeling decisions — CLO 3 Submit a pre-experiment prediction document for a canned aerospace experiment with reasoning that is graded independently of whether the prediction is correct — CLO 1, CLO 5 The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First How hindsight bias turns curve-fitting into fake validation The most dangerous validation failure in engineering looks exactly like success. A team runs an experiment, collects data, and builds a model that fits the data beautifully. R² (a measure of how well the model's curve fits the data it was computed from) is high. The curves overlap. Everyone is satisfied. But here is what actually happened: the team saw the data first, then built the model to match it. They did not produce a validated model. They produced a sophisticated description of data they already had. The moment they call it "validated," they have committed one of the oldest epistemic errors in science: postdiction . Postdiction means building a model after seeing the data, then treating the fit as evidence that the model can predict. It feels like validation. It produces outputs that look like validation. But structurally, it is curve fitting, and curve fitting has no predictive content by itself. A polynomial of sufficient degree will fit any dataset perfectly. That does not mean the polynomial predicts anything. That does not mean curve fitting is useless. It is useful for exploratory analysis, empirical calibration, parameter estimation, and experimentally informed model updates. If a parameter such as friction is hard to predict from first principles, collecting data and using it to inform the model can be exactly the right engineering move. The problem is not data -> model -> curve fitting/model updating . The problem is pretending that this workflow is the same as model -> data -> validation . Both have a place, but they answer different questions. *This image makes the invisible methodological difference visible by showing that prediction and postdiction can look identical in the final graph while meaning very different things. Psychologists have studied the cognitive mechanism behind postdiction extensively. Hindsight bias is the tendency to believe, after learning an outcome, that you would have predicted it all along. It is deeply embedded in human reasoning, and it operates even when people are warned against it. Once you see data, your brain cannot un-see it. Every modeling decision is shaped by what you know the answer should look like. The model converges toward the data because the physics may demand it, but also because your judgment has been contaminated. For example, a team builds a model after seeing that an experiment produced a nonlinear curve. They are convinced they would have predicted the nonlinearity. They almost certainly would not have. Confirmation bias makes the problem worse. Once a model fits the data, you notice the agreements and rationalize the discrepancies. Small systematic trends in the residuals become "experimental scatter." Anomalous points become "outlier runs." The model is right, and anything that says otherwise gets explained away. The validation report says, "agreement is within 10%, which is acceptable given the measurement uncertainty." But the team chose the 10% tolerance after looking at the data, not before. These failures are not only classroom hypotheticals. Documented engineering cases show why test/model agreement, model updating, and independent predictive validation have to be kept conceptually separate. NASA's X-33 liquid-hydrogen tank failure investigation describes how structural test behavior, analysis assumptions, and validation evidence all had to be re-examined after a major test article failure. In flight-test work, aerodynamic databases are sometimes updated from measured flight data using parameter estimation and regression; NRC Canada and AIAA describe this explicitly in work on aerodynamic database update/validation from flight data . Those are legitimate engineering activities, but they are not the same as showing that a model predicted independent data it had not already been tuned against. The sequence still matters. Postdiction is so insidious because it is invisible from the inside. If you have already seen the data, you cannot know how much it shaped your modeling choices. You cannot subtract the contamination later. The only protection is structural: commit the model before collecting data, and document that commitment. *This image reinforces the reading's hardest point to internalize: validation credibility comes from when decisions were locked in, not from how neat the final overlap looks. Prediction-before-exposure is not a stylistic preference or an academic exercise. It is the mechanism that makes validation meaningful. A model that accurately predicts data it has never seen gives evidence for something real. A model that fits data it was built on only shows that the modeler could adjust parameters. This is the same discipline you practiced in Tier 1 Phase 2. Your prediction memo was due before data was released for exactly this reason. The discipline has a formal structure, and you will practice it throughout E2. Before any Tier 2 experiment is run, your group commits to a specific prediction. It includes the expected value of the primary output quantity, the expected shape of the system response, the estimated uncertainty in your prediction, and a numerical criterion for adequate agreement. That commitment is documented, shown to the instructor or TA, and then frozen. It is not revised when the data comes in. It is not adjusted when the fit is off. When the data is released in Week 8, you will compare your committed prediction to what was actually measured. If the agreement is poor, or if your model missed, that is not a failed experiment. A poor prediction from a well-reasoned model is rich diagnostic information. It tells you something true about the physics that your model did not capture. That information is useful. A "successful validation" produced by postdiction tells you only what you already knew. In professional test engineering, prediction-before-exposure is not optional. A validation report that does not document when the model was committed relative to data collection is not credible validation. It is an anecdote. The sequence matters: model first, data second. That is the line between engineering analysis and curve fitting. The Takeaway: Postdiction feels exactly like success, which makes it dangerous. The only structural protection is committing your prediction, in writing, before the data exists. In E2, that commitment is a required gate, not a formality. Understanding why it exists is what separates a test engineer from a curve-fitter. The Week 6 lectures walk through the mechanics of the prediction document itself: the five required elements and the gate procedure.

chunks.text: E2 Week 1 (Course Week 6) Reading The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First Conceptual companion to W6 L1, approaching the postdiction problem from an epistemic and psychological angle rather than a procedural one. Covers the cognitive mechanisms that make postdiction feel like validation — hindsight bias, confirmation bias, and the "I knew it all along" effect — and why these biases are structurally invisible to the person experiencing them. Uses historical examples from engineering and science where post-hoc model fitting was mistaken for genuine predictive validity, with consequences. The reading establishes the core E2 discipline at the level of *why it matters*, leaving the formal mechanics (hypothesis framing, acceptance criteria) to the lecture. Students who

of *why it matters*, leaving the formal mechanics (hypothesis framing, acceptance criteria) to the lecture. Students who understand the psychological stakes will engage with the formal requirements as protection against a real failure mode, not as bureaucratic compliance. Closes with the connection to professional practice: in test engineering, prediction-before-exposure is not a preference — it is the line between a credible validation report and an anecdote. Explain why postdiction — fitting a model to data already collected — is fundamentally different from genuine validation, and identify cognitive biases that make postdiction feel like validation — CLO 3 Formulate a formal hypothesis for a complex aerospace system, specifying expected system response features and acceptable agreement

formal hypothesis for a complex aerospace system, specifying expected system response features and acceptable agreement criteria before any data is released — CLO 1, CLO 3 Define intended use as the primary framing constraint on all predictive modeling decisions — CLO 3 Submit a pre-experiment prediction document for a canned aerospace experiment with reasoning that is graded independently of whether the prediction is correct — CLO 1, CLO 5 The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First How hindsight bias turns curve-fitting into fake validation The most dangerous validation failure in engineering looks exactly like success. A team runs an experiment, collects data, and builds a model that fits the data beautifully. R² (a measure of how well the model's curve fits the data

lects data, and builds a model that fits the data beautifully. R² (a measure of how well the model's curve fits the data it was computed from) is high. The curves overlap. Everyone is satisfied. But here is what actually happened: the team saw the data first, then built the model to match it. They did not produce a validated model. They produced a sophisticated description of data they already had. The moment they call it "validated," they have committed one of the oldest epistemic errors in science: postdiction . Postdiction means building a model after seeing the data, then treating the fit as evidence that the model can predict. It feels like validation. It produces outputs that look like validation. But structurally, it is curve fitting, and curve fitting has no predictive content by

outputs that look like validation. But structurally, it is curve fitting, and curve fitting has no predictive content by itself. A polynomial of sufficient degree will fit any dataset perfectly. That does not mean the polynomial predicts anything. That does not mean curve fitting is useless. It is useful for exploratory analysis, empirical calibration, parameter estimation, and experimentally informed model updates. If a parameter such as friction is hard to predict from first principles, collecting data and using it to inform the model can be exactly the right engineering move. The problem is not data -> model -> curve fitting/model updating . The problem is pretending that this workflow is the same as model -> data -> validation . Both have a place, but they answer different questions.

that this workflow is the same as model -> data -> validation . Both have a place, but they answer different questions. *This image makes the invisible methodological difference visible by showing that prediction and postdiction can look identical in the final graph while meaning very different things. Psychologists have studied the cognitive mechanism behind postdiction extensively. Hindsight bias is the tendency to believe, after learning an outcome, that you would have predicted it all along. It is deeply embedded in human reasoning, and it operates even when people are warned against it. Once you see data, your brain cannot un-see it. Every modeling decision is shaped by what you know the answer should look like. The model converges toward the data because the physics may demand it,

ped by what you know the answer should look like. The model converges toward the data because the physics may demand it, but also because your judgment has been contaminated. For example, a team builds a model after seeing that an experiment produced a nonlinear curve. They are convinced they would have predicted the nonlinearity. They almost certainly would not have. Confirmation bias makes the problem worse. Once a model fits the data, you notice the agreements and rationalize the discrepancies. Small systematic trends in the residuals become "experimental scatter." Anomalous points become "outlier runs." The model is right, and anything that says otherwise gets explained away. The validation report says, "agreement is within 10%, which is acceptable given the measurement uncertainty."

pages.title: E2 Week 7: Signal Conditioning, Sampling & DAQ Fundamentals
pages.text: E2 Week 7: Signal Conditioning, Sampling & DAQ Fundamentals Before a complex system can be measured reliably, its signals must be understood. Week 7 provides the foundational treatment of signal conditioning, sampling, and data acquisition that the canned aerospace experiments make concrete. Three core lecture topics anchor the week: Nyquist sampling and aliasing, analog signal conditioning and filtering principles, and DAQ configuration choices and noise sources. Students... Apply the Nyquist sampling theorem to identify the minimum sampling rate required for a given signal bandwidth, and predict the consequences of aliasing when the theorem is violated — CLO 4 Explain the purpose and design rationale of an anti-aliasing filter, and identify appropriate filter cutoff frequency relative to signal bandwidth and sampling rate — CLO 4 Identify and describe at least three common DAQ configuration choices — channel configuration, resolution, sample rate — and articulate the tradeoff each choice imposes — CLO 4 Visualize a measured signal in both time and frequency domain using MATLAB and identify signal bandwidth from the frequency-domain representation — CLO 4 Produce a DAQ configuration justification section in the Tier 2 group report documenting sampling frequency selection, filtering rationale, and noise source identification with MATLAB signal visualizations — CLO 4, CLO 5 Read and interpret DAQ configuration documentation for a real instrument, identifying channel configuration, resolution, and sample rate choices and articulating the tradeoff each imposes — CLO 4 Nyquist Sampling, Aliasing & Signal Bandwidth Introduces the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem and aliasing as the fundamental constraints on any digital measurement system. Covers signal bandwidth as the property of the physical phenomenon being measured (not a property of the sensor), the aliasing mechanism — how undersampled signals fold back into the measured spectrum — and the practical consequences for aerospace measurements where signal content may span many orders of frequency. Students work through examples to develop intuition for when sampling rate matters critically. Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain Covers the signal conditioning chain between a physical sensor and a DAQ digitizer: amplification (gain and offset), filtering (low-pass anti-aliasing filter — purpose, order, cutoff selection), and noise sources (thermal noise, electromagnetic interference, ground loops). The design rationale for anti-aliasing filter cutoff selection is emphasized: the cutoff must be below the Nyquist frequency of the chosen sampling rate, but not so aggressive that it attenuates signal content of interest. Students see that signal conditioning choices are not technical defaults — they encode assumptions about signal structure and acceptable data quality. **Analog vs. digital filtering contrast (5–7 min segment):** This lecture should include an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles. The key message: anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain before the ADC — digital filters applied post-digitization cannot retroactively correct aliasing because the information is already lost. Additionally, digital filters introduce phase delay, passband ripple, and potential edge-case artifacts (e.g., ringing near sharp transitions) that are non-obvious. Students do not need to know how to design analog or digital filters — this is awareness-level content. The takeaway: signal conditioning choices must be made before sampling, not after, and the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision that must be documented in any credible experimental report. Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement Intuition-building pre-class read for W7's two lectures. Approaches Nyquist and aliasing from a physical intuition angle: what does it actually mean for a signal to be "undersampled," and why does the resulting artifact look like a real signal rather than obvious noise? Uses the stroboscopic wheel and helicopter blade visual analogies to make aliasing concrete before any mathematics appears. Covers signal bandwidth as a property of the physical phenomenon (not the sensor), and frames the sampling rate decision as a modeling commitment — you are claiming, before you sample, that your signal has no content above half your sampling rate. If that claim is wrong, the data is silently corrupted. The reading stops before filter design and DAQ configuration — those are covered in the companion reading (R2) and lectures. Its job is to ensure students arrive at the W7 lectures already understanding *why* these choices matter, so lecture time can address *how* to make them defensibly. DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference Reference-style companion to the W7 signal conditioning lecture, designed to be read alongside or immediately after class. Covers the DAQ configuration choices that experimentalists must document and justify: single-ended vs. differential channel configuration (ground loop implications), resolution (quantization error and its contribution to uncertainty), sample rate selection (Nyquist argument plus margin), triggering and synchronization, and practical setup documentation. The central message is that DAQ configuration is a written, justifiable design decision — not a lab default to accept without inspection. Students are expected to reference this reading when completing the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. **Bit rollover example:** Includes a concrete walkthrough of a bit-rollover or counter-overflow artifact that produces a plausible-looking oscillatory signal in the recorded data — where only uncertainty bounds and physical reasoning allow the artifact to be identified as non-physical. This is the key illustration for why DAQ configuration choices introduce their own error sources and why uncertainty analysis is the mechanism for distinguishing physical behavior from instrumentation artifacts. Unlike R1, this reading is reference-dense rather than narrative — students should expect to return to it when drafting their report. Questions to Think Through Before Class
