st frequency. That means the anti-aliasing filter is not actually providing aliasing protection at this sample rate. Any content between 500 and 1000 hertz would alias into the measurement, and the filter is not attenuating it. The fix is straightforward: either increase the sample rate to at least 2 kilohertz so the Nyquist frequency is above the filter cutoff, or add a supplementary analog filter with a lower cutoff. This is exactly the kind of configuration evaluation you will do in the Week 7 lab — reading the instrument documentation, checking whether the configuration is self-consistent, and identifying where it is not. slide 9: Connecting to the Improvement Proposal — What You Are Looking For (Image: A two-column illustration. Left column header: "What to look for in the existing

osal — What You Are Looking For (Image: A two-column illustration. Left column header: "What to look for in the existing configuration." Three items listed: "Is the sample rate high enough given the signal bandwidth?" | "Is the anti-aliasing filter cutoff appropriate?" | "Are there identifiable noise sources that could be reduced?" Right column header: "How to frame the improvement proposal." Three items listed: "Propose a specific sample rate with Nyquist margin calculation." | "Propose a specific filter cutoff and order with justification." | "Identify specific noise mitigation strategies (shielded cable, differential measurement, single-point ground)." A connecting arrow between the two columns is labeled "The improvement proposal is grounded in this analysis — not a generic 'use

ing arrow between the two columns is labeled "The improvement proposal is grounded in this analysis — not a generic 'use better equipment' suggestion." At the bottom: "Improvement proposal category (b): DAQ/signal conditioning — this is the CLO 4 graded deliverable in the Tier 2 report.") Talking Point: Section 6 of your Tier 2 group report asks you to propose improvements to the experiment, and category (b) is specifically DAQ and signal conditioning. This is the CLO 4 graded deliverable of E2, and the standard is higher than "use a better sensor." A credible improvement proposal is grounded in your data and your analysis. If you found from the FFT that the signal has significant energy at frequencies above the current Nyquist frequency, you propose a specific increased sample rate with

significant energy at frequencies above the current Nyquist frequency, you propose a specific increased sample rate with the Nyquist margin calculation shown. If you found that the anti-aliasing filter cutoff is poorly positioned relative to the sample rate, you propose a specific alternative cutoff with the filter order and the rationale. If you identified a ground loop in the noise floor, you propose differential measurement or cable shielding with an explanation of why the current configuration produces the noise. The improvement proposal is where the theory you learned this week gets applied to the specific experiment you ran. Here is what a fully quantified improvement argument looks like: the FFT reveals a structural mode at 380 Hz. Current sample rate is 500 Hz, giving a Nyquist

vement argument looks like: the FFT reveals a structural mode at 380 Hz. Current sample rate is 500 Hz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 250 Hz — below the 380 Hz mode. The 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 − 380 = 120 Hz and appears as a false spectral peak. Proposed improvement: increase sample rate to 2 kHz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 1 kHz — adequate margin above a potential 780 Hz secondary mode. That is the level of quantification the rubric awards for improvement proposal excellence. slide 10: The Signal Conditioning Choices Are Modeling Decisions (Image: A diagram showing the concept of signal conditioning as a modeling commitment. The center of the diagram has a bold statement: "Every signal conditioning choice encodes an assumption about the signal." Three examples branch from the

old statement: "Every signal conditioning choice encodes an assumption about the signal." Three examples branch from the center: "Sample rate choice" → "Assumes: no meaningful signal energy above fs/2." "Filter cutoff choice" → "Assumes: no signal content between fc and fs/2 that matters for the intended use." "Gain choice" → "Assumes: maximum signal amplitude will not exceed ADC input range." Each assumption has a consequence box attached: "Violated sample rate assumption → aliasing." "Violated filter cutoff assumption → attenuated signal content OR aliased content not removed." "Violated gain assumption → clipping — signal is permanently distorted." The visual style uses a spider diagram or concept map layout, with the central statement in a large gold circle and the branches radiating

ses a spider diagram or concept map layout, with the central statement in a large gold circle and the branches radiating outward.) Talking Point: I want to close this lecture with the unifying frame. Every signal conditioning choice you make is a modeling decision — it encodes an assumption about the signal you are trying to capture. Choosing a sample rate of 2 kilohertz means you are claiming the signal has no meaningful energy above 1 kilohertz. Choosing an anti-aliasing filter cutoff of 800 hertz means you are claiming that any signal content between 800 hertz and 1 kilohertz can be discarded without affecting your measurement quality for the intended use. Choosing a gain of 100 means you are claiming the maximum signal amplitude will not exceed 5 volts after amplification. Each of

g a gain of 100 means you are claiming the maximum signal amplitude will not exceed 5 volts after amplification. Each of these assumptions can be wrong, and when they are, the consequences range from reduced precision to completely corrupted data — and the corrupted data will not always be obviously wrong. The only protection is making these decisions based on analysis of the expected signal, documenting the reasoning, and being prepared to defend each choice when your measurement is scrutinized. That is what makes a measurement credible, and that is what the DAQ configuration section of your report is for. slide 11: Closure Signal conditioning is not a black box that happens before your MATLAB workspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the

hat happens before your MATLAB workspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the physical signal you are trying to capture. Amplification matches sensor output to ADC dynamic range and controls quantization error contribution. The anti-aliasing filter must be placed in the analog domain before the ADC, with a cutoff below the Nyquist frequency, because aliased content cannot be removed after digitization. Noise enters the chain at every stage — from the sensor, from the cable, from the amplifier, from the ground configuration — and identifying and documenting known noise sources is a required part of your experimental report. Analog and digital filtering serve different purposes and must not be confused: digital filters cannot retroactively correct

og and digital filtering serve different purposes and must not be confused: digital filters cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Every one of these choices belongs in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report, argued from the signal bandwidth and the intended use of the measurement. The Week 7 lab will give you the data and the documentation to perform that analysis for your own experiment. Agent Notes Content is grounded in the W7 L2 blueprint purpose statement: signal conditioning chain, amplification, anti-aliasing filter design rationale, noise sources, analog vs. digital filtering contrast. The analog vs. digital filtering contrast (slide 6) is specifically required by the blueprint: "an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles."

lide 6) is specifically required by the blueprint: "an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles." Slide 10 frames all signal conditioning choices as modeling commitments — connecting this lecture to the AMVF spine and the E2 prediction discipline. The worked example in slide 8 uses the sensor spec evaluation format that students will apply in the W7 lab. Slide 9 connects directly to the Tier 2 improvement proposal category (b), per blueprint note about the lab feeding into the group report. No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 4 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain Talking Point: In the last lecture, we established that choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment — you are claiming that the physical

e last lecture, we established that choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment — you are claiming that the physical signal has no meaningful content above the Nyquist frequency. But the sample rate is only part of the story. Between the physical sensor and the DAQ digitizer, the signal passes through a conditioning chain: it gets amplified, filtered, and modified in ways that can improve the measurement — or corrupt it — depending on the choices made. This lecture covers that chain: what each stage does, why it is there, and the most important design decisions you need to make and document before any data is collected. slide 2: The Signal Conditioning Chain — An Overview (Image: A horizontal block diagram of the complete signal conditioning chain from physical phenomenon to digital

verview (Image: A horizontal block diagram of the complete signal conditioning chain from physical phenomenon to digital data record. Five blocks connected by arrows, left to right: "Physical Sensor / Transducer" → "Signal Amplifier (Gain + Offset)" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." Each block has a small icon below it: the sensor has a waveform, the amplifier has a gain triangle symbol, the filter has a frequency roll-off curve, the ADC has a staircase digitization symbol, and the data record has a MATLAB plot icon. A bracket below the first three blocks is labeled "Analog domain — these stages happen before digitization." A bracket below the last two blocks is labeled "Digital domain — once here, analog information is

before digitization." A bracket below the last two blocks is labeled "Digital domain — once here, analog information is locked in." The color of the analog domain bracket is gold; the digital domain bracket is a lighter gray.) Talking Point: Let me give you the full picture of what happens between a physical event and a number in a MATLAB variable. The sensor transduces the physical quantity into an electrical signal — typically a voltage or current proportional to what is being measured. That signal then passes through an amplifier, which increases the signal level to a range that the ADC can resolve effectively. It then passes through the anti-aliasing filter, which removes frequency content above the Nyquist frequency. Then the ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at the chosen

removes frequency content above the Nyquist frequency. Then the ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at the chosen rate and converts each sample to a digital number. Every design decision in the analog domain — how much gain, what filter cutoff, what filter order — affects the quality of the digital record. And critically: none of these effects can be undone after digitization. If you amplified the wrong band, you cannot de-amplify it. If you filtered with the wrong cutoff, you cannot unfilter. The measurement is fixed the moment it crosses into the digital domain. slide 3: Gain and Offset — Why Amplification Is a Design Decision (Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: "Under-amplified signal" — a small-amplitude sensor output waveform (about 10% of the ADC input range)

tion. Left panel: "Under-amplified signal" — a small-amplitude sensor output waveform (about 10% of the ADC input range) plotted on a voltage axis labeled 0 to 10 V. The waveform only occupies 0 to 1 V of the range. A label reads: "Signal uses only 10% of ADC range — poor resolution, high quantization error contribution." Right panel: "Properly amplified signal" — the same waveform amplified to occupy 60–80% of the ADC input range. A label reads: "Signal occupies 70% of range — resolution optimized." Below both panels, a caption: "Gain selection directly controls how much of the ADC's resolution is used to represent the signal. An under-amplified signal wastes bits." A small inset shows the ADC quantization staircase to reinforce the concept.) Talking Point: The purpose of the amplifier

small inset shows the ADC quantization staircase to reinforce the concept.) Talking Point: The purpose of the amplifier stage is to match the sensor's output range to the ADC's input range — to use as much of the ADC's dynamic range as possible without clipping. An ADC converts an analog voltage to a digital integer within its input range. If your sensor output spans 0.1 volts but your ADC input range is 0 to 10 volts, you are using only one percent of the available digital counts to represent the entire signal. Every bit that you are not using is measurement precision that you are throwing away. Proper gain selection puts the signal in the operating region where the ADC provides meaningful resolution. This is directly related to quantization error — the uncertainty introduced by the

e ADC provides meaningful resolution. This is directly related to quantization error — the uncertainty introduced by the discrete nature of the ADC output. Quantization error is typically estimated as plus-or-minus half of one least-significant-bit, and its contribution to the total measurement uncertainty is smaller when the signal occupies more of the ADC range. slide 4: Noise Sources in the Signal Chain — What You Are Fighting (Image: A layered diagram of the signal conditioning chain with noise sources identified at each stage. The sensor block has an arrow labeled "Sensor noise: thermal noise (Johnson noise), sensitivity drift." The signal cable between sensor and amplifier has a lightning bolt icon labeled "Electromagnetic interference (EMI): coupling from nearby power lines,

r and amplifier has a lightning bolt icon labeled "Electromagnetic interference (EMI): coupling from nearby power lines, motors, RF sources." The amplifier block has an arrow labeled "Amplifier noise: thermal noise, bias current." A ground symbol between components is labeled "Ground loops: current flowing through shared ground path produces measurable voltage offset." Each noise source has an estimated contribution in parentheses: "sensor thermal noise: typically μV level," "EMI: can be mV level," "amplifier noise: typically nV to μV," "ground loops: can be mV to hundreds of mV." The visual is a chain diagram with noise injected at each stage — cumulative noise accumulates through the chain.) Talking Point: Noise enters the signal chain at every stage, and understanding where it comes

ulates through the chain.) Talking Point: Noise enters the signal chain at every stage, and understanding where it comes from is the first step toward controlling it. The sensor itself introduces thermal noise — a fundamental quantum-mechanical effect that is proportional to temperature and resistance. The cable between the sensor and the amplifier can pick up electromagnetic interference from nearby power lines, motors, or radio-frequency sources. The amplifier adds its own noise, and if the circuit does not have a clean, single-point ground reference, current flowing through the shared ground path can add a DC offset or a 60-hertz hum to the signal. Ground loops are one of the most common causes of unexplained noise in experimental measurements, and they are almost entirely preventable

ne of the most common causes of unexplained noise in experimental measurements, and they are almost entirely preventable with careful grounding practice. A ground loop typically shows up as a 60 Hz peak in the FFT — a regular spike at the power line frequency that doesn't move regardless of how the physical system is loaded or excited. The takeaway is that noise is not a fixed property of your measurement system — it depends on how the system is assembled and set up, and improving signal quality sometimes requires changing the setup, not the hardware. slide 5: The Anti-Aliasing Filter — Design Rationale and Cutoff Selection (Image: A frequency-domain plot showing the amplitude response of a low-pass filter on the y-axis (in dB, from 0 to −60 dB) and frequency on the x-axis (from 0 to

e amplitude response of a low-pass filter on the y-axis (in dB, from 0 to −60 dB) and frequency on the x-axis (from 0 to twice the sampling frequency). The filter has a flat passband extending from 0 to the cutoff frequency fc, a transition band from fc to the Nyquist frequency fs/2, and a stopband attenuating above fs/2. Three vertical dashed lines mark: fc (filter cutoff), fmax (highest frequency of signal interest), and fs/2 (Nyquist frequency). The relationships between these three frequencies are labeled: "fc must be above fmax — do not attenuate signal content," "fc must be below fs/2 — all content above Nyquist must be attenuated before ADC," "Transition band determines filter order — steeper rolloff requires higher-order filter." A note reads: "There is always a tradeoff: narrower

rmines filter order — steeper rolloff requires higher-order filter." A note reads: "There is always a tradeoff: narrower transition band = higher filter order = more phase distortion.") Talking Point: The anti-aliasing filter's job is precise: it must attenuate all frequency content above the Nyquist frequency sufficiently that no alias can contaminate the measurement, while passing all signal content of interest without attenuation. The cutoff frequency sits in the transition zone between "signal content I want" and "content that would alias." This means the cutoff must be above the highest frequency of interest — otherwise you are filtering out signal — and it must be below the Nyquist frequency — otherwise you are not completing the anti-aliasing job. The width of the transition band

e below the Nyquist frequency — otherwise you are not completing the anti-aliasing job. The width of the transition band is controlled by the filter order: a higher-order filter has a steeper rolloff, which gives you more separation between the passband and the stopband for a given sampling rate. But there is a tradeoff: higher-order filters introduce more phase distortion in the passband, which can affect the timing relationships in the signal. This is not a deep filter design course — but you need to be able to read a filter specification and understand whether the cutoff is in the right place relative to your signal bandwidth and sample rate. slide 6: Analog vs. Digital Filtering — The Critical Distinction (Image: A two-column comparison table. Column header left: "Analog filtering

igital Filtering — The Critical Distinction (Image: A two-column comparison table. Column header left: "Analog filtering (pre-ADC)." Column header right: "Digital filtering (post-ADC)." Row 1: "When it happens" | "Before digitization — in the signal conditioning chain" | "After digitization — in MATLAB or DSP hardware." Row 2 [BOLD/HIGHLIGHTED]: "Can prevent aliasing?" | "YES — that is its primary purpose" | "NO — aliased content is already mixed into the digital data; it cannot be separated." Row 3: "Introduces phase delay?" | "Yes, order-dependent" | "Yes, filter-type-dependent; can be zero-phase with filtfilt in MATLAB." Row 4: "Can introduce artifacts?" | "Rarely, if properly designed" | "Yes — ringing near sharp transitions, edge effects, passband ripple." Row 5: "Primary use" |

y, if properly designed" | "Yes — ringing near sharp transitions, edge effects, passband ripple." Row 5: "Primary use" | "Anti-aliasing before ADC; noise rejection" | "Post-processing: smoothing, band selection, noise removal from already-captured signal." A bold annotation at the bottom reads: "Anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain. This is not a preference — it is a physical requirement." The 'Can prevent aliasing?' row is visually emphasized — bold text or highlighted background — to distinguish it from the other rows.) Talking Point: You do not need to design filters — you need to know which domain handles which problem. The single most important row in this table is the one about aliasing prevention. I want to be direct about the most important practical implication of this

s table is the one about aliasing prevention. I want to be direct about the most important practical implication of this table. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain, before the analog-to-digital converter, because once a signal has been aliased into the digital record, no digital filter can separate it from the legitimate signal content at the same frequency. Digital filters — including MATLAB's filtfilt and butter functions — are powerful post-processing tools, and they have real uses: smoothing noisy data, extracting a specific frequency band from a captured record, or removing a known noise frequency. But they are applied to data that has already been digitized, which means they cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Additionally, digital filters introduce their

dy been digitized, which means they cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Additionally, digital filters introduce their own artifacts: zero-phase filters like filtfilt perform the filtering twice in opposite directions, which removes phase delay but doubles the transient effects at the edges of the data record. Finite impulse response filters can introduce ringing near sharp transitions. You do not need to design filters from scratch in this course. You do need to know that the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision, and it must be documented in any credible experimental report. slide 7: Documentation Requirements — Why Signal Conditioning Is a Required Report Section (Image: A structured documentation template showing required

ents — Why Signal Conditioning Is a Required Report Section (Image: A structured documentation template showing required entries for a DAQ configuration justification section. Six rows: Row 1 "Sample rate selected" — value and Nyquist frequency derived. Row 2 "Signal bandwidth estimate" — how estimated (from model, from literature, from preliminary measurement). Row 3 "Anti-aliasing filter" — type, cutoff frequency, order, vendor and model if applicable. Row 4 "Gain setting" — value and basis (ADC input range vs. expected sensor output range). Row 5 "Noise sources identified" — at least two named, estimated magnitude, mitigation applied. Row 6 "Known limitations" — any configuration choice that could affect data quality, with rationale for why it was accepted. The template is formatted as

onfiguration choice that could affect data quality, with rationale for why it was accepted. The template is formatted as a simple table with a header "DAQ Configuration Justification — [Experiment Name]" and a date field. The visual communicates that this is a formal required document, not a checkbox.) Talking Point: Every one of the choices we discussed today — sample rate, gain, filter cutoff, filter order, grounding configuration — is a required documentation item in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report. Not because documentation is bureaucratic, but because these choices affect whether your data is trustworthy, and a reader who wants to evaluate your validation claim needs to know that you made these choices deliberately and can explain them. The DAQ

s to evaluate your validation claim needs to know that you made these choices deliberately and can explain them. The DAQ configuration section is not a settings list — it is an argument. You are arguing that the choices you made are appropriate for the signal you are trying to capture, given the bandwidth of the physical phenomenon, the Nyquist criterion, the range of the sensor output, and the noise environment of the measurement. If you cannot justify each choice, you cannot defend your data, and you cannot make a credible validation claim. This documentation habit is exactly what distinguishes a well-run experimental campaign from a black-box measurement. slide 8: Worked Example — Evaluating a Sensor's Signal Conditioning Setup (Image: A one-page instrument specification excerpt mockup

ked Example — Evaluating a Sensor's Signal Conditioning Setup (Image: A one-page instrument specification excerpt mockup showing four key parameters: "Sensor output range: 0 to 50 mV (full scale)," "Noise floor: 5 μV RMS," "Recommended gain: 100 (amplified output: 0 to 5 V)," "Anti-aliasing filter: 2nd-order Butterworth, −3 dB at 1 kHz (−3 dB means the filter has reduced the signal amplitude to approximately 71% of its original value at this frequency)." Below the spec excerpt, a MATLAB figure shows the frequency-domain FFT of the amplified sensor output, with a spectral peak at 240 Hz labeled "Primary signal component" and a flat noise floor. A vertical dashed line at 1000 Hz is labeled "Filter cutoff," and a vertical dashed line at 500 Hz (half the cutoff, assuming a 1000 Hz sample

at 1000 Hz is labeled "Filter cutoff," and a vertical dashed line at 500 Hz (half the cutoff, assuming a 1000 Hz sample rate) is labeled "Nyquist frequency." An annotation reads: "Signal bandwidth (240 Hz) << Nyquist (500 Hz) — adequate margin. But: filter cutoff (1000 Hz) is above Nyquist (500 Hz) — the filter is NOT providing anti-aliasing protection at this sample rate." The problem is highlighted in red.) Talking Point: Let me walk through a real evaluation using actual instrument specifications. The sensor has a full-scale output range of 0 to 50 millivolts, a noise floor of 5 microvolts RMS, a recommended gain of 100 — which brings the output to 0 to 5 volts, matching a standard ADC input range — and a built-in second-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter with a negative-3-dB

matching a standard ADC input range — and a built-in second-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter with a negative-3-dB cutoff at 1 kilohertz. If you are sampling at 1000 hertz, the Nyquist frequency is 500 hertz. The filter cutoff is at 1 kilohertz — which is above the Nyquist frequency. That means the anti-aliasing filter is not actually providing aliasing protection at this sample rate. Any content between 500 and 1000 hertz would alias into the measurement, and the filter is not attenuating it. The fix is straightforward: either increase the sample rate to at least 2 kilohertz so the Nyquist frequency is above the filter cutoff, or add a supplementary analog filter with a lower cutoff. This is exactly the kind of configuration evaluation you will do in the Week 7 lab — reading the

er with a lower cutoff. This is exactly the kind of configuration evaluation you will do in the Week 7 lab — reading the instrument documentation, checking whether the configuration is self-consistent, and identifying where it is not. slide 9: Connecting to the Improvement Proposal — What You Are Looking For (Image: A two-column illustration. Left column header: "What to look for in the existing configuration." Three items listed: "Is the sample rate high enough given the signal bandwidth?" | "Is the anti-aliasing filter cutoff appropriate?" | "Are there identifiable noise sources that could be reduced?" Right column header: "How to frame the improvement proposal." Three items listed: "Propose a specific sample rate with Nyquist margin calculation." | "Propose a specific filter cutoff and

items listed: "Propose a specific sample rate with Nyquist margin calculation." | "Propose a specific filter cutoff and order with justification." | "Identify specific noise mitigation strategies (shielded cable, differential measurement, single-point ground)." A connecting arrow between the two columns is labeled "The improvement proposal is grounded in this analysis — not a generic 'use better equipment' suggestion." At the bottom: "Improvement proposal category (b): DAQ/signal conditioning — this is the CLO 4 graded deliverable in the Tier 2 report.") Talking Point: Section 6 of your Tier 2 group report asks you to propose improvements to the experiment, and category (b) is specifically DAQ and signal conditioning. This is the CLO 4 graded deliverable of E2, and the standard is higher

(b) is specifically DAQ and signal conditioning. This is the CLO 4 graded deliverable of E2, and the standard is higher than "use a better sensor." A credible improvement proposal is grounded in your data and your analysis. If you found from the FFT that the signal has significant energy at frequencies above the current Nyquist frequency, you propose a specific increased sample rate with the Nyquist margin calculation shown. If you found that the anti-aliasing filter cutoff is poorly positioned relative to the sample rate, you propose a specific alternative cutoff with the filter order and the rationale. If you identified a ground loop in the noise floor, you propose differential measurement or cable shielding with an explanation of why the current configuration produces the noise. The

differential measurement or cable shielding with an explanation of why the current configuration produces the noise. The improvement proposal is where the theory you learned this week gets applied to the specific experiment you ran. Here is what a fully quantified improvement argument looks like: the FFT reveals a structural mode at 380 Hz. Current sample rate is 500 Hz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 250 Hz — below the 380 Hz mode. The 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 − 380 = 120 Hz and appears as a false spectral peak. Proposed improvement: increase sample rate to 2 kHz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 1 kHz — adequate margin above a potential 780 Hz secondary mode. That is the level of quantification the rubric awards for improvement proposal excellence. slide 10: The Signal Conditioning

is the level of quantification the rubric awards for improvement proposal excellence. slide 10: The Signal Conditioning Choices Are Modeling Decisions (Image: A diagram showing the concept of signal conditioning as a modeling commitment. The center of the diagram has a bold statement: "Every signal conditioning choice encodes an assumption about the signal." Three examples branch from the center: "Sample rate choice" → "Assumes: no meaningful signal energy above fs/2." "Filter cutoff choice" → "Assumes: no signal content between fc and fs/2 that matters for the intended use." "Gain choice" → "Assumes: maximum signal amplitude will not exceed ADC input range." Each assumption has a consequence box attached: "Violated sample rate assumption → aliasing." "Violated filter cutoff assumption →

ption has a consequence box attached: "Violated sample rate assumption → aliasing." "Violated filter cutoff assumption → attenuated signal content OR aliased content not removed." "Violated gain assumption → clipping — signal is permanently distorted." The visual style uses a spider diagram or concept map layout, with the central statement in a large gold circle and the branches radiating outward.) Talking Point: I want to close this lecture with the unifying frame. Every signal conditioning choice you make is a modeling decision — it encodes an assumption about the signal you are trying to capture. Choosing a sample rate of 2 kilohertz means you are claiming the signal has no meaningful energy above 1 kilohertz. Choosing an anti-aliasing filter cutoff of 800 hertz means you are claiming

has no meaningful energy above 1 kilohertz. Choosing an anti-aliasing filter cutoff of 800 hertz means you are claiming that any signal content between 800 hertz and 1 kilohertz can be discarded without affecting your measurement quality for the intended use. Choosing a gain of 100 means you are claiming the maximum signal amplitude will not exceed 5 volts after amplification. Each of these assumptions can be wrong, and when they are, the consequences range from reduced precision to completely corrupted data — and the corrupted data will not always be obviously wrong. The only protection is making these decisions based on analysis of the expected signal, documenting the reasoning, and being prepared to defend each choice when your measurement is scrutinized. nting the reasoning, and being prepared to defend each choice when your measurement is scrutinized. That is what makes a measurement credible, and that is what the DAQ configuration section of your report is for. slide 11: Closure Signal conditioning is not a black box that happens before your MATLAB workspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the physical signal you are trying to capture. Amplification matches sensor output to ADC dynamic range and controls quantization error contribution. tent cannot be removed after digitization. Noise enters the chain at every stage — from the sensor, from the cable, from the amplifier, from the ground configuration — and identifying and documenting known noise sources is a required part of your experimental report. pages.title: Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement
pages.text: E2 Week 2 (Course Week 7) Reading Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement Intuition-building pre-class read for W7's two lectures. Approaches Nyquist and aliasing from a physical intuition angle: what does it actually mean for a signal to be "undersampled," and why does the resulting artifact look like a real signal rather than obvious noise? Uses the stroboscopic wheel and helicopter blade visual analogies to make aliasing concrete before any mathematics appears. Covers signal bandwidth as a property of the physical phenomenon (not the sensor), and frames the sampling rate decision as a modeling commitment — you are claiming, before you sample, that your signal has no content above half your sampling rate. If that claim is wrong, the data is silently corrupted. The reading stops before filter design and DAQ configuration — those are covered in the companion reading (R2) and lectures. Its job is to ensure students arrive at the W7 lectures already understanding *why* these choices matter, so lecture time can address *how* to make them defensibly. Apply the Nyquist sampling theorem to identify the minimum sampling rate required for a given signal bandwidth, and predict the consequences of aliasing when the theorem is violated — CLO 4 Explain the purpose and design rationale of an anti-aliasing filter, and identify appropriate filter cutoff frequency relative to signal bandwidth and sampling rate — CLO 4 Identify and describe at least three common DAQ configuration choices — channel configuration, resolution, sample rate — and articulate the tradeoff each choice imposes — CLO 4 Visualize a measured signal in both time and frequency domain using MATLAB and identify signal bandwidth from the frequency-domain representation — CLO 4 Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement Why the fastest thing you can't see can still destroy your data Watch a helicopter's rotor blades in a film and you may see them appear to rotate backward or hover perfectly still at full throttle. Nothing is wrong with the blades. Something is wrong with the camera. A film camera captures still frames at a fixed rate, typically 24 per second. If the blades complete almost exactly one full rotation between frames, they appear stationary in the footage. If they complete slightly more than one full rotation, they appear to spin backward. The blades are moving at full speed. The camera samples the scene too slowly relative to the rotation rate, so it produces an artifact that looks like real motion. This optical illusion is more than a curiosity. It is a precise physical analogy for one of the most consequential pitfalls in digital data acquisition: aliasing . Every digital measurement system works the same way the film camera does: every data acquisition (DAQ) board, every microcontroller analog input, every data logger. It takes samples of a continuous physical signal at discrete moments in time. Between samples, it knows nothing. The physical signal could be doing anything, and the measurement system will never know. The theorem formalizes the helicopter illusion. The same artifact appears in accelerometer data from a vibration test when the sample rate is too low. A 380 Hz structural mode can appear in your data file as a 120 Hz signal, with nothing in the data to tell you it is wrong. The formal statement is the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem : to faithfully capture a signal with frequency content up to some maximum frequency f_max , you must sample at a rate of at least 2f_max . This threshold, twice the highest frequency in the signal, is called the Nyquist rate . Sample faster than this and you can reconstruct the original signal. Sample slower and you cannot. What happens when you sample slower than the Nyquist rate? The same thing that happened to the helicopter blades. High-frequency content in the signal does not disappear. It folds back into the measured spectrum and appears as a false low-frequency signal. A vibration at 500 Hz, sampled at 600 samples per second, will appear in your data as a 100 Hz oscillation. That 100 Hz component is not in the physical system. It is an artifact of sampling, and it looks exactly like a real signal. There is no asterisk next to it, no error flag, no warning in the data file. It is a false signal wearing the costume of truth. *This image lets you watch a high-frequency signal turn into a believable but false low-frequency story, which is exactly why aliasing is so dangerous in practice. Here is the part that trips up experienced engineers: signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring, not a property of your sensor or DAQ system. If you are measuring acoustic vibrations in a rocket nozzle, the signal bandwidth comes from the dynamics of the combustion process. It does not come from your microphone, your DAQ board, or whatever sampling rate you inherited from a previous project. Before you choose a sampling rate, ask this question: what is the highest frequency my signal of interest actually contains? That sounds simple. In practice, it requires real knowledge of the physics. A structural vibration test on a beam might have fundamental modes below 100 Hz and no relevant content above 1 kHz. A 2,500 samples-per-second rate would be conservative and appropriate. A combustion pressure transient in a rocket engine test might contain critical content up to 50 kHz. That same 2,500 samples-per-second rate would alias catastrophically, producing clean-looking data that is entirely fictitious. When you commit to a sampling rate, you are making a claim. You are stating, before you collect data, that your signal contains no frequency content above half your chosen sampling rate. If that claim is wrong, and the signal has higher-frequency energy you did not anticipate, the data is silently corrupted. No error message. No obvious artifact. Just a dataset that reflects a physical reality that does not exist. Anti-aliasing filters enforce this claim in hardware. They attenuate signal content above the Nyquist frequency before the signal reaches the analog-to-digital converter, which prevents high-frequency content from folding into the measured spectrum. These filters are not optional safeguards. They are the mechanism that makes the Nyquist guarantee meaningful. Their design, especially where to set the cutoff frequency relative to the signal bandwidth and the sampling rate, is one of the topics the W7 lectures will address directly. *This image turns the sampling rule into a design picture so you can reason about filter placement and Nyquist protection before touching hardware. This reading stops here, before filter design, DAQ configuration choices, and MATLAB analysis. Those topics are covered in the companion reading (R2) and in the Week 7 lectures. This reading has a narrower job: make sure you understand why these choices matter before you walk into class. Sampling rate is not a technical setting you accept as a lab default. It is a modeling commitment. It encodes assumptions about the physical world. If those assumptions are wrong, the result is data that looks real and is wrong. When your Tier 2 canned experiment uses a specific sampling rate, you should be able to defend that choice on physical grounds and identify what you would miss if the rate were too low. The Takeaway: Aliasing is not an obscure artifact of bad equipment. It is a fundamental consequence of digital sampling, and the data itself will not reveal it. The only defense is understanding the physics of your signal well enough to make the Nyquist commitment confidently before you collect the first data point.

pages.title: DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference
pages.text: E2 Week 2 (Course Week 7) Reading DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference Reference-style companion to the W7 signal conditioning lecture, designed to be read alongside or immediately after class. Covers the DAQ configuration choices that experimentalists must document and justify: single-ended vs. differential channel configuration (ground loop implications), resolution (quantization error and its contribution to uncertainty), sample rate selection (Nyquist argument plus margin), triggering and synchronization, and practical setup documentation. The central message is that DAQ configuration is a written, justifiable design decision — not a lab default to accept without inspection. Students are expected to reference this reading when completing the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. **Bit rollover example:** Includes a concrete walkthrough of a bit-rollover or counter-overflow artifact that produces a plausible-looking oscillatory signal in the recorded data — where only uncertainty bounds and physical reasoning allow the artifact to be identified as non-physical. This is the key illustration for why DAQ configuration choices introduce their own error sources and why uncertainty analysis is the mechanism for distinguishing physical behavior from instrumentation artifacts. Unlike R1, this reading is reference-dense rather than narrative — students should expect to return to it when drafting their report. Apply the Nyquist sampling theorem to identify the minimum sampling rate required for a given signal bandwidth, and predict the consequences of aliasing when the theorem is violated — CLO 4 Explain the purpose and design rationale of an anti-aliasing filter, and identify appropriate filter cutoff frequency relative to signal bandwidth and sampling rate — CLO 4 Identify and describe at least three common DAQ configuration choices — channel configuration, resolution, sample rate — and articulate the tradeoff each choice imposes — CLO 4 Visualize a measured signal in both time and frequency domain using MATLAB and identify signal bandwidth from the frequency-domain representation — CLO 4 DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference Every configuration choice is a claim about your signal. Treat it that way. A data acquisition system handed to you with factory defaults is not a neutral tool. It is a collection of someone else's assumptions about what you are measuring. Quick-Reference: DAQ Configuration Decisions Choice Key Question Decision Rule Channel configuration Is the signal source floating or subject to ground loop noise? Default differential; use single-ended only for large signals with shared ground and short cables Input range What is the expected signal amplitude? Set to the smallest range that comfortably brackets the signal; do not accept ±10 V defaults for millivolt signals Sample rate What is the signal bandwidth, and what anti-aliasing margin is required? Sample at 5× to 10× the signal bandwidth; document the Nyquist argument explicitly Anti-aliasing filter cutoff What is the transition band of the filter relative to the chosen sample rate? Set cutoff at ≤ 0.4 × sample rate; document relationship to signal bandwidth Triggering Does the event onset need to be captured, or is the signal slowly varying? Hardware trigger for transient events; software trigger acceptable for quasi-static signals Understanding those assumptions is not optional bookkeeping. Channel configuration, resolution, sample rate, and triggering all constrain what the system can represent faithfully. Each constraint adds its own error source to the measurement. This reading is a reference document. Use it when setting up your Tier 2 experiment and again when drafting the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. Unlike the companion reading (R1), which builds physical intuition for aliasing and sampling, this reading is deliberately dense. It is meant to be consulted, not just read once. Channel Configuration: Single-Ended vs. Differential Most DAQ systems offer two channel configuration modes. The difference matters whenever noise or ground potential is a concern. Single-ended configuration measures voltage between the signal line and a shared system ground. It uses twice as many channels for a given number of signals compared to differential, which makes it attractive when channel count is limited. The vulnerability is ground-referencing error. Any noise voltage that appears between the sensor's ground reference and the DAQ system's ground reference gets measured as signal. In environments with multiple power supplies, motors, or long cable runs, this error, called a ground loop , can swamp a low-level sensor signal. Differential configuration measures voltage between two dedicated signal lines. It rejects any voltage that appears equally on both, which is called common-mode rejection . Differential inputs are the right choice when your signal source is floating, or when the signal amplitude is small enough that ground loop noise is a significant fraction of the signal. A floating signal source has no connection to the local measurement ground. Battery-powered sensors and isolated transducers are common examples, and their reference point may drift relative to the DAQ ground. Most precision measurement applications use differential configuration for this reason, including strain gauges, thermocouples, and load cells. The practical decision: single-ended is acceptable when the signal is large (volts, not millivolts), cable runs are short, and all equipment shares a common ground. In any other case, default to differential. Resolution: Quantization Error and Its Contribution to Uncertainty Every analog-to-digital converter ( ADC ) maps a continuous voltage range onto a finite number of discrete digital levels. A 12-bit ADC divides its input range into 2¹² = 4,096 discrete levels. A 16-bit ADC divides the same range into 65,536 levels. The voltage difference between adjacent levels is the quantization step size , also called the least significant bit (LSB) voltage. For a ±10 V input range on a 12-bit ADC, the quantization step size is 20 V / 4,096 ≈ 4.9 mV. That is the smallest voltage change the system can detect. Any signal change smaller than one LSB is invisible. It either rounds up to the next level or rounds down. The resulting quantization error contributes to the total measurement uncertainty. For most well-designed experiments, other error sources dominate quantization error, including sensor noise, thermal drift, and amplifier noise. But there are important exceptions. If you are measuring a slowly varying, low-amplitude signal and your input range is far wider than the signal amplitude, the effective resolution shrinks dramatically. Examples include structural creep, thermal expansion, and very low-level acoustic vibration. A signal that occupies only 1% of the ADC input range is being measured with roughly 100 times worse effective resolution than the ADC specification implies. The practical decision: set the input range to the smallest value that comfortably brackets your expected signal. Do not accept ±10 V defaults when your signal is ±100 mV. *This image shows that DAQ resolution is not only a datasheet number; your range choice directly decides how much of that resolution the signal actually gets to use. Sample Rate: Nyquist Argument Plus Engineering Margin The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem (covered in R1) establishes a lower bound: sample at least twice the highest frequency in your signal. In practice, that lower bound is a starting point, not a design target. A signal sampled at exactly twice its highest frequency requires a perfect, infinitely sharp anti-aliasing filter. That filter does not exist in hardware. Real anti-aliasing filters have a transition band, a frequency range where attenuation rolls off gradually from passband to stopband. To accommodate this transition band, the practical rule is to sample at a rate 5× to 10× the signal bandwidth , not 2×. This margin relaxes the filter requirements and provides buffer against uncertainty in the exact upper frequency of the signal. When documenting your sample rate choice in the Tier 2 report, include four pieces. First, give the estimated signal bandwidth, based on physical reasoning rather than the data. Second, state the anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency. Third, give the chosen sampling rate and its margin over the Nyquist minimum. Fourth, note any known limitations if the margin is tight. Triggering and Synchronization Many aerospace experiments involve transient events, such as a structure loaded to failure, a motor spun up to operating speed, or a thermal cycle initiated. In these cases, data collection must be synchronized to the event. Software triggering starts acquisition when a command is issued. The delay between the command and the first sample is on the order of milliseconds. That is acceptable for slow-varying signals and problematic for transient events. Hardware triggering starts acquisition when an analog voltage crosses a threshold or a digital line goes high, with sub-microsecond response. Any experiment where data-collection timing matters relative to the physical event requires hardware triggering. Pre-trigger buffers allow the DAQ system to capture data that occurred before the trigger event. The system records continuously, and the trigger selects the retention window. This is essential for capturing the onset of a transient before the threshold condition is detected. For example, suppose you want to capture the onset of a structural impact. Hardware triggering on a load cell threshold ensures data collection starts the moment the load exceeds a set value, not a few milliseconds later when the computer catches up. For slowly varying signals, such as thermal measurements and quasi-static loading, software triggering is usually sufficient. A Worked Example: The Bit-Rollover Artifact This example requires knowing that some sensors count events as unsigned integers (always positive, values 0 to 65,535) while many DAQ systems record data as signed integers (positive and negative). When a sensor wraps from its maximum count back to zero, a signed integer recording sees a large negative jump. Consider a counter-based displacement sensor that outputs a count value from 0 to 65,535 (a 16-bit unsigned integer) representing a full rotation. The sensor counts up as the shaft rotates in one direction. If the shaft reverses just past the 65,535 count, the next reading is 0, a rollover. When a DAQ system stores that output as a signed 16-bit integer, the jump from 65,535 to 0 appears in the data as a sudden, large negative-going step. In a time-series plot, this step looks like a real physical event: a rapid reversal, a discontinuity, an impulse. It is not. It is a sensor counter rollover recorded by a DAQ system that was not configured to handle unsigned output. Only knowledge of the sensor's output format and awareness of the DAQ configuration reveals the artifact. Uncertainty analysis cannot identify this type of artifact because it looks like a physical reading within sensor range. The defense is documentation: read the DAQ configuration against the sensor datasheet before collecting data, not after. *This image helps you see how a data-system artifact can masquerade as a physical event if you do not understand how the sensor and acquisition chain encode the signal. Documentation Requirement Every important DAQ setting used in your Tier 2 experiment must be documented in your report. In this course, many settings are provided by the experiment rather than chosen by your group. Your job is to record the provided configuration, explain what each setting controls, and assess whether it is sufficient or insufficient for the signal and intended use. If a setting is inadequate or leaves a tight margin, identify what change you would recommend and why. Choice What to document Channel configuration Provided mode; what it implies for ground reference and noise risk Input range Provided value; whether it brackets the expected signal and what limitation results if it does not Sample rate Provided value; Nyquist argument; margin above minimum or limitation if margin is tight Anti-aliasing filter cutoff Provided value; relationship to signal bandwidth and sample rate Triggering Provided type; whether the timing method is adequate for the event Resolution ADC bit depth; quantization step size; assessment of whether it limits the measurement A DAQ configuration section that lists settings without evaluating them does not show that the measurement was understood. The goal is to show that you know what each setting controls, why it matters physically, and whether the provided setup is adequate for the experiment. The Takeaway: DAQ configuration is not a setup task to complete before the real work begins. It is part of measurement design, and every undocumented default is an undocumented assumption about your signal that may or may not be valid for your experiment.

When a DAQ system stores that output as a signed 16-bit integer, the jump from 65,535 to 0 appears in the data as a sudden, large negative-going step. In a time-series plot, this step looks like a real physical event: a rapid reversal, a

udden, large negative-going step. In a time-series plot, this step looks like a real physical event: a rapid reversal, a discontinuity, an impulse. It is not. It is a sensor counter rollover recorded by a DAQ system that was not configured to handle unsigned output. Only knowledge of the sensor's output format and awareness of the DAQ configuration reveals the artifact. Uncertainty analysis cannot identify this type of artifact because it looks like a physical reading within sensor range. The defense is documentation: read the DAQ configuration against the sensor datasheet before collecting data, not after. *This image helps you see how a data-system artifact can masquerade as a physical event if you do not understand how the sensor and acquisition chain encode the signal. Documentation

erade as a physical event if you do not understand how the sensor and acquisition chain encode the signal. Documentation Requirement Every important DAQ setting used in your Tier 2 experiment must be documented in your report. In this course, many settings are provided by the experiment rather than chosen by your group. Your job is to record the provided configuration, explain what each setting controls, and assess whether it is sufficient or insufficient for the signal and intended use. If a setting is inadequate or leaves a tight margin, identify what change you would recommend and why. Choice What to document Channel configuration Provided mode; what it implies for ground reference and noise risk Input range Provided value; whether it brackets the expected signal and what limitation

ground reference and noise risk Input range Provided value; whether it brackets the expected signal and what limitation results if it does not Sample rate Provided value; Nyquist argument; margin above minimum or limitation if margin is tight Anti-aliasing filter cutoff Provided value; relationship to signal bandwidth and sample rate Triggering Provided type; whether the timing method is adequate for the event Resolution ADC bit depth; quantization step size; assessment of whether it limits the measurement A DAQ configuration section that lists settings without evaluating them does not show that the measurement was understood. The goal is to show that you know what each setting controls, why it matters physically, and whether the provided setup is adequate for the experiment. The

hat each setting controls, why it matters physically, and whether the provided setup is adequate for the experiment. The Takeaway: DAQ configuration is not a setup task to complete before the real work begins. It is part of measurement design, and every undocumented default is an undocumented assumption about your signal that may or may not be valid for your experiment.

pages.title: E2 Week 8: Model Adequacy, Discrepancy & Calibration Revisited
pages.text: E2 Week 8: Model Adequacy, Discrepancy & Calibration Revisited Phase I taught students to ask whether model and experiment agree. Phase II asks a harder question: when they disagree, what does that tell you about the model? Week 8 introduces model-form error, sensitivity to boundary condition assumptions, and the distinction between discrepancy as failure and discrepancy as diagnostic information. Students learn to interpret residuals structurally — not as noise to explain aw... Interpret model-experiment discrepancy as diagnostic information about model-form limitations rather than experimental failure — CLO 3 Distinguish between parametric uncertainty (imprecise knowledge of model parameters), model-form error (structural inadequacy of the model), and measurement error — CLO 2, CLO 3 Inspect residuals structurally — identifying systematic trends vs. random scatter — and articulate what each pattern implies about model adequacy — CLO 3 Perform a calibration status review with reference to instrument documentation and distinguish calibration activities from validation activities — CLO 1, CLO 3 Use experimental results to propose specific improvements to a predictive model — identifying what model parameters or assumptions the data reveals are inaccurate or incomplete — CLO 3 Begin the Tier 2 group report analysis sections using the released Canned Experiment 1 data — CLO 5 Model-Form Error & Discrepancy as Diagnostic Information Establishes the hardest conceptual shift of E2: discrepancy between model and experiment is not failure to be explained away — it is data about the model. Covers parametric uncertainty (you know the form is right but the parameters are imprecise), model-form error (the functional form itself is inadequate), and boundary condition realism (assumptions about system boundaries or inputs that were wrong). Students work through examples where systematic trends in residuals reveal which type of inadequacy is present. The framing: a model that disagrees with data in a structured way is more informative than one that agrees approximately everywhere. Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Revisits calibration at a depth beyond E1 Week 3's conceptual introduction. Covers calibration as a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget: input reference uncertainty, fit uncertainty (regression error), and residual uncertainty after correction. Students see that calibration uncertainty propagates into the final measurement outcome and must be documented in any credible experimental report. This lecture also introduces the calibration status review — checking whether instruments are within their calibration interval and what the practical consequence is if they are not. This maps directly to Section 3C of the Tier 2 group report. The calibration-vs-validation distinction from E1 Week 3 is deepened: calibrating an instrument can improve measurement fidelity, but if residuals from the calibration prompt the experimentalist to re-examine model assumptions, the process has crossed from calibration into model refinement — a fundamentally different activity. **Off-the-shelf sensor verification example:** Include a concrete worked example of verifying an off-the-shelf power sensor against first-principles measurement (P=IV using multimeters and Ohm's law). Students see that "verification" of a commercial sensor is distinct from calibrating it — you are checking whether the sensor's output is consistent with first principles, not adjusting it. This example connects to the Lab 1 P=IV work and illustrates the calibration-vs-validation distinction with a real commercial sensor scenario. What Residuals Reveal — Reading Model Failure as Information Conceptual companion to W8 L1, covering the interpretive shift at the heart of E2: residuals are not noise to be explained away — they are a structured signal about model limitations. The reading develops the vocabulary for reading residuals diagnostically: random scatter (consistent with measurement uncertainty — the model form may be adequate), systematic trend (the model is missing something structural — a nonlinearity, an unmodeled coupling, a boundary condition assumption that is wrong), and bias (the model parameters are wrong even if the form is right). Uses accessible examples — not aerospace-specific — to build the interpretive reflex before students apply it to their own data in the W8 lab. Importantly, the reading frames residual analysis as an act of intellectual honesty: a model that disagrees with data in a structured way is telling you something true about the physical system, and the experimentalist's job is to listen. Closes by previewing the three-way taxonomy (parametric uncertainty vs. model-form error vs. measurement error) that the W8 lecture will formalize. Evaluating a Validation Report — A Checklist for the Engineering Critique Discussion Evaluator's-perspective reference document introduced in Week 8 to prepare students for the upcoming engineering critique of a sample validation report. Organizes the analytical framework from Weeks 6-9 into six evaluative categories: prediction commitment, residual analysis, statistical vs. engineering significance, uncertainty budget, calibration status, and improvement proposal quality. Each category includes the key evaluator questions, a primary failure mode to look for, and a secondary failure mode. Ends with a summary table contrasting inadequate vs. credible treatment in each category. Students use this as an anchor checklist when evaluating the sample report; reading it in Week 8 balances the Week 9 load and lets students start recognizing validation-report weaknesses before the discussion exercise. ASEN 3501 — Tier 2 Lab Assignment # ASEN 3501 — Tier 2 Lab Assignment ## Predict, Execute, Analyze, Improve **Segment:** E2 — Predictive Modeling & Comparative Validation **Weeks:** Course Weeks 8–11 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 4, CLO... Tier 2 Lab Assignment — Frequently Asked Questions # Tier 2 Lab Assignment — Frequently Asked Questions ## 1. Is Tier 2 an individual assignment or a group assignment? Tier 2 is a group assignment. You develop the pre-lab prediction as a group, run the experiment as a... Questions to Think Through Before Class
