pages.title: Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review
pages.text: E2 Week 3 (Course Week 8) Lecture Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Revisits calibration at a depth beyond E1 Week 3's conceptual introduction. Covers calibration as a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget: input reference uncertainty, fit uncertainty (regression error), and residual uncertainty after correction. Students see that calibration uncertainty propagates into the final measurement outcome and must be documented in any credible experimental report. This lecture also introduces the calibration status review — checking whether instruments are within their calibration interval and what the practical consequence is if they are not. This maps directly to Section 3C of the Tier 2 group report. The calibration-vs-validation distinction from E1 Week 3 is deepened: calibrating an instrument can improve measurement fidelity, but if residuals from the calibration prompt the experimentalist to re-examine model assumptions, the process has crossed from calibration into model refinement — a fundamentally different activity. **Off-the-shelf sensor verification example:** Include a concrete worked example of verifying an off-the-shelf power sensor against first-principles measurement (P=IV using multimeters and Ohm's law). Students see that "verification" of a commercial sensor is distinct from calibrating it — you are checking whether the sensor's output is consistent with first principles, not adjusting it. This example connects to the Lab 1 P=IV work and illustrates the calibration-vs-validation distinction with a real commercial sensor scenario. Interpret model-experiment discrepancy as diagnostic information about model-form limitations rather than experimental failure — CLO 3 Distinguish between parametric uncertainty (imprecise knowledge of model parameters), model-form error (structural inadequacy of the model), and measurement error — CLO 2, CLO 3 Inspect residuals structurally — identifying systematic trends vs. random scatter — and articulate what each pattern implies about model adequacy — CLO 3 Perform a calibration status review with reference to instrument documentation and distinguish calibration activities from validation activities — CLO 1, CLO 3 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Talking Point: We introduced the calibration-versus-validation distinction in Week 3 of E1, and you have been applying that distinction conceptually ever since. This lecture deepens it. Calibration is not just a concept — it is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget, and the uncertainty it introduces propagates directly into your final measurement outcome. In Phase II, every group is required to document the calibration status of their instruments and assess whether that status is adequate for the intended use of the experiment. This lecture gives you the formal tools to do that correctly. slide 2: Calibration vs. Validation — The Distinction Deepened (Image: A side-by-side two-column comparison diagram. Left column header: "Calibration." Right column header: "Validation." Row 1 — "What it is": "Calibration: a measurement activity — comparing a sensor's output to a known reference and adjusting the relationship between input and output to reduce systematic bias." | "Validation: a modeling activity — testing whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's behavior." Row 2 — "What it operates on": "Calibration: the instrument — changes the sensor's calibration coefficients or correction curve." | "Validation: the model — provides evidence about whether the model is adequate for the intended use." Row 3 — "What 'success' looks like": "Calibration: the sensor output agrees with the reference standard within the stated calibration uncertainty." | "Validation: the model prediction agrees with the experimental outcome within the acceptance criterion." Row 4 — "What a 'fail' means": "Calibration: the instrument needs adjustment or replacement." | "Validation: the model is inadequate for the intended use — revision required." The table is clean and readable with alternating row shading. A caption at the bottom: "Conflating these two activities is one of the most common sources of credibility loss in experimental reports.") Talking Point: In Week 3 of E1, I told you that calibration and validation are distinct activities and that conflating them undermines experimental credibility. Now let me explain precisely why. Calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference — it is an operation on the instrument. When you recalibrate a load cell, you are changing the relationship between force applied and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical model predicts a physical system's behavior accurately — it is an operation on the model. When you compare a structural model's predicted deflection to the measured deflection, you are testing the model. The two activities operate on different objects and serve different purposes. The common error is running an experiment, comparing the data to a model, finding agreement, and concluding the model is validated — when what actually happened is that the sensor was calibrated against the physical system, which is a measurement activity, not a modeling validation. If the same data used to calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an independent validation test. You have demonstrated that your sensor is internally consistent, and nothing more. slide 3: The Calibration Uncertainty Budget — What Calibration Adds to the Error Stack (Image: A stacked bar chart illustration showing the total measurement uncertainty budget as a stack of three components. Component 1 (bottom, gold): "Random measurement noise — estimated from repeated trials." Component 2 (middle, lighter gold): "Sensor systematic bias — corrected by calibration." Component 3 (top, orange): "Residual calibration uncertainty — what remains after calibration." Below the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the systematic bias component but introduces its own uncertainty: the calibration uncertainty propagates into the corrected measurement." To the right, a simple equation: "u_total² = u_noise² + u_calibration²" where each term is labeled. A note below: "Calibration uncertainty comes from: (1) reference standard uncertainty, (2) curve-fit residuals, (3) environmental drift since last calibration." The visual communicates that calibration is not free — it trades one source of uncertainty for another, smaller one.) Talking Point: Every calibration has its own uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three components. First, the reference standard uncertainty — the known reference used to calibrate your sensor is itself not perfectly accurate; it has a stated uncertainty that transfers directly to your calibration. Second, the curve-fit residuals — when you fit a calibration curve to the reference-versus-sensor-output data, the fit is not perfect; the residual scatter around the calibration curve represents uncertainty in the corrected sensor output at any given input. Third, environmental drift since the last calibration — sensors change over time and with environmental conditions, so a sensor that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the magnitude of that drift is uncertain. The total calibration uncertainty is the root-sum-square (RSS) of these three components — the same quadrature combination you first used in E1 Week 2 — and it adds in quadrature to the random measurement noise in your total uncertainty budget. Calibration makes measurements more accurate by reducing systematic bias, but it does not make measurements perfect — it trades a large systematic error for a smaller residual one, and that residual must be documented and propagated. slide 4: Calibration Interval and Calibration Status — What to Look For (Image: An instrument calibration certificate mockup showing five key fields: "Instrument: Load Cell LCxxx, Serial XXXXXXXX," "Last calibration date: 2025-11-10," "Calibration interval: 12 months," "Calibration due date: 2026-11-10," "Calibration uncertainty: ±0.3% full scale (k=2, 95% confidence)." Below the certificate, a status assessment panel with three conditions color-coded: Green check — "In calibration: current date is before due date." Yellow caution — "Near expiration: within 30 days of due date — use with awareness." Red X — "Out of calibration: current date is past due date — result credibility is uncertain, document prominently." A small note: "The calibration uncertainty stated on the certificate (±0.3%) propagates into your final measurement outcome whether the instrument is in calibration or not. Document both the status and the uncertainty.") Talking Point: Record the calibration date, interval, status, stated uncertainty, and coverage factor for each instrument. Convert any expanded certificate value to standard uncertainty before combination and preserve the certificate's stated coverage basis. A coverage factor of two does not by itself make the result a frequentist 95 percent confidence interval. If an instrument is out of calibration, state that limitation and assess whether the certificate can still support the intended-use claim. slide 5: Verifying an Off-the-Shelf Sensor Against First Principles — A Worked Example (Image: A workbench setup diagram for the P=IV power sensor verification example. The setup shows a commercial power sensor connected to a resistive load, alongside a standard digital multimeter measuring voltage and a second multimeter measuring current. Below the setup: a table with three rows — "Power sensor reading: 12.4 W," "First-principles: P = V × I = 12.2 W," "Discrepancy: 0.2 W (1.6%)." A propagated uncertainty column shows "Power sensor stated accuracy: ±2% full scale (±0.3 W at 12.4 W)" and "First-principles uncertainty (from Lecture E1 W2): ±0.15 W." The two results are compared: "12.4 ± 0.3 W vs. 12.2 ± 0.15 W — overlap within combined uncertainty bounds." Caption: "Verification: checking sensor output against first principles. Distinct from calibration: you are not adjusting the sensor — you are checking whether it is consistent with an independent standard.") Talking Point: Let me show you a concrete example of sensor verification using first principles — this is related to the P=IV lab you did in Week 2 of E1, and I want you to see the connection explicitly. A commercial power sensor is attached to a resistive load. You want to verify that the sensor's output is physically consistent — not to recalibrate it, but to check whether it agrees with first principles using standard instruments. You use two multimeters: one measuring voltage, one measuring current. You compute power from P equals V times I, using the same partial-derivative uncertainty propagation you applied in Lab 1. The power sensor reads 12.4 watts. The first-principles computation gives 12.2 watts. The discrepancy is 0.2 watts. The propagated uncertainties overlap — 12.4 plus-or-minus 0.3 watts and 12.2 plus-or-minus 0.15 watts. This is a verification result: you are not adjusting the sensor's calibration, you are confirming that its output is consistent with an independent physical measurement within the combined uncertainty bounds. If the results did not overlap, you would have grounds to suspect a calibration issue and seek a formal recalibration before using the sensor for a validation claim. This is the calibration-versus-validation distinction applied to a commercial sensor in a real scenario. slide 6: What the Calibration Status Means for Your Validation Claim (Image: A two-by-two decision matrix. X-axis: "Calibration status" — labeled "In calibration" (left) and "Out of calibration" (right). Y-axis: "Model-data agreement" — labeled "Agreement within bounds" (top) and "Discrepancy beyond bounds" (bottom). Four cells with interpretations: Top-left (in calibration, agreement): "Strong validation case — measurement uncertainty is characterized and agreement is established." Top-right (out of calibration, agreement): "Weak validation case — agreement may be coincidental; calibration-related bias is unknown." Bottom-left (in calibration, discrepancy): "Credible diagnostic — discrepancy is real, calibration is not the cause; investigate model." Bottom-right (out of calibration, discrepancy): "Inconclusive — cannot determine whether discrepancy is from model or from out-of-calibration bias; instrument must be recalibrated before conclusions can be drawn." Each cell has a color code: Top-left green, Top-right yellow, Bottom-left orange, Bottom-right red.) Talking Point: This two-by-two matrix captures the practical relationship between calibration status and validation credibility. The strongest validation case is the top-left cell: the instruments are in calibration, the calibration uncertainty is documented, and the model agrees with the data within the uncertainty bounds. You can make a credible claim about model adequacy because the uncertainty is bounded and characterized. The bottom-left cell is the credible diagnostic case: the instruments are in calibration, but the model disagrees with the data. Because calibration is not the issue, the discrepancy is real and you can investigate the model confidently. The weakest cases are on the right side of the matrix. If the instrument is out of calibration and the model appears to agree, you cannot determine whether the agreement is genuine or coincidental — the uncalibrated sensor may be biased in a way that makes the model look correct by accident. And if the instrument is out of calibration and there is a discrepancy, you cannot determine whether the discrepancy is from the model or from the unmeasured calibration drift. The practical implication is clear: calibration status must be verified before you run the experiment, not discovered during the analysis. slide 7: Propagating Calibration Uncertainty into the Final Result (Image: A step-by-step calculation layout. Step 1: "Certificate expanded uncertainty: U_cal = 0.12 N, k=2." Step 2: "Convert to standard uncertainty: u_cal = U_cal/k = 0.06 N." Step 3: "Repeated-trial noise: u_noise = 0.08 N (standard uncertainty)." Step 4: "u_c = sqrt(0.06² + 0.08²) = 0.10 N." Step 5: "U = 2u_c = 0.20 N; F = 23.4 ± 0.20 N (expanded uncertainty, k=2), subject to the certificate's stated coverage basis." A note: "Noise is the larger standard component: 0.08 N > 0.06 N.") Talking Point: The certificate reports an expanded calibration uncertainty of 0.12 newtons with k equals two. We must first convert it to the same standard-uncertainty basis as the repeated-trial noise: u-cal equals 0.12 divided by two, or 0.06 newtons. The noise component is already a standard uncertainty of 0.08 newtons. Root-sum-square combination gives u-c equals the square root of 0.06 squared plus 0.08 squared, exactly 0.10 newtons. If a k equals two expanded result is appropriate under the certificate's coverage basis, U equals 0.20 newtons, so the result is 23.4 plus-or-minus 0.20 newtons. Noise, at 0.08 newtons, is the larger standard component; calibration, at 0.06 newtons, is not dominant. slide 8: The Section 3C Deliverable — What the Calibration Review Requires (Image: A structured documentation checklist for the Section 3C calibration status review. Six items, each with a checkbox: "1. Instrument name and serial number — for each sensor in the experiment." "2. Last calibration date — from certificate or instrument tag." "3. Calibration interval — stated on certificate (e.g., 12 months, 6 months)." "4. Current compliance status — in calibration / out of calibration / near expiration." "5. Calibration uncertainty — stated value from certificate, with coverage factor, propagated into final measurement uncertainty." "6. Conclusion — one sentence: given the calibration status and propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the intended use of this experiment?" A note at the bottom: "If any instrument is out of calibration, state this prominently and assess the implication for the validation conclusion. Do not ignore it.") Talking Point: The calibration status review is one of six required sections of your Tier 2 group report, and this checklist is exactly what it needs to contain. For every sensor in your Tier 2 experiment, you find the calibration certificate, document the last calibration date and the calibration interval, determine whether the instrument is currently in compliance, and record the calibration uncertainty with the coverage factor. You then propagate that uncertainty into the total measurement uncertainty budget using the root-sum-square method we just walked through. And finally, you write a one-sentence conclusion: given the calibration status and the propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the experiment's intended use? That sentence is the conclusion of Section 3C, and it feeds directly into the overall validation conclusion in the group report. If an instrument is out of calibration, you do not hide that — you document it, assess what it means for the uncertainty budget, and state explicitly whether the validation claim is weakened or invalidated by the calibration status. slide 9: A Complete Section 3C — What a Passing-Grade Entry Looks Like (Image: A formatted Section 3C mockup. "Certificate: U_cal = 0.12 N, k=2; standard basis: u_cal = 0.06 N. Noise: u_noise = 0.08 N. Combined: u_c = sqrt(0.06² + 0.08²) = 0.10 N. Expanded: U = 2u_c = 0.20 N. Conclusion: instrument is in calibration; noise is the larger standard component; the k=2 expanded uncertainty is adequate for detecting discrepancies above 0.5 N, subject to the certificate's coverage basis.") Talking Point: A complete Section 3C records the certificate value and coverage factor, converts that value to standard uncertainty, combines only standard components, and then applies the desired coverage factor to the combined result. Here, U-cal is 0.12 newtons at k equals two, so u-cal is 0.06 newtons. With u-noise equal to 0.08 newtons, u-c is 0.10 newtons and the k equals two expanded uncertainty is 0.20 newtons. The conclusion identifies noise as the larger standard component and compares the final expanded uncertainty with the pre-committed adequacy threshold. slide 11: When Residuals Prompt Recalibration — Crossing from Calibration into Model Refinement (Image: A flow diagram with a branch point. Starting node: "Run experiment, compute residuals." First branch: "Residuals random and within uncertainty band" → "Model form adequate — document and proceed." Second branch: "Residuals systematic — trend present" → "Diagnose model-form error." Third branch: "Residuals show constant bias beyond uncertainty band" → Branch point: "Is the instrument in calibration?" → Yes: "Bias is real — investigate model parameters or systematic setup error." → No: "Recalibrate — then re-examine residuals with updated uncertainty." A special arrow exits the "Recalibrate" step and points back to "Run experiment" labeled: "If recalibration substantially changes the predicted value, you may have just performed model refinement disguised as measurement correction — document this explicitly." A warning icon appears on the recalibration loop with the label: "Check: are you correcting the sensor or fitting the model?") Talking Point: There is a subtle but important boundary between calibrating an instrument and refining a model, and it is possible to cross it without realizing it. When you observe a constant bias in the residuals and the instrument is out of calibration, the right first action is to recalibrate. But here is the warning: if the recalibration substantially changes the sensor's calibration coefficients in a way that happens to reduce the bias in your validation comparison — and if you discovered this by looking at the residuals rather than by comparing the sensor to an independent standard — you may have just used the experimental data to adjust the model rather than to test it. That is postdiction at the calibration level. Here is what the failure mode looks like in practice: a group finds their load cell consistently over-reads relative to the model. They "recalibrate" by adjusting the sensitivity coefficient until the readings match the model prediction. This is not calibration — it is model fitting. The correct approach: recalibrate the load cell against an independent reference force standard, then re-examine whether the residual remains. The protection is to ensure that recalibration is done against an independent reference standard, not in response to the model comparison. If you recalibrate against a reference and the residuals still show a bias, the bias is real and the model needs attention. If the recalibration resolves the bias, you have corrected a measurement error — and you need to document that the recalibration was performed against an independent standard, not in response to the data comparison. slide 12: Connecting Calibration Status to the Quad Chart Presentation (Image: A quad chart cell mockup — specifically the bottom-left cell labeled "Calibration Status." The cell contains four elements: "1. Instruments reviewed: [load cell, pressure transducer, accelerometer]." "2. Compliance: [load cell — in calibration (±0.3% full scale), pressure transducer — out of calibration since 2026-03-01, accelerometer — in calibration (±1% full scale)]." "3. Propagated calibration uncertainty: 0.29 N combined." "4. Conclusion: Load cell and accelerometer are adequate. Pressure transducer out of calibration — result uncertainty in that channel is unbounded; validation conclusion for pressure-dependent quantities is weakened." The cell is bounded by a gold frame to indicate it is one of four quad chart cells. A note below the mockup: "This cell is self-contained — it must communicate the key conclusion (adequate or not adequate) without reference to the other cells.") Talking Point: In the Week 10 quad chart presentation, one of the four cells is dedicated to the calibration status review. This is not a summary of what you found — it is a complete, self-contained argument in a small space. You list the instruments, their compliance status, the propagated calibration uncertainty, and the one-sentence conclusion about adequacy for the intended use. Notice in this example that one instrument is out of calibration — the pressure transducer, which expired in March. The conclusion does not hide this — it states explicitly that validation conclusions for pressure-dependent quantities are weakened because the calibration uncertainty is unbounded for that channel. That is the honest engineering statement, and it is the kind of statement that earns credibility from the audience and from the Q&A. Trying to hide an out-of-calibration instrument in the quad chart, or glossing over it in the report, is not just intellectually dishonest — it is the kind of error that gets caught under direct questioning, and it damages credibility much more than acknowledging the limitation directly. slide 13: Closure Calibration is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget — reference standard uncertainty, curve-fit residuals, and environmental drift since the last calibration — and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome as a root-sum-square contribution alongside random noise. Calibration status review is not a bureaucratic checkbox: it determines whether your measurement system is adequate for the intended use of the experiment, and it shapes the credibility of every validation claim you make. An out-of-calibration instrument does not invalidate your work, but it must be documented honestly and its implications for the uncertainty budget must be assessed. The distinction between calibrating an instrument — correcting its output against an independent standard — and using experimental data to fit a model remains critical here: if you discover a calibration issue by examining the residuals, the recalibration must be against an independent reference, not against the data itself. In the lab session today, you will locate the calibration documentation for the instruments in your Tier 2 experiment, complete the calibration status review, and begin the Section 3C analysis. That documentation is your evidence that you ran the measurement system correctly — and evidence is what makes validation credible. Talking Point: The certificate reports an expanded calibration uncertainty of 0.12 newtons with k equals two. We must first convert it to the same standard-uncertainty basis as the repeated-trial noise: u-cal equals 0.12 divided by two, or 0.06 newtons. The noise component is already a standard uncertainty of 0.08 newtons. Root-sum-square combination gives u-c equals the square root of 0.06 squared plus 0.08 squared, exactly 0.10 newtons. If a k equals two expanded result is appropriate under the certificate's coverage basis, U equals 0.20 newtons, so the result is 23.4 plus-or-minus 0.20 newtons. Noise, at 0.08 newtons, is the larger standard component; calibration, at 0.06 newtons, is not dominant. slide 8: The Section 3C Deliverable — What the Calibration Review Requires (Image: A structured documentation checklist for the Section 3C calibration status review. Six items, each with a checkbox: "1. Instrument name and serial number — for each sensor in the experiment." "2. Last calibration date — from certificate or instrument tag." "3. Calibration interval — stated on certificate (e.g., 12 months, 6 months)." "4. Current compliance status — in calibration / out of calibration / near expiration." "5. Calibration uncertainty — stated value from certificate, with coverage factor, propagated into final measurement uncertainty." "6. Conclusion — one sentence: given the calibration status and propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the intended use of this experiment?" A note at the bottom: "If any instrument is out of calibration, state this prominently and assess the implication for the validation conclusion. Do not ignore it.") Talking Point: The calibration status review is one of six required sections of your Tier 2 group report, and this checklist is exactly what it needs to contain. For every sensor in your Tier 2 experiment, you find the calibration certificate, document the last calibration date and the calibration interval, determine whether the instrument is currently in compliance, and record the calibration uncertainty with the coverage factor. You then propagate that uncertainty into the total measurement uncertainty budget using the root-sum-square method we just walked through. And finally, you write a one-sentence conclusion: given the calibration status and the propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the experiment's intended use? That sentence is the conclusion of Section 3C, and it feeds directly into the overall validation conclusion in the group report. If an instrument is out of calibration, you do not hide that — you document it, assess what it means for the uncertainty budget, and state explicitly whether the validation claim is weakened or invalidated by the calibration status. slide 9: A Complete Section 3C — What a Passing-Grade Entry Looks Like (Image: A formatted Section 3C mockup. "Certificate: U_cal = 0.12 N, k=2; standard basis: u_cal = 0.06 N. Noise: u_noise = 0.08 N. Combined: u_c = sqrt(0.06² + 0.08²) = 0.10 N. Expanded: U = 2u_c = 0.20 N. Conclusion: instrument is in calibration; noise is the larger standard component; the k=2 expanded uncertainty is adequate for detecting discrepancies above 0.5 N, subject to the certificate's coverage basis.") Talking Point: A complete Section 3C records the certificate value and coverage factor, converts that value to standard uncertainty, combines only standard components, and then applies the desired coverage factor to the combined result. Here, U-cal is 0.12 newtons at k equals two, so u-cal is 0.06 newtons. With u-noise equal to 0.08 newtons, u-c is 0.10 newtons and the k equals two expanded uncertainty is 0.20 newtons. The conclusion identifies noise as the larger standard component and compares the final expanded uncertainty with the pre-committed adequacy threshold. slide 11: When Residuals Prompt Recalibration — Crossing from Calibration into Model Refinement (Image: A flow diagram with a branch point. Starting node: "Run experiment, compute residuals." First branch: "Residuals random and within uncertainty band" → "Model form adequate — document and proceed." Second branch: "Residuals systematic — trend present" → "Diagnose model-form error." Third branch: "Residuals show constant bias beyond uncertainty band" → Branch point: "Is the instrument in calibration?" → Yes: "Bias is real — investigate model parameters or systematic setup error." → No: "Recalibrate — then re-examine residuals with updated uncertainty." A special arrow exits the "Recalibrate" step and points back to "Run experiment" labeled: "If recalibration substantially changes the predicted value, you may have just performed model refinement disguised as measurement correction — document this explicitly." A warning icon appears on the recalibration loop with the label: "Check: are you correcting the sensor or fitting the model?") Talking Point: There is a subtle but important boundary between calibrating an instrument and refining a model, and it is possible to cross it without realizing it. When you observe a constant bias in the residuals and the instrument is out of calibration, the right first action is to recalibrate. But here is the warning: if the recalibration substantially changes the sensor's calibration coefficients in a way that happens to reduce the bias in your validation comparison — and if you discovered this by looking at the residuals rather than by comparing the sensor to an independent standard — you may have just used the experimental data to adjust the model rather than to test it. That is postdiction at the calibration level. Here is what the failure mode looks like in practice: a group finds their load cell consistently over-reads relative to the model. They "recalibrate" by adjusting the sensitivity coefficient until the readings match the model prediction. This is not calibration — it is model fitting. The correct approach: recalibrate the load cell against an independent reference force standard, then re-examine whether the residual remains. The protection is to ensure that recalibration is done against an independent reference standard, not in response to the model comparison. If you recalibrate against a reference and the residuals still show a bias, the bias is real and the model needs attention. If the recalibration resolves the bias, you have corrected a measurement error — and you need to document that the recalibration was performed against an independent standard, not in response to the data comparison. slide 12: Connecting Calibration Status to the Quad Chart Presentation (Image: A quad chart cell mockup — specifically the bottom-left cell labeled "Calibration Status." The cell contains four elements: "1. Instruments reviewed: [load cell, pressure transducer, accelerometer]." "2. Compliance: [load cell — in calibration (±0.3% full scale), pressure transducer — out of calibration since 2026-03-01, accelerometer — in calibration (±1% full scale)]." "3. Propagated calibration uncertainty: 0.29 N combined." "4. Conclusion: Load cell and accelerometer are adequate. Pressure transducer out of calibration — result uncertainty in that channel is unbounded; validation conclusion for pressure-dependent quantities is weakened." The cell is bounded by a gold frame to indicate it is one of four quad chart cells. A note below the mockup: "This cell is self-contained — it must communicate the key conclusion (adequate or not adequate) without reference to the other cells.") Talking Point: In the Week 10 quad chart presentation, one of the four cells is dedicated to the calibration status review. This is not a summary of what you found — it is a complete, self-contained argument in a small space. You list the instruments, their compliance status, the propagated calibration uncertainty, and the one-sentence conclusion about adequacy for the intended use. Notice in this example that one instrument is out of calibration — the pressure transducer, which expired in March. The conclusion does not hide this — it states explicitly that validation conclusions for pressure-dependent quantities are weakened because the calibration uncertainty is unbounded for that channel. That is the honest engineering statement, and it is the kind of statement that earns credibility from the audience and from the Q&A. Trying to hide an out-of-calibration instrument in the quad chart, or glossing over it in the report, is not just intellectually dishonest — it is the kind of error that gets caught under direct questioning, and it damages credibility much more than acknowledging the limitation directly. slide 13: Closure Calibration is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget — reference standard uncertainty, curve-fit residuals, and environmental drift since the last calibration — and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome as a root-sum-square contribution alongside random noise. Calibration status review is not a bureaucratic checkbox: it determines whether your measurement system is adequate for the intended use of the experiment, and it shapes the credibility of every validation claim you make. An out-of-calibration instrument does not invalidate your work, but it must be documented honestly and its implications for the uncertainty budget must be assessed. The distinction between calibrating an instrument — correcting its output against an independent standard — and using experimental data to fit a model remains critical here: if you discover a calibration issue by examining the residuals, the recalibration must be against an independent reference, not against the data itself. In the lab session today, you will locate the calibration documentation for the instruments in your Tier 2 experiment, complete the calibration status review, and begin the Section 3C analysis. That documentation is your evidence that you ran the measurement system correctly — and evidence is what makes validation credible. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded entirely in the W8 L2 blueprint purpose statement: calibration as a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget, calibration status review, off-the-shelf sensor verification example, calibration vs. validation distinction deepened. - The P=IV off-the-shelf sensor verification example (slide 5) is specifically required by the blueprint, and the connection to Lab 1 is made explicit. - Slide 9 addresses the boundary between recalibration and model refinement — the "crossing from calibration into model refinement" scenario described in the blueprint. - The quad chart cell mockup in slide 10 connects the lecture material directly to the W10 deliverable. - Calibration uncertainty budget arithmetic (slide 7) is worked through numerically, consistent with the course's expectation that every uncertainty claim is quantified. - No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 1, CLO 3 primary treatment.

chunks.text: E2 Week 3 (Course Week 8) Lecture Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Revisits calibration at a depth beyond E1 Week 3's conceptual introduction. Covers calibration as a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget: input reference uncertainty, fit uncertainty (regression error), and residual uncertainty after correction. Students see that calibration uncertainty propagates into the final measurement outcome and must be documented in any credible experimental report. This lecture also introduces the calibration status review — checking whether instruments are within their calibration interval and what the practical consequence is if they are not. This maps directly to Section 3C of the Tier 2 group report. The calibration-vs-validation distinction from

hey are not. This maps directly to Section 3C of the Tier 2 group report. The calibration-vs-validation distinction from E1 Week 3 is deepened: calibrating an instrument can improve measurement fidelity, but if residuals from the calibration prompt the experimentalist to re-examine model assumptions, the process has crossed from calibration into model refinement — a fundamentally different activity. **Off-the-shelf sensor verification example:** Include a concrete worked example of verifying an off-the-shelf power sensor against first-principles measurement (P=IV using multimeters and Ohm's law). Students see that "verification" of a commercial sensor is distinct from calibrating it — you are checking whether the sensor's output is consistent with first principles, not adjusting it. This

alibrating it — you are checking whether the sensor's output is consistent with first principles, not adjusting it. This example connects to the Lab 1 P=IV work and illustrates the calibration-vs-validation distinction with a real commercial sensor scenario. Interpret model-experiment discrepancy as diagnostic information about model-form limitations rather than experimental failure — CLO 3 Distinguish between parametric uncertainty (imprecise knowledge of model parameters), model-form error (structural inadequacy of the model), and measurement error — CLO 2, CLO 3 Inspect residuals structurally — identifying systematic trends vs. random scatter — and articulate what each pattern implies about model adequacy — CLO 3 Perform a calibration status review with reference to instrument

what each pattern implies about model adequacy — CLO 3 Perform a calibration status review with reference to instrument documentation and distinguish calibration activities from validation activities — CLO 1, CLO 3 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Talking Point: We introduced the calibration-versus-validation distinction in Week 3 of E1, and you have been applying that distinction conceptually ever since. This lecture deepens it. Calibration is not just a concept — it is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget, and the uncertainty it introduces propagates directly into your final measurement outcome. In Phase II, every group is required to document the calibration status of their instruments and assess whether

outcome. In Phase II, every group is required to document the calibration status of their instruments and assess whether that status is adequate for the intended use of the experiment. This lecture gives you the formal tools to do that correctly. slide 2: Calibration vs. Validation — The Distinction Deepened (Image: A side-by-side two-column comparison diagram. Left column header: "Calibration." Right column header: "Validation." Row 1 — "What it is": "Calibration: a measurement activity — comparing a sensor's output to a known reference and adjusting the relationship between input and output to reduce systematic bias." | "Validation: a modeling activity — testing whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's behavior." Row 2 — "What it operates on":

whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's behavior." Row 2 — "What it operates on": "Calibration: the instrument — changes the sensor's calibration coefficients or correction curve." | "Validation: the model — provides evidence about whether the model is adequate for the intended use." Row 3 — "What 'success' looks like": "Calibration: the sensor output agrees with the reference standard within the stated calibration uncertainty." | "Validation: the model prediction agrees with the experimental outcome within the acceptance criterion." Row 4 — "What a 'fail' means": "Calibration: the instrument needs adjustment or replacement." | "Validation: the model is inadequate for the intended use — revision required." The table is clean and readable with

" | "Validation: the model is inadequate for the intended use — revision required." The table is clean and readable with alternating row shading. A caption at the bottom: "Conflating these two activities is one of the most common sources of credibility loss in experimental reports.") Talking Point: In Week 3 of E1, I told you that calibration and validation are distinct activities and that conflating them undermines experimental credibility. Now let me explain precisely why. Calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference — it is an operation on the instrument. When you recalibrate a load cell, you are changing the relationship between force applied and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical model predicts a physical system's

and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical model predicts a physical system's behavior accurately — it is an operation on the model. When you compare a structural model's predicted deflection to the measured deflection, you are testing the model. The two activities operate on different objects and serve different purposes. The common error is running an experiment, comparing the data to a model, finding agreement, and concluding the model is validated — when what actually happened is that the sensor was calibrated against the physical system, which is a measurement activity, not a modeling validation. If the same data used to calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an independent validation test. You have

calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an independent validation test. You have demonstrated that your sensor is internally consistent, and nothing more. slide 3: The Calibration Uncertainty Budget — What Calibration Adds to the Error Stack (Image: A stacked bar chart illustration showing the total measurement uncertainty budget as a stack of three components. Component 1 (bottom, gold): "Random measurement noise — estimated from repeated trials." Component 2 (middle, lighter gold): "Sensor systematic bias — corrected by calibration." Component 3 (top, orange): "Residual calibration uncertainty — what remains after calibration." Below the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the systematic bias component but introduces its

elow the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the systematic bias component but introduces its own uncertainty: the calibration uncertainty propagates into the corrected measurement." To the right, a simple equation: "u_total² = u_noise² + u_calibration²" where each term is labeled. A note below: "Calibration uncertainty comes from: (1) reference standard uncertainty, (2) curve-fit residuals, (3) environmental drift since last calibration." The visual communicates that calibration is not free — it trades one source of uncertainty for another, smaller one.) Talking Point: Every calibration has its own uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three components. First, the reference standard

tes into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three components. First, the reference standard uncertainty — the known reference used to calibrate your sensor is itself not perfectly accurate; it has a stated uncertainty that transfers directly to your calibration. Second, the curve-fit residuals — when you fit a calibration curve to the reference-versus-sensor-output data, the fit is not perfect; the residual scatter around the calibration curve represents uncertainty in the corrected sensor output at any given input. Third, environmental drift since the last calibration — sensors change over time and with environmental conditions, so a sensor that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the magnitude of that drift is uncertain. The total

that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the magnitude of that drift is uncertain. The total calibration uncertainty is the root-sum-square (RSS) of these three components — the same quadrature combination you first used in E1 Week 2 — and it adds in quadrature to the random measurement noise in your total uncertainty budget. Calibration makes measurements more accurate by reducing systematic bias, but it does not make measurements perfect — it trades a large systematic error for a smaller residual one, and that residual must be documented and propagated. slide 4: Calibration Interval and Calibration Status — What to Look For (Image: An instrument calibration certificate mockup showing five key fields: "Instrument: Load Cell LCxxx, Serial XXXXXXXX," "Last

instrument calibration certificate mockup showing five key fields: "Instrument: Load Cell LCxxx, Serial XXXXXXXX," "Last calibration date: 2025-11-10," "Calibration interval: 12 months," "Calibration due date: 2026-11-10," "Calibration uncertainty: ±0.3% full scale (k=2, 95% confidence)." Below the certificate, a status assessment panel with three conditions color-coded: Green check — "In calibration: current date is before due date." Yellow caution — "Near expiration: within 30 days of due date — use with awareness." Red X — "Out of calibration: current date is past due date — result credibility is uncertain, document prominently." A small note: "The calibration uncertainty stated on the certificate (±0.3%) propagates into your final measurement outcome whether the instrument is in

ncertainty stated on the certificate (±0.3%) propagates into your final measurement outcome whether the instrument is in calibration or not. Document both the status and the uncertainty.") Talking Point: Record the calibration date, interval, status, stated uncertainty, and coverage factor for each instrument. Convert any expanded certificate value to standard uncertainty before combination and preserve the certificate's stated coverage basis. A coverage factor of two does not by itself make the result a frequentist 95 percent confidence interval. If an instrument is out of calibration, state that limitation and assess whether the certificate can still support the intended-use claim. slide 5: Verifying an Off-the-Shelf Sensor Against First Principles — A Worked Example (Image: A workbench

ed-use claim. slide 5: Verifying an Off-the-Shelf Sensor Against First Principles — A Worked Example (Image: A workbench setup diagram for the P=IV power sensor verification example. The setup shows a commercial power sensor connected to a resistive load, alongside a standard digital multimeter measuring voltage and a second multimeter measuring current. Below the setup: a table with three rows — "Power sensor reading: 12.4 W," "First-principles: P = V × I = 12.2 W," "Discrepancy: 0.2 W (1.6%)." A propagated uncertainty column shows "Power sensor stated accuracy: ±2% full scale (±0.3 W at 12.4 W)" and "First-principles uncertainty (from Lecture E1 W2): ±0.15 W." The two results are compared: "12.4 ± 0.3 W vs. 12.2 ± 0.15 W — overlap within combined uncertainty bounds." Caption:

." The two results are compared: "12.4 ± 0.3 W vs. 12.2 ± 0.15 W — overlap within combined uncertainty bounds." Caption: "Verification: checking sensor output against first principles. Distinct from calibration: you are not adjusting the sensor — you are checking whether it is consistent with an independent standard.") Talking Point: Let me show you a concrete example of sensor verification using first principles — this is related to the P=IV lab you did in Week 2 of E1, and I want you to see the connection explicitly. A commercial power sensor is attached to a resistive load. You want to verify that the sensor's output is physically consistent — not to recalibrate it, but to check whether it agrees with first principles using standard instruments. You use two multimeters: one measuring

but to check whether it agrees with first principles using standard instruments. You use two multimeters: one measuring voltage, one measuring current. You compute power from P equals V times I, using the same partial-derivative uncertainty propagation you applied in Lab 1. The power sensor reads 12.4 watts. The first-principles computation gives 12.2 watts. The discrepancy is 0.2 watts. The propagated uncertainties overlap — 12.4 plus-or-minus 0.3 watts and 12.2 plus-or-minus 0.15 watts. This is a verification result: you are not adjusting the sensor's calibration, you are confirming that its output is consistent with an independent physical measurement within the combined uncertainty bounds. If the results did not overlap, you would have grounds to suspect a calibration issue and seek

bined uncertainty bounds. If the results did not overlap, you would have grounds to suspect a calibration issue and seek a formal recalibration before using the sensor for a validation claim. This is the calibration-versus-validation distinction applied to a commercial sensor in a real scenario. slide 6: What the Calibration Status Means for Your Validation Claim (Image: A two-by-two decision matrix. X-axis: "Calibration status" — labeled "In calibration" (left) and "Out of calibration" (right). Y-axis: "Model-data agreement" — labeled "Agreement within bounds" (top) and "Discrepancy beyond bounds" (bottom). Four cells with interpretations: Top-left (in calibration, agreement): "Strong validation case — measurement uncertainty is characterized and agreement is established." Top-right (out

ement): "Strong validation case — measurement uncertainty is characterized and agreement is established." Top-right (out of calibration, agreement): "Weak validation case — agreement may be coincidental; calibration-related bias is unknown." Bottom-left (in calibration, discrepancy): "Credible diagnostic — discrepancy is real, calibration is not the cause; investigate model." Bottom-right (out of calibration, discrepancy): "Inconclusive — cannot determine whether discrepancy is from model or from out-of-calibration bias; instrument must be recalibrated before conclusions can be drawn." Each cell has a color code: Top-left green, Top-right yellow, Bottom-left orange, Bottom-right red.) Talking Point: This two-by-two matrix captures the practical relationship between calibration status and

tom-right red.) Talking Point: This two-by-two matrix captures the practical relationship between calibration status and validation credibility. The strongest validation case is the top-left cell: the instruments are in calibration, the calibration uncertainty is documented, and the model agrees with the data within the uncertainty bounds. You can make a credible claim about model adequacy because the uncertainty is bounded and characterized. The bottom-left cell is the credible diagnostic case: the instruments are in calibration, but the model disagrees with the data. Because calibration is not the issue, the discrepancy is real and you can investigate the model confidently. The weakest cases are on the right side of the matrix. If the instrument is out of calibration and the model

onfidently. The weakest cases are on the right side of the matrix. If the instrument is out of calibration and the model appears to agree, you cannot determine whether the agreement is genuine or coincidental — the uncalibrated sensor may be biased in a way that makes the model look correct by accident. And if the instrument is out of calibration and there is a discrepancy, you cannot determine whether the discrepancy is from the model or from the unmeasured calibration drift. The practical implication is clear: calibration status must be verified before you run the experiment, not discovered during the analysis. slide 7: Propagating Calibration Uncertainty into the Final Result (Image: A step-by-step calculation layout. Step 1: "Certificate expanded uncertainty: U_cal = 0.12 N, k=2."

Step 5: "U = 2u_c = 0.20 N; F = 23.4 ± 0.20 N (expanded uncertainty, k=2), subject to the certificate's stated coverage basis." A note: "Noise is the larger standard component: 0.08 N > 0.06 N.")
