We also introduce regression as a tool for assessing model adequacy across a full dataset — not at a single operating point, but across all the conditions you tested. These two tools complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit, and they map directly to sections of your Tier 2 group report. slide 2: Regression — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A scatter plot with

to sections of your Tier 2 group report. slide 2: Regression — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A scatter plot with two regression fits overlaid. The x-axis is labeled "Applied load (N)" and the y-axis is labeled "Measured deflection (mm)." Scatter data points are plotted in gray. Two model fit lines are overlaid: a linear regression line labeled "Linear fit: R² = 0.97" in gold, and a physical model prediction curve labeled "Theory (Euler-Bernoulli): no free parameters" in a dashed white line. The linear regression line passes through the scatter well, but diverges slightly from the theory curve at the upper end of the range. An annotation on the linear fit: "High R² — but this is not the same as validating the physical model." An annotation on the theory curve: "Physical model

High R² — but this is not the same as validating the physical model." An annotation on the theory curve: "Physical model prediction — tested against data independently." Caption: "Regression fits a curve to data. Validation tests a physical model against data. These are different activities.") Talking Point: Regression and model validation are not the same activity, and the distinction matters. Regression fits a mathematical curve — linear, polynomial, exponential — to a dataset to describe how the output varies with the inputs. A high R-squared value tells you that the curve explains most of the variance in the data. What it does not tell you is whether the curve is the physically correct model. In this example, a linear regression achieves R-squared of 0.97 — the fit is good. But the

the physically correct model. In this example, a linear regression achieves R-squared of 0.97 — the fit is good. But the physical model for this system predicts a specific nonlinear relationship based on the governing equations. The regression line does not know about the physics — it only knows about the data in this range. If you tested outside this range, the linear regression might extrapolate poorly even though it fits well here, while the physics-based model extrapolates correctly because it captures the governing behavior. This is why regression is a diagnostic tool for model adequacy — you compare the residuals from the physical model to the residuals from a purely data-driven fit — not a validation verdict in itself. slide 3: Goodness-of-Fit Measures — What R² Does and Does Not

rely data-driven fit — not a validation verdict in itself. slide 3: Goodness-of-Fit Measures — What R² Does and Does Not Tell You (Image: Three scatter plots in a row, each showing a model fit and residual plot below it. Plot 1: "R² = 0.98, random residuals." The fit is good and residuals are scattered randomly around zero. Caption: "Both R² and residuals support model adequacy." Plot 2: "R² = 0.98, systematic trend in residuals." The fit looks good visually, but the residual plot shows a clear nonlinear trend — the model overestimates at low and high values and underestimates in the middle. Caption: "High R² but systematic residuals — model is structurally wrong. R² alone is misleading." Plot 3: "R² = 0.82, random residuals." The fit is imperfect visually but residuals are randomly

R² alone is misleading." Plot 3: "R² = 0.82, random residuals." The fit is imperfect visually but residuals are randomly scattered. Caption: "Lower R² but random residuals — model form may be correct; precision is the limitation." An overall caption at the bottom: "Always inspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual structure tells you about model form adequacy." The three plots are compact but readable, with consistent axis scales.) Talking Point: R-squared is one of the most overused and misinterpreted statistics in engineering data analysis, and I want to spend a moment on what it actually measures. R-squared tells you what fraction of the variance in the data is explained by the model — equivalently, how much of the total scatter around the mean

tion of the variance in the data is explained by the model — equivalently, how much of the total scatter around the mean is captured by the fitted curve. A high R-squared does not mean the model is physically correct. R²=0.98 can coexist with systematic residuals because R² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is positive in one region and negative in another can average out, masking the structural failure. Look at the middle panel: R-squared is 0.98, which looks excellent, but the residuals — the pattern of errors — show a clear nonlinear trend. The model is overestimating at the extremes and underestimating in the middle. That pattern tells you the model is missing a physical effect — the form is wrong even though the overall fit looks good by the

ttern tells you the model is missing a physical effect — the form is wrong even though the overall fit looks good by the R-squared metric. The right panel shows the opposite: R-squared is 0.82, which might make you nervous, but the residuals are random scatter. The model form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the lower R-squared reflects measurement precision, not model error. The lesson is to always look at the residual plot alongside R-squared. R-squared is a summary statistic; the residual structure is the diagnostic. slide 4: Returning to Monte Carlo — From E1 to E2 Complexity (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column: "E1 Week 2 — Scalar Monte Carlo." Shows the P=IV example: single output (power), two input variables (V, I), simple product

Week 2 — Scalar Monte Carlo." Shows the P=IV example: single output (power), two input variables (V, I), simple product model, independent Gaussian inputs. A histogram of the simulated power distribution is shown. Caption: "10,000 trials, two independent inputs, one nonlinear function." Right column: "E2 Week 9 — Multi-variable Monte Carlo." Shows a more complex aerospace system: three input variables (structural stiffness k, damping c, applied force F), correlated inputs (k and c are correlated in a material sense), and a nonlinear governing equation. A histogram of the simulated peak deflection distribution is shown — slightly skewed, not perfectly Gaussian. Caption: "10,000 trials, three correlated inputs, nonlinear governing equation — output distribution is non-Gaussian." An

aption: "10,000 trials, three correlated inputs, nonlinear governing equation — output distribution is non-Gaussian." An annotation between the columns: "Same conceptual tool — different level of complexity. The core MATLAB structure is identical." The visual continuity between E1 and E2 is the central message of this slide.) Talking Point: We first used Monte Carlo in Week 2 to propagate uncertainty through the power equation: P equals IV. We had two independent inputs — voltage and current — and we sampled them from Gaussian distributions based on the instrument specifications. The output distribution was nearly Gaussian because the function was nearly linear. In Week 9, we apply the same conceptual tool to a more complex aerospace system, and three things change. First, there are more

9, we apply the same conceptual tool to a more complex aerospace system, and three things change. First, there are more input variables — perhaps stiffness, damping, and applied force. Second, some inputs may be correlated — for example, if both stiffness and damping come from the same material property measurement, their uncertainties are not independent. Third, the governing equation is nonlinear — the output distribution will not be Gaussian even if all the inputs are Gaussian. The MATLAB structure is almost identical to what you did in Week 2: sample each input, evaluate the model for each sample set, collect the output distribution, compute the standard deviation and percentiles. What changes is the interpretation — the non-Gaussian output requires you to report percentiles rather

and percentiles. What changes is the interpretation — the non-Gaussian output requires you to report percentiles rather than assuming a symmetric uncertainty interval, and the correlated inputs require a joint sampling strategy rather than independent draws. slide 5: Correlated Inputs — Advanced Case (Note) (Image: A simple note card layout on the slide. Gold border around a card-shaped area with the header: "Advanced case — beyond the scope of this course." Body text: "Most groups in E2 have independent inputs and should use standard randn(N,1) sampling for each input. If two or more of your inputs come from the same source (e.g., same calibration procedure or same material batch), their uncertainties are correlated — independent sampling will underestimate worst-case outcomes. See W9

aterial batch), their uncertainties are correlated — independent sampling will underestimate worst-case outcomes. See W9 Reading 2 for the Cholesky approach and MATLAB implementation." At the bottom of the card: a small arrow icon pointing to "R2: Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case." The rest of the slide is dark background with minimal text, emphasizing this is a brief informational note rather than lecture content.) Talking Point: I want to flag one case that is beyond the scope of the standard E2 workflow — I am mentioning it here for completeness, not because most groups will need it. If two or more of your input uncertainties come from the same source — the same calibration procedure, the same material property measurement, the same batch test — those inputs are

source — the same calibration procedure, the same material property measurement, the same batch test — those inputs are correlated, and sampling them independently in Monte Carlo will underestimate the worst-case output uncertainty. The standard fix uses a Cholesky decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. This is an advanced topic. The full explanation, geometric intuition, and MATLAB code are in Reading 2 this week. If you have genuinely independent inputs — which most of you do — use the standard randn approach from the previous slide and do not worry about this. If you have reason to believe two of your inputs are correlated, read R2 before implementing your Monte Carlo. slide 6: MATLAB Implementation — Multi-Variable Monte Carlo (Image: A MATLAB code

before implementing your Monte Carlo. slide 6: MATLAB Implementation — Multi-Variable Monte Carlo (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results, styled consistently with the previous lecture's code slides. Code: "% Multi-variable Monte Carlo for peak deflection"; "% NOTE: This example assumes independent inputs (standard case)."; "% For correlated inputs, see slide 5 and W9 Reading 2."; "N = 10000; % Number of trials"; "% Input distributions (mean, std)"; "k = 1200 + 50*randn(N,1); % Stiffness [N/m]"; "c = 0.15 + 0.01*randn(N,1); % Damping ratio [-]"; "F = 25.0 + 0.5*randn(N,1); % Applied force [N]"; "% Evaluate model"; "x_peak = F ./ k .* (1 ./ sqrt(1 - c.^2)); % Approx. peak response"; "% Statistics"; "mean_x = mean(x_peak);"; "std_x = std(x_peak);"; "CI_95 = prctile(x_peak, [2.5 97.5]);

peak response"; "% Statistics"; "mean_x = mean(x_peak);"; "std_x = std(x_peak);"; "CI_95 = prctile(x_peak, [2.5 97.5]); % 95% CI from percentiles" Results panel: "mean_x = 0.02087 m" | "std_x = 0.00094 m" | "CI_95 = [0.01903, 0.02271] m." A histogram of x_peak is shown to the right, slightly right-skewed. A MATLAB-style caption: "Peak deflection: 0.0209 m, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m. Non-Gaussian distribution — reported from percentiles.") Talking Point: This is the core Monte Carlo workflow: list each uncertain input, justify a simple input model from repeat data, a calibration statement, a manufacturer coverage statement, or an explicit conservative Type B assumption; sample inputs; evaluate the model; and summarize the output with a labeled coverage interval. Gaussian is not the

ption; sample inputs; evaluate the model; and summarize the output with a labeled coverage interval. Gaussian is not the automatic model for a sensor specification. The percentile bounds shown here are a central 95 percent Monte Carlo coverage interval, not a confidence interval, and they are meaningful only under the stated input and dependence assumptions. slide 7: Comparing Monte Carlo to Partial Derivative Propagation — When They Agree and When They Do Not (Image: A comparison table with three rows. Column headers: "Method," "E1 Power example result," "E2 structural example result." Row 1 — "Partial derivative propagation": "P = 12.3 ± 0.21 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209 ± 0.00096 m (symmetric, Gaussian assumed)." Row 2 — "Monte Carlo (10,000 trials)": "P = 12.3 ± 0.19 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209,

0.00096 m (symmetric, Gaussian assumed)." Row 2 — "Monte Carlo (10,000 trials)": "P = 12.3 ± 0.19 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m (non-symmetric, from percentiles)." Row 3 — "Agreement assessment": "Agree closely — power equation nearly linear, Gaussian inputs, symmetric output" | "Agree closely in mean; CI slightly asymmetric in Monte Carlo — nonlinearity causes minor departure from Gaussian." A caption: "When the model is linear and inputs are Gaussian, both methods agree. When the model is nonlinear, Monte Carlo captures asymmetric distributions that partial derivatives cannot." A note: "When the two methods give substantially different results, the Monte Carlo result should be used — it makes fewer assumptions about the model's linearity.") Talking Point: First-order

Monte Carlo result should be used — it makes fewer assumptions about the model's linearity.") Talking Point: First-order propagation linearizes the measurement model near the operating point; it does not require the true output to be Gaussian. Monte Carlo can represent nonlinear transformations and chosen input distributions, but it is not automatically more correct. If methods differ, diagnose before selecting a result: confirm common standard-uncertainty inputs, equations and units; check nonlinearity and truncation; verify distribution and percentile choices; test sample convergence; and identify any shared error sources. Report which assumption explains the difference. Covariance is awareness-level here unless the assignment provides evidence and a supported estimate. slide 8:

ifference. Covariance is awareness-level here unless the assignment provides evidence and a supported estimate. slide 8: Residual Analysis Across a Full Dataset — Applying Regression to Validate Model Adequacy (Image: A two-panel MATLAB figure. Top panel: Scatter data plotted against the independent variable (applied load, x-axis) with three overlaid lines: (1) the physical model prediction (dashed gold), (2) a linear regression fit to the data (solid white), and (3) the ±σ uncertainty band around the physical model (shaded region). Data points are gray circles. The physical model is slightly below the data in the middle range. Bottom panel: Residual plot (model minus data) vs. the same x-axis. Residuals are shown as scatter points with the ±σ uncertainty band shown as dashed lines. The

data) vs. the same x-axis. Residuals are shown as scatter points with the ±σ uncertainty band shown as dashed lines. The residuals in the middle range are consistently positive, indicating systematic over-prediction. A diagonal trend line through the residuals is labeled "Systematic trend — possible model-form issue at mid-range conditions." Caption: "Regression is not the model. The physical model prediction and the regression line diverge at mid-range — the residual analysis reveals the discrepancy.") Talking Point: Here is how regression connects to the model adequacy question. In the top panel, you see the physical model prediction, a linear regression fit to the data, and the experimental scatter. They look similar at a glance. But the bottom panel — the residual plot of the physical

a, and the experimental scatter. They look similar at a glance. But the bottom panel — the residual plot of the physical model versus the data — shows a systematic positive trend in the middle range: the physical model consistently over-predicts the measured deflection at intermediate loads. This is a model-form error signal: the linear regression tracks the data well because it has a free slope parameter that adjusts to fit, but the physical model is constrained by its governing equation and cannot adjust. The systematic residual tells you the physical model is missing something in the mid-range behavior — possibly a compliance effect at intermediate loads that the boundary condition assumption does not capture. This is exactly the analysis you did in Week 8 using residuals at individual

dary condition assumption does not capture. This is exactly the analysis you did in Week 8 using residuals at individual points, now applied systematically across the full dataset using regression as the reference. The regression is a diagnostic reference, not the answer. slide 9: Applying These Tools to the Tier 2 Lab — What the Section 4 Deliverable Requires (Image: A five-row checklist: define and justify each input model; run at least 10,000 samples and check stability; report a labeled coverage interval; compare with first-order propagation; diagnose differences before selecting a method. A small advanced box says "Shared source? Identify it. Quantify covariance only when evidence is supplied.") Talking Point: The Tier 2 requirement stays conceptual and traceable. For every input,

only when evidence is supplied.") Talking Point: The Tier 2 requirement stays conceptual and traceable. For every input, state the evidence and the simplest defensible distribution. Run enough samples to show the reported digits and percentiles are stable. Label the output as a Monte Carlo coverage interval. Compare it numerically with first-order propagation, use the diagnostic checklist if they differ, and connect the final interval to the committed acceptance criterion. slide 10: The Improvement Proposal — Grounding in Statistical Analysis (Image: A worked example of an improvement proposal entry, formatted as a short structured paragraph. Header: "Section 6 — Improvement Proposal, Category (c): Experimental Design." Body: "The current test matrix includes 8 repeated trials at each of

ement Proposal, Category (c): Experimental Design." Body: "The current test matrix includes 8 repeated trials at each of 3 operating conditions. The 95% confidence interval for the mean at each condition is ±1.8 N (from standard error analysis). The model-data discrepancy at the critical operating condition (Condition 2) is 2.1 N — just outside the confidence interval at the current sample size. To resolve the model adequacy question at this condition with 95% confidence, we estimate a sample size of approximately 14 trials per condition would be required (based on the observed standard deviation of 4.2 N and the target half-CI of 1.1 N). An additional 6 trials per condition would close the gap. Alternatively, reducing the random measurement noise through improved signal conditioning (DAQ

tion would close the gap. Alternatively, reducing the random measurement noise through improved signal conditioning (DAQ improvement category b) would achieve the same sensitivity improvement without additional trials." A gold border frames this example and a label reads: "This is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit — specific, grounded in data, actionable.") Talking Point: This is what a full-quality improvement proposal looks like when it is grounded in the statistical analysis. The group identified that their confidence interval width of 1.8 newtons is just barely larger than the model-data discrepancy of 2.1 newtons — close enough that they cannot definitively classify the discrepancy as either real or within noise. They then computed the sample size that would

nnot definitively classify the discrepancy as either real or within noise. They then computed the sample size that would be needed to narrow the confidence interval sufficiently to resolve the question. They arrived at a specific number of additional trials — six more per condition. And they noted an alternative path: improving measurement precision through signal conditioning would achieve the same sensitivity without requiring additional trials. Both paths are specific, quantified, and grounded in the analysis results. That is the standard. The improvement proposal is not a reflection on what went well — it is a forward-looking engineering recommendation, derived from your data, that would make the experiment more informative. That is the skill E3 is going to ask you to demonstrate

from your data, that would make the experiment more informative. That is the skill E3 is going to ask you to demonstrate independently for an experiment you design yourself. slide 11: Closure Regression is a diagnostic tool, not a validation verdict. R-squared describes variance explained; residual structure guides a broader diagnosis. Monte Carlo extends to nonlinear and multi-input systems, but it is only as defensible as the input and dependence assumptions. First-order propagation is a local linearization and does not inherently require Gaussian output. When methods disagree, diagnose inputs, equations, units, distributions, convergence, and dependence before choosing an interpretation. These tools support the Tier 2 adequacy argument only when connected to the intended use. ## Agent

ng an interpretation. These tools support the Tier 2 adequacy argument only when connected to the intended use. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded in the W9 L2 blueprint purpose statement: regression, goodness-of-fit, Monte Carlo for multi-variable systems, explicit connection to E1 W2 Monte Carlo activity. - The connection to E1 W2 Monte Carlo is made explicit in slides 1 and 4, per blueprint requirement: "The connection to E1 Week 2 is made explicit — the conceptual tool is the same, the domain is now more complex." - Correlated inputs (slide 5) are included because the blueprint specifies "several inputs have correlated uncertainty." - Slide 7 provides the method comparison (partial derivative vs. Monte Carlo) required by the Tier 2 Section 4 deliverable description. - The

e method comparison (partial derivative vs. Monte Carlo) required by the Tier 2 Section 4 deliverable description. - The improvement proposal example (slide 10) demonstrates the quality of Section 6 grounding in statistical analysis, per instructor_clarification_notes.md items on critique and improvement. - No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2, CLO 3 primary treatment.

pages.title: From One Point to Many — Regression, Confidence & What They Don't Tell You
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Reading From One Point to Many — Regression, Confidence & What They Don't Tell You Conceptual framing for the W9 statistical comparison lectures, to be read before class so the lectures can move directly into MATLAB application. Covers three ideas that students consistently misapply: (1) regression as a diagnostic tool — R² tells you how much variance your model explains, not whether your model is physically correct; a model can have high R² and be structurally wrong; (2) confidence intervals as statements about the estimation process, not the physical quantity — a 95% CI means that if you repeated the experiment many times, 95% of the intervals constructed would contain the true value, not that the true value is probably inside this particular interval; (3) the distinction between statistical significance and engineering significance — a result can be statistically distinguishable from a prediction and still be close enough for the intended use, or statistically indistinguishable and still fail the engineering requirement. The reading is explicit about what these tools cannot tell you, and frames that limitation as the motivation for connecting statistical results back to the intended use statement that was written before the experiment began. Students who internalize these distinctions will apply the tools in the W9 lab with appropriate skepticism rather than treating a good-looking R² as validation. Compare means and confidence intervals from multiple experimental datasets and distinguish statistical significance from engineering significance — CLO 2 Apply regression analysis to a dataset and interpret the goodness-of-fit measures in terms of model adequacy — CLO 2, CLO 3 Use Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to propagate uncertainty through a multi-variable aerospace system and interpret the resulting distribution — CLO 2 Identify systematic trends in residuals across multiple trials and articulate their implication for model adequacy — CLO 3 From One Point to Many — Regression, Confidence & What They Don't Tell You Three statistical ideas that engineers consistently misapply — and what they actually mean A high R² does not mean your model is right. A 95% confidence interval does not mean there is a 95% probability the true value is inside it. A statistically significant result does not mean the result is important. If any of those three statements surprised you, read carefully. Each represents a misuse common enough that you will see versions of it in reports, presentations, and sometimes even published technical work. In E1, the statistical work focused on a single operating point: measure a quantity, propagate uncertainty through a formula, and report a result with bounds. The comparison was straightforward. Does the predicted value lie within the measurement's uncertainty interval? Week 9 extends this to a harder problem: comparing a model to experimental data across a range of conditions, with statistical rigor that says something truthful about whether the model is adequate. Three tools dominate this work. Each is powerful. Each is routinely misapplied. This reading focuses on where the misapplications happen and what correct interpretation looks like. Regression: What R² Actually Measures Regression is the process of fitting a mathematical relationship to a set of data points. It finds the line, curve, or model form that minimizes some measure of the distance between the model and the data. The most familiar result of a regression is the coefficient of determination, R² , which reports what fraction of the variance in the observed data is explained by the model. R² = 0.97 sounds excellent. But R² cannot tell you whether the model is physically correct. A cubic polynomial fitted to data from a system that follows simple linear physics will have high R² because the polynomial is flexible enough to follow any smooth curve. That does not mean cubic dynamics are real. Goodness of fit measures how well the model matches the data it was fitted to. It says nothing about whether the model would match data from a different experiment, a different operating range, or a different system. Look at the residuals. A model with R² = 0.97 but systematic trends in the residuals, such as positive values at low loads and negative values at high loads, is missing structural physics despite the high R². A model with R² = 0.82 but purely random residuals may be a better physical model. It may capture the right functional form even if parameter uncertainty prevents a tighter fit. The reading from W8 established this framework. Week 9 applies it across a full dataset rather than a single comparison point. The correct practice: report R² alongside a residual plot, and let the residual plot carry the interpretive weight. *This image makes the trap visible: a better fit statistic can still hide a worse engineering model if the residual structure is trying to tell you something important. Confidence Intervals: What "95%" Actually Means Practical rule: When comparing two means, construct a confidence interval for their difference. Treat overlap between two separate intervals only as a visual clue. A confidence interval comes from a statistical estimation procedure, not from a single experiment. A "95% confidence interval" means this: if you repeated the experiment many times, each time constructing an interval using the same procedure, 95% of those intervals would contain the true value. It does not mean there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this particular interval. Once the interval is calculated, the true value either is or is not inside it. Probability no longer applies. This distinction matters in engineering practice. You cannot look at a 95% confidence interval and declare that you are "95% confident" the true mean is inside it. You can say that your estimation procedure is calibrated to contain the true value 95% of the time over many repetitions. For a single experiment, the interval is either right or wrong, and you do not know which. Imagine using this same estimation procedure on 100 different experiments. In 95 of them, the CI you calculated would contain the true value. In 5 of them, it would not, and you would not know which 5. Your specific CI either captured the true value or it did not. The "95%" describes your method's long-run reliability, not this particular result. In practice, interpret confidence intervals in terms of the estimation process, not the individual result. When comparing two results, form an interval for the difference. Use a Welch interval for independent groups when equal variance is not established, or an interval for paired differences when observations are matched. Ask whether that interval includes zero. Then compare the same interval with the engineering acceptance or equivalence region specified before the data were inspected. Two separate confidence intervals can overlap even when the difference interval excludes zero, so overlap alone is not the decision rule. This distinction between statistical significance and engineering significance leads to the third common misapplication. Statistical significance means results are distinguishable given the measurement noise level. Engineering significance means the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. Statistical vs. Engineering Significance A result can be statistically significant and engineering-irrelevant. If your uncertainty is small enough, you can detect a difference in mean structural stiffness of 0.01% between two configurations. That difference is real. The statistical machinery confirmed it is not sampling noise. But if your structural model requires agreement to within 5% for its intended use, a 0.01% discrepancy is well within acceptance. The statistical significance of the difference is beside the point. Conversely, a result can be statistically non-significant and engineering-critical. If your experiment has high noise and your confidence intervals are wide, you may be unable to distinguish a 15% stiffness difference from sampling variation. The two means are statistically indistinguishable. But a 15% stiffness difference in a load-bearing structure is an engineering problem regardless of what the statistics say. The experiment was not sensitive enough to detect the relevant difference. This is a measurement design failure, not a physical finding. *This image shows why statistical significance and engineering adequacy cannot be collapsed into one yes-or-no judgment. Effect size, the magnitude of the difference relative to the variability in the data, is the quantitative bridge between statistical significance and engineering significance. A small effect size with a large sample may be statistically significant but engineering-irrelevant. A large effect size with a small sample may not reach statistical significance but still be clearly engineering-critical. The connection back to Week 6 is direct: the acceptance criterion you wrote in your pre-lab prediction is the engineering significance threshold. When you compare your Tier 2 results to your committed prediction, that criterion is the frame. Statistical distinguishability is a prerequisite, because you need to know whether the discrepancy is real or just noise. Passing the statistical test is not the same as passing the engineering test. Both questions must be answered, and both must be answered against the criteria written before the data was collected. The Takeaway: Regression, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing are powerful tools with specific and limited meanings. Applying them correctly means knowing what each one cannot tell you, then connecting statistical results back to the intended-use criteria written before the experiment began.

The reading from W8 established this framework. Week 9 applies it across a full dataset rather than a single comparison point. The correct practice: report R² alongside a residual plot, and let the residual plot carry the interpretive weight. *This image makes the trap

report R² alongside a residual plot, and let the residual plot carry the interpretive weight. *This image makes the trap visible: a better fit statistic can still hide a worse engineering model if the residual structure is trying to tell you something important. Confidence Intervals: What "95%" Actually Means Practical rule: When comparing two means, construct a confidence interval for their difference. Treat overlap between two separate intervals only as a visual clue. A confidence interval comes from a statistical estimation procedure, not from a single experiment. A "95% confidence interval" means this: if you repeated the experiment many times, each time constructing an interval using the same procedure, 95% of those intervals would contain the true value. It does not mean there is a

g an interval using the same procedure, 95% of those intervals would contain the true value. It does not mean there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this particular interval. Once the interval is calculated, the true value either is or is not inside it. Probability no longer applies. This distinction matters in engineering practice. You cannot look at a 95% confidence interval and declare that you are "95% confident" the true mean is inside it. You can say that your estimation procedure is calibrated to contain the true value 95% of the time over many repetitions. For a single experiment, the interval is either right or wrong, and you do not know which. Imagine using this same estimation procedure on 100 different experiments. In 95 of them, the CI you calculated would

h. Imagine using this same estimation procedure on 100 different experiments. In 95 of them, the CI you calculated would contain the true value. In 5 of them, it would not, and you would not know which 5. Your specific CI either captured the true value or it did not. The "95%" describes your method's long-run reliability, not this particular result. In practice, interpret confidence intervals in terms of the estimation process, not the individual result. When comparing two results, form an interval for the difference. Use a Welch interval for independent groups when equal variance is not established, or an interval for paired differences when observations are matched. Ask whether that interval includes zero. Then compare the same interval with the engineering acceptance or equivalence

. Ask whether that interval includes zero. Then compare the same interval with the engineering acceptance or equivalence region specified before the data were inspected. Two separate confidence intervals can overlap even when the difference interval excludes zero, so overlap alone is not the decision rule. This distinction between statistical significance and engineering significance leads to the third common misapplication. Statistical significance means results are distinguishable given the measurement noise level. Engineering significance means the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. Statistical vs. Engineering Significance A result can be statistically significant and engineering-irrelevant. If your uncertainty is small enough, you can detect a difference in

tatistically significant and engineering-irrelevant. If your uncertainty is small enough, you can detect a difference in mean structural stiffness of 0.01% between two configurations. That difference is real. The statistical machinery confirmed it is not sampling noise. But if your structural model requires agreement to within 5% for its intended use, a 0.01% discrepancy is well within acceptance. The statistical significance of the difference is beside the point. Conversely, a result can be statistically non-significant and engineering-critical. If your experiment has high noise and your confidence intervals are wide, you may be unable to distinguish a 15% stiffness difference from sampling variation. The two means are statistically indistinguishable. But a 15% stiffness difference in a

fference from sampling variation. The two means are statistically indistinguishable. But a 15% stiffness difference in a load-bearing structure is an engineering problem regardless of what the statistics say. The experiment was not sensitive enough to detect the relevant difference. This is a measurement design failure, not a physical finding. *This image shows why statistical significance and engineering adequacy cannot be collapsed into one yes-or-no judgment. Effect size, the magnitude of the difference relative to the variability in the data, is the quantitative bridge between statistical significance and engineering significance. A small effect size with a large sample may be statistically significant but engineering-irrelevant. A large effect size with a small sample may not reach

ample may be statistically significant but engineering-irrelevant. A large effect size with a small sample may not reach statistical significance but still be clearly engineering-critical. The connection back to Week 6 is direct: the acceptance criterion you wrote in your pre-lab prediction is the engineering significance threshold. When you compare your Tier 2 results to your committed prediction, that criterion is the frame. Statistical distinguishability is a prerequisite, because you need to know whether the discrepancy is real or just noise. Passing the statistical test is not the same as passing the engineering test. Both questions must be answered, and both must be answered against the criteria written before the data was collected. The Takeaway: Regression, confidence intervals,

be answered against the criteria written before the data was collected. The Takeaway: Regression, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing are powerful tools with specific and limited meanings. Applying them correctly means knowing what each one cannot tell you, then connecting statistical results back to the intended-use criteria written before the experiment began.

pages.title: Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Reading Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case Optional advanced supplement for groups whose Monte Carlo inputs are not independent. Covers what correlated inputs mean in practice (two or more inputs coming from the same calibration procedure or material property source), why independent sampling underestimates worst-case uncertainty in this situation, and how to use a correlation matrix and Cholesky decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. Framed explicitly as beyond the scope of the standard E2 workflow — most groups will not need it. Students with genuinely independent inputs (the common case) can skip this reading. Intended for completeness and for the minority of groups whose experiment has correlated input sources. The W9 L2 lecture references this reading from slide 5 and directs students here for the full explanation and MATLAB implementation. Compare means and confidence intervals from multiple experimental datasets and distinguish statistical significance from engineering significance — CLO 2 Apply regression analysis to a dataset and interpret the goodness-of-fit measures in terms of model adequacy — CLO 2, CLO 3 Use Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to propagate uncertainty through a multi-variable aerospace system and interpret the resulting distribution — CLO 2 Identify systematic trends in residuals across multiple trials and articulate their implication for model adequacy — CLO 3 Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case When your uncertainties are not independent, and what to do about it Most introductory Monte Carlo simulations begin with independent inputs. But independence is an assumption to inspect, not a fact supplied by the software. This reading is for the cases where it is not. This reading is an advanced topic and is beyond the required E2 workflow. In E2, your required task is to identify whether inputs might share a calibration reference, environment, data-reduction step, or instrument. You are not required to calculate covariance or implement correlated sampling unless evidence and an estimate are provided. Read this if two or more input uncertainties come from the same source. Common examples include sensor outputs derived from the same calibration procedure or parameters estimated from the same material tests. Sampling them independently ignores that structure. The effect on output uncertainty depends on the covariance sign and on the signs and magnitudes of the model sensitivities. Correlation can widen or narrow the output distribution. *This image makes input correlation visible so you can see exactly what gets lost when a Monte Carlo model samples coupled variables as if they were independent. The correction requires two pieces: a correlation matrix and a Cholesky decomposition . The correlation matrix encodes the statistical relationships between your inputs. For two inputs with a correlation coefficient ρ, the matrix is simply [[1, ρ], [ρ, 1]]. A correlation of +1 means the two inputs move together perfectly. A correlation of 0 means they are independent. A correlation of −1 means when one is high, the other is low. You estimate ρ from the physical relationship between the two sources, or from the calibration records if both sensors were calibrated against the same reference artifact. The Cholesky decomposition is the matrix equivalent of taking a square root. Applied to the correlation matrix, it produces a lower triangular matrix L such that L × Lᵀ = R. Multiplying a set of independent standard normal draws by L transforms them into draws with the correct correlated structure. In MATLAB: R = [1 0.7; 0.7 1]; % Correlation matrix (ρ = 0.7) L = chol(R, 'lower'); % Cholesky decomposition X = randn(N, 2) * L'; % N correlated sample pairs The rows of X are sample pairs with the correct joint distribution. They form an elongated ellipse in two dimensions, not a circular cloud. Each row is then scaled by the appropriate standard uncertainty for each input before being passed into the model. Geometrically, the Cholesky factor stretches and rotates the independent standard normal cloud into the correct correlated shape. It does not change the marginal distributions. Each input still has its correct individual uncertainty, but the samples are linked so the joint behavior matches the physical relationship between the sources. The Takeaway: First identify shared sources. Correlated sampling is an advanced option only when the dependence can be supported; its effect is not necessarily to widen the result because covariance sign and model sensitivities both matter.

pages.title: Questions
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Questions to Think Through Before Class Statistical vs. engineering significance, regression limits, confidence intervals, and data-grounded improvement proposals. Which statement best reflects Week 9’s warning about R^2? Which statement best captures the difference between statistical significance and engineering significance? What is the strongest Week 9 reason to use Monte Carlo for a multi-variable system? A comparison is statistically different, but the gap is still inside the allowed engineering tolerance for the intended use. How should you describe that result? Why is a data-grounded improvement proposal stronger than a generic suggestion like “use better sensors”? Two independent configurations have an estimated mean difference of 0.30 N. A Welch 95% confidence interval for the difference is [0.02, 0.58] N, and the pre-specified engineering equivalence region is [−2.0, 2.0] N. What two conclusions should you report? Which is the correct interpretation of a p-value?

pages.title: E2 Week 10: Transition 2: Design Framing Workshop
pages.text: E2 Week 10: Transition 2: Design Framing Workshop Week 10 is a transition, not a content week. Students are given a set of design constraints — available hardware, time limitations, measurement resolution, sensor options, physical boundaries — and must propose a validation experiment without building anything yet. The deliverable is a design frame: a system response feature selected, a formal hypothesis stated, a validation plan sketched, dominant uncertainty sou... Present a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison, uncertainty analysis, calibration status review, and experimental improvement proposal on a single quad chart slide — CLO 5 Defend instrumentation and validation logic under direct questioning in a 5-minute Q&A — CLO 1, CLO 3, CLO 4, CLO 5 Evaluate another group's experimental design and analysis using a structured rubric, providing specific and actionable written feedback — CLO 5, CLO 6 Articulate the transition from analyzing and improving existing experiments (E2) to designing original experiments from scratch (E3) — CLO 1, CLO 3 Questions to Think Through Before Class

pages.title: Questions
pages.text: E2 Week 5 (Course Week 10) Questions to Think Through Before Class Quad chart communication, defending analysis choices, peer review quality, and transition from improving existing experiments to designing new ones. What is the main purpose of the quad chart presentation in Week 10? What makes a strong peer review comment in the Week 10 sense? Why is E2 a bridge to E3? If someone challenges one of your presentation conclusions during the Q&A, what does Week 10 suggest you should defend first: the conclusion itself, the evidence behind it, or something else? In one short paragraph, explain how the course’s idea of “good critique” is different from just being negative.

pages.title: E3 Week 11: Experimental Design Principles, Hypothesis Formulation & Prior Art
pages.text: E3 Week 11: Experimental Design Principles, Hypothesis Formulation & Prior Art E3 opens by formalizing something students have been doing implicitly since Week 1: making decisions about what to measure, how many times, and under what conditions. Week 11 makes experimental design explicit — independent and dependent variables, replication, randomization, control, confounding factors, and a structured introduction to design of experiments concepts at the exposure level. Hypothesis formulation... Apply formal experimental design vocabulary — independent and dependent variables, replication, randomization, blocking, control, and confounding factors — to an aerospace measurement problem — CLO 1 State a formal hypothesis that defines what a proposed experiment is expected to show, what data would confirm it, and what data would challenge it — CLO 1, CLO 3 Identify and cite ASME V&V 10-2006 and at least one additional relevant engineering standard, handbook, or prior publication applicable to their E3 design challenge — CLO 1, CLO 6 Describe what is already established in prior literature about their measurement domain and articulate what gap or question their experiment addresses — CLO 6 Frame the purpose of the E3 design challenge explicitly: the experiment exists to inform and improve a defined predictive model — not merely to validate it in a binary sense — CLO 3 Produce a preliminary experimental design proposal including problem statement, hypothesis, variable identification, relevant standards cited, and prior art summary — CLO 1, CLO 5, CLO 6 Formal Experimental Design — Variables, Replication & Control Formalizes what students have been doing implicitly since Week 1: making decisions about what to measure, how many times, and under what conditions. Covers independent and dependent variables, controlled variables and the challenge of maintaining control in physical experiments, confounding factors and how they masquerade as causal effects, replication as the only defense against random error, randomization and blocking as defenses against systematic bias, and a light exposure to design of experiments (DOE) concepts — factorial designs and Taguchi methods at the awareness level, not the mastery level. The central reframe: experimental design is modeling in hardware. Every design decision encodes an assumption about what matters. **AMVF framing (W11):** This lecture should open with an explicit return to the AMVF — reminding students that the entire course has been building toward this moment. The question "what does this experiment tell you about the system, and why does it matter?" first asked in E1 W3 and revisited in E2 W6 is now the question students must answer for themselves, for their own designed experiment. This is the connection between ASEN 3501 and the senior design project: the experiment exists to inform a model, the model exists to support an engineering decision, and the decision exists to move a project forward. Engineering Standards, Handbooks & Prior Art Introduces the professional ecosystem of engineering standards, measurement handbooks, and prior literature that experimental aerospace engineers draw on. ASME V&V 10-2006 is named explicitly as the primary required reference alongside domain-specific standards — AIAA measurement standards, ASME PTC measurement uncertainty standards, ASTM measurement standards, and relevant MIL-HDBK documents. This lecture establishes ASME V&V 10-2006 as the formal standards anchor that has been cited since Week 1 and is now a required citation in E3 design work. Students also practice locating a standard relevant to a sample measurement problem during the lecture session. Prior literature is covered: what a standard specifies and what it does not, how handbooks provide measurement method recommendations and uncertainty estimates, and how prior publications establish what is already known. Engineering Standards & Handbooks Resources A curated, annotated list of engineering standards resources and handbooks relevant to aerospace experimental methods — organized by measurement domain (fluid mechanics, structural, thermal, vibration). ASME V&V 10-2006 is listed first as the primary course standard. Additional pointers include AIAA standards, ASME PTC documents, ASTM measurement standards, relevant MIL-HDBK documents, and recommended handbooks (including Coleman & Steele 4th edition for uncertainty). Serves as the reference resource for both the W11 preliminary proposal and the W12 instrumentation justification report. Should be reviewed and updated annually as standards are revised. Experimental Design Proposal Worksheet A reading-format worksheet that helps students turn the W11 design challenge launch into a preliminary proposal. Students define the model claim, intended use, predictive model, response feature, hypothesis, variables, controls, confounders, control/randomization/blocking strategy, initial test matrix, scope boundary, and annotated source evidence. Includes a three-row annotated-source template for ASME V&V 10-2006, a datasheet or handbook, and a prior paper or application note. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week Students have defined their hypothesis, identified what is already known, and established the standards context for their design. Week 12 asks them to specify how they will measure — selecting sensors, DAQ configuration, and signal conditioning strategy — and to go beyond the provided course hardware to investigate what the broader instrumentation landscape offers for their specific measurement problem. Questions to Think Through Before Class

pages.title: Formal Experimental Design — Variables, Replication & Control
pages.text: E3 Week 1 (Course Week 11) Lecture Formal Experimental Design — Variables, Replication & Control Formalizes what students have been doing implicitly since Week 1: making decisions about what to measure, how many times, and under what conditions. Covers independent and dependent variables, controlled variables and the challenge of maintaining control in physical experiments, confounding factors and how they masquerade as causal effects, replication as the only defense against random error, randomization and blocking as defenses against systematic bias, and a light exposure to design of experiments (DOE) concepts — factorial designs and Taguchi methods at the awareness level, not the mastery level. The central reframe: experimental design is modeling in hardware. Every design decision encodes an assumption about what matters. **AMVF framing (W11):** This lecture should open with an explicit return to the AMVF — reminding students that the entire course has been building toward this moment. The question "what does this experiment tell you about the system, and why does it matter?" first asked in E1 W3 and revisited in E2 W6 is now the question students must answer for themselves, for their own designed experiment. This is the connection between ASEN 3501 and the senior design project: the experiment exists to inform a model, the model exists to support an engineering decision, and the decision exists to move a project forward. Apply formal experimental design vocabulary — independent and dependent variables, replication, randomization, blocking, control, and confounding factors — to an aerospace measurement problem — CLO 1 State a formal hypothesis that defines what a proposed experiment is expected to show, what data would confirm it, and what data would challenge it — CLO 1, CLO 3 Identify and cite ASME V&V 10-2006 and at least one additional relevant engineering standard, handbook, or prior publication applicable to their E3 design challenge — CLO 1, CLO 6 Describe what is already established in prior literature about their measurement domain and articulate what gap or question their experiment addresses — CLO 6 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Formal Experimental Design — Variables, Replication & Control Talking Point: Today we move from running experiments that someone else designed to designing experiments ourselves. The core question is no longer just "what did the data say?" but "what experiment would produce data that can actually inform the model?" Experimental design is modeling in hardware, and every choice we make reveals what we think matters. slide 2: Returning to the AMVF (Image: A clean AMVF flowchart with the physical modeling branch highlighted in gold and the mathematical modeling branch highlighted in blue. A callout points to the validation comparison node with the question "What would count as evidence?" and arrows show E1 labeled "measure," E2 labeled "predict," and E3 labeled "design.") Talking Point: We have been using the AMVF all semester, but E3 changes your role inside it. In E1, the experiment was given to you and your job was to measure honestly. In E2, the system was given to you and your job was to predict before measuring. Now the design responsibility shifts to you. You must decide what data the model needs, what response feature matters, and what measurement would be credible enough to support an engineering decision. slide 3: Experimental Design Is Modeling in Hardware (Image: Split-screen visual. Left side shows a symbolic predictive model with variables highlighted. Right side shows a physical test setup with matching labels on sensors, inputs, and controlled conditions. A bridge between the panels is labeled "design decisions encode assumptions.") Talking Point: A model says which variables matter and how they are related. An experiment is the physical arrangement we build to challenge, refine, or support that model. If your model predicts thrust as a function of voltage, airspeed, and propeller geometry, your experiment must create trustworthy information about those quantities. If the experiment ignores a variable the model depends on, the comparison will be weak before you collect a single point of data. slide 4: Independent, Dependent, and Controlled Variables (Image: A tabletop wind-tunnel-style test matrix diagram with an input knob labeled "independent variable: airspeed," a measured plot labeled "dependent variable: drag force," and surrounding constraint tags labeled temperature, angle of attack, mounting, and sensor calibration as controlled variables.) Talking Point: The independent variable is what you intentionally vary. The dependent variable is the system response you measure. Controlled variables are the conditions you try to hold fixed because they could otherwise change the response. In classroom problems these categories look obvious, but in physical experiments they blur quickly. A mounting bracket, a room temperature drift, or a sensor alignment angle can become an uncontrolled variable if you do not design for it. slide 5: Confounding Factors (Image: A cause-and-effect diagram showing two arrows entering one measured output. One arrow is labeled "intended cause: input voltage" and the second is labeled "confounder: motor temperature." The measured output is labeled "RPM," with a warning sign reading "apparent causal effect may be mixed.") Talking Point: A confounding factor is something that changes along with the variable you care about and makes the result hard to interpret. Suppose motor RPM changes as voltage increases, but the motor also heats up during the test sequence. If the heating always happens at the higher voltages, you cannot tell whether the RPM change came from voltage alone or from voltage plus temperature effects. Confounding is dangerous because it does not look like random noise. It looks like evidence until you realize the design allowed two causes to move together. slide 6: Replication as Defense Against Random Error (Image: Three side-by-side plots of repeated measurements at the same condition. The first has two scattered points with a wide uncertainty band, the second has ten points with a narrower mean confidence interval, and the third has thirty points with a much tighter confidence interval. A label reads "replication reduces uncertainty in the estimated mean, not the physics of the scatter.") Talking Point: Replication means repeating a measurement under the same nominal condition so you can estimate random variability. It is not busywork and it is not just a way to make plots look full. Replication tells you whether an observed difference is larger than the noise floor of your measurement process. If your design cannot distinguish the effect you care about from random scatter, then the experiment cannot inform the model at the required level. slide 7: Randomization and Blocking (Image: A test schedule board with condition cards A, B, C, and D. One schedule is ordered A-A-B-B-C-C-D-D with a red arrow labeled "time drift risk." A second schedule is randomized within two blocks labeled morning and afternoon, with a blue checkmark labeled "separate treatment effects from drift.") Talking Point: Randomization protects you from systematic bias caused by time, order, operator fatigue, or environmental drift. Blocking is what you do when you know a nuisance factor exists and you cannot eliminate it, so you group trials in a way that lets you account for it. In aerospace experiments, blocking might mean separating runs by tunnel configuration, battery state, specimen batch, or day of testing. These choices are part of the design, not statistical decoration added later. slide 8: Control Is Never Perfect (Image: A physical test stand surrounded by translucent uncertainty clouds labeled alignment, temperature, sensor zero, fixture compliance, and operator setup. A checklist panel beside it shows "measure, monitor, constrain, document.") Talking Point: Control does not mean the world stops moving. It means you have identified which conditions matter, constrained them as much as practical, and documented what remains. A mature experimental design admits that some variables cannot be perfectly controlled and then decides how to monitor or bound their influence. That honesty is what makes the resulting comparison credible. slide 9: DOE at Awareness Level (Image: A simple two-factor grid with factor A on one axis and factor B on the other. Four corner test points are highlighted as a 2x2 factorial design, and a small note says "screening: which factors matter most?" A second mini-panel shows a Taguchi-style orthogonal array as a compact test matrix.) Talking Point: Design of experiments, or DOE, is a formal way to plan test matrices so that you learn efficiently from a limited number of runs. We are not going deep into factorial design or Taguchi methods in this course, but you need to recognize the idea. When there are multiple possible inputs, a thoughtful test matrix can reveal interactions that one-variable-at-a-time testing would miss. For E3, the practical takeaway is this: your proposed measurements should be chosen because they answer the model question, not because they are the easiest sequence to imagine. slide 10: Hypothesis Before Hardware (Image: A design notebook page with three boxed statements: "Model claim," "Data that would support it," and "Data that would challenge it." Below the boxes is a locked equipment cabinet labeled "hardware access comes after the claim is defined.") Talking Point: A hypothesis is not a guess about what you hope happens. It is a structured claim about what the model predicts and what evidence would support or challenge that prediction. Before you specify hardware, you should be able to say what result would make the model more credible, what result would expose a model limitation, and what uncertainty level would make the comparison meaningful. If you cannot say those things, you are not ready to choose sensors. slide 11: From E2 Improvement to E3 Design (Image: A before-and-after storyboard. Left panel shows an E2 report section titled "Improve this experiment" with annotations around residuals and uncertainty bottlenecks. Right panel shows an E3 design proposal with the same annotations converted into design requirements, sensor choices, and validation metrics.) Talking Point: The improvement proposal in Tier 2 was not an isolated deliverable. It was rehearsal for E3. In E2 you looked at an existing experiment and asked how it could be made more credible. In E3 you start from that same logic but move one step upstream: you design the experiment so the credibility is built in from the beginning. That is a different level of ownership. slide 12: Closure The main idea today is that experimental design is not a list of equipment choices. It is a chain of reasoning from model claim, to variables, to controls, to replication, to comparison. Your design decisions determine whether the data can say anything useful about the model before the first measurement is taken. Next we will add the professional reference layer: standards, handbooks, and prior art. That is where your design stops being just your idea and starts becoming part of engineering practice. Agent Notes Grounded in E3 W11 Lecture 1 purpose: variables, control, confounding, replication, randomization, blocking, DOE awareness, and AMVF return. Treats E3 as paper experimental design while keeping the language tied to credible physical implementation. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Formal Experimental Design — Variables, Replication & Control Talking Point: Today we move from running experiments that someone else designed to designing experiments ourselves. The core question is no longer just "what did the data say?" but "what experiment would produce data that can actually inform the model?" Experimental design is modeling in hardware, and every choice we make reveals what we think matters. slide 2: Returning to the AMVF (Image: A clean AMVF flowchart with the physical modeling branch highlighted in gold and the mathematical modeling branch highlighted in blue. A callout points to the validation comparison node with the question "What would count as evidence?" and arrows show E1 labeled "measure," E2 labeled "predict," and E3 labeled "design.") Talking Point: We have been using the AMVF all semester, but E3 changes your role inside it. In E1, the experiment was given to you and your job was to measure honestly. In E2, the system was given to you and your job was to predict before measuring. Now the design responsibility shifts to you. You must decide what data the model needs, what response feature matters, and what measurement would be credible enough to support an engineering decision. slide 3: Experimental Design Is Modeling in Hardware (Image: Split-screen visual. Left side shows a symbolic predictive model with variables highlighted. Right side shows a physical test setup with matching labels on sensors, inputs, and controlled conditions. A bridge between the panels is labeled "design decisions encode assumptions.") Talking Point: A model says which variables matter and how they are related. An experiment is the physical arrangement we build to challenge, refine, or support that model. If your model predicts thrust as a function of voltage, airspeed, and propeller geometry, your experiment must create trustworthy information about those quantities. If the experiment ignores a variable the model depends on, the comparison will be weak before you collect a single point of data. slide 4: Independent, Dependent, and Controlled Variables (Image: A tabletop wind-tunnel-style test matrix diagram with an input knob labeled "independent variable: airspeed," a measured plot labeled "dependent variable: drag force," and surrounding constraint tags labeled temperature, angle of attack, mounting, and sensor calibration as controlled variables.) Talking Point: The independent variable is what you intentionally vary. The dependent variable is the system response you measure. Controlled variables are the conditions you try to hold fixed because they could otherwise change the response. In classroom problems these categories look obvious, but in physical experiments they blur quickly. A mounting bracket, a room temperature drift, or a sensor alignment angle can become an uncontrolled variable if you do not design for it. slide 5: Confounding Factors (Image: A cause-and-effect diagram showing two arrows entering one measured output. One arrow is labeled "intended cause: input voltage" and the second is labeled "confounder: motor temperature." The measured output is labeled "RPM," with a warning sign reading "apparent causal effect may be mixed.") Confounding is dangerous because it does not look like random noise. It looks like evidence until you realize the design allowed two causes to move together. slide 6: Replication as Defense Against Random Error (Image: Three side-by-side plots of repeated measurements at the same condition. The first has two scattered points with a wide uncertainty band, the second has ten points with a narrower mean confidence interval, and the third has thirty points with a much tighter confidence interval. A label reads "replication reduces uncertainty in the estimated mean, not the physics of the scatter.") Talking Point: Replication means repeating a measurement under the same nominal condition so you can estimate random variability. It is not busywork and it is not just a way to make plots look full. Replication tells you whether an observed difference is larger than the noise floor of your measurement process. If your design cannot distinguish the effect you care about from random scatter, then the experiment cannot inform the model at the required level. slide 7: Randomization and Blocking (Image: A test schedule board with condition cards A, B, C, and D. One schedule is ordered A-A-B-B-C-C-D-D with a red arrow labeled "time drift risk." A second schedule is randomized within two blocks labeled morning and afternoon, with a blue checkmark labeled "separate treatment effects from drift.") Talking Point: Randomization protects you from systematic bias caused by time, order, operator fatigue, or environmental drift. Blocking is what you do when you know a nuisance factor exists and you cannot eliminate it, so you group trials in a way that lets you account for it. In aerospace experiments, blocking might mean separating runs by tunnel configuration, battery state, specimen batch, or day of testing. These choices are part of the design, not statistical decoration added later. slide 8: Control Is Never Perfect (Image: A physical test stand surrounded by translucent uncertainty clouds labeled alignment, temperature, sensor zero, fixture compliance, and operator setup. A checklist panel beside it shows "measure, monitor, constrain, document.") Talking Point: Control does not mean the world stops moving. It means you have identified which conditions matter, constrained them as much as practical, and documented what remains. A mature experimental design admits that some variables cannot be perfectly controlled and then decides how to monitor or bound their influence. That honesty is what makes the resulting comparison credible. slide 9: DOE at Awareness Level (Image: A simple two-factor grid with factor A on one axis and factor B on the other. For E3, the practical takeaway is this: your proposed measurements should be chosen because they answer the model question, not because they are the easiest sequence to imagine. slide 10: Hypothesis Before Hardware (Image: A design notebook page with three boxed statements: "Model claim," "Data that would support it," and "Data that would challenge it." Below the boxes is a locked equipment cabinet labeled "hardware access comes after the claim is defined.") Talking Point: A hypothesis is not a guess about what you hope happens. It is a structured claim about what the model predicts and what evidence would support or challenge that prediction. Before you specify hardware, you should be able to say what result would make the model more credible, what result would expose a model limitation, and what uncertainty level would make the comparison meaningful. If you cannot say those things, you are not ready to choose sensors. slide 11: From E2 Improvement to E3 Design (Image: A before-and-after storyboard. Left panel shows an E2 report section titled "Improve this experiment" with annotations around residuals and uncertainty bottlenecks. Right panel shows an E3 design proposal with the same annotations converted into design requirements, sensor choices, and validation metrics.) Talking Point: The improvement proposal in Tier 2 was not an isolated deliverable. It was rehearsal for E3. In E2 you looked at an existing experiment and asked how it could be made more credible. In E3 you start from that same logic but move one step upstream: you design the experiment so the credibility is built in from the beginning. That is a different level of ownership. slide 12: Closure The main idea today is that experimental design is not a list of equipment choices. It is a chain of reasoning from model claim, to variables, to controls, to replication, to comparison. Your design decisions determine whether the data can say anything useful about the model before the first measurement is taken. Next we will add the professional reference layer: standards, handbooks, and prior art. chunks.text: E3 Week 1 (Course Week 11) Lecture Formal Experimental Design — Variables, Replication & Control Formalizes what students have been doing implicitly since Week 1: making decisions about what to measure, how many times, and under what conditions. Covers independent and dependent variables, controlled variables and the challenge of maintaining control in physical experiments, confounding factors and how they masquerade as causal effects, replication as the only defense against random error, randomization and blocking as defenses against systematic bias, and a light exposure to design of experiments (DOE) concepts — factorial designs and Taguchi methods at the awareness level, not the mastery level. The central reframe: experimental design is modeling in hardware. Every design decision

ss level, not the mastery level. The central reframe: experimental design is modeling in hardware. Every design decision encodes an assumption about what matters. **AMVF framing (W11):** This lecture should open with an explicit return to the AMVF — reminding students that the entire course has been building toward this moment. The question "what does this experiment tell you about the system, and why does it matter?" first asked in E1 W3 and revisited in E2 W6 is now the question students must answer for themselves, for their own designed experiment. This is the connection between ASEN 3501 and the senior design project: the experiment exists to inform a model, the model exists to support an engineering decision, and the decision exists to move a project forward. Apply formal

el, the model exists to support an engineering decision, and the decision exists to move a project forward.
