The worksheet makes critique quality auditable and keeps feedback tied to technical evidence rather than surface polish. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week The oral defenses and peer evaluations

tied to technical evidence rather than surface polish. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week The oral defenses and peer evaluations are complete. Week 15 is not another deliverable week — it is a reflection and integration week where students step back from the work they have produced and map it explicitly to the course framework, articulate what they learned about themselves as experimentalists, and close the arc that opened with a single question in Week 1. Deliver a structured oral defense of an experimental design and V&V plan to a technical audience, responding to questions about instrumentation choices, uncertainty analysis, and validation logic — CLO 5 Evaluate a peer's experimental design using a structured rubric, identifying specific strengths, genuine weaknesses, and concrete improvement

erimental design using a structured rubric, identifying specific strengths, genuine weaknesses, and concrete improvement recommendations — CLO 5, CLO 6 Apply technical communication principles — structured arguments, figures as arguments, results separated from interpretation, limitations stated explicitly — in the oral and written communication of experimental design work — CLO 5 Analyze sample experimental design work — from prior course iterations, professional reports, or AI-generated content — using the same evaluation rubric applied to peer presentations, demonstrating that critique quality is independent of the source of the work being evaluated — CLO 5, CLO 6 Peer Evaluation and Engineering Critique Worksheet A technical critique is useful only when it names the evidence A good

Peer Evaluation and Engineering Critique Worksheet A technical critique is useful only when it names the evidence A good critique does not sound impressive; it helps the next engineer make a better decision. Week 14 asks you to evaluate experimental designs from two directions. First, you will evaluate peer oral defenses. Second, you may evaluate sample, prior-student, professional, or AI-generated experimental design work. Use the same technical standard for both. The question is not whether the work is polished, confident, or easy to like. The question is whether the design would produce data capable of informing the model claim it identifies. <image description: A clean worksheet-style visual with two side-by-side panels. The left panel is labeled peer oral defense and shows a student

ean worksheet-style visual with two side-by-side panels. The left panel is labeled peer oral defense and shows a student presentation slide with callouts for hypothesis, instrumentation, uncertainty, and validation metric. The right panel is labeled sample or AI-generated design and shows a polished report page with the same callouts. A shared rubric bar underneath says same engineering standard, different source of work.> Use the prompts below while you listen, read, or review. Write in complete but compact engineering sentences. Avoid praise that could apply to any presentation. Avoid critique that only says something is missing. Name the consequence: why does the missing or weak element affect model credibility, measurement quality, uncertainty, feasibility, or intended use? Part 1 -

ssing or weak element affect model credibility, measurement quality, uncertainty, feasibility, or intended use? Part 1 - What Is Being Claimed? Model or decision being informed — What model, requirement, parameter, design decision, or engineering question does this experiment address? Response: Hypothesis testability — Is the hypothesis specific enough that future data could support or challenge it? What exact result would count as a challenge? Response: System response feature — What quantity, trend, time-series feature, distribution, residual pattern, or threshold is being measured? Is it the right evidence for the model claim? Response: Part 2 - Is The Measurement Chain Credible? Instrumentation adequacy — Are the sensors matched to the required range, accuracy, bandwidth, environment,

in Credible? Instrumentation adequacy — Are the sensors matched to the required range, accuracy, bandwidth, environment, and installation constraints? Name the strongest instrumentation choice and the weakest one. Response: DAQ, sampling, and filtering — Is the sample rate justified by expected signal bandwidth? Is filtering or anti-aliasing addressed where it matters? Is DAQ range or resolution connected to uncertainty rather than just named? Response: Calibration versus validation — Does the design distinguish checking an instrument against a known reference from validating the predictive model against independent system behavior? Response: Part 3 - Does The Uncertainty Support The Claim? Dominant uncertainty source — What appears to dominate the uncertainty budget? Is the source

ertainty Support The Claim? Dominant uncertainty source — What appears to dominate the uncertainty budget? Is the source identified with enough evidence to be believable? Response: Adequacy conclusion — Is the predicted uncertainty small enough to evaluate the acceptance criterion or intended-use decision? If the work does not answer this, say so directly. Response: Validation metric and acceptance criterion — Is the metric matched to the response feature? Is the criterion defined before data collection, and is it justified by intended use rather than convenience? Response: <image description: A compact critique matrix with rows labeled hypothesis, measurement chain, uncertainty, validation metric, feasibility, and model update. Columns are labeled evidence present, concern, consequence,

certainty, validation metric, feasibility, and model update. Columns are labeled evidence present, concern, consequence, and recommended improvement. One row is partially filled to show how a vague concern becomes an actionable engineering critique.> Part 4 - Feasibility, Safety, And Model Update Feasibility and safety — Are resources, hazards, operating limits, time, and practical constraints specific enough that a reviewer could decide whether the experiment is buildable and responsible? Response: Experimentally informed model update — Does the design state what data pattern would change a parameter, assumption, boundary condition, or model form? If not, what update pathway is missing? Response: Part 5 - Your Required Critique One specific strength — Name one feature of the work that

pathway is missing? Response: Part 5 - Your Required Critique One specific strength — Name one feature of the work that improves the design's credibility. Explain why it matters technically. Response: One specific weakness or unresolved risk — Name one issue that would most limit confidence in the design. Connect it to model, instrumentation, uncertainty, validation, feasibility, or communication. Response: One actionable improvement — Give one concrete change the team or author could make. A strong recommendation names what to change, where, and why it would improve the design. Response: AI-Generated Or Sample Work Check If the work being reviewed was AI-generated or otherwise supplied as sample work, add this final check: What looked plausible on the surface? What technical claim

d or otherwise supplied as sample work, add this final check: What looked plausible on the surface? What technical claim needed verification from a source? What was correct, incorrect, missing, or unsupported? What is the single most important revision before this work could be trusted? Response: The Takeaway: Your critique is graded on engineering judgment. Specific, evidence-based, actionable feedback is a technical contribution; generic approval or vague dissatisfaction is not.

pages.title: E3 Week 15: Integration, Reflection & CLO 6 Closure
pages.text: E3 Week 15: Integration, Reflection & CLO 6 Closure The final week of ASEN 3501 is not an exam week. It is a mapping exercise and a structured reflection. Students explicitly connect each phase of the course — E1's measurement and uncertainty work, E2's prediction and signal conditioning work, E3's design and V&V planning work — to the corresponding nodes of the AMVF. A named deliverable requires students to describe specifically how they went beyond the provided m... Map each phase of the course — E1's measurement and uncertainty work, E2's prediction and signal conditioning work, E3's design and V&V planning work — to the corresponding nodes of the AMVF — CLO 3 Describe specifically how they investigated sensors, platforms, methods, or standards beyond the course-provided materials during the E3 design activity, and evaluate what the investigation produced — CLO 6 Articulate how their understanding of uncertainty, prediction, and validation evolved across the 15-week arc, and identify what they would do differently if they were starting Week 1 again — CLO 5, CLO 6 Answer, with structured reasoning, the question that opened the course: *How do you know this prediction is right?* — CLO 3 AMVF Mapping and Reflection Worksheet A reading-format worksheet that scaffolds the W15 AMVF mapping, CLO 6 reflection, course reflection, and final answer to the course-opening question. Students trace one engineering decision across E1, E2, and E3; map it to AMVF nodes; document the beyond-provided method, sensor, platform, standard, or source they investigated; explain whether AI-assisted claims were verified; and use concrete evidence to describe how their understanding of uncertainty, prediction, validation, and experimental design changed. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week There is no Week 16. The course ends here — but the capability it developed does not. Students carry forward a structured, repeatable method for earning confidence in a prediction: design the experiment, specify the instrumentation, quantify the uncertainty, define the acceptance criterion, commit before measuring, compare honestly, and use the results to improve the model. That method has no expiration date. Questions to Think Through Before Class

pages.title: AMVF Mapping and Reflection Worksheet
pages.text: E3 Week 5 (Course Week 15) Reading / Worksheet AMVF Mapping and Reflection Worksheet A reading-format worksheet that scaffolds the W15 AMVF mapping, CLO 6 reflection, course reflection, and final answer to the course-opening question. Students trace one engineering decision across E1, E2, and E3; map it to AMVF nodes; document the beyond-provided method, sensor, platform, standard, or source they investigated; explain whether AI-assisted claims were verified; and use concrete evidence to describe how their understanding of uncertainty, prediction, validation, and experimental design changed. ## 3. Bridge to Next Week There is no Week 16. The course ends here — but the capability it developed does not. Students carry forward a structured, repeatable method for earning confidence in a prediction: design the experiment, specify the instrumentation, quantify the uncertainty, define the acceptance criterion, commit before measuring, compare honestly, and use the results to improve the model. That method has no expiration date. Map each phase of the course — E1's measurement and uncertainty work, E2's prediction and signal conditioning work, E3's design and V&V planning work — to the corresponding nodes of the AMVF — CLO 3 Describe specifically how they investigated sensors, platforms, methods, or standards beyond the course-provided materials during the E3 design activity, and evaluate what the investigation produced — CLO 6 Articulate how their understanding of uncertainty, prediction, and validation evolved across the 15-week arc, and identify what they would do differently if they were starting Week 1 again — CLO 5, CLO 6 Answer, with structured reasoning, the question that opened the course: *How do you know this prediction is right?* — CLO 3 AMVF Mapping and Reflection Worksheet The course ends by asking what changed in your engineering judgment The final reflection is not a victory lap; it is evidence that you now think differently about experiments. Week 15 closes ASEN 3501 by returning to the question that started the course: how do you know this prediction is right? Your answer should be more disciplined now. It should include a model claim, a measurement strategy, uncertainty, a validation metric, intended use, and a willingness to revise the model when evidence demands it. Use this worksheet to map that growth and to prepare your CLO 6 and course reflections. <image description: A three-stage course arc mapped onto the AMVF. E1 is labeled measure honestly, E2 is labeled predict before measuring, and E3 is labeled design the evidence. Each stage connects to AMVF nodes for model, experiment, data, uncertainty, comparison, and model update. A final reflection card asks what changed in my judgment.> Part 1 - Trace One Engineering Decision Across The Course Choose one engineering decision that appeared in more than one segment. Good options include sensor selection, uncertainty interpretation, calibration, sampling/filtering, validation metric choice, or model update. Decision chosen: E1 - Given experiment — How was this decision constrained when the experiment was mostly provided to you? Response: E2 - Canned system with prediction — How did the decision become more analytical when you had to predict, compare, and improve an existing experiment? Response: E3 - Student-designed experiment — How did the decision become your responsibility when you designed the experiment on paper? Response: AMVF mapping — Where does this decision sit on the AMVF? Mark the nodes it affects: model, physical realization, measurement, data reduction, uncertainty, comparison, intended use, or model update. Response: Part 2 - CLO 6 Evidence CLO 6 asks you to identify, evaluate, and create or adapt experimental methods beyond what was handed to you. This is not about making the project bigger. It is about showing independent professional judgment. What did you investigate beyond provided course materials? Name the sensor, method, standard, platform, datasheet, handbook, paper, application note, or analysis approach. Response: Why did you investigate it? What gap, uncertainty, limitation, or design question prompted the search? Response: What sources did you consult? List the specific sources and what each contributed. Response: What did you find? Compare the alternative to your baseline option using technical criteria such as range, accuracy, bandwidth, calibration, feasibility, cost, installation, uncertainty, or validation credibility. Response: Did it change the design? If yes, explain what changed. If no, explain whether it strengthened confidence in the original choice. Response: Professional growth — What does this investigation tell you about how you will approach unfamiliar measurement problems in senior design or professional work? Response: <image description: A reflection worksheet panel titled beyond provided investigation. It shows a baseline course option compared against an independently found alternative, with rows for source, technical advantage, limitation, feasibility, and effect on design decision. A final arrow points to professional judgment.> Part 3 - AI-Assisted Claim Verification If AI helped you brainstorm, compare options, draft checklists, or identify risks, identify one technical claim that came from or was shaped by AI. AI-assisted claim: How did you verify it? Name the source that confirmed, corrected, or rejected the claim. Response: What did this teach you about using AI in experimental engineering? Response: Part 4 - Course Reflection Prompts Answer with concrete examples from your own work. Uncertainty — What did you misunderstand early, and what do you understand now? Response: Prediction — How did committing before data changed your reasoning from E1/E2 to E3? Response: Validation — What does it mean to validate or evaluate a model now, compared with what you would have said in Week 1? Response: Experimental design — What would you do differently if you were starting Week 1 again? Response: Final answer — In your current engineering voice, answer: how do you know this prediction is right? Response: The Takeaway: A strong final reflection proves transfer. It shows that you can carry the course method into a new problem: define the model, design the evidence, quantify uncertainty, compare honestly, and update what you believe.
